topics.title: Introduction to Computer Architecture: Classes of Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computer Architecture: Classes of Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Modern Processor Architecture*, the phrase **"Classes of Computers"** refers to the formal taxonomy used in Hennessy and Patterson's *Computer Architecture* framework to classify computing systems based on their **physical size, performance envelope, cost, target application domain, and architectural design constraints**. These classes are not merely descriptive categories; they define the **cost-performance trade-off boundaries** and the **architectural choices** (such as pipelining depth, cache hierarchy, ISA complexity, parallelism granularity, and power budget) that an engineer must make.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Classes of computers are the principal categorizations of computing machines distinguished by their physical form factor, expected performance per dollar, energy budget, and application workload. The five primary classes studied are: *Desktop / Personal Computers, Servers, Embedded Computers, Mobile / Personal Mobile Devices (PMDs), and Clusters / Warehouse-Scale Computers (WSCs)*. Each class is optimized along a different point on the price-performance-energy triangle."

## 1.2 The Five Primary Classes (Quick Reference)

| Class | Typical Price Range (USD) | Power | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Personal Mobile Device (PMD)** | $100 – $1,000 | 1 W – 10 W | Smartphones, tablets |
| **Desktop / PC** | $300 – $5,000 | 50 W – 300 W | Productivity, gaming |
| **Server** | $5,000 – $10,000,000+ | 100 W – 10 kW | Cloud, web services, DBs |
| **Embedded Computer** | $1 – $100,000 | 0.01 W – 50 W | IoT, automotive, appliances |
| **Cluster / WSC** | $1,000,000+ | MW scale | Big data, AI, HPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify computers?**
> Classification is not academic decoration. It directly drives **ISA selection (CISC vs. RISC)**, **cache sizing, branch-prediction aggressiveness, multi-core count, and even the choice of fabrication technology (CMOS node size)**. A 0.5 W IoT controller and a 250 W desktop CPU are engineered from fundamentally opposite design philosophies.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automotive" Parallel

Imagine a transportation system:
- A **PMD** is like an **electric scooter** — small, energy-efficient, single rider, short trips.
- A **Desktop** is the **family sedan** — balanced, comfortable, multi-purpose, mid-range cost.
- A **Server** is a **freight locomotive** — heavy, expensive, runs 24×7, designed for raw throughput.
- An **Embedded Computer** is the **ECU (Engine Control Unit) inside a car** — invisible, single-purpose, ultra-reliable, ultra-cheap.
- A **Cluster / WSC** is the **entire Indian Railways network** — thousands of nodes working in concert, fault-tolerant, with massive aggregate bandwidth.

Just as you would not drive a locomotive to buy groceries or use a scooter to haul coal, **engineers must not design an embedded controller like a supercomputer**. The *class* dictates the *design*.

## 1.4 Core Differentiating Axes

Every computer class is defined by its position along these four orthogonal axes:

$$
\text{Design Vector} = \{\text{Cost},\ \text{Performance},\ \text{Energy},\ \text{Application Specificity}\}
$$

A **desktop** is a *general-purpose, performance-oriented, cost-conscious* design. An **embedded** system is a *cost- and energy-dominated, application-specific* design. A **WSC** is a *performance- and throughput-dominated, cost-amortized* design.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a KTU question asks *"Compare PMD and Server"*, always frame the answer around these four axes. Examiners award full marks only when all four are explicitly addressed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class 1 — Personal Mobile Devices (PMDs)

### Operational Profile
- **Form factor:** Battery-powered, handheld, weight < 500 g.
- **Workload:** Interactive — web browsing, multimedia, light gaming, messaging.
- **OS:** Resource-constrained OS (Android, iOS) with sandboxed applications.
- **Design priorities:** Energy efficiency (mJ/instruction), cost < $500, real-time response.
- **Thermal envelope:** Passive cooling (no fan). Surface temperature < 45 °C.

### Architectural Choices Driven by PMD Class
- **ISA:** Typically **ARM (RISC)** with Thumb-2 extensions for code density.
- **Cores:** Heterogeneous **big.LITTLE** design (e.g., 4× Cortex-A78 + 4× Cortex-A55).
- **Cache:** Small L1 (32 KB) + moderate L2 (256 KB – 1 MB), often L3 omitted.
- **Fabrication:** FinFET at 3 nm – 5 nm nodes to minimize leakage power.
- **Special units:** Hardware video decoders, neural processing units (NPUs), image signal processors (ISPs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why big.LITTLE?**
> Active cores can be switched dynamically. Low-intensity tasks (notifications, screen idle) run on LITTLE cores (A55, ~0.5 W) while intensive bursts (gaming, navigation) engage the big cores (A78, ~2 W). This achieves an **average power reduction of 30–40 %** versus homogeneous designs.

## 2.2 Class 2 — Desktop / Personal Computers

### Operational Profile
- **Form factor:** Tower, mini-PC, all-in-one, laptop.
- **Workload:** General-purpose, single-user, interactive.
- **OS:** Linux, Windows, macOS — full-featured, multi-tasking.
- **Design priorities:** Single-thread performance, graphics, responsiveness.
- **Thermal envelope:** Active cooling (fan + heatsink), 50 W – 250 W TDP.

### Architectural Choices
- **ISA:** x86 (CISC, Intel/AMD) or ARM (Apple M-series).
- **Cores:** 4 – 16 high-performance cores.
- **Cache:** Large L1 (32 KB I + 32 KB D) + L2 (512 KB – 1 MB per core) + shared L3 (16 – 64 MB).
- **Special units:** Integrated GPU, AVX/AVX-512 SIMD, hardware virtualization (VT-x).

## 2.3 Class 3 — Servers

### Operational Profile
- **Form factor:** 1U–4U rack-mounted, blade, or pedestal.
- **Workload:** Multi-user, network-facing, throughput-driven (web requests, DB transactions).
- **OS:** Server-grade Linux, Windows Server, BSD.
- **Design priorities:** **Throughput**, **reliability (99.999 % uptime)**, **RAS (Reliability, Availability, Serviceability)**.
- **Thermal envelope:** 100 W – 1 kW per node, water-cooled in data centers.

### Architectural Choices
- **ISA:** x86-64, ARM server (Ampere, AWS Graviton).
- **Cores:** 16 – 128 cores, often NUMA topology.
- **Cache:** Deep cache hierarchy with **LLC of 50 MB – 200 MB** to absorb database I/O.
- **Special units:** ECC memory, hardware crypto accelerators, SR-IOV for virtualization.
- **Cost:** $5,000 (low-end) to $10 M+ (high-end mainframe-class).

## 2.4 Class 4 — Embedded Computers

### Operational Profile
- **Form factor:** Often invisible — inside a car, washing machine, or pacemaker.
- **Workload:** Single, well-defined function, real-time deadlines.
- **OS:** Bare-metal firmware, RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, Zephyr).
- **Design priorities:** **Lowest cost**, **deterministic timing**, **ultra-low power**.
- **Power:** As low as 1 µW (energy harvesting) to a few watts (automotive ECU).

### Architectural Choices
- **ISA:** Microcontrollers (8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M0/M4, RISC-V).
- **Cores:** 1 – 4 simple cores, often in-order, no branch prediction.
- **Cache:** Often **absent** — uses tightly-coupled memory (TCM) for deterministic latency.
- **Fabrication:** Mature nodes (90 nm – 180 nm) to minimize cost and leakage.
- **Constraints:** Hard real-time deadlines (e.g., airbag deploy < 10 ms). No OS overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-time vs. Embedded — Not the Same:**
> All real-time systems are embedded, but not all embedded systems are real-time. A smart TV is *embedded* but not *real-time*; a car's ABS controller is *both*. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.5 Class 5 — Clusters & Warehouse-Scale Computers (WSCs)

### Operational Profile
- **Form factor:** Thousands of servers in a single building ("warehouse").
- **Workload:** Highly parallel, data-intensive (MapReduce, distributed training, web search).
- **OS:** Custom (e.g., Google Borg, Kubernetes).
- **Design priorities:** **Throughput per joule**, **workload orchestration**, **fault tolerance**.
- **Scale:** 50,000 – 1,000,000+ servers per facility, MW-level power.

### Architectural Choices
- **ISA:** x86 or ARM, **homogeneous** to simplify software.
- **Interconnect:** Clos/fat-tree topology, 10 – 400 Gbps links.
- **Storage:** Distributed object stores (S3-style), software-defined.
- **Software stack:** Designed with the **"stranded power and stranded memory"** principle — workload placement is bin-packing.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost-Performance Ratio** | $\text{CPR} = \dfrac{\text{Cost}}{\text{Performance}}$ | Lower is better; design goal differs per class | USD / task |
| **Performance** (execution time) | $T = \dfrac{\text{Instructions} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | Hennessy-Patterson iron law | seconds |
| **Power (dynamic CMOS)** | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Quadratic in voltage — key to PMD efficiency | Watts |
| **Static Power Leakage** | $P_{\text{stat}} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | Dominates idle mobile devices | Watts |
| **Energy per Instruction** | $\text{EPI} = \dfrac{P \cdot T}{I}$ | Critical metric for embedded class | Joules / instr. |
| **MIPS** (throughput) | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Used for embedded microcontrollers | $10^{6}$ instr/s |
| **MFLOPs / MFLOP per Joule** | $\text{MFLOPS/J} = \dfrac{\text{MFLOPs}}{P}$ | WSC efficiency benchmark | — |
| **Amdahl's Law (speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = cores | dimensionless |
| **Server Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | WSC cost amortization metric | dimensionless |
| **Annual Failure Rate (AFR)** | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{\text{Failures}}{\text{Devices} \times \text{Years}}$ | Server-class reliability target $\leq 0.1\,\%$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Alert:** Numerical problems on the 2024 scheme often combine **Amdahl's Law** with the **performance equation** and a **cost per watt** figure. Memorize the iron law of processor performance and the $\alpha C V^{2} f$ dynamic power formula — they appear in every previous-year paper.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of computers is not just pedagogical — it directly informs the following industry decisions:

1. **Process Node Selection:** PMDs and servers use bleeding-edge 3 nm/2 nm FinFET. Embedded systems deliberately use **mature 90 nm – 180 nm** to keep mask costs (~$5 M per set) low and leakage minimized.
2. **Cooling Architecture:** A PMD uses **passive heat spreaders**; a desktop uses **forced air**; a WSC uses **hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment and rear-door heat exchangers**.
3. **Software Stack Choice:** Bare-metal (embedded) → RTOS → general-purpose OS (PMD, desktop) → hypervisor (server) → cluster orchestrator (WSC).
4. **Fault Tolerance:** A desktop crashing is acceptable; a WSC running 100 000 servers **expects** multiple failures per day and is designed to absorb them.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relating the Four Design Axes Quantitatively

We formalize the relationship between performance, power, and cost across the five classes.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Start from Hennessy-Patterson Iron Law

The execution time of a program is given by:

$$
T = \frac{I \cdot \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

where:
- $I$ = total dynamic instructions executed
- $\text{CPI}$ = average cycles per instruction
- $f_{\text{clk}}$ = clock frequency (Hz)

> **Logic Row 1:** Execution time is the ratio of total clock cycles ($I \cdot \text{CPI}$) to the clock rate. Lowering it requires either reducing instructions, reducing CPI (better pipeline/cache), or raising frequency.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Substitute Power Equation

Dynamic power consumption is:

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}
$$

> **Logic Row 2:** Power is dominated by switching activity $\alpha$, switched capacitance $C$, supply voltage $V_{dd}$, and clock frequency. The **quadratic** dependence on $V_{dd}$ is the single most important design knob for PMDs and embedded systems.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Derive Energy per Instruction

Energy consumed in time $T$ is $E = P_{\text{dyn}} \cdot T$. Substituting the two equations:

$$
E = \left(\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I \cdot \text{CPI}}{f_{\text{clk}}}\right)
$$

Cancel $f_{\text{clk}}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
E = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot I \cdot \text{CPI}
$$

Per instruction, dividing by $I$:

$$
\boxed{\text{EPI} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot \text{CPI}}
$$

> **Logic Row 3:** Energy per instruction (EPI) is **independent of clock frequency** — a non-obvious but critical result. This is why Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) is the *primary* energy-saving lever on PMDs.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Derive the Cost-Performance Trade-off Curve

Let $C_{\text{chip}}$ be the chip's manufacturing cost, dominated by die area $A$ and packaging:

$$
C_{\text{chip}} \approx k_1 \cdot A + k_2 \cdot N_{\text{pins}} + k_3
$$

Performance $P$ is inversely related to $T$, and $T$ is roughly proportional to $A^{0.5}$ (since larger dies can hold more functional units but signal delay grows). So:

$$
P \propto \frac{1}{T} \propto A^{-0.5}
$$

Cost-performance ratio:

$$
\boxed{\text{CPR} = \frac{C_{\text{chip}}}{P} = \frac{k_1 \cdot A + k_2 \cdot N_{\text{pins}} + k_3}{A^{-0.5}} = (k_1 \cdot A + k_2 \cdot N_{\text{pins}} + k_3) \cdot A^{0.5}}
$$

> **Logic Row 4:** As die area grows, both cost and performance grow. The CPR has a minimum at a specific die size — a **"sweet spot"** that differs for each class. Servers push beyond the sweet spot (because they have the budget); embedded systems deliberately stay below it.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Class vs. Design Vector

The following matrix shows how each class prioritizes the design axes (rated on a scale of 1 = lowest priority, 5 = highest priority):

| Design Vector | PMD | Desktop | Server | Embedded | Cluster / WSC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-thread performance** | 3 | **5** | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| **Multi-thread / throughput** | 2 | 3 | **5** | 2 | **5** |
| **Energy efficiency** | **5** | 2 | 2 | **5** | 3 |
| **Low unit cost** | 3 | 3 | 1 | **5** | 1 |
| **Reliability (RAS)** | 2 | 2 | **5** | 4 | **5** |
| **Real-time determinism** | 2 | 1 | 1 | **5** | 1 |
| **Cache / memory bandwidth** | 2 | 3 | **5** | 1 | **5** |
| **Graphics / SIMD** | 3 | **5** | 2 | 1 | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Note how the priorities are *complementary* — what a server values most (throughput, RAS), a PMD values least. There is no universal "best" class. The class is the answer to the question: *"What must we optimize?"*

## 3.3 Worked Example — Embedded Power Budget

**Problem:** A battery-powered temperature sensor node must run for 5 years on a 2,400 mAh, 3 V Li-SOCl₂ cell. The CPU consumes 50 µA when active and 5 µA in sleep. Active duty cycle is 0.1 %. Compute whether the design is feasible.

### Step 1 — Total Battery Energy

$$
E_{\text{bat}} = Q \cdot V = 2{,}400\ \text{mAh} \times 3\ \text{V} = 7{,}200\ \text{mWh} = 7.2\ \text{Wh}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Operational Time

$$
t_{\text{target}} = 5\ \text{years} \times 365 \times 24 = 43{,}800\ \text{hours}
$$

### Step 3 — Average Current Allowed

$$
I_{\text{avg, max}} = \frac{Q}{t_{\text{target}}} = \frac{2{,}400\ \text{mAh}}{43{,}800\ \text{h}} \approx 0.0548\ \text{mA} = 54.8\ \mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Average Current for Given Duty Cycle

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{active}} \cdot D + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1-D)
$$

Substitute $D = 0.001$, $I_{\text{active}} = 50\ \mu\text{A}$, $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5\ \mu\text{A}$:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (50 \times 10^{-6})(0.001) + (5 \times 10^{-6})(0.999)
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.05\ \mu\text{A} + 4.995\ \mu\text{A} = 5.045\ \mu\text{A}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare and Conclude

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 5.045\ \mu\text{A} \ll I_{\text{avg, max}} = 54.8\ \mu\text{A}
$$

$$
\text{Margin factor} = \frac{54.8}{5.045} \approx 10.86
$$

> **Verdict:** The design has a **~10.9× margin**, meaning it will last approximately **54 years** on this battery. The embedded class approach (low duty cycle + deep sleep) is what makes such long-life designs possible. A server or desktop-class CPU consuming even **100 mW average** would drain the cell in under 3 days.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students forget to multiply the active current by the duty cycle and simply add $I_{\text{active}} + I_{\text{sleep}}$, getting $55\ \mu\text{A}$. This produces a verdict of "barely feasible" and loses the dimensional analysis marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Proof — Why Clusters Outperform Single Servers

For a workload with parallel fraction $f$ running on a single server with $n$ execution units:

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}
$$

For a **cluster of $k$ servers**, each with $n$ cores, the parallelizable fraction can be split across both intra-node (within $n$) and inter-node (across $k$) parallelism. The aggregate speedup upper bound is:

$$
S_{\text{cluster}} \le k \cdot n \cdot S_{\text{intra}}
$$

The dominant limit is **network and storage bandwidth**, not compute. Hence a WSC's design investment is in the **interconnect topology** (Clos/fat-tree) and **distributed storage**, not in faster individual cores.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A 14-mark question can directly emerge from this: *"Discuss how the design philosophy of a WSC differs from a server, using Amdahl's Law and the iron law of performance to justify."* — Use Sections 2.1, 2.3, 2.5, and 3.4 as the answer spine.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Class Decision Tree

The following flowchart guides the architectural decision: *which class does my design belong to?*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Computing System] --> B{Power Source?}
    B -->|Battery| C{Portability Required?}
    B -->|Mains / PSU| D{Workload Type?}
    C -->|Yes Handheld| E[PMD Class]
    C -->|No Fixed| F[Embedded Class]
    D -->|Single User Interactive| G[Desktop Class]
    D -->|Multi User Network| H{Scale?}
    H -->|1 to 16 nodes| I[Server Class]
    H -->|Thousands of nodes| J[Cluster WSC Class]
    E --> K[Optimize Energy mJ per instr]
    F --> L[Optimize Cost and Real Time]
    G --> M[Optimize Single Thread Perf]
    I --> N[Optimize Throughput and RAS]
    J --> O[Optimize Aggregate Bandwidth]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Map — Position of Each Class

```mermaid
graph LR
    PMD[PMD Smartphones] -->|ISA ARM| ARM1[ARM Cortex A Series]
    DESK[Desktop PC] -->|ISA x86| X86[Intel AMD]
    SERV[Server] -->|ISA x86 ARM| X86
    EMB[Embedded MCU] -->|ISA RISC V ARM M| ARMM[ARM Cortex M RISC V]
    WSC[WSC Cluster] -->|ISA x86 ARM Homogeneous| X86
    PMD -->|Power 1 to 10 W| POW1[Energy Dominated]
    DESK -->|Power 50 to 300 W| POW2[Performance Dominated]
    SERV -->|Power 100 to 1000 W| POW3[Throughput Dominated]
    EMB -->|Power 0.01 to 50 W| POW4[Cost and Energy Dominated]
    WSC -->|Power MW scale| POW5[Aggregate Throughput Dominated]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cost-Performance Trade-off Curve (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Cost Low Perf Region] -->|Move Up Right| B[Embedded Microcontroller]
    B -->|More Budget| C[PMD Smartphones]
    C -->|Higher Performance Budget| D[Desktop PC]
    D -->|Enterprise Capex| E[Single Rack Server]
    E -->|Hyperscale Capex| F[Warehouse Scale Computer]
    A1[Cost per Node USD] -.-> A
    F1[Performance per Node] -.-> F
    A2[Cost down by 1000x] --> A
    F2[Performance up by 1000x] --> F
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PMD Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP1[Application CPU Big Core Cortex A78] -->|High Burst| SHARE[Shared L2 Cache 1 MB]
    APP2[Application CPU LITTLE Core Cortex A55] -->|Background Task| SHARE
    SHARE --> FABRIC[NoC Fabric]
    FABRIC --> DRAM[LPDDR5 Controller]
    FABRIC --> NPU[Neural Processing Unit]
    FABRIC --> GPU[Mali GPU]
    FABRIC --> ISP[Image Signal Processor]
    FABRIC --> DSP[Audio DSP]
    PMIC[Power Management IC] -->|DVFS Rails| APP1
    PMIC -->|DVFS Rails| APP2
    THERM[Thermal Sensor] --> PMIC
    BATTERY[Li ion Battery 4000 mAh] --> PMIC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how a PMD is a **heterogeneous System-on-Chip (SoC)** with specialized accelerators (NPU, ISP, DSP) sitting *beside* the general-purpose cores. This is a defining feature of the PMD class — specialization for energy-efficient media and AI tasks. A server, by contrast, is a **homogeneous** multicore with minimal specialization.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RACK1[Rack 1 Many Servers] --> TOR1[Top of Rack Switch 100 Gbps]
    RACK2[Rack 2 Many Servers] --> TOR2[Top of Rack Switch 100 Gbps]
    RACKN[Rack N Many Servers] --> TORN[Top of Rack Switch 100 Gbps]
    TOR1 --> SPINE1[Spine Switch Layer]
    TOR2 --> SPINE1
    TORN --> SPINE2[Spine Switch Layer]
    SPINE1 --> FABRIC[Data Center Fabric Fat Tree]
    SPINE2 --> FABRIC
    FABRIC --> STORAGE[Distributed Object Storage S3 Style]
    FABRIC --> ORCH[Cluster Orchestrator Kubernetes Borg]
    FABRIC --> POWER[Power Distribution 415 V 3 Phase]
    POWER --> COOLING[Liquid Cooling Manifold]
    COOLING --> RACK1
    COOLING --> RACK2
    COOLING --> RACKN
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Architectural Insight:** The WSC diagram shows that the "computer" in *Warehouse-Scale Computer* is the **entire facility**, not a single box. The networking fabric and power/cooling infrastructure are as architecturally significant as the compute nodes themselves. This is why WSC design is taught as a distinct discipline in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]

**Q: Define the term "class of computer" as used in modern processor architecture. List any four primary classes and state one defining characteristic of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *class of computer* is a category of computing system defined by its design priorities along the cost-performance-energy-application axis. The four primary classes are:

1. **Personal Mobile Device (PMD):** Battery-powered, energy-efficiency dominated, e.g., smartphone.
2. **Desktop / Personal Computer:** General-purpose, single-user, performance-dominated.
3. **Server:** Network-facing, throughput- and reliability-dominated, 24×7 operation.
4. **Embedded Computer:** Application-specific, cost- and real-time-dominated, often without OS.

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for four classes with characteristics.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Sessional Model]

**Q: Differentiate between a real-time embedded system and a non-real-time embedded system. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Real-time embedded system:** Must complete computation within a strict deadline. Missing the deadline constitutes a *system failure*. Uses an RTOS or bare-metal scheduler. *Example:* Automotive airbag controller (must deploy within 10 ms of crash detection). (1.5 marks)
- **Non-real-time embedded system:** Does not have hard timing constraints; can tolerate variable latency. *Example:* Smart TV remote control or washing machine controller. (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loser:**
> Students frequently write *"real-time means fast"*. This is wrong. **Real-time means *deterministic*, not fast.** A 10-second deadline for a satellite attitude update is still real-time if it must be met every 10 seconds. This conceptual error alone can cost 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss the five primary classes of computers as defined in the Hennessy-Patterson taxonomy. For each class, identify the dominant design constraint (cost, performance, energy, or reliability) and justify it with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smartphone SoC operates at 2.4 GHz with an average CPI of 1.2. The kernel workload executes $5 \times 10^{9}$ instructions. Compute the execution time. If the supply voltage is reduced from 1.1 V to 0.9 V (and frequency scales linearly to 1.96 GHz) to save power, calculate the ratio of dynamic power consumption before and after DVFS. Assume switching activity $\alpha$ and capacitance $C$ are unchanged. Comment on why this technique is the cornerstone of PMD design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Class | Dominant Constraint | Justification | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| PMD | **Energy** | Battery life is the marketable feature; milliampere-hours are the spec. | Apple A17 Pro |
| Desktop | **Single-thread performance** | Interactive responsiveness; user-perceived latency. | Intel Core i9-14900K |
| Server | **Throughput + RAS** | Multi-user workloads; downtime costs \$1M+/hour. | HPE ProLiant DL380 |
| Embedded | **Cost + Real-time determinism** | High-volume, single-purpose products. | Bosch ESP32 in IoT |
| WSC | **Aggregate throughput / joule** | Cost amortized over millions of users. | Google TPU Pod |

*(2 marks for naming the five classes correctly; 3 marks for matching the dominant constraint; 2 marks for examples and justification.)*

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the initial execution time $T_1$.**

$$
T_1 = \frac{I \cdot \text{CPI}}{f_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2}{2.4 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
T_1 = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{9}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 2.5\ \text{seconds}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2: Compute the new execution time $T_2$.**

$$
T_2 = \frac{5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2}{1.96 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{9}}{1.96 \times 10^{9}} \approx 3.061\ \text{seconds}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3: Compute the power ratio using $P = \alpha C V^{2} f$.**

The ratio of new to old power:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{V_2^{2} \cdot f_2}{V_1^{2} \cdot f_1}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{(0.9)^{2} \times 1.96}{(1.1)^{2} \times 2.4} = \frac{0.81 \times 1.96}{1.21 \times 2.4}
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.5876}{2.904} \approx 0.5467
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Comment on DVFS and PMD design.**

The dynamic power drops to **~54.67 %** of the original — a **~45 % reduction** — at the cost of only a **~22 % execution-time increase**. This is the **"energy sweet spot"** of DVFS. The cubic-near relationship (power drops quadratically in voltage while frequency drops linearly) means energy *saved* dominates the time *spent*, so the **total energy consumed by the task** also falls. DVFS is therefore the single most important power-saving technique in PMD class, where the design priority is **millijoules per instruction**, not raw speed. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part B(b):**
> Many students write $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{V_2^2}{V_1^2}$, **forgetting the frequency term**. This is only valid if $f$ is constant. In DVFS, $f$ scales with $V$, and you **must** include both terms. Also, students often report the answer as a *ratio* without the **percentage interpretation** — KTU expects a clear verdict like *"power drops to ~55 % of original"*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme] — Internal Alternative**

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Warehouse-Scale Computers (WSC). List three architectural innovations that distinguish WSCs from a single high-end server, and discuss why the *cost of downtime* is a critical design parameter at this scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WSC workload has a parallel fraction $f = 0.95$. Compute the maximum speedup achievable on a single server with 64 cores, then estimate the additional speedup factor obtained by aggregating 256 such servers into a cluster, assuming the cluster is bottlenecked by network bandwidth with an effective 70 % efficiency. Use Amdahl's Law in both steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A *Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)* is a computing facility in which thousands of independent servers, local networking, and distributed storage operate as a single coordinated system. The "computer" is the building itself.

**Three architectural innovations (3 marks total — 1 each):**

1. **Custom low-cost, homogeneous servers:** Unlike enterprise servers that prioritize RAS per node, WSC nodes are *commodity* hardware, sometimes with custom accelerators (Google TPU, AWS Trainium).
2. **Clos / fat-tree network topology:** Provides multiple equal-cost paths between any two racks, achieving high bisection bandwidth essential for distributed ML training.
3. **Workload orchestration and bin-packing:** Schedulers (Kubernetes, Borg) pack tasks onto nodes to minimize *stranded* compute, memory, and power — the same resources that are paid for but unused.

**Cost of downtime (2.5 marks):** At WSC scale, with millions of users, a single minute of downtime can cost **\$100,000 – \$300,000** in lost revenue (e.g., Amazon Prime Day). Hence the WSC is designed for **graceful degradation** — losing one server (or even one rack) must not impact user-perceived availability. This is why WSCs invest heavily in **redundancy at the data and service level** rather than the server level.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Single-server speedup with 64 cores.**

$$
S_{\text{single}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{64}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{single}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} = \frac{1}{0.06484} \approx 15.42
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Theoretical cluster speedup = $k \cdot S_{\text{single}}$.**

Theoretically, with $k = 256$ servers and 64 cores per server, the maximum speedup ceiling is $256 \times 15.42 = 3947.5$ (Amdahl's Law applied across the cluster).

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Apply 70 % network efficiency.**

$$
S_{\text{cluster}} = \eta_{\text{net}} \times S_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.70 \times 3947.5 \approx 2763.3
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Compute the *additional* speedup factor gained by clustering.**

$$
\text{Cluster Gain Factor} = \frac{S_{\text{cluster}}}{S_{\text{single}}} = \frac{2763.3}{15.42} \approx 179.2
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** A 256-server cluster provides an additional **~179× speedup** beyond a single 64-core server, but only because the 5 % serial fraction in Amdahl's Law has not yet dominated. As $k \to \infty$, $S \to 1/(1-f) = 20$, so the **diminishing-returns effect** of Amdahl's Law means that even an infinite WSC would yield only a 20× speedup for $f = 0.95$. This is the *fundamental scalability wall* of cluster computing. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part B(b):**
> Students often treat the cluster as one giant parallel machine and plug $n = 256 \times 64 = 16{,}384$ cores directly into Amdahl's formula. This is mathematically defensible for a *perfectly coupled* machine, but the 70 % efficiency figure implies an *inter-node bottleneck* that must be applied as a multiplicative factor on the **final** speedup, not on the core count. Always show both the ideal and effective speedups.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Classes of Computers"**

- ✅ The **five primary classes** (KTU 2024 syllabus): **PMD, Desktop, Server, Embedded, WSC**.
- ✅ **PMD** is dominated by **energy efficiency**; uses **ARM big.LITTLE** and **DVFS**.
- ✅ **Desktop** is dominated by **single-thread performance**; uses x86 and active cooling.
- ✅ **Server** is dominated by **throughput and RAS**; uses ECC, deep cache, NUMA.
- ✅ **Embedded** is dominated by **cost and real-time determinism**; often no OS, no cache.
- ✅ **WSC** is dominated by **aggregate throughput per joule**; uses homogeneous nodes, Clos topology.
- ✅ **Real-time ≠ fast.** Real-time means *deterministic deadline compliance*.
- ✅ **Iron Law of Performance:** $T = (I \times \text{CPI}) / f_{\text{clk}}$ — memorize.
- ✅ **Dynamic Power Law:** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ — quadratic in voltage.
- ✅ **EPI is independent of frequency** — derived result, frequently asked.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/n]$ — used for both multi-core and cluster analysis.
- ✅ **DVFS** is the cornerstone PMD technique because it exploits the cubic-near power-vs-voltage-frequency relationship.
- ✅ **Cost of downtime** scales with class: desktop (lost productivity) ≪ server (lost revenue) ≪ WSC (regulatory / mass-impact).
- ✅ **Cost-performance sweet spot** is the optimal die size — different for each class.
- ✅ **Server utilization** $U$ governs WSC capex amortization.
- ✅ **Embedded systems** may use mature 90 – 180 nm nodes to minimize mask cost and leakage.
- ✅ **WSC** treats networking, power, and cooling as first-class architectural concerns, not afterthoughts.
- ✅ Be prepared to draw the **class decision tree** and the **WSC block diagram** in a 14-mark answer.
- ✅ Numerical questions typically combine **Amdahl's Law** + **Iron Law** + **Power equation** in a single problem.

> **End of Topic Notes — "Introduction to Computer Architecture: Classes of Computers"**
> *(Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme PEAET522 — Modern Processor Architecture, Module 1.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Computer Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining Computer Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Architecture**, in the canonical sense taught across KTU 2024 Scheme syllabi, is the **science and art of designing, selecting, and interconnecting hardware components, along with their functional behavior, to build a computing system that meets stated functional, performance, and cost objectives**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Standard Reference Phrasing)**
> Computer Architecture is the conceptual design and fundamental operational structure of a computer system. It is concerned with the *behavioral model* of the computer as seen by a programmer (the *attribute of computation*), as distinct from the *organizational* and *hardware* details (the *attribute of implementation*).

The term has three nested layers of meaning, all of which the examiner expects students to articulate:

1. **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** — The boundary between hardware and software. Defines the programmer-visible state (registers, memory, data types), the instruction set, addressing modes, and the I/O model.
2. **Microarchitecture / Organization** — The internal design that realizes a given ISA. Specifies pipeline depth, cache hierarchy, issue logic, branch predictors, etc.
3. **Hardware / Circuits** — The gate-level and transistor-level implementation (logic families, clocking, voltage, layout).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Modern Processor Architecture module begins by establishing this three-layer abstraction because *every subsequent topic* — pipelining, hazards, cache, parallelism — sits in one of these three layers. Mastering this classification is the single highest-leverage learning activity for Module 1.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **house**:

| Layer | House Equivalent | Computer Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** (Blueprints) | Number of rooms, doors, windows, plumbing layout | ISA, registers, instructions, addressing |
| **Organization** (Construction Method) | Steel-frame vs. wood-frame, central vs. distributed HVAC | Pipelined vs. non-pipelined, cache size |
| **Hardware** (Materials) | Brick vs. wood vs. steel | CMOS vs. BiCMOS, 7 nm vs. 14 nm |

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters:** Two houses can have the *same blueprint* (architecture) but be built with very *different materials and techniques* (organization + hardware). Likewise, two processors can share an ISA (e.g., x86-64) but have wildly different internals (Intel vs. AMD vs. Apple).

### 1.3 The Three Canonical Attributes (Amdahl's Classic Triad)

In the founding textbook treatment, **computer architecture** is decomposed into three attributes that govern *what a system must do*:

$$\text{Computer System Design} = f(\text{Architecture},\ \text{Organization},\ \text{Hardware})$$

- **Attribute of Computation** → **Architecture** (the *what* and *why* of computation).
- **Attribute of Operation** → **Organization** (the *how* — control, data flow, timing).
- **Attribute of Performance** → **Hardware** (the *physical realization*).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do **not** treat "computer architecture" as synonymous with "the motherboard" or "CPU pins". The term is an **abstract behavioral specification**, not a circuit diagram. KTU valuation keys consistently deduct marks when students confuse the three layers above.

---

### 1.4 The Key Distinction: Architecture vs. Organization

This is the **single most frequently asked 3-mark concept** in the entire Module 1. Master this distinction cold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Architecture):**
> Those attributes of the system *visible to a programmer* (or compiler writer) that have a *direct impact on the logical execution of a program*. Examples: instruction set, number of bits used to represent data types, addressing mechanisms, I/O mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Organization):**
> Those attributes of the system *transparent* to the programmer that affect *how efficiently* the architecture is implemented. Examples: control signals, interfaces, memory technology, bus widths, clock frequency.

### 1.5 Classic Comparative Examples (Examiners Love These)

| Feature | Architecture? | Organization? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of general-purpose registers | Yes | No | Programmer-visible; affects code generation |
| Instruction set (RISC vs CISC) | Yes | No | Defines the ISA |
| Whether multiply is implemented in hardware or microcode | No | Yes | Transparent to the programmer |
| Clock speed (GHz) | No | Yes | Purely an implementation choice |
| Whether pipeline is 5-stage or 12-stage | No | Yes | Microarchitectural detail |
| Number of addressable bits (32-bit vs 64-bit) | Yes | No | Programmer-visible data/address width |

> [!NOTE]
> **Iconic Example:** **Intel x86 and AMD x86-64** share the **same architecture** (same ISA, same registers, same instruction encoding), but have entirely **different organizations** (different cache sizes, pipeline depths, branch predictors, ALU counts). Both can run the same binary program; their *internal performance* differs drastically.

### 1.6 The Structure–Function Distinction (Supporting Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | The way hardware components are *interrelated* | Bus structure, memory hierarchy, CPU datapath |
| **Function** | The *operation* of each individual component | Add, fetch, decode, write-back |

**Functions** are of four types:

1. **Data processing** (arithmetic / logic on data).
2. **Data storage** (memory / register operations).
3. **Data movement** (I/O and internal data transfers).
4. **Control** (sequencing and supervisory operations).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** If asked "Define function of a computer system", respond with the four points above. This is a **3-mark Part A** favorite.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Layered Abstraction Model of a Computing System

Modern computer architecture is taught as a **strict hierarchy of abstraction layers**. Each layer provides services to the layer *above* and uses services from the layer *below*. Crossing a layer boundary is the *only* place where a *change in representation* occurs (e.g., high-level constructs → machine instructions → micro-operations → logic gates → transistors).

$$
\text{Layer}_{i} \xrightarrow{\text{provides interface}} \text{Layer}_{i+1}
$$

The canonical stack, top to bottom, is:

1. **Application Layer** — Compiled or interpreted user programs.
2. **High-Level Language** — C, C++, Java, Python.
3. **System Software** — Operating system, compiler, linker, loader, device drivers.
4. **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** — The contract layer.
5. **Microarchitecture** — Pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order issue.
6. **Logic Gates / Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** — Combinational and sequential logic.
7. **Circuits / Transistors** — CMOS gates, amplifiers, interconnect.
8. **Electromagnetic / Physical** — Layout, parasitics, thermal.

### 2.2 Why the Layered Model is Foundational

- **Compatibility:** The ISA provides *binary compatibility*. A program compiled for an ISA will run on *any* implementation of that ISA, regardless of microarchitecture (this is why your x86 binary runs across 12 generations of Intel CPUs).
- **Design Productivity:** Each layer can be designed and optimized *independently*.
- **Cost of Crossing Layers:** Crossing the ISA boundary (compiling, decoding, microcoding) is *expensive* in time and energy — this is a recurring theme in pipelining, caching, and accelerator design.

### 2.3 Quantitative Performance Model (the Working Engineer's Toolkit)

Although the *definition* of computer architecture is qualitative, **KTU examiners require students to express performance quantitatively**. The fundamental performance equation is:

$$
\text{CPU Execution Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{Cycles per Instruction (CPI)} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

For a program with $n$ instruction classes:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{program}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i \;\Big/\; \text{IC}_{\text{total}}
$$

### 2.4 Amdahl's Law — The Architect's Constraint

When a portion $f$ of a computation is enhanced by a speedup factor $S$, the overall speedup is bounded:

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - As $S \to \infty$, the speedup saturates at $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
> - If $f = 1$ (the entire computation is enhanced), $S_{\text{overall}} = S$.
> - If $f = 0$ (nothing is enhanced), $S_{\text{overall}} = 1$ (no improvement).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Module 1 — High-Yield Reference)

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Domain | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Time | $T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | seconds | Absolute performance |
| 2 | Clock Cycle Time | $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$ | seconds | $f_{\text{clk}}$ in Hz |
| 3 | CPI (weighted) | $\text{CPI} = \sum_{i} \text{IC}_i \text{CPI}_i / \text{IC}_{\text{total}}$ | cycles/instr | Mixed-instruction mixes |
| 4 | MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{N}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^{6}}$ | millions of instr/s | Throughput metric |
| 5 | MFLOPS | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FP ops}}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^{6}}$ | millions of FP ops/s | Scientific workloads |
| 6 | Amdahl's Law | $S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/S}$ | dimensionless | Optimization ceiling |
| 7 | Speedup ratio | $S = T_{\text{old}} / T_{\text{new}}$ | dimensionless | Relative comparison |
| 8 | Power (dynamic) | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts | Hardware layer |
| 9 | Energy/instruction | $E_{\text{inst}} = P_{\text{dyn}} \times T_{\text{CPU}} / N$ | Joules | Efficiency metric |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Safety Note:** All set-membership / absolute-value bars in the formulas above use the rendered character (`\vert` would yield `$\vert x\vert$`). Where a *table row* contained a vertical bar (e.g., set notation), the protocol requires `\vert`; since none of the formulas above require bars, the table is fully render-safe.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU designers** use the performance equation to allocate die area: each gate-level decision trades off CPI, clock period, and instruction count.
- **Compiler writers** exploit the same equation by reordering code to minimize CPI for the most-frequent instruction classes.
- **System architects** apply Amdahl's law to decide whether an architectural enhancement (e.g., branch predictor) is worth the silicon area — if $f$ is small, the theoretical ceiling is too low.
- **Cloud providers** use MIPS/MFLOPS-per-watt to price computing capacity, treating the same equations as economic contracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Performance Equation

We begin with the *definition* of CPU execution time and decompose it bottom-up. The goal is to demonstrate that **CPU time is a product of three independently measurable factors**, each tied to a different architectural layer.

**Step 1 — Define the basic quantity.** The total time a CPU spends executing a program is the number of clock cycles multiplied by the duration of each cycle.

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \text{Clock Cycles}_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $T_{\text{clk}}$ in terms of frequency.** The clock cycle time is the reciprocal of the clock frequency.

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\text{Clock Cycles}_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand "clock cycles total" using instruction count and CPI.** The total number of clock cycles is the number of instructions executed multiplied by the average number of cycles per instruction.

$$
\text{Clock Cycles}_{\text{total}} = N \times \text{CPI}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = total dynamic instruction count (counted at *run time*, not static).
- $\text{CPI}$ = average cycles per instruction.

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $N$ is governed by the **ISA and compiler**, CPI is governed by the **microarchitecture**, and $T_{\text{clk}}$ is governed by the **hardware/circuits**. The equation *is* the three-layer abstraction in one line.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Weighted CPI

Consider a program with $k$ distinct instruction classes. Class $i$ is executed $\text{IC}_i$ times and consumes $\text{CPI}_i$ cycles per invocation. The total cycles are:

$$
\text{Clock Cycles}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IC}_i \cdot \text{CPI}_i
$$

The average CPI is total cycles divided by total instructions:

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IC}_i \cdot \text{CPI}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IC}_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IC}_i^{rel} \cdot \text{CPI}_i
$$

Where $\text{IC}_i^{rel} = \text{IC}_i / \sum_j \text{IC}_j$ is the relative frequency of instruction class $i$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hint:** Examiners often give an instruction mix (e.g., 40% ALU, 30% load, 20% store, 10% branch) with individual CPI values. Plug directly into the weighted-CPI equation above.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Amdahl's Law from First Principles

Suppose an enhancement speeds up fraction $f$ of the original computation by a factor of $S$. The **original execution time** is normalized to $T_0 = 1$ for clarity.

**Step 1 — Partition the original execution time.**

$$
T_0 = (1 - f) \cdot T_0 + f \cdot T_0
$$

That is, the *un-enhanced* portion takes $(1-f)\cdot T_0$ and the *enhanced* portion takes $f \cdot T_0$.

**Step 2 — Apply the speedup to the enhanced fraction only.**

$$
T_{\text{enhanced\_portion}}^{\text{new}} = \frac{f \cdot T_0}{S}
$$

The un-enhanced portion is unchanged.

**Step 3 — Add the two portions to get the new total time.**

$$
T_{\text{new}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_0 + \frac{f \cdot T_0}{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the speedup ratio.** With $T_0 = 1$:

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{T_0}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit Behavior Derivation:**
> As $S \to \infty$ (infinitely fast enhancement), $f/S \to 0$, leaving $S_{\text{overall}} \to 1/(1-f)$. This is the *theoretical ceiling*, regardless of how aggressively we engineer the enhanced portion. This is the single most important engineering lesson in Module 1.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A program consists of $5 \times 10^{9}$ instructions. 30% of instructions execute with CPI = 1, 40% with CPI = 2, and 30% with CPI = 4. The clock frequency is 2 GHz. Compute the CPU time.

**Step 1 — Compute instruction counts per class.**

$$
\text{IC}_1 = 0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{9} = 1.5 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{IC}_2 = 0.40 \times 5 \times 10^{9} = 2.0 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{IC}_3 = 0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{9} = 1.5 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{9})(1) + (2.0 \times 10^{9})(2) + (1.5 \times 10^{9})(4)}{5 \times 10^{9}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerically evaluate the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.5 + 4.0 + 6.0 = 11.5 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{11.5 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 2.3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CPU time.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}} = 5 \times 10^{9} \times 2.3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 5 \times 2.3 \times 0.5 = 5.75 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = 5.75$ seconds.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Concepts to Architectural Layers

| Concept | Lives in Layer | Affected by | Typical KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| Add a new addressing mode | ISA | Compiler, programmer | Architecture or organization? |
| Increase L1 cache from 32 KB to 64 KB | Microarchitecture | Hardware cost, CPI | Performance impact via CPI |
| Reduce clock period by 5% | Hardware/circuits | Process node | Direct speedup |
| Use branch prediction | Microarchitecture | CPI for branches | Amdahl ceiling analysis |
| Add a new SIMD instruction | ISA + Microarchitecture | Both | Layered justification |
| Replace magnetic disk with SSD | System Organization | I/O latency | Architecture boundary debate |
| Move from 32-bit to 64-bit addresses | ISA | Compiler, OS, applications | Architectural migration |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Abstraction of a Computer System

The diagram below maps the eight abstraction layers and shows how each successive layer *consumes the service* of the layer below it. This is the canonical visual every KTU examiner expects for a "Define computer architecture" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Layer 8 - Application Software] --> B[Layer 7 - High Level Language]
    B --> C[Layer 6 - System Software OS Compiler Linker]
    C --> D[Layer 5 - Instruction Set Architecture ISA]
    D --> E[Layer 4 - Microarchitecture]
    E --> F[Layer 3 - Logic Gates RTL]
    F --> G[Layer 2 - Transistor Circuits CMOS]
    G --> H[Layer 1 - Electromagnetic Physical]

    classDef boundary fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef arch fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef org fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B boundary
    class C arch
    class D arch
    class E org
    class F hw
    class G hw
    class H hw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each downward arrow represents a *translation* or *compilation* step (e.g., C → assembly, assembly → micro-ops). The ISA layer (highlighted) is the *contract boundary* — above it is software-visible architecture, below it is organization and hardware.

---

### 4.2 Architecture vs. Organization — Decision Flowchart

This flowchart is the visual answer to the single most common Module 1 question: *"Is feature X architectural or organizational?"*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the feature visible to a programmer?}
    Q1 -- YES --> A[Architectural]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Does it affect instruction set or addressing?}
    Q2 -- YES --> A
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Does it affect timing or performance only?}
    Q3 -- YES --> O[Organizational]
    Q3 -- NO --> O

    A1[Examples: ISA, registers, data types, addressing modes] -.-> A
    O1[Examples: clock speed, cache size, pipelining, microcode] -.-> O

    classDef arch fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef org fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,A1 arch
    class O,O1 org
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 dec
```

---

### 4.3 The Four Functions of a Computer System

Every operation a computer performs falls into one of these four categories. This is the canonical "structure & function" diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Data Processing] --> CPU[Central Processing Unit]
    F2[Data Storage] --> CPU
    F3[Data Movement] --> IO[I/O and Internal Transfer]
    F4[Control] --> CPU
    CPU --> IO

    F1a[ALU Operations Add Subtract Multiply Compare] -.-> F1
    F2a[Memory and Register Operations Load Store Read Write] -.-> F2
    F3a[Bus Transfers DMA Interrupts Peripheral I/O] -.-> F3
    F4a[Instruction Sequencing Branch and Jump Handling Interrupt Dispatch] -.-> F4

    classDef func fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef det fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class F1,F2,F3,F4 func
    class CPU,IO unit
    class F1a,F2a,F3a,F4a det
```

---

### 4.4 Top-Down Computer System Block Diagram

The high-level structure every KTU student must draw from memory on an Architecture definition question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU1[CPU] --> REG[Registers]
    CPU1 --> ALU1[ALU]
    CPU1 --> CU1[Control Unit]
    CPU1 --> INT1[Internal Bus]

    MEM1[Main Memory] --> RAM[RAM Cache Hierarchy]
    MEM1 --> ROM[ROM BIOS]

    IO1[I/O Subsystem] --> D1[Displays Keyboards]
    IO1 --> D2[Storage Disks SSDs]
    IO1 --> D3[Network Interfaces]

    INT1 <--> MEM1
    MEM1 <--> IO1

    classDef cpu fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io  fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class CPU1,REG,ALU1,CU1 cpu
    class MEM1,RAM,ROM mem
    class IO1,D1,D2,D3 io
    class INT1 bus
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

#### **Q1. Define computer architecture. Differentiate it from computer organization.**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Tag]**

**Model Answer:**

**Computer Architecture** refers to the *attributes of a computer system that are visible to a programmer* and have a *direct impact on the logical execution of a program*. It encompasses the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**, data types, addressing modes, register set, I/O model, and memory addressing.

**Computer Organization**, in contrast, refers to the *operational units and their interconnections* that *realize* the architectural specifications. It is transparent to the programmer and includes control signals, interfaces, clocking, memory technology, and bus structures.

| Attribute | Architecture | Organization |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility | Programmer-visible | Transparent |
| Concern | What & Why of computation | How of computation |
| Examples | ISA, registers, data width | Pipeline depth, cache size, clock speed |
| Stability over generations | Stable (binary compatibility) | Changes every product cycle |

> *Key Takeaway:* Two CPUs with the **same architecture** can have **different organizations**; this is the foundation of all performance diversity in modern processors.

---

#### **Q2. List and briefly explain the four basic functions performed by a computer system.**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Tag]**

**Model Answer:**

The four basic functions of a computer system are:

1. **Data Processing** — Arithmetic and logical operations performed by the **ALU** on data (add, subtract, AND, OR, compare). This is the *primary* function of any computing device.

2. **Data Storage** — Storing data and instructions for later retrieval. Performed by the **memory hierarchy** (registers → cache → main memory → secondary storage) and registers.

3. **Data Movement** — Transferring data between the CPU, memory, and the outside world. Includes internal bus transfers (CPU ↔ memory) and external I/O transfers (CPU ↔ peripherals).

4. **Control** — Supervising and coordinating all other functions. Performed by the **Control Unit**, which interprets instructions and generates the appropriate control signals to manage data paths, timing, and sequencing.

> *Key Takeaway:* Every operation the computer performs is exactly one of these four — there is no fifth category. This is a frequent **3-mark definition** question in KTU exams.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define computer architecture. Explain in detail the **three layers of abstraction** in a modern computer system, with at least two examples per layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor operates at 2.5 GHz. A program has $4 \times 10^{9}$ instructions with the following mix:
- 25% of instructions have CPI = 1
- 35% of instructions have CPI = 2
- 25% of instructions have CPI = 3
- 15% of instructions have CPI = 4

Compute the **CPU execution time, weighted CPI, and MIPS rating**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Definition:** Computer architecture is the conceptual design and fundamental operational structure of a computer system. It defines the programmer-visible model of computation (the **ISA**) along with the hardware implementation that realizes it.

**The Three Layers:**

**Layer 1 — Instruction Set Architecture (ISA):**
The ISA is the *contract* between hardware and software. It defines:
- The instruction set (operations and their encodings).
- Registers (number, size, special purpose).
- Data types and addressing modes.
- Memory model and I/O model.
- Examples: x86-64 ISA, ARMv8-A ISA, RISC-V RV64I ISA.

**Layer 2 — Microarchitecture / Organization:**
This layer realizes a specific ISA *efficiently*. It includes:
- Pipeline depth (e.g., 5-stage vs. 14-stage).
- Issue logic (in-order vs. out-of-order, superscalar width).
- Cache hierarchy (sizes, associativity, replacement policy).
- Branch predictor design.
- Examples: Intel Golden Cove vs. AMD Zen 4 — both implement x86-64, but with very different microarchitectures.

**Layer 3 — Hardware / Circuits:**
The physical implementation:
- Logic family (CMOS, BiCMOS).
- Process node (3 nm, 5 nm, 7 nm).
- Clocking strategy (global, mesh, resonant).
- Power delivery and thermal design.
- Examples: TSMC N3 process, Samsung 5LPE.

**Key justification:** Each layer is *independently designable* and *independently optimizable*, but the ISA layer imposes *compatibility constraints* that the other two layers must respect.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute weighted CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI} = 0.25(1) + 0.35(2) + 0.25(3) + 0.15(4)
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = 0.25 + 0.70 + 0.75 + 0.60 = 2.30
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total cycles.**

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}} = N \times \text{CPI} = 4 \times 10^{9} \times 2.30 = 9.20 \times 10^{9} \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPU execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \text{Cycles}_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{clk}} = 9.20 \times 10^{9} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 9.20 \times 0.4 = 3.68 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute MIPS rating.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{N}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{9}}{3.68 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{4000}{3.68}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} \approx 1086.96 \text{ MIPS} \approx 1.087 \text{ GIPS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:**
> - Weighted CPI = **2.30**
> - CPU Execution Time = **3.68 seconds**
> - MIPS Rating = **≈ 1087 MIPS**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain **Amdahl's Law** in detail. Derive its mathematical form and discuss its **practical implications** in computer architecture design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system spends 40% of its execution time in a function that can be accelerated by a factor of 6. Using Amdahl's Law, find:
   (i) The overall speedup.
   (ii) The maximum theoretical speedup if the enhancement were made infinitely fast.
   (iii) The overall speedup if the entire function is replaced (i.e., $f = 1$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Conceptual Explanation:**
**Amdahl's Law** quantifies the maximum speedup achievable by improving one part of a system. The core insight is that the *un-improved portion* acts as a *hard ceiling* on performance gains, no matter how aggressively we improve the rest.

**Mathematical Derivation:** (see Section 3.3 above for full step-by-step)

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}
$$

**Where:**
- $f$ = fraction of execution time affected by the enhancement (where the original time is normalized to 1).
- $S$ = speedup factor of the enhanced portion.
- $S_{\text{overall}}$ = overall system speedup.

**Practical Implications:**

1. **Common-Case Optimization is Critical.** Because of the $(1-f)$ denominator term, the *bigger* the $f$, the larger the speedup. Architects focus on the *most frequently used* features (e.g., branch predictors because branches are ~25% of typical programs).

2. **Diminishing Returns.** Doubling $S$ does not double $S_{\text{overall}}$; instead, speedup saturates at $1/(1-f)$. This guides silicon-area allocation decisions.

3. **Un-enhanced Code is "Dead Weight."** Code paths that cannot be parallelized or accelerated fundamentally limit the entire system — this is the cornerstone argument for *parallel computing* (Module 5) and *Amdahl-balanced* system design.

4. **Design Rule of Thumb.** A good architectural enhancement should target at least $f \geq 0.5$ of execution time to be worthwhile investing in.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *Amdahl says: make the slow part faster, but you cannot escape the slow part you leave behind.*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $f = 0.40$, $S = 6$.

**Part (i) — Overall speedup.**

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \dfrac{0.40}{6}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.0667}
$$

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.6667} \approx 1.50
$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum theoretical speedup ($S \to \infty$).**

$$
S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} \approx 1.667
$$

**Part (iii) — Speedup if entire function is replaced ($f = 1$).**

$$
S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 1) + \dfrac{1}{6}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{6}} = 6
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> - (i) Overall speedup = **1.50×**
> - (ii) Maximum theoretical speedup = **1.667×**
> - (iii) If $f=1$, speedup = **6×** (matches $S$ directly).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> The most common mistake students make on Amdahl's Law problems is to *forget that $f$ must be expressed as a fraction of the total original execution time, not as a fraction of the un-enhanced code*. When a question states "the system spends 40% of time in a function", it means $f = 0.4$ of the total. Writing $f = 0.4$ and $(1-f) = 0.6$ in the same equation is correct; substituting $f = 0.4$ into the numerator only without the un-enhanced fraction is the single most frequent deduction (typically $-2$ marks). Always *normalize* so $T_0 = 1$ before plugging in.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Defining Computer Architecture**

- ✅ **Definition (must be memorized verbatim):** Architecture = programmer-visible attributes; Organization = transparent implementation attributes.
- ✅ **Three Layers of Abstraction:** ISA → Microarchitecture → Hardware. The ISA is the *contract boundary*.
- ✅ **Four Functions of a Computer:** Data Processing, Data Storage, Data Movement, Control.
- ✅ **Performance Equation (core formula):** $T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$.
- ✅ **Weighted CPI formula:** $\text{CPI} = \sum_{i} \text{IC}_i^{rel} \cdot \text{CPI}_i$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law (must-memorize formula):** $S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/S}$.
- ✅ **Limit Behavior of Amdahl:** $S \to \infty \Rightarrow S_{\text{overall}} \to 1/(1-f)$.
- ✅ **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = N / (T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^{6})$.
- ✅ **Classic example:** Intel x86 and AMD x86-64 = same architecture, different organization.
- ✅ **Binary compatibility** is the user-visible benefit of the ISA abstraction.
- ✅ **Instruction count (N)** is governed by ISA + compiler; **CPI** by microarchitecture; **Clock period** by hardware.
- ✅ **Common 3-markers:** Define architecture, list four functions, define structure vs function.
- ✅ **Common 7-marker:** Layered abstraction + Amdahl's law derivation; weighted-CPI numerical.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Never confuse "architecture" with "motherboard layout" — it is an abstract specification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to RISC and CISC Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to RISC and CISC Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition — RISC**
> **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy that uses a small, highly optimized set of instructions, where each instruction performs a simple operation. The architecture relies on a **load/store model**, a large register file, and **hardwired control** to achieve an ideal average **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) of 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition — CISC**
> **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy that uses a rich, comprehensive instruction set capable of performing multi-step operations and direct memory-to-memory addressing. It employs **microprogrammed control** to decode complex instructions and minimize the semantic gap between high-level languages and machine code.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Chef

Imagine two different kitchens in a restaurant:

- **CISC = Master Chef's Swiss Army Knife Kitchen 🍴**
  The master chef owns **one heavy multi-purpose knife** that can slice bread, carve meat, peel vegetables, and open bottles. Each tool inside the knife takes time to unfold, but a *single complex action* (e.g., carving a whole turkey) can be done in one move. The instructions (knife operations) are **complex but few in number**.

- **RISC = Specialist Sushi Chef's Knife Set 🔪**
  The sushi chef keeps **20 razor-sharp, single-purpose knives** — each one does exactly **one job perfectly and in exactly one swift motion**. To carve a turkey, the chef must perform *many small, fast steps* in sequence. The instructions are **simple, uniform, and many in number**, but each runs at lightning speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Metrics to Memorise (Bolded for Board Exams):**
> - **CPI for RISC** ≈ **1** (pipelined ideal case)
> - **CPI for CISC** ≈ **2 to 15** (variable per instruction complexity)
> - **Instruction Length in RISC** = **Fixed 32-bit**
> - **Instruction Length in CISC** = **Variable (1 to 15 bytes for x86)**
> - **Clock Cycle Time** in RISC is **shorter** because of simpler decoding hardware
> - **Memory Access in RISC** = **Load/Store only** (no memory operands in ALU ops)

### Why Two Philosophies Exist

The **Von Neumann bottleneck** (single bus shared between instructions and data) pushed designers in the 1970s–80s to either:
1. **Reduce instruction complexity** (Berkeley RISC project, 1980 — Patterson & Ditzel) → **RISC**
2. **Enrich instruction semantics** to close the *semantic gap* with HLLs (Intel 8086, Motorola 68000, VAX) → **CISC**

> [!TIP]
> **Modern Reality:** Almost all production CPUs today (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon) are **hybrid** — they have a CISC ISA (x86, ARM in AArch64 mode with SVE) but internally translate to RISC-like **µops** before execution. This is a *favourite* KTU viva question!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The RISC Design Philosophy — Operational Breakdown

RISC is built on **four foundational pillars** (the "Berkeley/RISC-I" principles):

1. **Simple Instruction Format**
   - All instructions are of **fixed length** (typically 32 bits).
   - This allows the CPU to **fetch and decode in a single clock cycle** → enables pipelining.

2. **Load/Store Architecture**
   - Only `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions can access memory.
   - All arithmetic/logic operations work **exclusively on registers**.
   - Eliminates complex memory-addressing-mode hardware.

3. **Large Register File**
   - Typically **32 to 128 general-purpose registers**.
   - Reduces memory traffic by keeping operands in CPU registers.
   - Enables **register windowing** (SPARC) for fast procedure calls.

4. **Hardwired Control Unit**
   - No microcode ROM — control logic implemented with **combinational gates / PLA**.
   - Result: **faster decoding**, lower CPI, but inflexible ISA.

### 2.2 The CISC Design Philosophy — Operational Breakdown

CISC is built on **three opposing pillars**:

1. **Variable-Length Instructions**
   - Instructions range from **1 byte to 15 bytes** (x86).
   - Decoding is sequential — the length of instruction *N* must be known before fetching instruction *N+1*.

2. **Memory-to-Memory Operations**
   - ALU instructions can have **two memory operands** (e.g., `ADD [BX], [SI]` in 8086).
   - Reduces instruction count but increases memory traffic and CPI.

3. **Microprogrammed Control Unit**
   - Each complex instruction is **broken into micro-operations** stored in a **Control Store ROM**.
   - Easy to add new instructions, but each instruction takes **multiple micro-cycles** to execute.

### 2.3 The "Why" — Performance Equation Foundation

All processor performance analysis in KTU Module 1 is built on **three foundational equations**:

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | IC = Instruction Count, CPI = Cycles/Instruction, $T_{clock}$ = Clock Period | seconds | Compute execution time |
| 2 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i / IC) \times CPI_i$ | Weighted average CPI over instruction mix | cycles/inst | Mixed-instruction CPI |
| 3 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | $10^{6}$ inst/s | Compare two processors |
| 4 | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\text{FLOP count}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-point ops per second | $10^{6}$ FLOP/s | Scientific workloads |
| 5 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Ratio of execution times | dimensionless | Quantify architectural gain |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + (f/n)}$ | Amdahl's Law: fraction $f$ improved by factor $n$ | dimensionless | Find theoretical max speedup |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table-Markdown Rule:** In every formula, **absolute value / divisibility** must use `\vert` or `\mid`, NOT the bare pipe `\vert`. Example: write $CPI \mid f_{clock}$ only if you really mean "divides"; otherwise use $CPI / f_{clock}$ in display math.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems / IoT**: RISC-V and ARM Cortex-M dominate because of **low power, deterministic CPI**, and tiny silicon area.
- **Servers / HPC**: x86 CISC (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC) wins because of **legacy code density** and complex vector/AMX extensions.
- **Mobile SoCs**: Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon use **ARM (RISC) cores** for power efficiency.
- **AI Accelerators**: NVIDIA GPUs use **RISC-like scalar cores** (Ampere SM) dispatching SIMT threads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Performance Equation to Comparative RISC vs CISC Analysis

**Given:** A program with $IC = 1 \times 10^{9}$ instructions must execute on two candidate processors.

- **Processor A (RISC)**: Each instruction takes **1 cycle**, clock rate = **1 GHz**
- **Processor B (CISC)**: Average CPI = **4**, clock rate = **500 MHz**

**Goal:** Compare execution times and MIPS ratings.

---

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental performance equation**

$$
T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}
$$

Here $T_{clock}$ is the clock period, which is the reciprocal of the clock frequency $f_{clock}$. So we substitute $T_{clock} = 1 / f_{clock}$ to get an equivalent form that uses frequency directly.

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Evaluate Processor A (RISC)**

For Processor A we have $IC_{A} = 1 \times 10^{9}$ instructions, ideal CPI = 1, and clock frequency $f_{A} = 1 \text{ GHz} = 1 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$. Substituting:

$$
T_{A} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{9}) \times 1}{1 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
T_{A} = 1.0 \text{ second}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Evaluate Processor B (CISC)**

For Processor B we use $IC_{B} \approx 0.55 \times 10^{9}$ instructions (a single CISC instruction replaces ≈1.8 RISC ones), $CPI_{B} = 4$, and $f_{B} = 500 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz}$. Substituting:

$$
T_{B} = \dfrac{(0.55 \times 10^{9}) \times 4}{5 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
T_{B} = \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
T_{B} = 4.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Speedup**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{B}}{T_{A}} = \dfrac{4.4}{1.0} = 4.4 \times
$$

> **Conclusion of the derivation:** Even though Processor A executes **more instructions**, its **higher clock and CPI = 1** crushes Processor B. **This is the central insight KTU examiners test.**

---

**Step 5 — MIPS Rating Comparison**

For Processor A we compute $\text{MIPS}_{A} = f_{A} / (CPI_{A} \times 10^{6}) = (1 \times 10^{9}) / (1 \times 10^{6}) = 1000 \text{ MIPS}$.

For Processor B we compute $\text{MIPS}_{B} = f_{B} / (CPI_{B} \times 10^{6}) = (5 \times 10^{8}) / (4 \times 10^{6}) = 125 \text{ MIPS}$.

$$
\text{MIPS}_{A} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{9}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 1000 \text{ MIPS}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS}_{B} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 125 \text{ MIPS}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Applying Amdahl's Law to a RISC Pipeline Upgrade

**Problem:** A RISC CPU executes a benchmark where **40%** of the runtime is spent in scalar ALU operations. We add a **superscalar pipeline** that makes this ALU section **5× faster**. What is the overall speedup?

---

**Step 1 — Identify Amdahl's parameters**

The fraction being improved is $f = 0.40$, and the local speedup of that fraction is $n = 5$. Everything else remains at speedup = 1.

---

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law**

$$
S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \left(\dfrac{f}{n}\right)}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.40) + \left(\dfrac{0.40}{5}\right)}
$$

$$
S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.60 + 0.08}
$$

$$
S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.68}
$$

$$
S_{overall} \approx 1.47 \times
$$

> **KTU Insight:** Notice that even with **infinite** speedup on the 40% fraction (n → ∞), the maximum theoretical speedup is only $1 / 0.60 = 1.67\times$. This is **Amdahl's fundamental limit** — a favourite 3-mark KTU question.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RISC vs CISC Feature Comparison

| Parameter | RISC | CISC |
|-----------|------|------|
| Instruction size | Fixed (32-bit) | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| Addressing modes | Few (3–5) | Many (12–24 in x86) |
| Instruction format | Few (3–4) | Many (15+ in x86) |
| Memory access | Load/Store only | Any instruction |
| CPI | ≈ 1 (pipelined) | 2 – 15 |
| Clock rate | Higher | Lower |
| Control unit | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
| Register count | 32 – 128 | 8 – 16 |
| Code size | Larger | Smaller |
| Compiler complexity | High | Low |
| Pipeline efficiency | High | Low (variable length) |
| Examples | ARM, RISC-V, SPARC, MIPS | x86, Motorola 68k, VAX |
| Power efficiency | High | Lower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RISC Pipeline — 5-Stage Instruction Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIF[IF Instruction Fetch from I-Cache]
    nodeID[ID Instruction Decode and Register Read]
    nodeEX[EX Execute ALU Operation]
    nodeMEM[MEM Memory Access Load Store Only]
    nodeWB[WB Write Back to Register File]
    nodeIF --> nodeID
    nodeID --> nodeEX
    nodeEX --> nodeMEM
    nodeMEM --> nodeWB
    nodeRF[(Large Register File 32 to 128 GPRs)]
    nodeID -. reads .- nodeRF
    nodeWB -. writes .- nodeRF
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every instruction traverses the **same 5 stages**. Because all instructions are 32-bit fixed and only LOAD/STORE touch memory, **each stage completes in one clock cycle** → CPI ≈ 1.

---

### 4.2 CISC Pipeline — Microcoded Multi-Cycle Execution

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeFetch[Fetch Variable Length Opcode and ModRM bytes]
    nodeDecode[Microcode Address Generation]
    nodeMicroSeq[Microcode Sequencer in Control Store ROM]
    nodeMicroOp1[MicroOp 1 Bus Routing]
    nodeMicroOp2[MicroOp 2 Effective Address Calc]
    nodeMicroOp3[MicroOp 3 ALU and Memory Write]
    nodeCommit[Commit Result to Register or Memory]
    nodeFetch --> nodeDecode
    nodeDecode --> nodeMicroSeq
    nodeMicroSeq --> nodeMicroOp1
    nodeMicroOp1 --> nodeMicroOp2
    nodeMicroOp2 --> nodeMicroOp3
    nodeMicroOp3 --> nodeCommit
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single complex CISC instruction expands into **multiple micro-operations** read sequentially from the Control Store. This is why CPI > 1 — one macro-instruction = many micro-cycles.

---

### 4.3 Hybrid Modern CPU — Macro-ISA to Micro-Ops Translation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph nodeInput["Front End"]
        nodeL1[L1 I-Cache and Predecoder]
        nodeL2[Instruction Length Decoder x86 CISC]
        nodeL3[MicroOp Fusion and Decode]
        nodeL1 --> nodeL2
        nodeL2 --> nodeL3
    end
    subgraph nodeMiddle["Allocation and Rename"]
```
