```mermaid
        nodeR1[Register Allocation and Renaming]
        nodeR2[Reorder Buffer ROB]
        nodeL3 --> nodeR1
        nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    end
    subgraph nodeBackend["Back End RISC like"]
        nodeE1[Issue Queue and Scheduler]
        nodeE2[Reservation Station and ALU]
        nodeE3[Load Store Unit and LSU]
        nodeE4[Retire Unit Writes Arch State]
        nodeR2 --> nodeE1
        nodeE1 --> nodeE2
        nodeE1 --> nodeE3
        nodeE2 --> nodeE4
        nodeE3 --> nodeE4
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The CPU exposes a **CISC macro-ISA to software**, but internally the hardware breaks every macro-instruction into **RISC-like µops** that execute in an out-of-order superscalar back-end. This is the dominant paradigm in Intel "P6 / Sandy Bridge / Skylake" and AMD "Zen" cores.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph nodeRISC["RISC Core Block"]
        nR1[Fixed 32 bit Instruction Queue]
        nR2[Hardwired Decoder Combinational PLA]
        nR3[Register File 32 x 32 bit]
        nR4[ALU and Barrel Shifter]
        nR5[Load Store Unit]
        nR1 --> nR2 --> nR3 --> nR4
        nR3 --> nR5
    end
    subgraph nodeCISC["CISC Core Block"]
        nC1[Variable Length Prefetch Buffer]
        nC2[Microcode ROM Control Store]
        nC3[Microinstruction Sequencer]
        nC4[ALU and Microbus Datapath]
        nC5[Memory Operand Buses]
        nC1 --> nC2 --> nC3 --> nC4
        nC4 --> nC5
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define RISC and CISC. List any four distinguishing features between them. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) is a design philosophy that uses a **small, fixed-length instruction set** with a **load/store memory model**, achieving CPI ≈ 1 through pipelining and hardwired control.
CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) is a design philosophy that uses a **large, variable-length instruction set** with **memory-to-memory operands**, executed via microprogrammed control.

| Four distinguishing features | RISC | CISC |
|------|------|------|
| Instruction size | Fixed 32-bit | Variable 1–15 bytes |
| Memory access | Load/Store only | Any operand in memory |
| CPI | ≈ 1 | 2 to 15 |
| Control | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |

---

**Q2.** What is meant by a "load/store architecture"? Why is it a key feature of RISC? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **load/store architecture** is one in which **only LOAD and STORE instructions can access memory**; all other arithmetic/logic operations work **exclusively on registers**.
It is a key RISC feature because:
1. It **simplifies the instruction decoder** → enables single-cycle execution.
2. It **eliminates complex memory-addressing modes** from ALU ops → shorter clock period.
3. It **enables deep pipelining** because every instruction has a predictable memory-access pattern.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **design philosophy of RISC** with reference to its key characteristics. Discuss how these characteristics enable efficient pipelining. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The RISC design philosophy emerged from the Berkeley RISC-I project (Patterson, 1980) and is built on **five core principles**:

1. **Fixed-length, simple instructions (32-bit):** All instructions occupy exactly one word, allowing the fetch unit to read a new instruction every cycle without complex length-decoding.
2. **Load/Store memory model:** ALU instructions operate only on registers. Memory is touched only by explicit LOAD/STORE — this separates the data-path from memory timing.
3. **Large register file (≥ 32 GPRs):** Reduces memory traffic and supports **register-windowing** for fast procedure call/return (SPARC).
4. **Hardwired control:** Implemented as a combinational PLA/ROM-less decoder. Each instruction has a single-cycle datapath — no microcode loops.
5. **Few addressing modes (3–5 typical):** Simplifies register-read multiplexers and shortens critical path.

**How these enable efficient pipelining:**

- Because **all instructions are 32 bits**, the IF stage always reads exactly one cache line.
- Because **only LOAD/STORE touch memory**, the MEM stage is **disabled** for ALU ops → no structural hazard.
- Because **CPI = 1** for each stage, the **classic 5-stage pipeline** (IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB) achieves **throughput = 1 instruction/cycle** after fill.
- **Data hazards** are resolved via **register forwarding** (bypassing from EX/MEM outputs back to ALU inputs) and **delayed-branch slots**.

**(b)** Compare RISC and CISC architectures across **10 parameters**. Using the performance equation, show why a RISC processor with CPI = 1.2 and clock = 800 MHz outperforms a CISC processor with CPI = 4 and clock = 400 MHz on a program of $10^{8}$ instructions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 10-parameter comparison table is presented in **Section 3.3** of these notes (Analytical Matrix). Key entries: instruction size, CPI, addressing modes, register count, control unit, memory access, code density, pipeline depth, compiler role, examples.

**Performance derivation:**

The fundamental performance equation relates CPU time to instruction count, cycles per instruction, and clock period.

$$
T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}}
$$

**RISC processor (A):**

$$
T_{A} = \dfrac{(1 \times 10^{8}) \times 1.2}{800 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{A} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{8 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
T_{A} = 0.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**CISC processor (B):** Assume CISC reduces instruction count by 30%, so $IC_{B} = 0.7 \times 10^{8}$.

$$
T_{B} = \dfrac{(0.7 \times 10^{8}) \times 4}{400 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{B} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
T_{B} = 0.70 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{B}}{T_{A}} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.15} \approx 4.67 \times
$$

> **Conclusion:** The RISC processor is **~4.67× faster** despite executing more raw instructions. The *clock-rate advantage (2×)* combined with *CPI advantage (3.33×)* more than compensates for the *instruction-count deficit (1.43×)*.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **CISC design philosophy**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages in the context of semiconductor technology of the 1980s. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CISC Design Philosophy:**
The CISC approach (exemplified by VAX, Intel x86, Motorola 68k) attempted to **bridge the semantic gap** between high-level languages (HLL) and machine code. Designers packed as much "work" as possible into a single instruction to:
1. Reduce the *fetch overhead* (when memory was slow and expensive).
2. Make assembly programming easier (before mature compilers).
3. Achieve **higher code density** (fewer bytes per program).

**Key characteristics:**
- Variable-length instructions (1–15 bytes).
- Many addressing modes (> 12 in x86).
- Memory-to-memory ALU operations (e.g., `ADD [BX+SI], CX`).
- Microprogrammed control store (Writable Control Store in some).
- Single instruction may take 2–30+ cycles.

**1980s-era Advantages:**
- Semiconductor memory was **expensive and slow** → smaller programs were a real win.
- Compilers were primitive → complex instructions helped hand-written assembly.
- Microcode made **bug-fixes and ISA extensions easy** without re-silicon.

**1980s-era Disadvantages:**
- Variable instruction length made **fetching the next instruction serial** → poor pipelining.
- Microcode decoding made each instruction **multi-cycle** → high CPI.
- As DRAM speeds caught up (1985+), the *code-density advantage* diminished.

**(b)** A benchmark program has **40% ALU instructions** with CPI = 1, **30% LOAD/STORE** with CPI = 5, and **30% branch** with CPI = 2. Compute **(i)** average CPI, **(ii)** MIPS rating if clock = 500 MHz, and **(iii)** execution time for $IC = 10^{7}$ instructions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Average CPI (weighted by instruction mix):**

The CPI of an instruction class $i$ contributes to the average CPI in proportion to its share of the total instruction count. We multiply each class CPI by its fractional occurrence and sum.

$$
CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{IC_i}{IC}\right) \times CPI_i
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = (0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 2)
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = 0.40 + 1.50 + 0.60 = 2.50
$$

**(ii) MIPS rating:**

MIPS is computed as the clock frequency divided by the product of average CPI and one million. This measures how many millions of useful instructions complete per second.

$$
MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
MIPS = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{6}}{2.50 \times 10^{6}} = 200 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(iii) Execution time:**

Total CPU time is instruction count times average CPI times clock period. Using frequency in the denominator is equivalent.

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clock}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{(10^{7}) \times 2.50}{500 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{7}}{5 \times 10^{8}} = 0.05 \text{ seconds} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing units**: Forgetting to convert **MHz → Hz** before computing MIPS or execution time. This **alone loses 2 marks** out of 7 in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing MIPS with MFLOPS**: MIPS = integer instructions; MFLOPS = floating-point operations. KTU deducts 1 mark if mixed up.
> 3. **Using bare `|` in formula tables**: This breaks markdown rendering. Always use `\vert` or write "divided by" in prose.
> 4. **Forgetting "Amdahl's Law" in optimisation questions**: If asked "how much speedup if we improve fraction $f$ by $n$?", the formula $\frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ is **mandatory** — guessing numbers loses all marks.
> 5. **Drawing the pipeline wrong in diagrams**: Always show stages in order **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB** with arrows pointing forward only.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ **RISC** = Reduced Instruction Set Computer → fixed-length, load/store, hardwired, CPI ≈ 1
- ✅ **CISC** = Complex Instruction Set Computer → variable-length, memory-operand, microcoded, CPI > 1
- ✅ **Performance equation**: $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ (the *single most important formula* in Module 1)
- ✅ **MIPS formula**: $MIPS = f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^{6})$
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1 / [(1-f) + (f/n)]$ — limits speedup regardless of $n$
- ✅ **Load/Store architecture** is the *defining* feature of RISC, not just "fewer instructions"
- ✅ **Modern CPUs are hybrid**: CISC ISA front-end + RISC µop back-end (Intel, AMD, Apple M-series)
- ✅ **Code density vs Speed**: CISC has higher density; RISC has higher speed — a frequent KTU compare-contrast point
- ✅ **Examples to memorise**: ARM, RISC-V, SPARC, MIPS = RISC; x86 (Intel/AMD), Motorola 68k, VAX = CISC
- ✅ **1980 origin**: Patterson & Ditzel (Berkeley RISC-I) and Hennessy (Stanford MIPS) — both founders later won the **Turing Award (2017)**
- ✅ **Pipeline depth** in RISC directly maps to **stage count** (5-stage classic); CISC has variable micro-cycle counts
- ✅ **Compiler role**: RISC pushes complexity into the compiler (register allocation, scheduling); CISC pushes it into hardware (microcode)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Instruction Set Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Instruction Set Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** is the *contractual interface* between the hardware and the software, defining the visible programmer's model of a processor: the set of instructions, registers, memory addressing modes, data types, and the precise behaviour of every operation.

**Classification of Instruction Set Architectures** is the systematic categorization of ISAs based on:
1. **Where operands are stored internally** (Stack, Accumulator, or General-Purpose Registers).
2. **How operands are addressed in instructions** (the number of explicit memory/register addresses encoded in each instruction).
3. **The internal storage class** used by the ALU during computation.

This classification forms the basis of the seminal academic work by **G. J. Myers (1977, 1982)** and the unified treatment by **Hennessy & Patterson**, which KTU 2024 follows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEAET522, Module 1):**
> The student must be able to differentiate **Stack, Accumulator, Register-Register (Load/Store), Register-Memory, and Memory-Memory** architectures, and quantify their trade-offs in terms of *instruction count*, *code size*, *memory traffic*, and *CPI*.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Three Kitchens"

Imagine a chef preparing a dish **A = B + C**. The chef has three kinds of kitchen setups:

| Kitchen Type | Analogy | Real CPU |
|---|---|---|
| **Stack Kitchen** | All ingredients piled on a single counter; top of pile is the only place to grab from | Stack machine (e.g., Java Virtual Machine, x87 FPU) |
| **Accumulator Kitchen** | One "magic bowl" (the Accumulator) holds one ingredient; new ones replace old | Accumulator machine (e.g., early EDVAC, PDP-8) |
| **GPR Kitchen** | A large *workbench* with many labeled jars; the chef can grab any jar and put back any result | General-Purpose Register (GPR) machine (e.g., MIPS, ARM, x86) |

The **GPR workbench** is so powerful that it spawned the modern era of RISC processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A GPR-based ISA is the *de-facto* standard for almost every general-purpose processor shipped after 1990. The classification is therefore not just historical — it explains **why** Load-Store RISC processors dominate mobile, desktop, and server computing today.

## 1.3 The Five Canonical ISA Classes

| # | Class | Where operands live | Where result goes | Memory addresses per instruction |
|---|-------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Stack** | Top of stack | Top of stack (pushed) | **0** (implicit) |
| 2 | **Accumulator** | Implicit accumulator | Implicit accumulator | **1** |
| 3 | **Register–Memory** (CISC style) | Registers + memory | Register **or** memory | **2** (typical) |
| 4 | **Register–Register (Load/Store, RISC)** | Registers only | Registers only | **3** (loads/stores = 1) |
| 5 | **Memory–Memory** | Memory | Memory | **3** (all in memory) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Classify ISAs? — The Three Engineering Drivers

1. **Code Density** — How many bytes does it take to express a given algorithm?
2. **Memory Traffic** — How many data memory references per high-level operation?
3. **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) & Pipelining Ease** — Can we keep the pipeline full with a single instruction length and simple decode?

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off is fundamental: **more addresses per instruction ⇒ fewer dynamic instructions, but bigger instruction size and harder pipelining.**

## 2.2 Detailed Class Analysis

### 2.2.1 Stack Architecture
- **Implicit operand location**: the top-of-stack element(s).
- Push, Pop, and binary operations (ADD, MUL) all use the stack implicitly.
- *Pros:* Extremely compact code; simple decoder; easy expression evaluation.
- *Cons:* Top-of-stack becomes a *bottleneck*; cannot be efficiently pipelined; stack traffic is on the critical path.

### 2.2.2 Accumulator Architecture
- **One implicit operand** — the Accumulator (AC).
- Every arithmetic op reads AC and writes AC.
- *Pros:* Very short instructions (1 address).
- *Cons:* The single AC is a severe bottleneck; no instruction-level parallelism is possible without renaming.

### 2.2.3 General Purpose Register (GPR) Architecture — *the dominant style*
Split into three sub-classes based on operand access:

#### (a) Register–Register (Load/Store, RISC) — *e.g., MIPS, RISC-V, SPARC, ARM (Thumb variant)*
- All ALU operands **must** be in registers.
- Only `LOAD` and `STORE` access memory.
- **Fixed 32-bit instruction length**; easy pipelining.

#### (b) Register–Memory (CISC) — *e.g., x86, Motorola 68000*
- One operand may be in memory, the other in a register.
- Result can be written back to either.
- *Pros:* Good code density; fewer instructions per program.
- *Cons:* Variable instruction length ⇒ complex decoder; memory access can occur in *any* stage ⇒ pipeline hazards.

#### (c) Memory–Memory — *e.g., VAX*
- **All** operands (and result) may be in memory.
- 2 or 3 addresses per instruction.
- *Cons:* Every ALU instruction performs up to 3 memory references ⇒ high memory bandwidth demand; very few modern designs use this.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Memory addresses per instruction (typ.) | Stack = 0, Accumulator = 1, Reg-Reg = 3, Reg-Mem = 2, Mem-Mem = 3 | addresses/inst | For ALU instructions |
| 2 | Data memory references per ALU inst | Reg-Reg = 0, others = 1–3 | mem refs/inst | Lowest for Reg-Reg |
| 3 | Dynamic instruction count ratio | $N_{alu}^{Reg\text{-}Reg} \approx 1.7 \times N_{alu}^{Stack}$ | dimensionless | Empirical Hennessy–Patterson |
| 4 | CPI for ideal pipeline | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | cycles/inst | Reg-Reg only with no hazards |
| 5 | Effective CPI | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CPI_i \times \frac{IC_i}{IC}$ | cycles/inst | Mix of instruction classes |
| 6 | Program execution time | $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | The fundamental iron law |
| 7 | Memory traffic (bytes) | $M = IC \times (\text{inst\_size} + \text{data\_refs} \times \text{word\_size})$ | bytes | Used to compare ISAs |
| 8 | Code size ratio | $Size_{Reg\text{-}Reg} \approx 1.2 \times Size_{Stack} \approx 0.8 \times Size_{Reg\text{-}Mem}$ | bytes | Empirical trend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of ISA Design (Myers / Hennessy–Patterson):**
> *Registers, if exposed to the programmer (user-visible), are 10× faster than cache, and cache is 10× faster than main memory.* Therefore, **minimize memory accesses** by keeping operands in registers. This single principle is why the GPR Load/Store architecture won the RISC vs CISC war.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design**: Code generators for a Register-Register ISA must perform *register allocation* (graph colouring, linear scan) to keep temporaries in registers. For a Stack ISA, the compiler emits push/pop sequences.
- **Performance Modelling**: A benchmark (e.g., Dhrystone, CoreMark) compiled for a Load/Store ISA exhibits a **CPI close to 1.0** when the pipeline is full, whereas a Memory–Memory ISA rarely beats CPI ≈ 1.5.
- **Hardware Verification**: Different ISA classes imply very different *reference models* for ISA-level simulation (ISS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: A Single Arithmetic Statement

Evaluate the high-level statement:

$$Z = (A \times B) - (C + D) - E$$

We will **count dynamic instructions, memory references, and code size** for each ISA class. Assume a 32-bit word, 32-bit instruction width, and 8-bit (1-byte) opcode.

### 3.1.1 Stack Architecture
```text
PUSH A         ; 0 mem addresses
PUSH B         ; 0
MUL            ; 0
PUSH C         ; 0
PUSH D         ; 0
ADD            ; 0
SUB            ; 0
PUSH E         ; 0
SUB            ; 0
POP  Z         ; 0
```
- **Dynamic Instructions (IC)** = **10**
- **Memory references (data)** = **0** (assuming stack is in CPU-internal register file)
- **Code size** ≈ 10 × 1 byte opcode = **10 bytes** (ignoring operand field)

### 3.1.2 Accumulator Architecture
```text
LOAD  A        ; 1 mem address
MUL   B        ; 1
STORE T1       ; 1
LOAD  C        ; 1
ADD   D        ; 1
STORE T2       ; 1
LOAD  T1       ; 1
SUB   T2       ; 1
STORE T3       ; 1
LOAD  T3       ; 1
SUB   E        ; 1
STORE Z        ; 1
```
- **IC = 12**
- **Memory references = 12**
- **Code size** ≈ 12 × (1 + 1 byte addr) = **24 bytes**

### 3.1.3 Register–Memory Architecture (2-address)
```text
LOAD  R1, A    ; 1 mem ref
MUL   R1, B    ; 1
LOAD  R2, C    ; 1
ADD   R2, D    ; 1
SUB   R1, R2   ; 0
SUB   R1, E    ; 1
STORE R1, Z    ; 1
```
- **IC = 7**
- **Memory references = 6**
- **Code size** ≈ 7 × (1 + 2×1) = **21 bytes**

### 3.1.4 Register–Register (Load/Store) — 3-address ALU form
```text
LOAD  R1, A    ; 1 mem ref
LOAD  R2, B    ; 1
MUL   R3, R1, R2   ; 0
LOAD  R4, C    ; 1
LOAD  R5, D    ; 1
ADD   R6, R4, R5   ; 0
SUB   R7, R3, R6   ; 0
LOAD  R8, E    ; 1
SUB   R9, R7, R8   ; 0
STORE R9, Z    ; 1
```
- **IC = 10**
- **Memory references = 6**
- **Code size** ≈ 10 × (1 + 3×1) = **40 bytes**

### 3.1.5 Memory–Memory Architecture (3-address)
```text
MUL   T1, A, B ; 3 mem refs
ADD   T2, C, D ; 3
SUB   T3, T1, T2 ; 3
SUB   Z,  T3, E ; 3
```
- **IC = 4**
- **Memory references = 12**
- **Code size** ≈ 4 × (1 + 3×1) = **16 bytes**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Metric | Stack | Accumulator | Reg–Memory | **Reg–Reg (RISC)** | Mem–Mem |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IC (for Z = (A×B)−(C+D)−E)** | 10 | 12 | 7 | **10** | 4 |
| **Data mem refs** | 0 | 12 | 6 | **6** | 12 |
| **Code size (bytes)** | 10 | 24 | 21 | 40 | **16** |
| **Mem addresses per ALU inst** | 0 | 1 | 1–2 | 3 (but 0 in ALU) | 3 |
| **Pipeline friendliness** | Poor | Poor | Medium | **Excellent** | Poor |
| **Compiler complexity** | Low | Low | Medium | **High** (reg alloc) | Low |
| **Hardware complexity** | Medium | Low | High | **Low** | Very High |
| **Modern examples** | JVM | PDP-8 | **x86** | **MIPS, RISC-V, ARM** | VAX (historical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Conclusion:**
> The **Register–Register (Load/Store)** ISA sacrifices *code size* to win the three decisive battles: **fewest data memory references, simplest hardware, and best pipeline utilization**. This is exactly why every modern smartphone, laptop, and server CPU is a RISC-style core (even x86 chips decode CISC instructions into RISC-like µops internally).

## 3.3 Quantitative Trade-off — Derivation of CPI

For a 5-stage pipeline with full forwarding, the **ideal** CPI of a RISC Load/Store machine is:

$$
CPI_{ideal} = 1
$$

When **load-use hazards** and **branch penalties** are included, the effective CPI becomes:

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + \text{Stall}_{load\text{-}use} + \text{Stall}_{branch}
$$

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + \Big( \text{LU\_hazard\_rate} \times 1 \Big) + \Big( \text{branch\_freq} \times \text{penalty} \Big)
$$

For a typical RISC workload (Hennessy–Patterson benchmark):
- Load-use hazard rate = 0.20 ⇒ 0.20 stall cycles
- Branch frequency = 0.15, penalty = 2 cycles ⇒ 0.30 stall cycles
- **$CPI_{eff} \approx 1.50$**

For a Memory–Memory ISA in the same 5-stage pipeline, every ALU instruction can stall the pipeline **twice** (two operand fetches + one write), so:

$$
CPI_{mem\text{-}mem} \approx 1 + 0.30 + (2 \times 0.15) \approx 1.60
$$

Combined with the **higher IC** needed for explicit register-name encoding, the **Register–Register ISA executes the program fastest** in nearly every benchmark.

## 3.4 Memory Traffic and Code Density Formula

Let:
- $S$ = static code size (bytes)
- $D$ = data memory references per dynamic instruction
- $W$ = word size (bytes)
- $L$ = average instruction length (bytes)

$$
\text{Total memory traffic (bytes)} = S \times \text{fetch} + IC \times D \times W
$$

$$
T = S + IC \times D \times W
$$

For our example (Z = (A×B)−(C+D)−E, W = 4):

| ISA | S | IC | D | T (bytes) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stack | 10 | 10 | 0 | **10** |
| Accumulator | 24 | 12 | 1.0 | 24 + 12·4 = **72** |
| Reg–Mem | 21 | 7 | 0.86 | 21 + 7·4 ≈ **49** |
| **Reg–Reg** | 40 | 10 | 0.6 | 40 + 10·4 = **80** |
| Mem–Mem | 16 | 4 | 3.0 | 16 + 4·4·4 = **80** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The *fetched code* of RISC is larger, but because the *working set* stays in registers, the **cache miss rate** is dramatically lower. For large programs, RISC usually wins on total memory traffic too.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ISA CLASSIFICATION"] --> B["STACK BASED"]
    A --> C["ACCUMULATOR BASED"]
    A --> D["GPR BASED"]

    B --> B1["Push Pop Paradigm"]
    B --> B2["Zero Address Format"]
    B --> B3["Examples: JVM, x87 FPU"]

    C --> C1["One Implicit Operand AC"]
    C --> C2["One Address Format"]
    C --> C3["Examples: PDP-8, EDVAC"]

    D --> D1["Register Register Load Store"]
    D --> D2["Register Memory"]
    D --> D3["Memory Memory"]

    D1 --> D1a["Three Address ALU Format"]
    D1 --> D1b["Fixed Length 32 bit"]
    D1 --> D1c["Examples: MIPS, RISC-V, ARM, SPARC"]

    D2 --> D2a["Two Address Format"]
    D2 --> D2b["Variable Length"]
    D2 --> D2c["Examples: x86, Motorola 68000"]

    D3 --> D3a["Three Address All Memory"]
    D3 --> D3b["High Memory Bandwidth"]
    D3 --> D3c["Example: VAX historical"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STK["STACK MACHINE DATA PATH"]
        STK1["Instruction Decoder"] --> STK2["Stack Pointer Logic"]
        STK2 --> STK3["Top of Stack Register TOS"]
        STK3 --> STK4["ALU"]
        STK4 --> STK3
    end

    subgraph ACC["ACCUMULATOR MACHINE DATA PATH"]
        ACC1["Instruction Decoder"] --> ACC2["Memory Address Register MAR"]
        ACC2 --> ACC3["Data Memory"]
        ACC3 --> ACC4["Accumulator AC"]
        ACC4 --> ACC5["ALU"]
        ACC5 --> ACC4
    end

    subgraph GPR["GPR LOAD STORE MACHINE DATA PATH"]
        GPR1["Instruction Decoder"] --> GPR2["Register File 32 x 32 bit"]
        GPR2 --> GPR3["ALU"]
        GPR3 --> GPR2
        GPR2 --> GPR4["Memory via Load Store Unit"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Operand-Access Spectrum (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S["STACK\n0 addresses\n0 data mem refs"] --> A["ACCUMULATOR\n1 address\n1 data mem ref"]
    A --> RM["REG MEMORY\n2 addresses\n1 data mem ref"]
    RM --> RR["REG REGISTER\n3 addresses ALU\n0 data mem refs in ALU"]
    RR --> MM["MEM MEMORY\n3 addresses\n3 data mem refs"]
```

## 4.4 Pipeline Impact Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RISC["RISC Load Store Pipeline"]
        IF["IF"] --> ID["ID"]
        ID --> EX["EX"]
        EX --> MEM["MEM"]
        MEM --> WB["WB"]
    end

    subgraph CISC["CISC Register Memory Pipeline"]
        IF2["IF var len"] --> ID2["ID complex"]
        ID2 --> AG["AG address gen"]
        AG --> EX2["EX ALU"]
        EX2 --> MEM2["MEM access"]
        MEM2 --> WB2["WB partial"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The RISC pipeline executes *one simple operation per cycle*. The CISC pipeline must perform *address generation, ALU, and memory access* within a single complex instruction, increasing the critical path and the branch penalty.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three classifications of Instruction Set Architectures based on the location of operands.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Stack Architecture** (1 mark): Operands are implicitly on the top of the stack. ALU operations such as `ADD` pop two operands and push the result. Example: Java Virtual Machine.
2. **Accumulator Architecture** (1 mark): One operand is implicit in the Accumulator (AC). Example: PDP-8. Instructions are 1-address format.
3. **General Purpose Register (GPR) Architecture** (1 mark): Operands are explicitly named registers. Sub-divided into Register–Register (Load/Store), Register–Memory, and Memory–Memory. Example: MIPS (Load/Store), x86 (Register–Memory).

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Register–Register and Register–Memory ISA with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Register–Register (RISC) | Register–Memory (CISC) |
|---|---|---|
| Operand location for ALU | Both in registers (1 mark) | One in register, one in memory (1 mark) |
| Memory access | Only LOAD/STORE | Inside ALU instruction (1 mark) |
| Example | MIPS, RISC-V | x86, Motorola 68000 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing *code size* with *execution speed***. A shorter program is **not** always faster — RISC is bigger but faster due to pipelining.
> 2. **Forgetting the implicit operand** when counting addresses in a Stack or Accumulator ISA.
> 3. **Ignoring data memory references** when calculating total memory traffic — a Memory–Memory ISA has *zero* unused data refs.
> 4. **Writing 0 memory addresses for Register–Register ALU** but **1 for LOAD/STORE** — both must be stated explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the table comparison** — KTU examiners award at least 3 marks for a well-drawn comparative table.

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the five classifications of Instruction Set Architectures based on operand storage and addressing. For each, give the typical number of memory addresses per ALU instruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the high-level expression **X = (A + B) × (C − D)** for **Stack, Accumulator, Register–Memory, and Register–Register** architectures. Tabulate the *dynamic instruction count, data memory references, and code size in bytes* (assume 32-bit instructions, 1-byte opcode, 1-byte operand field). Conclude which architecture is most pipeline-friendly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

For each of the five classes: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for memory addresses ≈ 1.4 marks per class, with 1 mark for a labelled table.

| Class | Storage | Mem Addr/ALU inst | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stack | TOS | 0 | JVM |
| Accumulator | AC | 1 | PDP-8 |
| Reg–Reg (Load/Store) | Registers | 3 (ALU), 1 (LD/ST) | MIPS |
| Reg–Memory | Reg + Mem | 2 (typical) | x86 |
| Memory–Memory | Memory | 3 | VAX |

*(1.5 marks for table, remaining 5.5 marks split across 5 classes, ~1 mark each for the 5 bullet descriptions.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Stack — IC = 9, mem refs = 0, code size ≈ 9 bytes** (1.5 marks)
```
PUSH A; PUSH B; ADD; PUSH C; PUSH D; SUB; MUL; POP X
```

**Accumulator — IC = 7, mem refs = 7, code size ≈ 14 bytes** (1.5 marks)
```
LOAD A; ADD B; STORE T1; LOAD C; SUB D; STORE T2; LOAD T1; MUL T2; STORE X
```
Correction (typical 2-step): 9 inst, ~18 bytes

**Register–Memory (2-addr) — IC = 6, mem refs = 5, code size ≈ 18 bytes** (1.5 marks)
```
LOAD R1,A; ADD R1,B; STORE R1,T1
LOAD R2,C; SUB R2,D
MUL R1,R2
STORE R1,X
```

**Register–Register (Load/Store) — IC = 9, mem refs = 6, code size ≈ 36 bytes** (1.5 marks)
```
LOAD R1,A; LOAD R2,B; ADD R3,R1,R2; STORE R3,T1
LOAD R4,C; LOAD R5,D; SUB R6,R4,R5
LOAD R7,T1; MUL R8,R7,R6; STORE R8,X
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Register–Register (Load/Store) is the most pipeline-friendly because all ALU instructions have the same fixed length, no memory access in the ALU stage, and only LOAD/STORE access memory — yielding the lowest effective CPI.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **data path** of a Stack, Accumulator, and General-Purpose-Register machine. Compare the **clock cycle time** of a 5-stage pipeline executing the *same* program on each of these three ISAs, justifying your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has the following instruction-mix statistics for a benchmark:
- ALU Register–Register: 40%, CPI = 1
- Loads: 25%, CPI = 2
- Stores: 15%, CPI = 2
- Branches: 20%, CPI = 3

Compute the **average CPI** and **CPU time** for $IC = 100$ million instructions and clock period $T_{clk} = 1$ ns. Then, repeat the calculation if the compiler optimises the code to reduce Loads by 50% (replaced by Register–Register instructions, CPI = 1). Comment on the *Amdahl-style bottleneck*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

(2.5 marks: block diagram; 2.5 marks: clock cycle comparison; 2 marks: justification)

**Block diagram (textual):**
- **Stack**: $\text{PC} \to \text{IR} \to \text{Decoder} \to \text{Stack Pointer} \to \text{TOS Reg} \to \text{ALU} \to \text{TOS Reg}$
- **Accumulator**: $\text{PC} \to \text{IR} \to \text{Decoder} \to \text{MAR} \to \text{Memory} \to \text{AC} \to \text{ALU} \to \text{AC}$
- **GPR Load/Store**: $\text{PC} \to \text{IR} \to \text{Decoder} \to \text{Reg File (32×32)} \to \text{ALU} \to \text{Reg File}$; Memory accessed only via separate LS unit.

**Cycle time analysis:**
- Stack and Accumulator: The critical path includes **memory access** inside the ALU stage ⇒ $T_{clk}$ must accommodate **cache/memory latency** (≈ 2–5 ns).
- GPR Load/Store: ALU stage accesses **only registers** (≈ 0.3 ns) ⇒ $T_{clk}$ ≈ 0.5–1 ns. Even with separate Load/Store stages, the *per-stage* critical path is shorter.

Therefore, **GPR Load/Store has the shortest clock cycle** and the *highest clock frequency*, giving the lowest execution time per instruction.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Original CPI** (1.5 marks):
$$
CPI_{avg} = 0.40(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.15(2) + 0.20(3) = 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.60 = 1.80
$$

**Original CPU Time** (1 mark):
$$
T_1 = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 1.80 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 180 \text{ ms}
$$

**Optimised mix** (2 marks):
- Loads drop from 25% to 12.5%; the freed 12.5% becomes ALU RR (CPI = 1).
- New mix: ALU RR = 52.5%, Loads = 12.5%, Stores = 15%, Branches = 20%

$$
CPI_{new} = 0.525(1) + 0.125(2) + 0.15(2) + 0.20(3)
$$
$$
CPI_{new} = 0.525 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.60 = 1.675
$$

**New CPU Time** (1 mark):
$$
T_2 = 100 \times 10^6 \times 1.675 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 167.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Speedup** (0.5 mark):
$$
S = T_1 / T_2 = 180 / 167.5 \approx 1.075 \quad (\text{only 7.5% faster})
$$

**Amdahl Bottleneck Comment (1 mark):**
Even though *half* the loads were eliminated, the **branches (CPI = 3) and stores (CPI = 2)** remain. Branches alone account for 60% of the original CPI. *Conclusion:* Optimising only one class of instruction gives diminishing returns — the *highest-CPI* instruction class (here, branches) is the Amdahl bottleneck.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] ISA = the *programmer-visible* interface; everything below it (micro-architecture, gates) is implementation.
- [ ] Five classes: **Stack, Accumulator, Register–Register, Register–Memory, Memory–Memory**.
- [ ] Memory addresses per ALU inst: **0, 1, 3, 2, 3** respectively.
- [ ] **GPR Load/Store (RISC)** is the modern winner — fixed 32-bit length, simple pipeline, CPI ≈ 1.
- [ ] **Register–Memory (CISC)** gives best code density; modern x86 chips *translate* CISC → RISC µops internally.
- [ ] **Stack** is great for expression evaluation but creates a *top-of-stack bottleneck*.
- [ ] **Accumulator** is now mostly of historical interest (PDP-8 era).
- [ ] **Memory–Memory** ISAs (VAX) failed because memory bandwidth could not keep up with the 3 refs per ALU op.
- [ ] The fundamental iron law: $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$.
- [ ] Effective CPI = 1 + load-use stalls + branch stalls.
- [ ] Code-size does **not** equal speed; bigger RISC code often runs faster than smaller CISC code.
- [ ] Modern smartphone, laptop, and server CPUs are all RISC-style cores at the µop level.
- [ ] For KTU exams: **always draw the comparison table** — examiners award 2–3 marks for a clean table alone.
- [ ] Be explicit about *operand location* (where the ALU reads from) and *destination* (where the result is written).
- [ ] Remember the empirical Hennessy–Patterson ratios: Reg–Reg IC ≈ 1.7 × Stack IC, but Reg–Reg CPI < Stack CPI.
- [ ] The "GPR Kitchen" analogy (workbench with many jars) is your mental model — keep it ready for viva.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> When asked to *compare* ISAs, structure the answer as: **(i) one-line definition, (ii) operand location, (iii) instruction format, (iv) pipeline impact, (v) modern example**. A response that hits all five points in a single table reliably scores full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Performance Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Performance Measurements # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Performance Measurement in Computer Architecture** is the quantitative and qualitative evaluation of a computer system's ability to execute instructions, process data, and complete tasks within a defined time, energy, or cost envelope. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this forms the foundational basis for understanding the trade-offs between hardware design, instruction set design, and program execution efficiency. In the context of **Modern Processor Architecture**, performance measurement is governed by three cardinal metrics: 1. **Execution Time (Latency)** — The total wall-clock time taken to complete a given task. 2. **Throughput** — The number of tasks completed per unit of time. 3. **Energy Efficiency** — The amount of work performed per joule of energy consumed. The **golden rule** of performance measurement in computer architecture is: > **"The only consistent and reliable definition of performance is TIME."** Therefore, for any two computers, **X** and **Y**: - **X is faster than Y** if and only if: `Execution_Time(X) < Execution_Time(Y)` - The **Speedup** of X relative to Y is defined as: $$ Speedup = \frac{Execution_Time(Y)}{Execution_Time(X)} = \frac{Performance(X)}{Performance(Y)} $$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Car Race Scenario** > > Imagine two cars, **Car A (Standard Sedan)** and **Car B (Sports Coupe)**, competing on a 100 km track. > - Car A finishes the race in **2 hours**. > - Car B finishes the race in **1 hour**. > > Clearly, Car B is **faster** because it takes **less time** to complete the same task (the race). The performance is inversely proportional to execution time. > > Now, suppose Car A can carry **5 passengers**, while Car B can carry only **2 passengers**. In a *throughput* context (e.g., transporting people to a destination), Car A may actually be more efficient in terms of **passenger-kilometers per hour**, even though it is slower in terms of *latency*. > > This is exactly the trade-off studied in **processor performance** — we often balance *latency* (single task speed) against *throughput* (number of tasks completed per cycle) and *energy* (joules per task). --- ## 1.3 Classification of Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes performance metrics into the following hierarchical levels: | Metric Level | Examples | Unit | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Micro-architectural** | Clock Cycle Time, CPI | Nanoseconds, Cycles/Instruction | Hardware Design | | **Instruction-level** | MIPS, MFLOPS, IPC | Millions of Instructions/Second | ISA Evaluation | | **System-level** | Execution Time, Throughput | Seconds, Tasks/Second | Application Benchmarking | | **Workload-level** | SPECscore, Transactions/sec | Benchmark Score | Industry Comparison | ### Key Definitions for KTU Board Examination - **Clock Cycle Time (T)**: The time duration of one clock pulse, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**. - **Clock Rate (f)**: The reciprocal of clock cycle time, measured in **Hz**, **MHz**, or **GHz**. - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**: The average number of clock cycles required to execute one instruction. - **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)**: A measure of instruction execution rate. - **MFLOPS (Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second)**: A measure of floating-point computation rate. $$ f = \frac{1}{T} $$ $$ CPI = \frac{Clock\ Cycles\ for\ a\ Program}{Instruction\ Count\ of\ a\ Program} $$ $$ Execution\ Time = Instruction\ Count \times CPI \times Clock\ Cycle\ Time $$ > [!NOTE] > This three-factor equation is the **most important formula** in computer architecture performance analysis. It is virtually guaranteed to appear in some form in the KTU End Semester Examination. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CPU Performance Equation (Hierarchical Decomposition) The total execution time of a program can be decomposed into three multiplicative factors. This decomposition is the **single most critical theoretical framework** in modern processor architecture. ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the CPU Equation 1. **Start with the definition of execution time:** Execution time depends on the number of clock cycles and the duration of each cycle. $Execution\ Time = \#Clock\ Cycles \times Clock\ Cycle\ Time$ 2. **Decompose clock cycles using CPI:** The number of clock cycles equals the number of instructions multiplied by the average cycles per instruction. $\#Clock\ Cycles = Instruction\ Count \times CPI$ 3. **Substitute back into Step 1:** $Execution\ Time = Instruction\ Count \times CPI \times Clock\ Cycle\ Time$ 4. **Convert to clock rate form:** Since $Clock\ Rate = \frac{1}{Clock\ Cycle\ Time}$, we can also write: $Execution\ Time = \frac{Instruction\ Count \times CPI}{Clock\ Rate}$ 5. **Final canonical form (the **CPU Performance Equation**):** $$ T_{exec} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}} $$ --- ## 2.2 MIPS and MFLOPS — Instruction Throughput Metrics ### MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) MIPS is an **instruction-level throughput** metric. It is computed as: $$ MIPS = \frac{Instruction\ Count}{Execution\ Time \times 10^6} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} $$ > [!WARNING] > **MIPS Limitation**: MIPS can be misleading across different ISAs because instruction sets vary in power. A RISC instruction may do less work than a CISC instruction, so a high MIPS rating does not always mean higher performance. ### MFLOPS (Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second) MFLOPS focuses only on floating-point operations, which is useful for scientific workloads: $$ MFLOPS = \frac{\#Floating\ Point\ Operations\ in\ Program}{Execution\ Time \times 10^6} $$ --- ## 2.3 Amdahl's Law — The Fundamental Limit of Optimization > [!IMPORTANT] > **Amdahl's Law** is a cornerstone principle taught in KTU Module 1. It quantifies the theoretical speedup achievable from improving one component of a system. **Statement of Amdahl's Law:** If a fraction **F** of a task can be improved by a speedup factor **S**, then the overall speedup of the entire task is: $$ Speedup_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}} $$ **Boundary Conditions:** - If $F = 1$ (entire task is improved), $Speedup = S$ - If $S \to \infty$, $Speedup \to \frac{1}{1 - F}$ (maximum theoretical speedup) --- ## 2.4 Benchmarks — Standardized Performance Evaluation > [!NOTE] > A **benchmark** is a standardized program (or set of programs) used to measure and compare the performance of computer systems under controlled conditions. ### Types of Benchmarks 1. **Real Programs** — Actual applications (compilers, databases, scientific codes). 2. **Kernels** — Small, critical code snippets extracted from real programs (e.g., matrix multiply, sorting). 3. **Toy Benchmarks** — Artificially simple programs (e.g., Sieve of Eratosthenes, Puzzle). 4. **Synthetic Benchmarks** — Artificially crafted programs that mimic the statistical behavior of real workloads (e.g., Whetstone, Dhrystone). ### The SPEC Benchmark Suite The **Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation (SPEC)** is the industry standard for benchmarking. SPEC provides: - **SPEC CPU 2017** — For CPU-intensive workloads. - **SPEC Power** — For energy efficiency. - **SPECjbb** — For Java server performance. The **SPECscore** is computed as a geometric mean of individual benchmark speedups relative to a reference machine. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **every critical formula** required for KTU 2024 exam problems on this topic. **Memorize this table.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CPU Performance | $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$ | Seconds | | 2 | CPU Performance (Rate Form) | $T = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$ | Seconds | | 3 | Average CPI | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i) / IC$ | Cycles/Instruction | | 4 | MIPS | $MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions/s | | 5 | MFLOPS | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{FLOPs}{T \times 10^6}$ | Millions of FLOPs/s | | 6 | Amdahl's Law | $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Speedup Definition | $Speedup = T_{old} / T_{new}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Performance Ratio | $Performance = 1 / Execution\ Time$ | Inverse seconds | | 9 | Weighted CPI | $CPI = \sum (CPI_i \times F_i)$ | $F_i$ = frequency of class $i$ | | 10 | Clock Rate | $f = 1 / T_{cycle}$ | Hz | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in industry?** > - **Chip Designers (Intel, AMD, ARM)** use CPI and clock rate to balance **pipeline depth vs. IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)**. > - **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure, GCP)** use benchmark scores (SPEC, TPC) to price virtual machines and SLAs. > - **Embedded Systems Engineers** use MIPS/Watt to optimize **energy efficiency** in IoT and mobile devices. > - **Compiler Engineers** use instruction mix and CPI to optimize code generation for specific ISAs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: CPU Performance Equation with Instruction Mix > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A processor executes three classes of instructions with the following characteristics. Derive the **average CPI** and total **execution time**. | Instruction Class | CPI | Instruction Mix (%) | |---|---|---| | ALU / Integer | 1 | 40% | | Load / Store | 2 | 30% | | Branch | 3 | 20% | | Floating Point | 4 | 10% | Assume: $IC = 100 \times 10^6$ instructions, $f_{clock} = 2\ GHz$ (i.e., $T_{cycle} = 0.5\ ns$). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Compute the weighted average CPI.** The average CPI is the sum over all instruction classes of (CPI of class × frequency of class). $$ CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times F_i) $$ Substituting values: $$ \begin{aligned} CPI_{avg} &= (1 \times 0.40) + (2 \times 0.30) + (3 \times 0.20) + (4 \times 0.10) \\ &= 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\ &= 2.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Verify using instruction counts.** Convert instruction mix percentages to actual counts: - ALU: $100M \times 0.40 = 40M$ instructions - Load/Store: $100M \times 0.30 = 30M$ instructions - Branch: $100M \times 0.20 = 20M$ instructions - Floating Point: $100M \times 0.10 = 10M$ instructions Compute total clock cycles: $$ \begin{aligned} \#Cycles &= (40M \times 1) + (30M \times 2) + (20M \times 3) + (10M \times 4) \\ &= 40M + 60M + 60M + 40M \\ &= 200M\ cycles \end{aligned} $$ Compute average CPI by dividing total cycles by total instructions: $$ CPI_{avg} = \frac{200M\ cycles}{100M\ instructions} = 2.00\ cycles/instruction $$ Both methods agree, confirming the result. **Step 3: Compute execution time using the canonical CPU performance equation.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{exec} &= IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle} \\ &= 100 \times 10^6 \times 2.00 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 100 \times 10^6 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 0.1\ seconds \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute MIPS rating for the processor.** $$ \begin{aligned} MIPS &= \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 10^9}{2.00 \times 10^6} \\ &= 1000\ MIPS \\ &= 1.0\ GFLOPS\ (instructional\ throughput) \end{aligned} $$ **Final Summary Box:** > - **Average CPI** = 2.00 > - **Execution Time** = 0.1 s > - **MIPS Rating** = 1000 MIPS --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law Application > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A program spends 25% of its time in vector processing code that can be accelerated 10× by a new SIMD unit. Compute the overall speedup. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Identify the parameters from the problem statement.** - Fraction of improved code: $F = 0.25$ - Speedup of improved portion: $S = 10$ **Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law directly.** $$ \begin{aligned} Speedup_{overall} &= \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}} \\ &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.25) + \frac{0.25}{10}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.75 + 0.025} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.775} \\ &\approx 1.290 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Interpret the result.** The overall speedup is approximately **1.29×** (i.e., 29% faster), despite the vector portion being made 10× faster. This demonstrates Amdahl's key insight: **the unimproved portion dominates the bottleneck.** **Step 4: Compute the maximum theoretical speedup if the vector portion were made infinitely fast.** $$ \begin{aligned} Speedup_{max} &= \frac{1}{1 - F} = \frac{1}{0.75} \approx 1.333 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Summary Box:** > - **Actual Speedup** ≈ 1.29× > - **Maximum Theoretical Speedup** ≈ 1.33× > - **Key Insight**: Even with infinite vector speedup, the overall speedup is capped at 1.33×. --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Performance Comparison Between Two Processors > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** Compare two processors A and B for the same workload. | Parameter | Processor A | Processor B | |---|---|---| | Clock Rate | 3.0 GHz | 2.5 GHz | | CPI (avg) | 1.5 | 1.0 | | Instruction Count | $100 \times 10^6$ | $80 \times 10^6$ | ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Compute execution time for Processor A.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_A &= \frac{IC_A \times CPI_A}{f_A} \\ &= \frac{100 \times 10^6 \times 1.5}{3.0 \times 10^9} \\ &= \frac{150 \times 10^6}{3.0 \times 10^9} \\ &= 0.05\ seconds \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute execution time for Processor B.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_B &= \frac{IC_B \times CPI_B}{f_B} \\ &= \frac{80 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{2.5 \times 10^9} \\ &= \frac{80 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^9} \\ &= 0.032\ seconds \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the speedup of B over A.** $$ \begin{aligned} Speedup &= \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{0.05}{0.032} = 1.5625 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Conclusion.** Processor B is **1.5625× faster** than Processor A, even though it has a *slower clock rate*. This illustrates the critical lesson: **clock rate alone does not determine performance** — the combination of CPI, IC, and clock rate matters. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance Summary | Metric | Processor A | Processor B | Winner | |---|---|---|---| | Clock Rate | 3.0 GHz | 2.5 GHz | A (faster clock) | | CPI | 1.5 | 1.0 | B (more efficient) | | Instruction Count | 100 M | 80 M | B (fewer instructions) | | MIPS | $f/(CPI \times 10^6) = 2000$ | $f/(CPI \times 10^6) = 2500$ | B | | Execution Time | 0.050 s | 0.032 s | **B** | | Performance Ratio | 1.0 | 1.5625× | **B** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lesson from the matrix**: Despite A having a 20% higher clock rate, B wins decisively due to its better CPI and reduced instruction count. This is a classic KTU exam scenario. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of CPU Performance > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical decomposition** of the CPU performance equation. This visualizes the inter-relationship between the three multiplicative factors that determine execution time. ```mermaid graph TD A["CPU Performance
Execution Time T"] --> B["Instruction Count IC
Software/Compiler Factor"] A --> C["Average CPI
Microarchitecture Factor"] A --> D["Clock Cycle Time T_cycle
Technology/Physics Factor"] B --> B1["Number of Instructions Executed
Affected by: ISA, Compiler, Algorithm"] C --> C1["Cycles Per Instruction
Affected by: Pipeline, Cache, Branch Prediction"] D --> D1["Inverse of Clock Rate f
Affected by: Transistor Speed, VLSI Process"] B1 --> R["T = IC x CPI x T_cycle"] C1 --> R D1 --> R R --> OUT["Total Execution Time
in Seconds"] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style R fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style OUT fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.2 Amdahl's Law — Speedup Visualization > [!NOTE] > The following diagram maps the **conceptual flow of Amdahl's Law**, showing how the *unimproved fraction* of a program creates a hard ceiling on speedup regardless of how much the *improved fraction* is accelerated. ```mermaid graph LR P["Original Program
Execution Time = 1.0"] --> F["Improved Fraction F
Accelerated by Speedup S"] P --> U["Unimproved Fraction 1-F
Cannot be optimized"] F --> F1["Optimized Execution
F divided by S"] U --> U1["Stays as 1-F
The Bottleneck"] F1 --> SUM["Total New Execution Time
equals 1-F plus F/S"] U1 --> SUM SUM --> SP["Overall Speedup
equals 1 divided by Total"] SP --> LIMIT["As S approaches infinity
Speedup approaches 1 divided by 1-F"] style P fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style SUM fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style SP fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style LIMIT fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Benchmark Classification Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the **classification hierarchy of benchmarks** used in modern processor performance evaluation, as defined by Hennessy and Patterson's taxonomy. ```mermaid graph TD BM["Performance Benchmarks"] --> R1["Real Programs
Actual Applications"] BM --> R2["Kernels
Critical Code Snippets"] BM --> R3["Toy Benchmarks
Artificial Small Programs"] BM --> R4["Synthetic Benchmarks
Workload Simulators"] R1 --> S1["Compilers, Databases
SPEC CPU 2017"] R2 --> S2["Matrix Multiply, Sorting
Livermore Loops"] R3 --> S3["Sieve of Eratosthenes
Puzzle, N-Queens"] R4 --> S4["Whetstone, Dhrystone
SPEC JVM"] S1 --> M["Performance Score
Geometric Mean"] S2 --> M S3 --> M S4 --> M M --> FINAL["SPECscore / Benchmark Rating"] style BM fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style M fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style FINAL fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.4 Performance Measurement Workflow > [!NOTE] > The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end workflow** of measuring processor performance, from workload selection to result interpretation. This is the **functional flow** followed in any performance evaluation lab. ```mermaid graph TD A["Step 1: Define Workload
Select representative programs"] --> B["Step 2: Measure Baseline
Run on Reference System"] B --> C["Step 3: Measure Target
Run on System Under Test"] C --> D["Step 4: Compute Metrics
Execution Time, MIPS, MFLOPS"] D --> E["Step 5: Normalize Scores
Relative to Reference"] E --> F["Step 6: Aggregate Results
Arithmetic or Geometric Mean"] F --> G["Step 7: Interpret
Compare and Analyze"] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style D fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style G fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > The following Part A questions are modeled after the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Each carries 3 marks. Provide crisp, definition-style answers. --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define the term CPI. Why is CPI considered more useful than raw clock rate for comparing two processors running the same program?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** is defined as the average number of clock cycles required by the processor to execute each instruction in a given program. It is computed as: > $CPI = \dfrac{\text{Total Clock Cycles for the Program}}{\text{Total Instruction Count of the Program}}$ **Why CPI is more useful than raw clock rate (2 marks):** 1. **A program has mixed instruction types** (ALU, memory, branch, FP), each with different CPI. The average CPI captures this **instruction mix** effect. 2. **Two processors with the same clock rate can have different CPIs** due to differing microarchitecture (e.g., pipelining, cache hit rate). CPI exposes the **architectural efficiency** that clock rate alone hides. 3. Therefore, **execution time = IC × CPI × T_cycle** depends on all three factors. A lower CPI with a slightly lower clock can outperform a higher clock rate processor. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **State and explain Amdahl's Law. What does it imply about the practical limits of performance optimization?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** Amdahl's Law states that if a fraction **F** of a task's execution time can be improved by a speedup factor **S**, then the **overall speedup** of the system is: $$ Speedup = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}} $$ **Explanation (2 marks):** - The term $(1 - F)$ represents the **unimproved fraction**, which remains a bottleneck. - As $S \to \infty$, the maximum overall speedup is capped at $\dfrac{1}{1-F}$. - **Implication**: Optimizing a frequently occurring fraction yields higher returns; improving a rarely executed part, even by a huge factor, yields negligible overall benefit. This is why engineers focus on the **common case** (the Amdahl principle). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically of **7 marks each**. Below are two full alternative 14-mark questions. --- ### **Question B1 — OR — Question B2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### ✅ QUESTION B1 (14 Marks) **(a)** A processor runs at 3.0 GHz and executes a benchmark of $200 \times 10^6$ instructions. The instruction mix and CPI are given below: | Instruction Class | CPI | Instruction Count (M) | |---|---|---| | ALU | 1 | 80 | | Load/Store | 3 | 60 | | Branch | 4 | 40 | | FP Multiply | 8 | 20 | Compute: 1. The average CPI of the program. 2. The total execution time. 3. The MIPS rating of the processor. 4. The MFLOPS rating, given that there are $20 \times 10^6$ floating-point multiply operations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **Amdahl's Law** with a suitable derivation. A program spends 40% of its time in I/O operations. If we replace the I/O subsystem with a new one that is 5 times faster, what is the overall speedup achieved? What would be the speedup if the new I/O subsystem were 100 times faster? Comment on the results. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER B1(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Compute the total clock cycles using weighted CPI.** Total clock cycles = sum of (CPI × count) for each class: $$ \begin{aligned} \#Cycles &= (1 \times 80M) + (3 \times 60M) + (4 \times 40M) + (8 \times 20M) \\ &= 80M + 180M + 160M + 160M \\ &= 580M\ cycles \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the average CPI.** $$ \begin{aligned} CPI_{avg} &= \frac{\#Cycles}{IC_{total}} = \frac{580M}{200M} = 2.90 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute execution time.** $T_{cycle} = 1 / 3\ GHz = 0.333\ ns$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{exec} &= IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle} \\ &= 200M \times 2.90 \times 0.333 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 0.193\ seconds \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute MIPS.** $$ \begin{aligned} MIPS &= \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{2.90 \times 10^6} \approx 1034.48\ MIPS \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute MFLOPS.** Only FP multiply (8 cycles) contributes to floating-point ops. Wait — the problem states there are $20 \times 10^6$ FP multiplies. So: $$ \begin{aligned} MFLOPS &= \frac{FLOPs}{T \times 10^6} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{0.193 \times 10^6} \approx 103.6\ MFLOPS \end{aligned} $$ > **Answer Summary**: CPI = 2.90, $T_{exec} = 0.193$ s, MIPS = 1034.48, MFLOPS = 103.6 --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER B1(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: State and derive Amdahl's Law.** Let the original execution time be $T$. Let fraction $F$ be improved by speedup $S$, so its new time is $\frac{F \cdot T}{S}$. The unimproved fraction takes $(1 - F) \cdot T$. Total new time: $$ T_{new} = (1 - F) T + \frac{F \cdot T}{S} $$ Therefore: $$ Speedup = \frac{T}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}} $$ **Step 2: Apply with F = 0.40, S = 5.** $$ \begin{aligned} Speedup &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.471 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply with F = 0.40, S = 100.** $$ \begin{aligned} Speedup &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{100}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.004} = \frac{1}{0.604} \approx 1.656 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Comment.** Even though we made I/O **20× more powerful** (from 5× to 100×), the overall speedup improved only marginally (from 1.471× to 1.656×). This vividly demonstrates **Amdahl's insight**: the unimproved 60% of the program dominates the bottleneck. To get further speedup, we must optimize the *unimproved* portion as well. > **Answer Summary**: Speedup (S=5) ≈ 1.471×; Speedup (S=100) ≈ 1.656×; Amdahl's Law clearly shows diminishing returns. --- #### ✅ QUESTION B2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare two processors A and B using the CPU performance equation. Processor A has a clock rate of 2.5 GHz, CPI = 1.2, and executes $150 \times 10^6$ instructions. Processor B has a clock rate of 3.0 GHz, CPI = 1.8, and executes $100 \times 10^6$ instructions. Determine which processor is faster and by how much. Also calculate the MIPS rating of each processor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the different types of **benchmarks** used in computer performance evaluation. Explain the **SPEC benchmark suite** and how the SPECscore is computed using the **geometric mean**. Why is the geometric mean used instead of the arithmetic mean? **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER B2(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Compute execution time for Processor A.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_A &= \frac{IC_A \times CPI_A}{f_A} = \frac{150 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{2.5 \times 10^9} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.072\ s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute execution time for Processor B.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_B &= \frac{IC_B \times CPI_B}{f_B} = \frac{100 \times 10^6 \times 1.8}{3.0 \times 10^9} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{3.0 \times 10^9} = 0.060\ s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the speedup.** $$ Speedup = \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{0.072}{0.060} = 1.20 $$ **Step 4: Compute MIPS ratings.** - Processor A: $MIPS_A = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^9}{1.2 \times 10^6} \approx 2083.33\ MIPS$ - Processor B: $MIPS_B = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^9}{1.8 \times 10^6} \approx 1666.67\ MIPS$ > **Conclusion**: Processor **B is 1.20× faster** than Processor A, despite A having a *higher MIPS rating*. This shows the **MIPS paradox** — MIPS is not a reliable cross-architecture performance metric. --- #### ✅ MODEL ANSWER B2(b) — 7 Marks **Benchmarks Classification (3.5 marks):** 1. **Real Programs**: Actual application software (e.g., GCC compiler, Apache web server, MATLAB). Most representative but harder to standardize. 2. **Kernels**: Extracted critical code (e.g., Livermore Loops, Linpack matrix multiply). 3. **Toy Benchmarks**: Simple, small programs (e.g., Sieve of Eratosthenes). Easy to run but not representative. 4. **Synthetic Benchmarks**: Artificially crafted to mimic real workload statistics (e.g., Whetstone, Dhrystone, SPEC JVM). **SPEC Benchmark Suite (2.5 marks):** The **Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation (SPEC)** provides industry-standard benchmarks: - **SPEC CPU 2017** for CPU performance. - **SPEC Power** for energy efficiency. - **SPECjbb** for Java business servers. The **SPECscore** is the **geometric mean** of the speedup ratios of individual benchmarks relative to a **reference machine**: $$ SPECscore = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_{ref,i}}{T_{test,i}} \right)^{1/n} $$ where $T_{ref,i}$ is the reference time and $T_{test,i}$ is the test time for benchmark $i$. **Why Geometric Mean (1 mark):** - The geometric mean gives **equal weight to equal ratios**, regardless of absolute execution time. A benchmark that runs twice as fast contributes the same factor to the score whether it originally took 1 s or 1000 s. - It is also **dimensionally consistent** and handles ratios of widely different magnitudes better than the arithmetic mean. - The arithmetic mean would unfairly weight long-running benchmarks. > **Answer Summary**: SPECscore uses geometric mean to ensure proportional fairness; benchmarks are classified into real programs, kernels, toys, and synthetic. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing MIPS and Performance**: A high MIPS rating does **NOT** mean a faster processor. MIPS is instruction throughput, not work throughput. *Examiner's tip*: Always verify using the **CPU performance equation** ($T = IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$). > > 2. **Forgetting to convert units**: $f$ is in Hz, IC is in instructions, T_cycle is in seconds. A common error is plugging in MHz instead of Hz. *Examiner's tip*: Write $f$ in scientific notation ($2.5 \times 10^9$ Hz) to avoid unit errors. > > 3. **Amdahl's Law mistake**: Students often use the wrong fraction. $F$ is the fraction *being improved*, and $(1-F)$ is the *unimproved* part. Reversing these gives a wrong answer. > > 4. **Instruction count vs. Cycle count**: In the CPU equation, "Instruction Count" is the **number of instructions**, not the number of cycles. Cycles = IC × CPI. > > 5. **Geometric vs. Arithmetic Mean**: SPECscore uses **geometric mean**. Using arithmetic mean is a frequent error costing 1–2 marks. > > 6. **CPI calculation**: CPI must be computed as a **weighted average** over all instruction classes, not a simple average of CPI values. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Performance Measurements"** - ✅ **The Golden Rule**: Performance is inversely proportional to Execution Time. *Faster = Less Time.* - ✅ **CPU Performance Equation**: $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$ (or equivalently $T = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$). - ✅ **Three Factors**: - **IC** → Controlled by ISA, compiler, and algorithm. - **CPI** → Controlled by microarchitecture (pipelining, cache, branch prediction). - **T_cycle** → Controlled by VLSI technology and circuit design. - ✅ **Average CPI**: $CPI = \sum (CPI_i \times F_i)$, where $F_i$ is the frequency of class $i$. - ✅ **MIPS**: $MIPS = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$ — misleading across ISAs. - ✅ **MFLOPS**: Floating-point operations per second — useful for scientific workloads. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$ — diminishing returns; optimize the common case. - ✅ **Benchmarks**: Real programs > Kernels > Synthetic > Toy (in order of representativeness). - ✅ **SPEC**: Industry standard; SPECscore uses **geometric mean**. - ✅ **Speedup Definition**: $Speedup = T_{old} / T_{new} = Performance_{new} / Performance_{old}$. - ✅ **Key Insight**: Clock rate alone does NOT determine performance — CPI and IC matter equally. > [!NOTE] > **One-Line Mnemonic for the CPU Equation**: > *"**I**nstruction × **C**lock × **C**ycle — **ICC** gives you the **Total Time**."* > $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{cycle}$ → **"I see, see — Total time"**

topics.title: Basic Parallel Processing Techniques: Instruction Level, Thread Level, and Process Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Parallel Processing Techniques: Instruction Level, Thread Level, and Process Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Parallel Processing — The Foundational Concept

**Parallel Processing** is a computational paradigm in which multiple computational units (functional units, cores, processors, or machines) cooperate to solve a single, decomposed problem or execute multiple tasks simultaneously, thereby reducing the wall-clock execution time compared to strictly sequential execution. In the context of modern processor architecture, parallelism is exploited at three distinct granularities — *Instruction*, *Thread*, and *Process* — each operating on a different scale of the software-hardware contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Parallelism is the simultaneous execution of two or more operations/entities. In computer architecture, the three canonical levels are: **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)**, **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)**, and **Process-Level Parallelism (PLP)**. The hierarchy reflects an increasing granularity of work and a decreasing coupling between the parallel entities.

### 1.2 Instruction Level Parallelism (ILP)

**Formal Definition:** Instruction-Level Parallelism is the simultaneous execution of *multiple instructions drawn from a single instruction stream (single program / single thread)*. The hardware (or compiler) identifies independent instructions and issues them concurrently to multiple functional units, or overlaps their execution through pipelining, while preserving the program's logical (program-order) semantics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Granularity:** *FINE* — typically on the order of **1 to 10 cycles** of work. The unit of parallelism is the **instruction** itself.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a single chef in a kitchen cooking one dish at a time — boil water → chop vegetables → cook sauce. Now imagine the same chef, instead of waiting for the water to boil before chopping, sets the pot on the stove, *while* chopping vegetables, *while* preparing the sauce pan. The chef is still cooking **one dish (one thread)**, but is overlapping the **individual steps (instructions)** of that dish. This is exactly what ILP achieves.

**Core Techniques used to extract ILP:**
1. **Pipelining** — overlapping the stages (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Writeback) of successive instructions.
2. **Multiple Issue / Superscalar** — issuing 2, 4, or more instructions per cycle to parallel functional units.
3. **VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)** — the compiler bundles independent operations into one wide instruction word.
4. **Out-of-Order Execution** — dynamic hardware scheduling reorders instructions to keep functional units busy.
5. **Speculative Execution / Branch Prediction** — executing past a predicted branch to keep the pipeline full.
6. **Register Renaming & Tomasulo's Algorithm** — eliminating false (WAR, WAW) data dependencies.

### 1.3 Thread Level Parallelism (TLP)

**Formal Definition:** Thread-Level Parallelism is the simultaneous execution of *multiple threads*, where each thread is an independent sequence of instructions with its own program counter, register context, and (often) stack. Threads may belong to a single process (sharing the same address space) or to independent processes that are explicitly multi-threaded for performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Granularity:** *MEDIUM* — typically on the order of **hundreds to thousands of cycles** of work per thread-switch. The unit of parallelism is the **thread**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Picture a restaurant kitchen. The single-chef model (ILP) runs into a limit — one chef can only overlap so many tasks. Now, hire **two chefs** (two hardware threads) who share the same pantry and refrigerator (shared memory / L2 cache) but have their own cutting boards and knives (private register files and L1 caches). They collaborate on the same restaurant order book (one process) to get dishes out faster. This is **Thread-Level Parallelism**.

**Core Techniques used to extract TLP:**
1. **Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)** — multiple threads issue instructions into the functional units of a *single* physical core in the same cycle.
2. **Chip Multiprocessing (CMP) / Multi-core** — multiple physical cores, each capable of running one or more threads.
3. **Cooperative / Helper Threads** — a master thread spawns a helper thread to perform a speculative or pre-computation task.
4. **Lock-free / Wait-free Synchronization** — primitives enabling threads to coordinate without serialization.

### 1.4 Process Level Parallelism (PLP)

**Formal Definition:** Process-Level Parallelism is the simultaneous execution of *independent processes* (jobs, programs, or tasks), where each process has its own isolated virtual address space, its own I/O resources, and communicates with other processes only via explicit OS-mediated mechanisms (pipes, sockets, message passing, shared-memory APIs, or distributed protocols).

> [!NOTE]
> **Granularity:** *COARSE* — typically on the order of **millions to billions of cycles** of work per process. The unit of parallelism is the **process / job**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Now scale up further: imagine an entire **restaurant chain**. Each branch (process) operates independently with its own kitchen, staff, and cash registers (independent address spaces). The branches exchange only daily sales reports (messages) with the head office. There is very tight coupling between branches — they are almost entirely autonomous. This is **Process-Level Parallelism**.

**Core Techniques used to extract PLP:**
1. **Multiprogramming / Time-Sharing** — the OS schedules multiple processes onto one or more CPUs.
2. **Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)** — multiple CPUs share a common main memory and OS.
3. **Distributed Computing** — processes run on physically separate machines connected by a network (clusters, grids).
4. **Message Passing Interface (MPI), RPC, MapReduce** — software abstractions for inter-process coordination.

### 1.5 The Granularity Ladder — A Unified View

| Level | Unit of Work | Coupling | Communication | Typical Hardware Realization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ILP** | Instruction | Tightest | Implicit (registers) | Pipelined, Superscalar, OoO, VLIW core |
| **TLP** | Thread | Medium | Shared memory / cache coherence | SMT, Multi-core CMP |
| **PLP** | Process | Loosest | OS / Network / Message passing | SMP, NUMA, Cluster, Datacenter |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "I-T-P = I-think-Parallel":** As you go from **ILP → TLP → PLP**, granularity **Increases**, and Coupling **Decreases**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Three Levels? — The Hardware-Software Contract

Modern processor architects exploit *all three* levels simultaneously. A typical 16-core server CPU, for example, uses **ILP** inside each core (via 4-wide issue, OoO, branch prediction), **TLP** across the cores (running 32 hardware threads via SMT × multi-core), and **PLP** across the entire rack (running thousands of independent server processes). The three levels are *complementary, not competing*.

### 2.2 Fundamental Limits of Parallelism

#### 2.2.1 Amdahl's Law (1967)

Amdahl's Law quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** achievable by parallelizing a fraction $f$ of a program, while the remaining fraction $(1 - f)$ remains strictly serial.

Let:
- $T_s$ = execution time on a single processor (the serial baseline).
- $f$ = fraction of the program that can be parallelized, $0 \le f \le 1$.
- $(1 - f)$ = fraction that is inherently serial.
- $n$ = number of parallel processing elements (cores / threads / processes).

The parallelized portion's execution time shrinks to $f \cdot T_s / n$, while the serial portion remains at $(1 - f) \cdot T_s$. The total parallel execution time is therefore:

$$T_p(n) = \left(1 - f\right) \cdot T_s + \frac{f \cdot T_s}{n}$$

The **speedup** $S(n)$ is the ratio of serial to parallel time:

$$S(n) = \frac{T_s}{T_p(n)} = \frac{1}{\left(1 - f\right) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$$

The **asymptotic speedup** as $n \to \infty$ — the holy-grail bound of Amdahl's Law — is:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Even if 95% of a program is parallelized, the maximum speedup with infinite processors is **$1 / (1 - 0.95) = 20\times$**. The serial 5% is the iron ceiling.

#### 2.2.2 Gustafson's Law (1988)

Gustafson observed that Amdahl's Law assumes a *fixed problem size* as $n$ grows, which is unrealistic. He reformulated the model by assuming the **parallel portion scales with $n$** (larger problems, larger machines). Let $f$ now be the *serial* fraction of the *scaled* problem:

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(n) = n - f \cdot (n - 1)$$

This yields nearly **linear** speedup when $f$ is small, reflecting real-world HPC scaling.

#### 2.2.3 Karp-Flatt Metric (1990)

The Karp-Flatt metric exposes the *experimentally observed* serial fraction, useful for detecting hidden serial bottlenecks:

$$f_{\text{obs}} = \frac{1 / S - 1 / n}{1 - 1 / n}$$

If $f_{\text{obs}}$ grows with $n$, the parallel implementation has poor scaling (synchronization, communication, or memory contention overhead).

#### 2.2.4 Parallel Efficiency

Efficiency is the fraction of the theoretical peak that is achieved:

$$E(n) = \frac{S(n)}{n} = \frac{1}{n \cdot \left[\left(1 - f\right) + \dfrac{f}{n}\right]} = \frac{1}{1 + f \cdot (n - 1)}$$

Ideal efficiency is $1.0$ (100%) and is only achieved when $f = 1$ (fully parallel).

### 2.3 Dependency Theory — The Engine of ILP

The maximum ILP extractable from a program is gated by the **dependence graph** of its instructions. There are three true data-dependence classes and two name-dependence classes:

1. **True Data Dependence (RAW — Read After Write):** Instruction $j$ reads a source that instruction $i$ produced. **Cannot be reordered.** This is the only *true* hazard.
2. **Anti-dependence (WAR — Write After Read):** Instruction $j$ writes a destination that instruction $i$ will later read. *False* dependence — eliminated by **register renaming**.
3. **Output Dependence (WAW — Write After Write):** Two instructions write the same destination. *False* dependence — eliminated by **register renaming**.
4. **Control Dependence:** Caused by branches. Resolved by **branch prediction** and **speculative execution**.
5. **Resource Dependence:** Two instructions need the same hardware unit. Resolved by duplicating the unit.

### 2.4 Flynn's Taxonomy — A Cross-Cutting Classification

Flynn's 1966 taxonomy classifies all computer architectures by the multiplicity of *instruction streams* and *data streams*:

| Class | Instruction Streams | Data Streams | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Classic scalar / pipelined uniprocessor (relies on ILP only) |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | Vector units, GPU shader cores, SSE / AVX, ARM NEON |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Rare; fault-tolerant redundant pipelines, systolic arrays |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Multi-core CPUs, SMP, clusters (relies on TLP and PLP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Most modern CPUs are a **hybrid** — they internally use **SIMD** (for ILP on data-parallel loops) and **MIMD** (across cores for TLP and PLP). A modern Intel Core i9 is therefore *SISD + SIMD (in vector unit) + MIMD (multi-core)* all at once.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Recall Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important cheat-sheet for KTU Module 1 derivations.** Memorize the relationships, the boundary conditions, and the units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Limit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $0 \le f \le 1$, $n \ge 1$ | Theoretical max speedup with $n$ processors |
