| 2 | Amdahl's Asymptote | $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | $n \to \infty$ | Hard upper bound on speedup |
| 3 | Gustafson's Law | $S_{\text{G}}(n) = n - f(n-1)$ | scaled problem | Scaled (weak) speedup |
| 4 | Karp-Flatt Metric | $f_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{1/S - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$ | empirically observed serial fraction | Detecting hidden serial bottlenecks |
| 5 | Parallel Efficiency | $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n} = \dfrac{1}{1 + f(n-1)}$ | $0 \le E \le 1$ | How well $n$ processors are utilized |
| 6 | Pipeline Speedup (ideal) | $S_{\text{pipe}} = k$ (k stages) | no hazards, infinite stages | ILP via pipelining |
| 7 | Pipeline Throughput | $T_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | $t_{\text{cycle}} = \max(t_{\text{stage}}) + t_{\text{latch}}$ | ILP throughput bound |
| 8 | CPI (no-pipeline ideal) | $CPI_{\text{ideal}} = 1$ | one instruction completes per cycle | Baseline for ILP gain |
| 9 | Amdahl CPI | $CPI = CPI_{\text{ideal}} + \sum (\text{stalls} \times \text{freq})$ | $f$ replaces stalls | Multicycle & parallel CPI model |
| 10 | ILP Bound (perfect) | $ILP_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\text{avg data-dep distance}}$ | instructions per cycle in window | Theoretical ILP limit |
| 11 | Granularity Ratio | $G = \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}}}{T_{\text{comm}}}$ | $G \gg 1 \Rightarrow$ coarse | ILP vs TLP vs PLP classifier |
| 12 | SMT Speedup (ideal) | $S_{\text{SMT}} = \dfrac{n \cdot IPC_{\text{single}}}{1 + \alpha}$ | $n$ threads, contention $\alpha$ | TLP upper bound on a single core |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ILP (superscalar, OoO, branch prediction)** is the reason a single modern core can retire **4–8 instructions per cycle** at clock rates of **3–5 GHz**, delivering **~20–40 GIPS** per core.
- **TLP (multi-core, SMT)** is the foundation of every server, smartphone SoC, and laptop CPU. A 16-core server with 2-way SMT exposes **32 logical processors** to the OS.
- **PLP (clusters, datacenters, MPI/Hadoop/Spark)** is the substrate of cloud computing, scientific simulation (climate, genomics), and modern AI training (distributed deep learning over thousands of GPUs).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Amdahl's Law from First Principles

We start from the decomposition of a program into a *parallel fraction* and a *serial fraction*, then derive the speedup formula rigorously.

**Step 1 — Decompose the serial execution time.**
Let the total serial execution time on 1 processor be $T_s$. Partition the program into two disjoint parts:

$$T_s = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}}$$

Define the *parallelizable fraction* $f$ as the ratio of the parallel part to the total:

$$f = \frac{T_{\text{parallel}}}{T_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{parallel}} = f \cdot T_s$$

Consequently, the *serial fraction* satisfies:

$$1 - f = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 2 — Apply $n$ parallel processors.**
On $n$ processors, the parallel portion's work is divided *equally* (ideal load balance assumption) among the $n$ units, so its execution time becomes:

$$T_{\text{parallel}}^{(n)} = \frac{T_{\text{parallel}}}{n} = \frac{f \cdot T_s}{n}$$

The serial portion is *unchanged* — it must execute on a single unit:

$$T_{\text{serial}}^{(n)} = (1 - f) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 3 — Add to get total parallel time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p(n) &= T_{\text{serial}}^{(n)} + T_{\text{parallel}}^{(n)} \\
&= (1 - f) \cdot T_s + \frac{f \cdot T_s}{n} \\
&= T_s \cdot \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{n} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Form the speedup ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \frac{T_s}{T_p(n)} \\
&= \frac{T_s}{T_s \cdot \left[(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}\right]} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is Amdahl's Law in canonical form. $\blacksquare$

**Step 5 — Take the asymptotic limit.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - f}
\end{aligned}$$

Even with **infinitely many** processors, the speedup is bounded by $1/(1-f)$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem.** A program takes **100 s** on a uniprocessor. The *parallel fraction* is $f = 0.90$. Compute:
(a) The execution time and speedup on **$n = 8$** processors.
(b) The maximum achievable speedup as $n \to \infty$.
(c) The speedup on **$n = 64$** processors.
(d) The parallel efficiency at $n = 8$ and $n = 64$.

**Solution.**

Given: $T_s = 100$ s, $f = 0.90$, $1 - f = 0.10$.

**Part (a) — $n = 8$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p(8) &= T_s \cdot \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{n} \right] \\
&= 100 \cdot \left[ 0.10 + \frac{0.90}{8} \right] \\
&= 100 \cdot \left[ 0.10 + 0.1125 \right] \\
&= 100 \cdot 0.2125 \\
&= 21.25 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Speedup:

$$\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{T_s}{T_p(8)} = \frac{100}{21.25} \approx 4.706
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Asymptote.**

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10.0$$

**Part (c) — $n = 64$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p(64) &= 100 \cdot \left[ 0.10 + \frac{0.90}{64} \right] \\
&= 100 \cdot \left[ 0.10 + 0.0140625 \right] \\
&= 100 \cdot 0.1140625 \\
&= 11.40625 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \frac{100}{11.40625} \approx 8.767
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (d) — Efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(8) &= \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{4.706}{8} \approx 0.5883 = 58.83\% \\
E(64) &= \frac{S(64)}{64} = \frac{8.767}{64} \approx 0.1370 = 13.70\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Observation:** Note how efficiency collapses from 58.8% (8 cores) to 13.7% (64 cores). This is the *fundamental scaling problem* with a fixed serial bottleneck — a key KTU conceptual trap.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Gustafson's Law

In Gustafson's reformulation, the problem size *scales* with $n$ so that the *total work* grows, while the *runtime* is held fixed. Let the total work done by $n$ processors in time $T_p$ be decomposed into the parallel and serial parts. Define $f$ as the *serial fraction of the scaled problem*:

$$T_p = (1 - f) \cdot T_p + f \cdot T_p$$

The serial work is $W_s = (1 - f) \cdot T_p$ (one unit does it).
The parallel work is $W_p = f \cdot T_p$ (distributed across $n$ units).
On a single processor, all of $W_s + W_p$ would have been done in time:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s &= (1 - f) \cdot T_p + f \cdot T_p \cdot n \\
&= T_p \cdot \left[ (1 - f) + f \cdot n \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Speedup:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{G}}(n) &= \frac{T_s}{T_p} = (1 - f) + f \cdot n \\
&= n - f \cdot (n - 1)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison to Amdahl:** Gustafson *adds* the parallel and serial contributions, while Amdahl *inverts* the sum. For the same $f$ and $n$, Gustafson always gives a more optimistic (and often realistic) speedup, because the problem size is allowed to grow.

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix: ILP Extraction Limits

The maximum exploitable ILP for a typical program window is bounded by three structural quantities:

| Structural Quantity | Typical Value | Effect on ILP |
|---|---|---|
| Average basic-block size (instr.) | 4–8 | Hard upper bound on ILP *within* a basic block |
| Branch prediction accuracy | 90–98% | Limits how far the window can be extended across blocks |
| True data-dependence distance | 1–5 cycles | Limits how soon a consumer can issue |
| Register-renaming table size | 128–256 entries | Limits how many WAW/WAR are eliminated |
| Reorder buffer (ROB) entries | 128–512 | Hard cap on in-flight window |

For an *ideal* machine with infinite resources, perfect prediction, and unbounded renaming, the empirical **ILP ceiling is ~150–200 instructions per cycle** in scientific code and **~4–8 IPC** in integer/scalar code with realistic constraints. This is why **TLP and PLP are indispensable** — ILP alone is bounded by the program's own dependence graph.

### 3.5 Mapping Each Level to Amdahl's $f$

A subtle but high-yield KTU insight is that the three levels attack *different* serial fractions:

- **ILP** reduces the *intra-instruction* serial component (pipeline stalls, data hazards).
- **TLP** reduces the *intra-thread* serial component (e.g., a loop's iterations can be split across threads).
- **PLP** reduces the *cross-process* serial component (different jobs run truly in parallel).

A well-designed parallel application must attack **all three** simultaneously to approach peak performance.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Always state the unit and domain** of $f$ (it is *dimensionless*, $0 \le f \le 1$) — many students write "$f = 0.9$ s" which is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Never confuse Amdahl's $f$ (parallel fraction) with Gustafson's $f$ (serial fraction of the scaled problem).** They are *complementary*. Examiners award a full 2-mark bonus for stating the definition.
> 3. In derivation questions, **carry every step explicitly** — do not skip the algebra from $T_s / T_p$ to the final fraction. KTU award scheme gives partial credit *per step*.
> 4. When asked "compare ILP, TLP, PLP," do not just list bullet points — *contrast* them along the axes of granularity, coupling, communication, and hardware realization. This is the standard 14-mark framework.

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP). List any two hardware techniques used to exploit ILP in modern processors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ILP is the simultaneous execution of multiple instructions belonging to a *single instruction stream* (i.e., a single thread/program), achieved by overlapping their execution while preserving program-order semantics. Two hardware techniques:
1. **Pipelining** — overlapping fetch, decode, execute, memory, and writeback stages of successive instructions.
2. **Multiple-issue (superscalar) with dynamic scheduling (Tomasulo's algorithm / OoO)** — issuing more than one instruction per cycle to parallel functional units, with hardware register renaming to eliminate false dependencies.

*(Acceptable alternatives: VLIW, branch prediction, speculative execution, scoreboarding. Award 1.5 marks for definition, 0.75 each for techniques.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional II]**
*State Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum speedup achievable if 80% of a program is parallelizable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law states that the speedup $S(n)$ obtained by running a program on $n$ processors, of which a fraction $f$ is parallelizable and $(1-f)$ is inherently serial, is:

$$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$$

Given $f = 0.80$, the asymptotic maximum speedup as $n \to \infty$ is:

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

Therefore, even with an *infinite* number of processors, the program cannot run more than **5× faster** than on a single processor. (1.5 marks for the formula, 1.0 for substitution, 0.5 for the conclusion and interpretation.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations:** Module 1 of a 14-mark question typically awards **7 marks for sub-part (a)** and **7 marks for sub-part (b)**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the three basic parallel processing techniques — Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP), Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP), and Process-Level Parallelism (PLP). Compare them on the basis of granularity, communication mechanism, and hardware realization.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Parallel processing is the simultaneous execution of multiple operations to reduce wall-clock time. The three canonical levels are ILP, TLP, and PLP, each differing in *granularity* and *coupling*.

**ILP (2 marks):** The simultaneous execution of multiple instructions *within a single thread*. Granularity is *fine* (1–10 cycles). Communication is *implicit* via the register file and bypassing network. Hardware realization: pipelined superscalar, out-of-order, VLIW cores. Example: Intel's Haswell core issues 4–8 µops/cycle with a 192-entry ROB.

**TLP (2 marks):** The simultaneous execution of multiple *threads* that share an address space. Granularity is *medium* (hundreds–thousands of cycles per thread switch). Communication is *shared memory* with cache coherence (MESI/MOESI). Hardware realization: multi-core CMP, SMT. Example: an 8-core SMT-2 chip exposes 16 logical CPUs.

**PLP (2 marks):** The simultaneous execution of multiple *independent processes* with isolated address spaces. Granularity is *coarse* (millions of cycles per process). Communication is via *OS primitives* (pipes, sockets, shared-memory segments, MPI). Hardware realization: SMP, NUMA, clusters, datacenters.

| Axis | ILP | TLP | PLP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit of work | Instruction | Thread | Process |
| Granularity | Fine | Medium | Coarse |
| Coupling | Tightest | Medium | Loosest |
| Comm. mechanism | Register file | Shared memory | OS / network |
| Hardware | Pipeline, OoO, VLIW | Multi-core, SMT | SMP, cluster |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Modern systems exploit *all three* simultaneously — ILP inside each core, TLP across the cores of one chip, and PLP across the rack or datacenter.

**(b) [7 marks]** A program executes in **200 s** on a single processor. **75%** of the program is parallelizable, while 25% is inherently serial. Compute:
(i) Execution time and speedup on **$n = 4$** processors.
(ii) Execution time and speedup on **$n = 16$** processors.
(iii) The maximum achievable speedup as $n \to \infty$.
(iv) The parallel efficiency at $n = 4$ and $n = 16$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_s = 200$ s, $f = 0.75$, $1 - f = 0.25$.

**(i) For $n = 4$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p(4) &= T_s \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{n} \right] \\
&= 200 \left[ 0.25 + \frac{0.75}{4} \right] \\
&= 200 \left[ 0.25 + 0.1875 \right] \\
&= 200 \times 0.4375 = 87.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{200}{87.5} \approx 2.286
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) For $n = 16$ (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p(16) &= 200 \left[ 0.25 + \frac{0.75}{16} \right] \\
&= 200 \left[ 0.25 + 0.046875 \right] \\
&= 200 \times 0.296875 = 59.375 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \frac{200}{59.375} \approx 3.368
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Asymptotic limit (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\infty} &= \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(4) &= \frac{S(4)}{4} = \frac{2.286}{4} \approx 0.5714 = 57.14\% \\
E(16) &= \frac{S(16)}{16} = \frac{3.368}{16} \approx 0.2105 = 21.05\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Despite increasing $n$ from 4 to 16, the speedup improves only modestly (2.29× → 3.37×) due to the *fixed* 25% serial bottleneck. Efficiency halves, confirming the diminishing-returns principle of Amdahl's Law.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a block diagram the architecture of a **4-wide superscalar, out-of-order processor core** that exploits Instruction-Level Parallelism. Discuss how it overcomes data hazards, structural hazards, and control hazards.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):** A 4-wide superscalar OoO core issues, executes, and retires up to *four* instructions per cycle, exploiting ILP dynamically by reordering instructions while preserving program order at commit.

**Block-diagram description (3 marks — see Section 4.2 above):** The fetch unit pulls 4–8 instructions from the I-cache; the branch predictor (BTB + gshare) redirects fetch; the decoder splits complex µops; the **register rename map table** eliminates WAW/WAR false dependencies; the **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** holds ~256 in-flight instructions; the **issue queue** wakes up ready µops and dispatches them to the *parallel* functional units (2× INT ALU, 2× FP ALU, 2× LSU, branch unit); the LSU accesses the D-cache; results are written to the **physical register file** and committed to the **architectural register file** in program order at retirement.

**Hazard mitigation (3 marks):**
- **Data hazard (RAW):** Mitigated by *forwarding/bypassing* paths and the issue-queue wakeup logic. The renamed tag on the producer is broadcast to dependent consumers.
- **Structural hazard:** Mitigated by *duplicating* functional units (2× INT ALU, 2× FP ALU) and by multi-ported register files and caches.
- **Control hazard:** Mitigated by *speculative fetch* past predicted branches and a *speculative ROB*; mispredictions are squashed at the recovery point.
- **WAW and WAR hazards:** Mitigated by *register renaming* (each write is given a fresh physical register).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** This architecture routinely achieves an *effective IPC* of 2.5–4.0 on integer workloads and 4–6 on floating-point workloads, but is fundamentally bounded by the program's true-dependence graph, motivating the move to TLP (multi-core).

**(b) [7 marks]** Define *parallel efficiency*. A parallel program shows a speedup of **6.0** on **8 processors**. Compute the experimentally observed serial fraction using the **Karp-Flatt metric**, and comment on what it indicates about the program's scaling.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Parallel efficiency is the ratio of the achieved speedup to the number of processors used:

$$E(n) = \frac{S(n)}{n}, \quad 0 \le E(n) \le 1$$

**Given:** $S = 6.0$, $n = 8$.

**Step 1 — Compute efficiency (1 mark):**

$$E(8) = \frac{6.0}{8} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply Karp-Flatt formula (3 marks):**

The Karp-Flatt metric is:

$$f_{\text{obs}} = \frac{1/S - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{obs}} &= \frac{1/6.0 - 1/8}{1 - 1/8} \\
&= \frac{0.1667 - 0.1250}{0.8750} \\
&= \frac{0.0417}{0.8750} \\
&\approx 0.0476
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):** The observed serial fraction is **$f_{\text{obs}} \approx 4.76\%$**. This is the *combined* effect of all sources of serialization — true serial code, synchronization, communication, load imbalance, and memory contention. Because $f_{\text{obs}} \ll 1 - 1/S = 14.3\%$, the implementation is *not* simply suffering from a single dominant serial bottleneck; rather, the missing 33% efficiency is distributed across multiple smaller overheads (cache misses, lock contention, MPI barriers, etc.).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The Karp-Flatt metric is a diagnostic tool — it does not identify the cause but quantifies the *total* unparallelizable overhead. A growing $f_{\text{obs}}$ with $n$ would signal poor scaling.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three levels of parallelism** — ILP, TLP, PLP — differ in *granularity* and *coupling*.
- [x] **ILP** is fine-grained, single-thread, single-cycle window, extracted by **pipelining, superscalar, OoO, VLIW, branch prediction, register renaming**.
- [x] **TLP** is medium-grained, multi-thread, shared-memory, extracted by **multi-core, SMT, helper threads**.
- [x] **PLP** is coarse-grained, multi-process, OS/network-mediated, extracted by **SMP, cluster, MPI, MapReduce**.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$, with the iron ceiling $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law:** $S_{\text{G}}(n) = n - f(n-1)$ — assumes the problem *scales* with $n$ (weak scaling).
- [x] **Karp-Flatt metric:** $f_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{1/S - 1/n}{1 - 1/n}$ — quantifies *all* serial overheads.
- [x] **Parallel efficiency:** $E(n) = S(n)/n = \dfrac{1}{1 + f(n-1)}$; $E = 1$ is ideal.
- [x] **Data hazards** in ILP: RAW (true), WAR (anti, renameable), WAW (output, renameable).
- [x] **Flynn's Taxonomy:** SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD. Modern CPUs are *hybrids* of all four.
- [x] **Modern processor stacks ILP + TLP + PLP simultaneously** — inside a single core (ILP), across cores of a chip (TLP), and across racks of a datacenter (PLP).
- [x] **ILP ceiling** in realistic integer code is **~4–8 IPC**; in scientific code with perfect prediction it can reach **~150 IPC** in theory.
- [x] **Speedup is dimensionless**; **time is in seconds**; **$f$ is a fraction in $[0,1]$** — never attach units to $f$.
- [x] **Amdahl's $f$ = parallel fraction**; **Gustafson's $f$ = serial fraction of the scaled problem** — the most common KTU trap.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"State the formula, define every symbol, substitute the numbers, write the result with units, and end with a one-sentence interpretation."* This single discipline alone routinely adds 2–3 marks to a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Parallel Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Parallel Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Parallel Architecture?

A **Parallel Architecture** is a class of computing systems in which multiple processing elements (PEs) cooperate concurrently to solve a single computational problem by dividing the work into discrete parts that can be executed simultaneously. The classification of such architectures provides a systematic framework to understand **how instructions and data streams** are distributed across processors and memory modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Parallel architectures are classified based on the multiplicity of instruction streams and data streams available to the processor, the degree of coupling between processing elements, and the memory organisation (shared vs. distributed). The most widely accepted academic classification is **Flynn's Taxonomy (1966)**, which forms the foundational vocabulary for all modern parallel system design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Brigade" Model

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen preparing 100 dishes:

- **One Chef, One Dish at a Time (SISD):** A single chef takes one order, prepares it completely, then moves to the next. Slow, but no coordination needed.
- **One Recipe, Many Ingredients Simultaneously (SIMD):** One head chef announces "CHOP!" and every assistant chops the vegetable in front of them in parallel. Same instruction, different data.
- **Many Recipes, One Shared Pantry (Shared-Memory MIMD):** Multiple chefs cook different dishes independently, but they all fetch ingredients from the *same* pantry. Fast, but pantry becomes a bottleneck.
- **Many Recipes, Many Private Pantries (Distributed-Memory MIMD):** Each chef has their own pantry and stove. No contention, but they must message each other when they need an ingredient from another station.

This analogy maps **directly** onto the four classes of Flynn's Taxonomy.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Programming Model Selection** | SIMD requires vectorised code; MIMD supports threads/MPI. |
| **Hardware Cost Estimation** | Shared memory needs fast interconnect; distributed memory scales cheaper. |
| **Performance Bottleneck Identification** | UMA suffers from bus contention; NUMA suffers from remote latency. |
| **Application Suitability** | Scientific computing → SIMD/MIMD; Embedded DSP → systolic. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Historical Note:** Michael J. Flynn's 1966 paper *"Very High-Speed Computing Systems"* introduced the taxonomy. It remains the **single most cited** classification framework in computer architecture, even though it is *not exhaustive* (e.g., it cannot categorise dataflow or systolic machines cleanly).

## 1.4 The Two Orthogonal Axes of Classification

Parallel architectures are classified along **two fundamental dimensions**:

1. **Instruction Stream (I):** The sequence of instructions read from memory.
2. **Data Stream (D):** The sequence of data manipulated by the instructions.

Each stream is either **Single (S)** or **Multiple (M)**, yielding the famous $2 \times 2$ Flynn matrix, plus the **multi-instruction multi-data** extensions for sub-categories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flynn's Taxonomy — The Four Canonical Classes

### 2.1.1 SISD — Single Instruction, Single Data

**Definition:** A traditional sequential computer. One instruction operates on one data item at each clock cycle.

- **Instruction Stream:** Single
- **Data Stream:** Single
- **Examples:** Classic Von-Neumann machines, early PCs (Intel 8086), uniprocessor systems.

**Block Diagram Logic:**
$$\text{Control Unit} \rightarrow \text{Processing Unit} \rightarrow \text{Memory Unit}$$
A single fetch-decode-execute pipeline; no inherent parallelism at the instruction-issue level (though pipelining provides *temporal* overlap, not *spatial*).

**Performance Metric:**
$$\text{Throughput}_{SISD} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle} \times N_{instructions}}$$

where $T_{cycle}$ is the clock period and $N_{instructions}$ is the dynamic instruction count.

### 2.1.2 SIMD — Single Instruction, Multiple Data

**Definition:** A single instruction is broadcast to multiple processing elements, each operating on a different data item in lockstep.

- **Instruction Stream:** Single
- **Data Stream:** Multiple
- **Examples:** Vector processors (Cray-1, NEC SX), GPU compute units (NVIDIA CUDA cores operating in lockstep within an SM), Intel SSE/AVX units.

**Architectural Variants:**

| Sub-class | Organisation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Array Processor** | Tightly coupled PEs under one CU | Scientific matrix math |
| **Vector Processor** | Pipelined vector functional units | Long sequential arrays |
| **GPU SIMT** | Threads grouped into warps, lockstep | Graphics & ML |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** SIMD efficiency depends on the *regularity* of the data. Branch divergence (different threads taking different paths) severely degrades SIMD performance. The metric is **SIMD utilisation factor**:
> $$U_{SIMD} = \frac{N_{active\_PEs}}{N_{total\_PEs}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.3 MISD — Multiple Instruction, Single Data

**Definition:** Multiple instructions operate on the same data item simultaneously. Conceptually rare in commercial hardware.

- **Instruction Stream:** Multiple
- **Data Stream:** Single
- **Theoretical Examples:** Systolic arrays, fault-tolerant TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) systems, pipelined datapaths (debatable classification).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** MISD is the **most debated** class. Most modern texts argue it has **no commercially significant** general-purpose realisation, but it survives in the literature because of systolic and fault-tolerant architectures.

### 2.1.4 MIMD — Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data

**Definition:** Multiple processors, each executing its own instruction stream on its own data, concurrently. **The dominant class for modern HPC.**

- **Instruction Stream:** Multiple
- **Data Stream:** Multiple
- **Examples:** Multi-core CPUs, clusters (Beowulf), supercomputers (Cray XT, IBM Blue Gene).

**Further Sub-Classification (critical for KTU):**

```
MIMD
├── Shared Memory (Tightly Coupled)
│   ├── UMA (Uniform Memory Access)
│   ├── NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)
│   └── COMA (Cache-Only Memory Architecture)
└── Distributed Memory (Loosely Coupled)
    ├── MPP (Massively Parallel Processors)
    ├── Cluster (COW — Cluster of Workstations)
    └── NOW (Network of Workstations)
```

## 2.2 Shared-Memory MIMD Sub-Classes

### 2.2.1 UMA (Uniform Memory Access)

All processors see **identical** memory access latency, regardless of which memory bank they access. Typically realised via a shared bus or crossbar.

$$\forall i, j: \quad T_{access}(PE_i, M_j) = T_{uniform}$$

- **Examples:** Sun Enterprise servers, Intel front-side-bus multi-socket boards.
- **Limitation:** Bus saturation limits scalability to $\approx 16$–$32$ processors.

### 2.2.2 NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)

Each processor has a *local* memory with low latency, and can also access *remote* memories through an interconnect with higher latency.

$$T_{access} = \begin{cases} T_{local}, & \text{if memory is local} \\ T_{remote} = T_{local} + T_{interconnect}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Examples:** SGI Origin 2000, AMD Opteron multi-socket, modern Intel QPI/UPI systems.
- **Performance Ratio:** $R_{NUMA} = T_{remote} / T_{local}$, typically between 1.5 and 5.

### 2.2.3 COMA (Cache-Only Memory Architecture)

A special case of NUMA where the entire local memory is treated as a large **cache** (called an *Attraction Memory*). Data migrates to where it is used.

- **Examples:** Kendall Square Research KSR-1, Swedish Institute of Computer Science (SICS) DDM.
- **Distinguishing Feature:** No memory hierarchy beyond caches — locality is exploited via hardware.

## 2.3 Distributed-Memory MIMD Sub-Classes

### 2.3.1 MPP (Massively Parallel Processor)

- Hundreds to millions of processors.
- Each PE has its own CPU, memory, and OS image.
- Connected via proprietary high-bandwidth, low-latency network (e.g., Cray Torus, IBM Blue Gene torus).
- **Examples:** Cray XC40, Fujitsu K computer, Tianhe-2.

### 2.3.2 Cluster (COW)

- Collection of **off-the-shelf** commodity computers connected by standard networks (Ethernet, InfiniBand).
- Single System Image (SSI) is **optional**.
- **Examples:** Beowulf clusters, Google warehouse-scale computers.
- **Cost Metric:** Performance-per-dollar favours clusters strongly.

## 2.4 Beyond Flynn — Alternative Classifications

### 2.4.1 Dataflow Architecture

Execution is driven by the **availability of data** (dataflow tokens), not by sequential program counters. Architectures: MIT Tagged-Token Dataflow, Manchester Dataflow.

### 2.4.2 Reduction Architecture

Opposite of dataflow: a computation is triggered only when *all* of its operands are available (demand-driven). Basis of functional languages (Haskell, Lisp machines).

### 2.4.3 Systolic Architecture

A network of PEs rhythmically computes and passes data through the system. Suited to regular compute patterns (matrix multiply, convolution).

### 2.4.4 VLIW & Superscalar (Inside-CPU Parallelism)

| Feature | VLIW | Superscalar |
|---|---|---|
| Parallelism Discovery | **Compiler** | **Hardware** |
| Issue Logic | Static | Dynamic |
| Binary Compatibility | Poor | Excellent |
| Examples | Itanium, TI C6x | Intel Core, AMD Zen |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Metrics

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_p$ | Parallel Speedup | $S_p = T_s / T_p$ | dimensionless |
| $E_p$ | Parallel Efficiency | $E_p = S_p / p$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $U_{SIMD}$ | SIMD Utilisation | $U = N_{active} / N_{total}$ | ratio |
| $R_{NUMA}$ | NUMA Latency Ratio | $R = T_{remote} / T_{local}$ | dimensionless |
| $A$ | Amdahl's Limit | $A = 1 / (f + (1-f)/p)$ | dimensionless |
| $f$ | Serial Fraction | $f = T_{serial} / T_{total}$ | dimensionless |
| $C_p$ | Cost | $C_p = p \times T_p$ | time-units |
| $CF$ | Cost-Optimal | $CF \approx 1$ | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law (Critical for KTU):** The maximum achievable speedup is bounded by the serial portion of the program.
> $$S_{max} = \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} = \frac{1}{f}$$
> This is the *single most tested* formula in KTU Modern Processor Architecture papers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **GPU Computing (SIMT):** NVIDIA A100/H100, AMD MI300 — used in deep learning training, CFD, molecular dynamics.
- **Datacentre NUMA Servers:** AWS, Azure bare-metal instances — multi-socket Intel/AMD with NUMA.
- **HPC Clusters (MPP):** Frontier (ORNL), LUMI (CSC) — Top500 supercomputers.
- **Embedded SIMD:** ARM NEON, RISC-V Vector extension — smartphones, IoT.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law for Parallel Speedup

**Problem Setup:** A program has total sequential execution time $T_s$ on a single processor. A fraction $f$ of $T_s$ is *strictly serial* (cannot be parallelised), and the remaining $(1-f)$ can be perfectly parallelised across $p$ processors.

**Step 1:** Express the total sequential time.
$$T_s = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}$$
$$T_s = f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 2:** Compute the parallel execution time on $p$ processors.
$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 3:** Form the speedup ratio.
$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{T_s}{f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot T_s / p}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $T_s$ from numerator and denominator.
$$S_p = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$$

**Step 5:** Take the limit as $p \to \infty$ to find the asymptotic bound.
$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} = \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 6:** Practical implication — for $f = 0.05$ (5% serial), the maximum speedup even with infinite processors is $1/0.05 = 20\times$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Scaled Speedup (Scaled Systems)

**Problem Setup:** Amdahl assumes *fixed problem size* with more processors. Gustafson observes that in practice, *problem size scales* with available hardware.

**Step 1:** Define the scaled parallel time with problem size scaled by $p$.
$$T_p' = f \cdot T_s' + (1-f) \cdot T_s'$$
where $T_s' = p \cdot T_s$ (sequential equivalent of the scaled problem).

**Step 2:** Form the scaled speedup.
$$S_{scaled} = f \cdot p + (1-f)$$

**Step 3:** Compare with Amdahl.
- Amdahl (fixed size): $S_p = 1 / (f + (1-f)/p)$
- Gustafson (scaled size): $S_{scaled} = p - f(p-1)$

**Step 4:** For $p = 1000, f = 0.01$:
- Amdahl: $S = 1/0.01 = 100$
- Gustafson: $S = 1000 - 0.01 \times 999 \approx 990$

This explains why HPC codes scale well in practice.

## 3.3 NUMA Speed-of-Light Analysis

**Setup:** A dual-socket system with local access time $T_L = 80$ ns and remote access time $T_R = 140$ ns. A program executes 60% local, 40% remote memory accesses.

**Step 1:** Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) calculation.
$$AMAT = (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss} + h \cdot T_{hit}$$
where $h$ is hit rate to local memory. Here, $h = 0.6$.

**Step 2:** Compute AMAT.
$$AMAT = 0.6 \times 80 + 0.4 \times 140$$
$$= 48 + 56 = 104 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with ideal UMA where every access is local.
$$\text{Penalty} = \frac{104}{80} = 1.3 \times$$

This is why OS-level NUMA-aware thread pinning is critical in production HPC.

## 3.4 SIMD Utilisation with Branch Divergence

**Setup:** 32-thread warp (NVIDIA architecture). All threads execute a branch; $k$ threads take the *then* path, $(32-k)$ threads take the *else* path.

**Step 1:** Active PE count for the *then* path.
$$N_{active,then} = k$$
$$N_{active,else} = 32 - k$$

**Step 2:** SIMD utilisation (active threads / total threads).
$$U = \frac{\max(k, 32-k)}{32}$$

**Step 3:** Worst case is $k = 16$: $U = 16/32 = 0.5$ — half the warp is idle.
$$U_{min} = 0.5$$

This is the *divergence penalty* that GPU programmers must minimise.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Architecture Class | Instruction | Data | Memory Model | Coupling | Coupling | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SISD | Single | Single | Single | Tight | Tight | Pentium (uniprocessor mode) |
| SIMD | Single | Multiple | Shared | Tight | Tight | Cray-1, GPU SM |
| MISD | Multiple | Single | N/A | Tight | Tight | Systolic Array |
| MIMD-UMA | Multiple | Multiple | Shared | Tight | Tight | Sun E10K |
| MIMD-NUMA | Multiple | Multiple | Shared (partitioned) | Tight | Tight | SGI Origin, Opteron |
| MIMD-COMA | Multiple | Multiple | Cache-only | Tight | Tight | KSR-1 |
| MIMD-MPP | Multiple | Multiple | Distributed | Loose | Loose | Cray XC, Tianhe |
| MIMD-Cluster | Multiple | Multiple | Distributed (message passing) | Loose | Loose | Beowulf, AWS |
| Dataflow | Data-driven | Multiple | Token-based | Tight | Tight | MIT TTDA |
| Reduction | Demand-driven | Multiple | Functional | Tight | Tight | Lisp Machine |
| VLIW | Multiple (static) | Multiple | Shared | Tight (chip) | Tight (chip) | Itanium, DSP |
| Superscalar | Multiple (dynamic) | Multiple | Shared | Tight (chip) | Tight (chip) | Core i9, Zen 5 |

## 3.6 Karplus' Theorem on Mapping (Reference)

For an $N \times N$ mesh-connected processor array, the *bisection bandwidth* scales as $B = O(N)$, while total data movement for a standard algorithm (e.g., FFT) is $O(N^2 \log N)$. Hence, the parallel time bound is:
$$T_p \geq \frac{O(N^2 \log N)}{O(N)} = O(N \log N)$$

This is fundamental to the analysis of array processors and is occasionally asked in KTU Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flynn's Taxonomy — Mermaid Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    PAR[Parallel Architectures] --> FLYNN[Flynn Taxonomy 1966]
    FLYNN --> SISD[SISD - Single Instruction Single Data]
    FLYNN --> SIMD[SIMD - Single Instruction Multiple Data]
    FLYNN --> MISD[MISD - Multiple Instruction Single Data]
    FLYNN --> MIMD[MIMD - Multiple Instruction Multiple Data]

    SISD --> SISD1[Von Neumann Uni Processor]
    SISD --> SISD2[Classic PC Intel 8086]

    SIMD --> SIMD1[Array Processor]
    SIMD --> SIMD2[Vector Processor Cray 1]
    SIMD --> SIMD3[GPU SIMT Warp]

    MISD --> MISD1[Systolic Array]
    MISD --> MISD2[Fault Tolerant TMR]

    MIMD --> MIMDSHR[Shared Memory Tightly Coupled]
    MIMD --> MIMDDIS[Distributed Memory Loosely Coupled]

    MIMDSHR --> UMA[UMA Uniform Memory Access]
    MIMDSHR --> NUMA[NUMA Non Uniform Memory Access]
    MIMDSHR --> COMA[COMA Cache Only Memory]

    MIMDDIS --> MPP[MPP Massively Parallel]
    MIMDDIS --> CLUST[Cluster COW]
    MIMDDIS --> NOW[NOW Network of Workstations]

    classDef def fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef shr fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef dis fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef leaf fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff;
    class PAR,FLYNN def;
    class SISD,SIMD,MISD,MIMD def;
    class MIMDSHR shr;
    class MIMDDIS dis;
    class SISD1,SISD2,SIMD1,SIMD2,SIMD3,MISD1,MISD2,UMA,NUMA,COMA,MPP,CLUST,NOW leaf;
```

## 4.2 UMA vs NUMA vs COMA — Memory Access Latency

```mermaid
graph LR
    PE_A[PE 0]:::proc
    PE_B[PE 1]:::proc
    PE_C[PE 2]:::proc
    PE_D[PE 3]:::proc

    BUS((Shared Bus or Crossbar)):::bus

    M0[Memory Bank 0]:::mem
    M1[Memory Bank 1]:::mem
    M2[Memory Bank 2]:::mem
    M3[Memory Bank 3]:::mem

    PE_A --- BUS
    PE_B --- BUS
    PE_C --- BUS
    PE_D --- BUS
    BUS --- M0
    BUS --- M1
    BUS --- M2
    BUS --- M3

    note1[UMA: All accesses identical latency T]:::note
    BUS -.- note1
```
