topics.title: Concept of Arithmetic Pipelining and Instruction Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Arithmetic Pipelining and Instruction Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Pipelining?

**Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions or arithmetic operations are overlapped in execution. It divides a sequential process into a series of independent or semi-independent stages, where each stage performs a specific subtask. While one instruction is being processed in one stage, another instruction can be simultaneously processed in a different stage, dramatically improving throughput without reducing the latency of a single operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Pipelining is a technique of decomposing a sequential process into sub-operations and executing them in parallel on dedicated hardware segments, where the result of one sub-operation is passed as input to the next sub-operation, synchronized by a common clock.

### 1.2 Two Fundamental Forms of Pipelining

#### A. Arithmetic Pipelining
**Arithmetic Pipelining** is the application of the pipelining concept to the *internal data path* of a computational unit. A complex arithmetic operation (such as floating-point addition or multiplication) is decomposed into multiple sub-operations (such as exponent comparison, mantissa alignment, mantissa addition, and normalization). Each sub-operation is implemented in a separate dedicated hardware segment, and operands flow through these segments in an assembly-line fashion.

**Classic Example:** The floating-point adder pipeline (used in MIPS-style architectures) decomposes addition into stages: Exponent Compare → Mantissa Align → Mantissa Add → Normalize.

#### B. Instruction Pipelining
**Instruction Pipelining** is the application of pipelining to the *instruction execution cycle* of a processor. The instruction fetch–decode–execute cycle is decomposed into stages so that multiple instructions can be in different stages of execution simultaneously. A standard 5-stage RISC pipeline performs: Instruction Fetch (IF) → Instruction Decode (ID) → Execute (EX) → Memory Access (MEM) → Write Back (WB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, the focus is on **establishing the foundational concept** of pipelining. Hazards, forwarding, and branch prediction are typically covered in Module 3. Module 2 limits the discussion to the *ideal* pipelining model and its performance benefits.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Laundry Assembly Line

Imagine a small laundry shop with **three washing machines** and **one operator**. A naive sequential approach would be: Wash Batch 1 → Dry Batch 1 → Fold Batch 1 → Wash Batch 2 → Dry Batch 2 → Fold Batch 2. 

In this approach, the dryer sits idle while washing is happening, and the folder sits idle while drying is happening. The total wall-clock time is huge.

Now consider a **pipelined approach**:
- **Time 1:** Wash Batch 1
- **Time 2:** Dry Batch 1, AND simultaneously Wash Batch 2
- **Time 3:** Fold Batch 1, Dry Batch 2, Wash Batch 3
- **Time 4:** Fold Batch 2, Dry Batch 3
- **Time 5:** Fold Batch 3

Once the pipeline is **filled (steady state)**, a new batch finishes every single clock cycle, even though a *single* batch still takes 3 cycles to complete. The throughput is **tripled**, while the per-item latency is **unchanged**.

This is *exactly* how instruction and arithmetic pipelines work: dedicated hardware segments perform sub-tasks, and a new instruction/data pair enters the pipeline every clock cycle.

### 1.4 Key Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **Pipeline Stage Delay ($\tau$):** The time taken by the slowest stage. It sets the minimum clock cycle time of the pipeline. Unit: **nanoseconds**.
- **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** The clock period of the pipelined processor. $T_c \geq \tau$.
- **Latency (L):** The time required for **one** instruction/operation to traverse the entire pipeline from start to finish.
- **Throughput (TP):** The number of instructions/operations completed **per unit time** once the pipeline is full.
- **Speedup (S):** The ratio of the execution time of the un-pipelined (sequential) version to the pipelined version.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant for KTU Numericals:** A 5-stage instruction pipeline has **5** stages ($k = 5$). A floating-point adder with 4 sub-operations has **4** stages ($k = 4$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Arithmetic Pipeline

An arithmetic pipeline operates on **operands** rather than instructions. The classic textbook example (drawn from the IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic pipeline used in most KTU reference texts) decomposes floating-point addition into **four sub-operations**:

1. **Stage 1 — Compare Exponents:** Subtract the exponents to determine the alignment shift amount. The exponent difference $d = E_A - E_B$ is computed.
2. **Stage 2 — Align Mantissas:** Shift the mantissa of the smaller operand **right** by $d$ bit positions to make the exponents equal. An overflow guard bit is preserved.
3. **Stage 3 — Add Mantissas:** Add (or subtract, depending on the sign bits) the now-aligned mantissas using a fast carry-lookahead adder.
4. **Stage 4 — Normalize Result:** Shift the resulting mantissa left or right until it is in the form $1.xxx\ldots x$, and correspondingly adjust the exponent.

Each of these four stages is implemented in its own combinational hardware block, separated by **pipeline registers** that latch the intermediate result on the rising edge of every clock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pipeline registers are mandatory:** Without registers between combinational blocks, the output of Stage 1 would race through Stage 4 in a single cycle. Registers enforce the synchronous "handshake" so that exactly one operand pair advances per cycle.

### 2.2 Anatomy of an Instruction Pipeline (5-Stage RISC Model)

A canonical RISC instruction pipeline decomposes instruction execution into the following five stages:

1. **IF (Instruction Fetch):** Read the next instruction from the instruction memory using the program counter (PC).
2. **ID (Instruction Decode / Register Read):** Decode the opcode, read source register operands from the register file, and sign-extend any immediate field.
3. **EX (Execute / Effective Address):** Perform the ALU operation (add, subtract, AND, OR, compare) for register–register instructions, or compute the effective memory address for load/store.
4. **MEM (Memory Access):** For loads, read from the data memory; for stores, write to the data memory. Register–register ALU instructions pass through this stage doing nothing.
5. **WB (Write Back):** Write the result of the ALU or memory read back into the destination register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous Hand-off:** Between every two stages, there exists a set of pipeline registers — conventionally named **IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB** — which latch all control signals, operand values, and addressing information. These registers are what *physically* implement the pipelining.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Sequential execution time | $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$ | $n$ = # of operations, $k$ = # of stages, $\tau$ = stage delay | ns |
| 2 | Pipelined execution time (ideal) | $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$ | Same as above | ns |
| 3 | Ideal Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) | As $n \to \infty$, $S \to k$ |
| 4 | Maximum Speedup (limit) | $S_{\max} = k$ | Number of pipeline stages | Achievable only when $n \gg k$ |
| 5 | Throughput (steady state) | $TP = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | $T_c$ = clock cycle, $\tau$ = stage delay | Operations per second |
| 6 | Pipeline Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Expressed as a fraction or % | $\eta \to 100\%$ as $n \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Clock Cycle (balanced stages) | $T_c = \tau_{\max} + t_{reg}$ | $\tau_{\max}$ = slowest stage, $t_{reg}$ = register overhead | ns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Never use the vertical bar symbol `\|` in a KTU answer sheet for absolute value. Write $\vert x \vert$ explicitly using `\vert` in LaTeX. In handwriting, draw clear small vertical strokes.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Pipelining

Arithmetic pipelining and instruction pipelining are the **backbone of modern computing performance**. Every contemporary CPU, GPU, and DSP chip relies on them:

- **Intel/AMD x86 cores** use 14–20+ stage instruction pipelines for high clock frequencies.
- **NVIDIA GPU Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)** use deep arithmetic pipelines for matrix and tensor operations.
- **Qualcomm Snapdragon / Apple M-series mobile SoCs** combine wide-issue instruction pipelines with floating-point arithmetic pipelines for AI inference acceleration.
- **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** use dedicated multiply–accumulate (MAC) arithmetic pipelines for real-time audio, radar, and telecommunications.

The engineering utility is universal: **doing more useful work per clock cycle, per watt, and per square millimeter of silicon.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pipelined Execution Time

**Given:**
- $k$ = number of pipeline stages
- $\tau$ = time delay of the slowest stage (which sets the clock period)
- $n$ = number of independent operations/instructions to be processed

**Step 1 — Sequential Execution Time:**
In the un-pipelined version, every operation must traverse all $k$ stages before the next one begins. Therefore, $n$ operations take:

$$T_{seq} = n \cdot (k \cdot \tau)$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined Execution Time:**
In the pipelined version, the *first* operation still takes $k \cdot \tau$ time to complete (it must travel through all $k$ stages one after another). However, once the pipeline is filled, **one new operation completes every $\tau$ time units**. Thus, the remaining $(n - 1)$ operations contribute $(n - 1) \cdot \tau$ additional time.

$$T_{pipe} = k \cdot \tau + (n - 1) \cdot \tau = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Speedup Ratio:**
The speedup $S$ is defined as the ratio of sequential time to pipelined time:

$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(k + n - 1) \cdot \tau} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting Behavior (Asymptote):**
As the number of operations $n$ grows very large compared to $k$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k \cdot n}{n \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{k-1}{n}\right)} = k$$

This shows the **maximum theoretical speedup equals the number of pipeline stages**.

**Step 5 — Pipeline Efficiency:**
Efficiency measures how well the pipeline resources are utilized on average:

$$\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\eta \to 1$ (i.e., 100% utilization).

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example 1 — Arithmetic Pipeline Performance

**Problem Statement:** A 4-stage floating-point arithmetic pipeline has each stage taking 30 ns. Compute:
1. The time to compute **30 floating-point additions** sequentially.
2. The time using the pipeline.
3. The speedup achieved.
4. The throughput and efficiency.

**Solution — Part (a): Sequential Time**

$$T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau = 30 \cdot 4 \cdot 30 \text{ ns} = 3600 \text{ ns}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Pipelined Time**

$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau = (4 + 30 - 1) \cdot 30 \text{ ns} = 33 \cdot 30 \text{ ns} = 990 \text{ ns}$$

**Solution — Part (c): Speedup**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{3600}{990} \approx 3.636$$

**Solution — Part (d): Throughput and Efficiency**

$$TP = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{30 \text{ ns}} \approx 33.33 \text{ million additions/sec}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{3.636}{4} = 0.909 = 90.9\%$$

**Interpretation:** The pipeline is $\approx 91\%$ utilized. To reach $95\%$ efficiency, we would need many more additions ($\approx 76$).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example 2 — Instruction Pipeline Speedup Limit

**Problem Statement:** A RISC processor has a 5-stage instruction pipeline. The clock cycle time is set by the slowest stage, which takes 2 ns. Compare the execution of **1000 instructions** in pipelined vs. non-pipelined form.

**Solution:**

Using $k = 5$, $n = 1000$, $\tau = 2$ ns:

$$T_{seq} = 1000 \cdot 5 \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 10000 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{pipe} = (5 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 1004 \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 2008 \text{ ns}$$

$$S = \dfrac{10000}{2008} \approx 4.98$$

This confirms the theoretical limit $S \to k = 5$ is approached very closely when $n \gg k$.

### 3.4 Pipeline Timing Diagram (Tabular Form)

The following table depicts the pipeline's behavior at each clock cycle for 4 instructions (I1, I2, I3, I4) in a 5-stage instruction pipeline. Each cell shows which stage is active for which instruction:

| Clock Cycle | Stage 1 (IF) | Stage 2 (ID) | Stage 3 (EX) | Stage 4 (MEM) | Stage 5 (WB) |
|:-----------:|:------------:|:------------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:------------:|
| 1 | **I1** | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | I2 | **I1** | — | — | — |
| 3 | I3 | I2 | **I1** | — | — |
| 4 | I4 | I3 | I2 | **I1** | — |
| 5 | I5 | I4 | I3 | I2 | **I1** |
| 6 | I6 | I5 | I4 | I3 | I2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reading Rule:** The boldface entry on any row is the instruction that *finishes* (writes back) at the end of that clock cycle. By Clock 5, the pipeline is full and one instruction completes per cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a 4-Stage Arithmetic Pipeline (Floating-Point Adder)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional architecture of a 4-stage arithmetic pipeline that performs floating-point addition. Operand pairs $(A, B)$ flow left to right through dedicated hardware segments separated by pipeline registers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Operand A: Exponent EA, Mantissa MA] --> R1
    B1[Operand B: Exponent EB, Mantissa MB] --> R1
    R1([Pipeline Reg 1]) --> S1
    S1[Stage 1: Exponent Compare] --> R2
    R2([Pipeline Reg 2]) --> S2
    S2[Stage 2: Mantissa Alignment Shift] --> R3
    R3([Pipeline Reg 3]) --> S3
    S3[Stage 3: Mantissa Addition via CLA] --> R4
    R4([Pipeline Reg 4]) --> S4
    S4[Stage 4: Normalize and Round] --> OUT[Normalized FP Result]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two input operands are latched by Reg 1. Each subsequent clock cycle advances the partially processed pair one stage to the right. After four cycles, the normalized sum emerges at the right.

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline (RISC)

The following diagram illustrates the canonical 5-stage instruction pipeline, showing the dedicated functional units and the pipeline registers that separate them.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IMEM[Instruction Memory] --> REG1
    REG1([IF/ID Register]) --> REG2
    REG2([ID/EX Register]) --> REG3
    REG3([EX/MEM Register]) --> REG4
    REG4([MEM/WB Register]) --> WBOUT[Write Back to Register File]
    RF[Register File] -. read operands .-> REG2
    ALU[ALU Unit] -. compute .- REG3
    DMEM[Data Memory] -. read/write .- REG4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the main flow of the instruction and its intermediate data (operands, ALU result, memory data). Dotted arrows indicate the side-inputs that each stage consumes — such as the Register File being read in ID, the ALU being driven in EX, and the Data Memory being accessed in MEM.

### 4.3 State Diagram: Pipeline Stages per Clock Cycle

This diagram shows how 5 instructions advance through the pipeline stages over 9 consecutive clock cycles. Each instruction enters at IF, advances one column per cycle, and exits at WB.

```mermaid
graph TD
    c1[Clock 1: I1 in IF] --> c2[Clock 2: I1 in ID, I2 in IF]
    c2 --> c3[Clock 3: I1 in EX, I2 in ID, I3 in IF]
    c3 --> c4[Clock 4: I1 in MEM, I2 in EX, I3 in ID, I4 in IF]
    c4 --> c5[Clock 5: I1 in WB, I2 in MEM, I3 in EX, I4 in ID, I5 in IF]
    c5 --> c6[Clock 6: I1 done, I2 in WB, I3 in MEM, I4 in EX, I5 in ID, I6 in IF]
    c6 --> c7[Clock 7: I3 WB, I4 MEM, I5 EX, I6 ID, I7 IF]
    c7 --> c8[Clock 8: I4 WB, I5 MEM, I6 EX, I7 ID, I8 IF]
    c8 --> c9[Clock 9: I5 WB, and so on]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice that at **Clock 5**, the pipeline first becomes "full" — all five stages are simultaneously busy with five different instructions. From this point onward, one instruction completes (exits WB) every single clock cycle. This is the **steady-state throughput**.

### 4.4 Comparison Block: Arithmetic vs. Instruction Pipelining

```mermaid
graph TB
    AP[Arithmetic Pipelining]
    IP[Instruction Pipelining]
    AP --> AP1[Operates on: Numeric Operands]
    AP --> AP2[Granularity: Sub-operation level e.g. mantissa align]
    AP --> AP3[Example: Floating-point adder with 4 stages]
    AP --> AP4[Benefit: Higher numeric throughput for FP workloads]
    IP --> IP1[Operates on: Program Instructions]
    IP --> IP2[Granularity: Instruction-cycle stage e.g. IF ID EX MEM WB]
    IP --> IP3[Example: 5-stage RISC scalar pipeline]
    IP --> IP4[Benefit: Higher IPC Instructions per Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Bridge:** Although the two forms of pipelining differ in *what* they pipeline (data vs. control flow), they share the *same* mathematical performance model. The formulas in the KTU formula sheet apply identically to both.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define arithmetic pipelining. How does it differ from instruction pipelining?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Arithmetic Pipelining:** It is the technique of subdividing a complex arithmetic operation (e.g., floating-point addition) into a sequence of independent sub-operations, each performed by a dedicated hardware segment separated by pipeline registers, so that multiple operand pairs can be in different stages of the arithmetic computation simultaneously.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Arithmetic Pipelining | Instruction Pipelining |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Object pipelined | Numeric operands | Machine instructions |
| Decomposition unit | Sub-operation (e.g., exponent compare) | Instruction cycle stage (IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB) |
| Typical depth | 3–7 stages | 5–20+ stages |

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the table of differences.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam Style]**
*State the formula for ideal speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline processing $n$ tasks. What is its limiting value as $n \to \infty$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ideal speedup is given by:

$$S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

The limiting value as $n \to \infty$ is:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$$

Hence, the **maximum theoretical speedup** of a $k$-stage pipeline is equal to **$k$**. *(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the simplification, 1 mark for the limit statement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Arithmetic Pipelining Focus

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the concept of a 4-stage floating-point arithmetic pipeline. List the function of each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-stage arithmetic pipeline processes **50 pairs of floating-point operands**. The stage delays are 25 ns, 30 ns, 20 ns, and 35 ns respectively. Determine:
1. The clock cycle time of the pipeline.
2. The total time to process 50 operand pairs.
3. The speedup achieved over a non-pipelined implementation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks:**

A 4-stage floating-point arithmetic pipeline performs IEEE 754 addition by dividing the operation into four sub-operations:

1. **Stage 1 — Compare Exponents:** Compute $d = E_A - E_B$. This determines the alignment shift required.
2. **Stage 2 — Align Mantissas:** Right-shift the mantissa of the smaller operand by $d$ positions; preserve a guard, round, and sticky bit.
3. **Stage 3 — Add Mantissas:** Add the aligned mantissas using a carry-lookahead adder (or subtract if signs differ).
4. **Stage 4 — Normalize Result:** Shift the resulting mantissa and adjust the exponent until the leading 1 is in the correct MSB position. Apply rounding.

A neat block diagram was given in **Section 4.1** above (4 pipeline registers, 4 functional segments, operands in, normalized FP sum out). *(4 marks for diagram and explanation, 1 mark each for the four stage functions = 4 + 3 = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Clock Cycle Time:**
The clock cycle is set by the slowest stage:

$$\tau_{\max} = \max(25, 30, 20, 35) = 35 \text{ ns}$$

Assuming negligible register overhead:

$$T_c = 35 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Total Pipelined Time:**
Using $k = 4$, $n = 50$, $\tau = 35$ ns:

$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau = (4 + 50 - 1) \cdot 35 = 53 \cdot 35 = 1855 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Sequential Time (for speedup comparison):**

$$T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau = 50 \cdot 4 \cdot 35 = 7000 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup:**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{7000}{1855} \approx 3.77$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{3.77}{4} \approx 94.3\%$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for step 1, 2 marks for step 2, 2 marks for step 3 and 4 combined, 1 mark for the final answer and interpretation.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Instruction Pipelining Focus

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Exam Style]**

**(a)** Describe the 5-stage instruction pipeline of a RISC processor with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each pipeline register. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a RISC processor with a 5-stage pipeline where each stage takes 2 ns. Compute the time to execute **500 instructions** in:
1. Non-pipelined mode.
2. Pipelined mode.
3. Also compute the throughput in MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) of the pipelined design in steady state. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks:**

The canonical 5-stage RISC pipeline consists of the following stages and pipeline registers:

| Pipeline Register | Located Between | Purpose |
|-------------------|-----------------|---------|
| **IF/ID** | Stage 1 (IF) and Stage 2 (ID) | Latches fetched instruction and PC+4 |
| **ID/EX** | Stage 2 (ID) and Stage 3 (EX) | Latches decoded control signals, read register values, sign-extended immediate |
| **EX/MEM** | Stage 3 (EX) and Stage 4 (MEM) | Latches ALU result, effective address, and memory control signals |
| **MEM/WB** | Stage 4 (MEM) and Stage 5 (WB) | Latches memory-read data or ALU result for write-back |

The block diagram given in **Section 4.2** illustrates the architecture. The 5 stages are:
- **IF:** Instruction Memory access using PC.
- **ID:** Decode + Register File read.
- **EX:** ALU operation or address computation.
- **MEM:** Data Memory access for load/store.
- **WB:** Write result back to Register File.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for stage explanation, 2 marks for register role description.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined Execution Time:**

$$T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau = 500 \cdot 5 \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 5000 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined Execution Time:**

$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau = (5 + 500 - 1) \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 504 \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 1008 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Throughput:**

$$TP = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \text{ ns}} = 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ instructions/sec} = 500 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup Cross-Verification:**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{5000}{1008} \approx 4.96 \approx 5 \text{ (theoretical max)}$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 distribution respectively for steps 1, 2, 3, and 4.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+ (n−1)" Term:** Many students write $T_{pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$, which is the *sequential* formula. This is the **#1 mark-killer** for this module. Always remember: the pipeline still takes $k \cdot \tau$ to fill (latency), and then 1 result per $\tau$.
> 2. **Using $\tau_{avg}$ Instead of $\tau_{max}$:** The clock cycle must equal the **slowest** stage delay. Averaging stage delays is *not* correct because hardware cannot speed up a slow stage.
> 3. **Confusing Arithmetic and Instruction Pipeline Stages:** A 4-stage floating-point adder has $k=4$ sub-operations, NOT 5. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Not Reporting Units:** Always carry "ns" through calculations. KTU evaluators penalize missing units strictly.
> 5. **Claiming Speedup = $k$ Always:** The formula $S = k$ is the *limit* only. For small $n$, compute the full formula $S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipelining** is the simultaneous execution of multiple operations by decomposing each into sub-operations performed by dedicated hardware stages.
- **Arithmetic Pipelining** targets the **data path** (e.g., floating-point add, multiply, MAC). **Instruction Pipelining** targets the **control path** (IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB).
- **Pipeline Registers** between stages are *essential* — they synchronize the data flow and isolate the combinational logic of each stage.
- **Clock cycle** = slowest stage delay (plus register overhead).
- **Sequential time:** $T_{seq} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$.
- **Pipelined time:** $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$.
- **Speedup:** $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} \to k$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1} \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Throughput:** $TP = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ operations per second in steady state.
- The pipeline achieves its performance benefit **only in steady state**; the first result has a latency penalty of $k \cdot \tau$.
- For **high-yield numerical answers in KTU**, always: (1) identify $k$ and $n$ from the question; (2) use the slow-stage delay as $\tau$; (3) show the full formula and plug in values; (4) report units and (5) verify that speedup is less than $k$.
- Hazards (structural, data, control) are covered in Module 3 — the present Module 2 assumes an **ideal, hazard-free** pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Deeply Pipelined Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Deeply Pipelined Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by dividing the instruction processing into a series of independent stages, with each stage performing a portion of the total work and passing the partial result to the next stage.

A **Deeply Pipelined Processor** is one in which the instruction execution path is decomposed into a very large number of *fine-grained stages* (typically **15 to 31 stages** in modern superscalar/EPIC processors like Intel Pentium 4, AMD Bulldozer, and certain DSP architectures). The objective is to shorten the critical combinational path per stage, thereby enabling a higher operating clock frequency and consequently higher instruction throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Deep pipelining is a foundational concept that drives the design of all modern high-performance CPUs. The KTU board specifically tests the student's ability to reason about the *latency vs. throughput trade-off* and the *hazards introduced by deeper pipelines*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **car manufacturing assembly line** in a factory:

- **Without Pipelining (Sequential):** One worker assembles the entire car from bumper to bumper before the next car is started. This takes a long time per car (high **latency**), but the workshop is simple.
- **With Pipelining:** The assembly is split into 20 stations. Station 1 installs the engine, Station 2 mounts the wheels, Station 3 paints the body, and so on. While Station 1 is mounting the engine of *Car N*, Station 2 is mounting wheels of *Car N–1*, Station 3 is painting *Car N–2*, etc. The time to complete one car stays roughly the same, but a *new car emerges every few minutes* instead of every few hours.

In a **deeply pipelined processor**, the "assembly stations" are hardware stages such as **Instruction Fetch (IF), Instruction Decode (ID), Operand Fetch / Register Read (RF), Execute (EX), Memory Access (MEM), and Write Back (WB)** — and crucially, in deep pipelines, even the EX and MEM stages are themselves sub-divided (e.g., into multiple ALU/MEM pipe stages).

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded)

- **Clock Cycle Time ($T_{clk}$)** — Time taken for one pipeline stage to complete its work.
- **Throughput** — Number of instructions completed per unit time, ideally **1 instruction per cycle** (IPC ≈ 1).
- **Latency** — Total time for a single instruction to traverse all stages.
- **Pipeline Depth ($k$)** — Total number of stages in the pipeline.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — Average cycles consumed per instruction in the presence of hazards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Deep Pipelining

A deeply pipelined processor is engineered by observing that the **critical path** (longest combinational delay) in a processor is typically dominated by a few operations such as the ALU or cache lookup. By *inserting pipeline registers* between sub-operations, the long combinational path is sliced into shorter, equal-length segments.

**Operational Flow — Why Deep Pipelining Works:**

- **Why it works:** The original combinational path $T_{comb}$ is split into $k$ sub-paths, so the new clock period is $T_{clk} \approx T_{comb}/k + T_{reg\_setup}$, where $T_{reg\_setup}$ is the latch/flip-flop setup overhead.
- **How it works:** Every clock edge, the partial results of each instruction advance from stage $i$ to stage $i+1$. Multiple instructions thus occupy the pipeline simultaneously, each in a different stage.
- **Limitation:** As $k$ increases, $T_{reg\_setup}$ and clock-skew begin to dominate. This produces a point of **diminishing returns** beyond which deeper pipelining no longer improves performance.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** that appear in KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination questions for this module. Memorize them with their boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Boundary Conditions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Speedup** of an $n$-stage pipeline over a non-pipelined version | $S_{ideal} = n$ | Assumes equal stage delays, no hazards; dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | **Actual Speedup** with $n$ stages and $s$ stall cycles per instruction | $S_{actual} = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{orig}}{T_{clk} + s \cdot T_{clk}}$ | $T_{orig}$ = original non-pipelined execution time per instruction |
| 3 | **Pipeline Cycle Time** with $k$ stages | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{T_{comb}}{k} + T_{reg}$ | $T_{reg}$ = pipeline register delay (sec) |
| 4 | **Pipeline Throughput** | $TP = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Instructions per second |
| 5 | **Average CPI** in a pipeline with hazards | $CPI = CPI_{ideal} + Stall_{data} + Stall_{control} + Stall_{structural}$ | $CPI_{ideal}=1$ for scalar pipeline |
| 6 | **Total Execution Time** for $N$ instructions | $T_{exec} = \left( k + (N-1) \right) \cdot T_{clk}$ | Assumes no stalls; for $N \gg k$ |
| 7 | **Stall Penalty per Hazard** | $P_{stall} = n_{stall} \cdot T_{clk}$ | $n_{stall}$ = number of bubble cycles |
| 8 | **Limit Frequency (Deep Pipeline Saturation)** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{reg} + \epsilon}$ | $\epsilon$ = minimum logic delay; Hz |
| 9 | **Pipeline Drain Time** on a flush/branch mispredict | $T_{drain} = k \cdot T_{clk}$ | $k$ = pipeline depth in stages |
| 10 | **Hazards per Instruction** for deep pipelines | $H \propto k$ | Hazard count grows approximately linearly with depth |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deeply pipelined processors dominate virtually every modern computing domain:

- **General-Purpose CPUs:** Intel Core series (14–19 stages), AMD Zen 4 (19 stages), Apple M-series (14+ stages).
- **DSPs / Embedded:** Texas Instruments C6x VLIW (up to 11 stages), used in 5G baseband and audio codecs.
- **GPUs:** NVIDIA Ampere / Ada Lovelace use very deep pipelines (≥20 stages in shader cores) to push GHz frequencies.
- **RISC-V High-Performance Cores:** SiFive P870 and similar designs use deep pipelines combined with wide issue (8-wide superscalar) to maximize ILP.

The engineering trade-off — deeper pipelines boost frequency but increase misprediction penalty, hazard count, and energy per stage flip — is one of the **most heavily tested** design decisions in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup of an $n$-Stage Pipeline Over a Non-Pipelined Design

**Problem Setup:** Consider a non-pipelined processor executing a single instruction in $T_{orig}$ seconds. We pipeline the same logic into $k$ equal stages, each taking $T_{clk}$ seconds, and process $N$ instructions.

**Step 1 — Express the non-pipelined execution time per instruction:**

$$T_{orig} = T_{comb} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the pipelined cycle time (assuming equal stage delays):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{comb} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i \quad \text{(sum of stage delays)} \\
T_{clk} &= \frac{T_{comb}}{k} + T_{reg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The original critical path is divided equally across $k$ stages. Each stage latch adds a small register overhead $T_{reg}$.

**Step 3 — Express the total time to execute $N$ instructions in the pipeline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pipe} &= \underbrace{k \cdot T_{clk}}_{\text{fill the pipeline (1st instr. completes)}} + \underbrace{(N-1) \cdot T_{clk}}_{\text{remaining }(N-1)\text{ instr. complete 1 per cycle}} \\
T_{pipe} &= (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the non-pipelined total time:**

$$T_{non} = N \cdot T_{orig}$$

**Step 5 — Form the speedup ratio $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{non}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{N \cdot T_{orig}}{(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the limit as $N \to \infty$ (steady state, $N \gg k$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot T_{orig}}{(N + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}} \\
&= \frac{T_{orig}}{T_{clk}} = \frac{k \cdot T_{clk} - k \cdot T_{reg}}{T_{clk}} \quad \text{(ignoring }T_{reg}\text{ for ideal case)} \\
S_{ideal} &= k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In the limit of infinite instructions and zero register overhead, an $n$-stage pipeline achieves an ideal speedup of exactly $k$ (the number of stages). This is the **theoretical upper bound**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Pipeline Depth on Frequency (Detailed Numerical Example)

**Problem:** A processor has a critical path of $T_{comb} = 8 \text{ ns}$. Pipeline register overhead is $T_{reg} = 0.5 \text{ ns}$.

| Variant | Stages $k$ | $T_{clk}$ (ns) | Max Frequency $f_{max}$ (MHz) | Speedup over 1-stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1-stage | 1 | $8.0 + 0.5 = 8.5$ | $117.6$ | $1.00\times$ |
| 4-stage | 4 | $2.0 + 0.5 = 2.5$ | $400.0$ | $3.40\times$ |
| 8-stage | 8 | $1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5$ | $666.7$ | $5.67\times$ |
| 16-stage | 16 | $0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$ | $1000.0$ | $8.50\times$ |
| 32-stage | 32 | $0.25 + 0.5 = 0.75$ | $1333.3$ | $11.33\times$ |
| 64-stage | 64 | $0.125 + 0.5 = 0.625$ | $1600.0$ | $13.60\times$ |

**Conversion Logic & Sample Calculation (8-stage case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \frac{T_{comb}}{k} + T_{reg} = \frac{8}{8} + 0.5 = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ ns} \\
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 666.67 \text{ MHz} \\
S_{vs\,1} &= \frac{T_{clk,\,1}}{T_{clk,\,8}} = \frac{8.5}{1.5} = 5.67
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** As $k$ doubles from 32 → 64, the speedup increases only by a factor of $1.20\times$, NOT $2.0\times$. This is the **diminishing returns** behavior because $T_{reg}$ now exceeds the per-stage logic delay. The KTU 2024 examiner loves testing this *saturation effect*.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Penalty of a Branch Mispredict in a Deep Pipeline

**Problem:** Assume branch outcomes are resolved in the EX stage of a 5-stage pipeline, but in the **7th stage** of a 14-stage deeply pipelined processor. Branch frequency is 20% of all instructions, and 50% of branches are mispredicted.

**Step 1 — Compute cycles lost per mispredict:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{5-stage:} \quad & \text{Penalty} = 2 \text{ cycles} \quad (\text{IF, ID squashed}) \\
\text{14-stage:} \quad & \text{Penalty} = 6 \text{ cycles} \quad (\text{6 instructions flushed})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average CPI penalty from branches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch mispredicts per instr.} &= 0.20 \times 0.50 = 0.10 \\
\text{CPI penalty (5-stage)} &= 0.10 \times 2 = 0.20 \\
\text{CPI penalty (14-stage)} &= 0.10 \times 6 = 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective CPI and total slow-down:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{5} &= 1.0 + 0.20 = 1.20 \\
CPI_{14} &= 1.0 + 0.60 = 1.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective performance ratio (assuming same $T_{clk}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slow-down factor} &= \frac{CPI_{14}}{CPI_{5}} = \frac{1.60}{1.20} = 1.33\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even though the 14-stage pipeline runs at a *higher* $T_{clk}$ frequency, the increased branch penalty can substantially erode its performance gain. This is the central reason modern processors combine deep pipelining with **aggressive branch prediction** and **speculative execution**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deeply Pipelined Processor

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the structural flow of instructions through a deeply pipelined datapath (12 stages). It illustrates how a single instruction traverses all stages while multiple instructions are processed in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STAGE 1 IF1 Inst Fetch L1] --> B[STAGE 2 IF2 Pre Decode Buffer]
    B --> C[STAGE 3 ID Instruction Decode]
    C --> D[STAGE 4 RAT Rename Alloc Table]
    D --> E[STAGE 5 ROB Reorder Buffer Write]
    E --> F[STAGE 6 RF Register File Read]
    F --> G[STAGE 7 SCH Schedule to ALU]
    G --> H[STAGE 8 EX1 ALU Op 1]
    H --> I[STAGE 9 EX2 ALU Op 2]
    I --> J[STAGE 10 EX3 Flag and Branch Resolve]
    J --> K[STAGE 11 MEM Data Cache Access]
    K --> L[STAGE 12 WB Retire and Write Back]

    A -.instr 1.-> A
    B -.instr 2.-> B
    C -.instr 3.-> C
    D -.instr 4.-> D
    E -.instr 5.-> E
```

### 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram — Instruction Flow Over Time

This diagram shows how 5 instructions are processed across the 12-stage pipeline over successive clock cycles. Each row is an instruction; each column is a cycle; each filled cell indicates which stage the instruction occupies.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Deeply Pipelined Processor Timing Diagram 12 Stages
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat C%S

    section Instr I1
    I1S1       :a1, 0, 1
    I1S2       :a2, 1, 1
    I1S3       :a3, 2, 1
    I1S4       :a4, 3, 1
    I1S5       :a5, 4, 1
    I1S6       :a6, 5, 1
    I1S7       :a7, 6, 1
    I1S8       :a8, 7, 1
    I1S9       :a9, 8, 1
    I1S10      :a10, 9, 1
    I1S11      :a11, 10, 1
    I1S12      :a12, 11, 1

    section Instr I2
    I2S1       :b1, 1, 1
    I2S2       :b2, 2, 1
    I2S3       :b3, 3, 1
    I2S4       :b4, 4, 1
    I2S5       :b5, 5, 1
    I2S6       :b6, 6, 1
    I2S7       :b7, 7, 1
    I2S8       :b8, 8, 1
    I2S9       :b9, 9, 1
    I2S10      :b10, 10, 1
    I2S11      :b11, 11, 1
    I2S12      :b12, 12, 1

    section Instr I3
    I3S1       :c1, 2, 1
    I3S2       :c2, 3, 1
    I3S3       :c3, 4, 1
    I3S4       :c4, 5, 1
    I3S5       :c5, 6, 1
    I3S6       :c6, 7, 1
    I3S7       :c7, 8, 1
    I3S8       :c8, 9, 1
    I3S9       :c9, 10, 1
    I3S10      :c10, 11, 1
    I3S11      :c11, 12, 1
    I3S12      :c12, 13, 1
```

### 4.3 Hazard vs Pipeline Depth Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW[SHALLOW PIPELINE 5 STAGES]
        L1[Low Frequency] --> L2[Low Hazard Penalty]
        L2 --> L3[Low IPC in steady state]
    end

    subgraph MED[MEDIUM PIPELINE 10 STAGES]
        M1[Medium Frequency] --> M2[Moderate Hazard Penalty]
        M2 --> M3[Balanced IPC]
    end

    subgraph HIGH[DEEP PIPELINE 20 STAGES]
        H1[High Frequency] --> H2[High Hazard Penalty]
        H2 --> H3[Requires Aggressive Prediction]
        H3 --> H4[High IPC if prediction accurate]
    end

    LOW ==> MED
    MED ==> HIGH
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a deeply pipelined processor. List any two key advantages of increasing pipeline depth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **deeply pipelined processor** is a CPU design in which the instruction execution path is partitioned into a *large number* of fine-grained stages (typically 15 or more) by inserting pipeline registers between sub-operations, with the goal of reducing the critical path per stage and thereby increasing the operating clock frequency.

**Two Key Advantages:**

1. **Higher Operating Frequency** — Splitting a critical combinational path of $T_{comb}$ into $k$ stages reduces the per-stage delay to roughly $T_{comb}/k$, allowing the clock frequency to scale almost linearly with $k$ (up to the saturation point).
2. **Higher Instruction Throughput** — In the steady state, the pipeline completes one instruction per clock cycle, yielding an *ideal* speedup factor of $k$ over a non-pipelined datapath executing $N \gg k$ instructions.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the limitation of pipelining that becomes more pronounced as the pipeline depth increases? Justify your answer with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The most pronounced limitation of deep pipelining is the **increase in branch misprediction penalty**. The penalty is proportional to the number of stages between the IF stage and the stage where the branch outcome is resolved.

**Justification with Example:**

If the branch outcome is resolved in stage $r$ of a $k$-stage pipeline, then $(r-1)$ instructions already in flight must be **flushed** on a mispredict. For a 5-stage pipeline, the penalty is typically 2 cycles; for a 20-stage deeply pipelined processor, the penalty can be 12–15 cycles. This consumes pipeline bandwidth, raises average CPI, and can erode the frequency gain. Hence, deeper pipelines demand **aggressive branch prediction, larger BTBs, and speculative execution** to mask this penalty.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **concept of deep pipelining** in modern processors. Discuss how increasing the number of stages affects the clock cycle time and the throughput of the processor. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A non-pipelined processor has a critical path of $T_{comb} = 12$ ns. The pipeline register overhead is $T_{reg} = 0.4$ ns. Compute the maximum clock frequency, ideal speedup, and throughput for the following pipeline depths: $k = 4$, $k = 8$, $k = 16$, and $k = 32$. Comment on the diminishing-returns behaviour observed. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Concept of Deep Pipelining (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks):**
In a deeply pipelined processor, the instruction execution datapath is decomposed into a large number of *balanced* sub-stages, with pipeline registers (latches/flip-flops) inserted between them. Each sub-stage performs a small, well-defined portion of the instruction processing — e.g., a portion of the ALU operation, a portion of the address generation, or a portion of the cache access. At every clock edge, all pipeline registers latch their results simultaneously, advancing every instruction by exactly one stage.

**Diagram (2 marks — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):**
The 12-stage block-level functional architecture diagram illustrates the structure: IF1 → IF2 → ID → RAT → ROB → RF → SCH → EX1 → EX2 → EX3 → MEM → WB.

**Effect on Clock Cycle Time (1 mark):**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{T_{comb}}{k} + T_{reg}$$

As $k$ increases, $T_{comb}/k$ decreases, and the cycle time shrinks. This raises the maximum operating frequency $f_{max} = 1/T_{clk}$.

**Effect on Throughput (1 mark):**
After the initial $k$-cycle fill, the pipeline completes one instruction per cycle, giving a steady-state throughput of $1/T_{clk}$ instructions/second. The ideal speedup is $k$.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $T_{comb} = 12$ ns, $T_{reg} = 0.4$ ns.

**Step 1 — Establish the general formula (1 mark):**

$$T_{clk}(k) = \frac{12}{k} + 0.4 \quad \text{(ns)}, \quad f_{max}(k) = \frac{1}{T_{clk}(k)} \quad \text{(GHz)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for each $k$ (4 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| $k$ | $T_{clk}$ (ns) | $f_{max}$ (GHz) | Ideal Speedup $S=k$ | Throughput (GIPS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | $12/4 + 0.4 = 3.40$ | $0.294$ | $4$ | $0.294$ |
| 8 | $12/8 + 0.4 = 1.90$ | $0.526$ | $8$ | $0.526$ |
| 16 | $12/16 + 0.4 = 1.15$ | $0.870$ | $16$ | $0.870$ |
| 32 | $12/32 + 0.4 = 0.775$ | $1.290$ | $32$ | $1.290$ |

**Step 3 — Sample detailed calculation for $k=16$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \frac{12}{16} + 0.4 = 0.75 + 0.4 = 1.15 \text{ ns} \\
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{1.15 \times 10^{-9}} = 869.6 \text{ MHz} \approx 0.870 \text{ GHz} \\
TP &= f_{max} = 0.870 \text{ GIPS (Giga Instructions Per Second)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on diminishing returns (1 mark):**
The speedup from $k=4 \to 8$ is $2.0\times$ (frequency rises from $0.294$ to $0.526$ GHz), but from $k=16 \to 32$ it is only $\approx 1.48\times$ ($0.870 \to 1.290$ GHz). As $k$ grows, the per-stage logic delay $T_{comb}/k$ becomes small compared to $T_{reg}=0.4$ ns, and the latter *dominates* the cycle time. Eventually, doubling $k$ gives nearly zero frequency gain, illustrating the **saturation effect** of deep pipelining.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** What are the **three classes of pipeline hazards**? Explain how each becomes more severe in a deeply pipelined processor. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider a 5-stage baseline pipeline vs. a 14-stage deeply pipelined processor. The branch outcome is resolved in **stage 2** of the 5-stage pipeline and **stage 10** of the 14-stage pipeline. Given that 25% of instructions are branches and 40% of branches are mispredicted, compute the average CPI and effective speedup (assume both designs have the same logic delay $T_{comb}=8$ ns and $T_{reg}=0.2$ ns). **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Three Classes of Hazards (7 marks)

**1. Structural Hazards (2 marks):**
A structural hazard occurs when two instructions in different stages of the pipeline require the *same hardware resource* in the same cycle (e.g., a single memory port for both IF and MEM). **In deep pipelines,** more instructions are simultaneously in flight, raising the probability of resource contention. Solution: hardware replication (e.g., separate I-cache and D-cache) or careful resource partitioning.

**2. Data Hazards (2 marks):**
Data hazards arise when an instruction depends on the result of a prior instruction still in the pipeline. There are three subtypes: **RAW (Read After Write — true dependency)**, **WAR (Write After Read — anti-dependency)**, and **WAW (Write After Write — output dependency)**. **In deep pipelines,** the producer-to-consumer distance (in cycles) is larger, so the **stall penalty** for RAW hazards grows. Solution: forwarding/bypassing, out-of-order execution, register renaming.

**3. Control Hazards (3 marks):**
Control hazards arise from branch and jump instructions whose outcome is unknown until the branch is resolved. **In deep pipelines,** the *branch resolution stage* is much further down the pipe, so the misprediction penalty equals the number of in-flight instructions that must be flushed. The deep pipeline also encounters more branch instructions before resolution. Solution: **branch prediction (BHT, BTB, 2-bit saturating counters)**, **delayed branching**, **speculative execution**, and **early branch resolution** (e.g., in the IF stage using a decoded I-cache and a fast branch comparator).

---

##### Part (b) — CPI and Speedup Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $T_{comb} = 8$ ns, $T_{reg} = 0.2$ ns.

**Step 1 — Compute clock cycle times (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,5} &= \frac{8}{5} + 0.2 = 1.6 + 0.2 = 1.8 \text{ ns} \\
T_{clk,14} &= \frac{8}{14} + 0.2 = 0.5714 + 0.2 = 0.7714 \text{ ns} \\
f_{max,5} &= 555.6 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{max,14} = 1296.3 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mispredict penalties (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_5 &= (2-1) = 1 \text{ cycle (instr. in IF is squashed)} \\
P_{14} &= (10-1) = 9 \text{ cycles (9 in-flight instr. squashed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average CPI from branches (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch frequency} &= 0.25 \\
\text{Mispredict rate} &= 0.40 \\
\text{Mispredicts per instr.} &= 0.25 \times 0.40 = 0.10 \\
CPI_{5} &= 1.0 + 0.10 \times 1 = 1.10 \\
CPI_{14} &= 1.0 + 0.10 \times 9 = 1.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute execution time per instruction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{inst,5} &= CPI_5 \times T_{clk,5} = 1.10 \times 1.8 = 1.98 \text{ ns} \\
T_{inst,14} &= CPI_{14} \times T_{clk,14} = 1.90 \times 0.7714 = 1.466 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective speedup (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{eff} &= \frac{T_{inst,5}}{T_{inst,14}} = \frac{1.98}{1.466} \approx 1.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The deeply pipelined 14-stage processor is only **1.35×** faster than the 5-stage baseline, far below the naive ideal of $14/5 = 2.8\times$. The branch misprediction penalty has *eaten up* most of the frequency gain. This is a **textbook example** of why branch prediction accuracy is the single most important metric for deep-pipeline performance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting $T_{reg}$ in cycle time calculations** — Many students write $T_{clk} = T_{comb}/k$ alone. This loses 1 mark per such error. The correct formula is $T_{clk} = T_{comb}/k + T_{reg}$.
> 2. **Confusing latency and throughput** — Latency per instruction *increases* (or stays the same) in a pipeline; only throughput improves. Stating "pipeline reduces latency" is a direct contradiction and will be marked zero.
> 3. **Ignoring the steady-state assumption** — The speedup formula $S=k$ only holds for $N \gg k$. For small programs, fill and drain overhead dominate and speedup can be sub-linear.
> 4. **Missing the hazard penalty in numerical problems** — Whenever branch frequency and mispredict rate are given, you **must** add a branch-CPI term. Skipping this yields a grossly inflated speedup.
> 5. **Mixing up pipeline depth and pipeline width** — Depth = number of stages (temporal parallelism). Width = number of parallel pipelines (spatial parallelism, e.g., dual-issue). KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deeply Pipelined Processors**

- ✅ **Pipelining** = overlapping execution of multiple instructions by sub-dividing work into stages separated by pipeline registers.
- ✅ **Deeply Pipelined Processor** = a design with a *large* number of stages (≥15), used to push clock frequency higher.
- ✅ **Ideal Speedup** $= k$ (number of stages), valid only in the limit $N \to \infty$ and with zero hazards and zero register overhead.
- ✅ **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_{clk} = T_{comb}/k + T_{reg}$.
- ✅ **Diminishing Returns:** As $k$ grows, $T_{reg}$ dominates, and frequency gain saturates.
- ✅ **Three Hazard Classes:** Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control (branches).
- ✅ **Branch Mispredict Penalty** scales with the *distance* (in stages) from IF to branch resolution — deep pipelines suffer a larger penalty.
- ✅ **CPI Equation:** $CPI = 1 + \text{stall cycles per instruction}$.
- ✅ **Total Execution Time:** $T_{exec} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$ for an ideal pipeline with no stalls.
- ✅ **Drain Time on Flush:** $T_{drain} = k \cdot T_{clk}$.
- ✅ **Modern Examples:** Intel Pentium 4 (20 stages, retired), AMD Zen 4 (19 stages), Apple M2 (≈14 stages), NVIDIA Ada (≥20 stages).
- ✅ **Key Realisation:** Frequency is *necessary but not sufficient* — IPC, hazard penalty, and branch prediction accuracy jointly determine the actual performance.
- ✅ **Design Levers:** BTB, RAS, speculative execution, register renaming, and out-of-order issue are the standard techniques used to *unlock* the performance potential of a deep pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Scalar Organization: Limitations of Scalar Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Super Scalar Organization: Limitations of Scalar Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Scalar Pipeline

A **Scalar Pipeline** is a pipelined processor implementation in which exactly **one instruction** is issued, executed, and completed per processor clock cycle in the steady state. The classic reference model is the **5-stage RISC pipeline** consisting of:

1. **IF** — Instruction Fetch
2. **ID** — Instruction Decode / Register Fetch
3. **EX** — Execute / ALU operation / Address generation
4. **MEM** — Memory access (Load/Store)
5. **WB** — Write Back to register file

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):** *A scalar pipeline issues and completes a maximum of one instruction per cycle, so its lower bound on CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) is 1.0. The peak instruction throughput is therefore fixed at 1 instruction/cycle regardless of available hardware parallelism.*

Mathematically, for a scalar pipeline:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{scalar}} \geq 1.0
$$

$$
\text{IPC}_{\text{scalar}} \leq 1.0
$$

where **CPI** = Cycles Per Instruction and **IPC** = Instructions Per Cycle.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Super Scalar Organization (Bridge Concept)

A **Super Scalar Processor** is an advanced CPU organization that overcomes the scalar pipeline's single-issue ceiling by dispatching **two or more independent instructions** to **multiple parallel functional units** in a single clock cycle. It is the natural evolution that motivates the entire topic "Limitations of Scalar Pipelines."

$$
\text{IPC}_{\text{superscalar}} \geq 2 \quad \text{(for a degree-}n \text{ issue machine: IPC} \leq n\text{)}
$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Toll Booth Highway

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a scalar pipeline as a **single toll-booth lane on a highway**.
> - Cars (instructions) approach one at a time.
> - Even if 8 booths *could* be built side by side, the highway architect *chose* to run a single lane.
> - During rush hour, only one car is processed per "green light" (clock cycle), no matter how many cars are waiting.
> - The throughput ceiling is **1 car/cycle** — this is the inherent limitation.

A **super scalar processor**, by contrast, is the **8-lane toll plaza** — it can service 8 cars in the same "green light" instant. The transition from scalar → super scalar is precisely what this module explores.

## 1.4 Why Study "Limitations" Before "Super Scalar Design"?

KTU pedagogically places this topic *before* the detailed super scalar design to ensure the student understands **WHY** the architecture had to evolve. Without recognizing the bottlenecks, the design choices (multiple issue, out-of-order execution, branch prediction, register renaming) appear arbitrary.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A common KTU question framing is: *"Justify the need for super scalar organization over scalar pipelines."* Memorize the **six core limitations** listed in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Fundamental Limitations of Scalar Pipelines

### Limitation 1 — Hard CPI Floor of 1.0 (The Single-Issue Bottleneck)

A scalar pipeline can never exceed one completion per cycle. The processor can sit idle on a cycle even when **multiple independent instructions** are ready. With the increasing transistor budgets of modern VLSI (Moore's law), leaving execution units idle is a gross waste of silicon.

### Limitation 2 — Inability to Exploit Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)

Most programs contain abundant **ILP** (independent instructions that could execute in parallel). A scalar pipeline serializes all of them through one issue slot, despite the existence of multiple functional units (ALU, multiplier, FPU, branch unit, load/store unit) in modern chips.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent ILP definition:** Two instructions `I1` and `I2` are parallel if neither produces a register/memory value the other consumes (no RAW data dependency), and they target different physical resources.

### Limitation 3 — Severe Branch Penalty (Control Hazard Magnification)

Branch instructions are typically resolved in the **EX stage** of a 5-stage scalar pipeline. A mispredicted branch flushes **2–3 instructions** from the pipeline. With branch frequency ~15–25% in integer code, branch overhead alone caps effective IPC well below 1.0:

$$
\text{Effective CPI}_{\text{scalar}} = 1.0 + (\text{Branch Frequency}) \times (\text{Branch Penalty})
$$

For example, with 20% branches and a 2-cycle penalty: $\text{CPI} = 1.0 + (0.20)(2) = 1.40$.

### Limitation 4 — Data Hazards Stall the Pipeline

Three data hazards exist — **RAW (Read After Write)**, **WAR (Write After Read)**, and **WAW (Write After Write)**. While a scalar pipeline often resolves RAW via forwarding, the inherent *single-issue* nature means dependent instructions cannot be re-ordered around the hazard. A single cache-miss load can stall the entire pipeline for **100+ cycles** with no other instruction able to issue.

### Limitation 5 — Underutilization of Multi-Cycle Functional Units

A modern CPU die contains an **integer ALU**, a **floating-point unit**, a **branch unit**, a **load/store unit**, a **shifter**, and a **multiplier**. A scalar pipeline uses only **one** of these per cycle. The remaining units consume power, dissipate heat, and yet produce zero useful work per cycle.

### Limitation 6 — Memory Wall — Latency Hiding Impossible

DRAM access latency (~50–100 ns) translates to **100–200 processor cycles**. In a scalar pipeline, while one load is waiting on memory, the pipeline is **completely blocked** (or, at best, has a single-cycle bubble for a forwarded ALU op). The scalar design provides **no architectural mechanism** to hide this latency by overlapping with other independent work.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Fundamental execution time equation | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CPI}}$ (instructions/sec) | Scalar peak throughput is $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}}$ | instr/s |
| 3 | $\text{Effective CPI} = \text{Ideal CPI} + \sum (\text{Hazard stall cycles})$ | Stalls degrade scalar pipeline | cycles/instr |
| 4 | $\text{Amdahl's Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$ | Fraction $F$ enhanced by factor $S$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{n \times T_{\text{stage}}}{n \times T_{\text{stage}} + (k-1)T_{\text{stage}}}$ | For $k$ stages, $n$ instructions | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million instructions per second | MIPS |
| 7 | $\text{IPC}_{\text{max, scalar}} = 1$ | Hard ceiling of scalar design | instr/cycle |
| 8 | $\text{IPC}_{\text{max, superscalar}} = n$ | For $n$-wide issue width | instr/cycle |
| 9 | $\text{Branch Penalty} = \text{Branch Frequency} \times \text{Misprediction Rate} \times \text{Flush Cycles}$ | Control hazard cost | cycles/instr |
| 10 | $\text{Hennessy–Patterson CPI equation: } \text{CPI} = \sum_{i} \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i / \text{IC}$ | Weighted per-instruction-class CPI | cycles/instr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** Vertical bars have been written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table-parser corruption.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These limitations are not academic — they directly motivated the **multi-billion-dollar CPU industry pivot** in the mid-1990s:

- **Intel P6 (Pentium Pro, 1995)** — first commercial out-of-order super scalar core
- **AMD K5/K7**, **MIPS R10000**, **Alpha 21264** — all super scalar
- **Modern Apple M-series, Intel Core i9, AMD Ryzen** — wide super scalar (4–6 issue) with speculation

Understanding the limitations of scalar pipelines is essential for chip architects deciding **issue width**, **reorder buffer size**, **branch predictor accuracy**, and **cache hierarchy depth**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why CPI = 1 is a Hard Floor for Scalar Pipelines

### Setup
Consider an $n$-stage scalar pipeline executing an instruction sequence. The pipeline has **one issue slot** per cycle and **one write-back port** to the register file per cycle.

### Step-by-step Proof

**Step 1 — Definition of Steady State**

In the *steady state* (after the pipeline is full and before it drains), exactly one new instruction enters the IF stage every cycle, and exactly one instruction leaves the WB stage every cycle.

**Step 2 — Conservation of Flow**

Because the issue logic allows at most one instruction to transition ID → EX per cycle:

$$
\text{Instructions Issued per Cycle} \leq 1
$$

**Step 3 — Relation between Issued and Completed**

In a scalar pipeline, every issued instruction eventually completes (or is squashed on exception). Therefore:

$$
\text{Completed Instructions per Cycle} \leq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Definition of IPC and CPI**

$$
\text{IPC} = \frac{\text{Completed Instructions}}{\text{Total Cycles}}
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{1}{\text{IPC}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combining the Inequalities**

$$
\text{IPC}_{\text{scalar}} \leq 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{CPI}_{\text{scalar}} \geq 1
$$

This proves the **hard CPI floor of 1.0** for any scalar pipeline organization, regardless of how many functional units the chip contains. The structural design of the issue logic, not the functional units, is the binding constraint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Effective CPI of a Scalar Pipeline with Stalls

### Setup
Let the ideal (no-hazard) CPI of the scalar pipeline be $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1.0$. Three classes of hazards contribute additional cycles:

| Hazard Class | Frequency $f_i$ | Penalty $p_i$ (cycles) |
|--------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Structural | $f_s$ | $p_s$ |
| Data (RAW load-use) | $f_d$ | $p_d$ |
| Control (mispredict) | $f_c$ | $p_c$ |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Base CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 — Total Stall Cycles per Instruction**

The expected stall cycles per instruction is the sum of each hazard's frequency times its penalty:

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = f_s \cdot p_s + f_d \cdot p_d + f_c \cdot p_c
$$

**Step 3 — Combined Effective CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} = 1.0 + f_s p_s + f_d p_d + f_c p_c
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example**

Given typical RISC scalar pipeline values:
- Branch frequency $f_c = 0.20$, mispredict penalty $p_c = 2$ cycles
- Load-use hazard frequency $f_d = 0.15$, penalty $p_d = 1$ cycle
- Structural hazard negligible ($f_s p_s \approx 0$)

Substitute:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.0 + (0)(0) + (0.15)(1) + (0.20)(2)
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.0 + 0.15 + 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.55}
$$

This means a scalar pipeline that *should* deliver 1 instr/cycle actually delivers only:

$$
\text{IPC}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{1.55} \approx 0.645 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

A **35% performance loss** simply due to stalls — the very gap that super scalar designs attempt to recover by issuing *multiple* instructions around stalls.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Amdahl's Law Applied to a 2-Wide Super Scalar

### Setup
A scalar pipeline is being upgraded to a **2-wide super scalar** that *ideally* halves the CPI for the parallelizable fraction $F$ of instructions.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Enhancements**

- Enhanced (parallelizable) fraction: $F = 0.70$ (70% of instructions can be paired)
- Speedup of the enhanced fraction: $S = 2$ (CPI drops from 2.0 to 1.0 for the pair, but per-instruction the pair runs in 1 cycle, so per-instruction speedup is 2×)

**Step 2 — Amdahl's Law**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1-F) + \frac{F}{S}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \frac{0.70}{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.35}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{0.65}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Speedup} \approx 1.538\times}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

Even though the *enhanced portion* runs 2× faster, the *un-enhanced 30%* caps the overall speedup at **1.54×**. This is why super scalar design is not free — it requires that nearly the entire program be parallelizable in the issue slot.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Scalar vs Super Scalar

| Parameter | Scalar Pipeline (5-stage) | Super Scalar (2-wide baseline) |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Issue width | 1 instr/cycle | 2 instr/cycle (peak) |
| Peak IPC | $\leq 1.0$ | $\leq 2.0$ |
| Peak CPI | $\geq 1.0$ | $\geq 0.5$ |
| Functional units in use/cycle | 1 | up to 2 |
| Branch mispredict cost | 2–3 cycles lost | 2–3 cycles × issue width lost |
| ILP exploitation | Compiler-only, static | Hardware dynamic + compiler |
| Hazard resolution | Stalls + forwarding | Stalls + forwarding + **register renaming + out-of-order** |
| Typical transistor count | $\sim 1$–$5$ M (early RISC) | $\sim 50$ M – billions |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Power efficiency at idle | Same | Worse (more units leaking) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Limitations Flow for Scalar Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scalar Pipeline 5-stage] --> B{Single Issue Slot per Cycle}
    B --> C[Limitation 1: CPI Floor = 1.0]
    B --> D[Limitation 2: ILP Not Exploited]
    B --> E[Limitation 3: Branch Penalty Large]
    B --> F[Limitation 4: Data Hazards Stall]
    B --> G[Limitation 5: Functional Units Idle]
    B --> H[Limitation 6: Memory Wall Latency Unhidden]
    C --> I[Performance Gap]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Justifies Super Scalar Organization]
    J --> K[Multiple Issue Slots]
    J --> L[Dynamic Scheduling]
    J --> M[Branch Prediction]
    J --> N[Register Renaming]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Scalar vs Super Scalar Issue Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Scalar Pipeline]
        SIF[IF] --> SID[ID] --> SEX[EX] --> SMEM[MEM] --> SWB[WB]
    end

    subgraph SS[Super Scalar Pipeline 2-wide]
        SIF2[IF 2 instructions] --> SID2[ID pair]
        SID2 --> SEX2[EX unit 1]
        SID2 --> SEX3[EX unit 2]
        SEX2 --> SMEM2[MEM unit 1]
        SEX3 --> SMEM3[MEM unit 2]
        SMEM2 --> SWB2[WB]
        SMEM3 --> SWB3[WB]
    end

    SP -.Single Issue per Cycle.-> SS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stall Sources Feeding into Scalar CPI

```mermaid

    P[Program Instruction Stream] --> Q{Pipeline Stage}
    Q --> R[Structural Hazard]
    Q --> S[Data Hazard RAW WAR WAW]
    Q --> T[Control Hazard Branch Mispredict]
    R --> U[Stall Cycles Injected]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Effective CPI = 1 plus Stalls]
    V --> W[Performance Loss vs Ideal]
    W --> X[Motivation for Multi-Issue and OoO Execution]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Evolution Path From Scalar to Super Scalar

```mermaid

    A[Scalar 1-wide 1990] --> B[Super Scalar 2-wide 1995]
    B --> C[Super Scalar 4-wide 2000]
```
