```mermaid
    D --> E[Wide Super Scalar with Speculation 2024 plus]
    A2[Fix: Multiple Issue] --> B
    B2[Fix: Dynamic Scheduling] --> C
    C2[Fix: Deep Pipelines] --> D
    D2[Fix: Better Branch Predictors] --> E
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `SIF`, `SIF2`, `P`, `Q`, `A2`). No reserved Mermaid keywords were used as node names. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text, with no markdown formatting tags inside the quoted label strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define a scalar pipeline. State its peak instruction throughput.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **scalar pipeline** is a pipelined processor in which **only one instruction is issued and completed per clock cycle** in the steady state. It uses a single issue slot regardless of how many functional units exist on the die.

The classic example is the 5-stage RISC pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB).

**Peak throughput = 1 instruction per cycle**, i.e., $\text{IPC}_{\text{max}} = 1.0$ and $\text{CPI}_{\text{min}} = 1.0$. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three limitations of a scalar pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Hard CPI floor of 1.0** — cannot issue more than one instruction per cycle. (1 mark)
2. **Branch penalty** — control hazards flush 2–3 instructions, lowering effective IPC. (1 mark)
3. **Inability to hide memory latency** — single load miss stalls the entire pipeline since no other instruction can be issued. (1 mark)

*(Any three from the six limitations in Section 2.1 are acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B Module-2 question carries 14 marks with internal choice (either OR). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Model solutions below match this split.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **six fundamental limitations of a scalar pipeline** with reference to ILP exploitation, branch penalty, and resource underutilization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A scalar pipeline runs at 2.5 GHz with an ideal CPI of 1.0. The branch frequency is 25% with a misprediction penalty of 2 cycles. Load-use hazards occur 15% of instructions with a 1-cycle penalty. Compute the **effective CPI**, **MIPS rating**, and **execution time for a program of 100 million instructions**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to B-A (a)

The **six fundamental limitations** of a scalar pipeline are:

1. **CPI Floor of 1.0** — The issue logic permits only one instruction per cycle. No matter how fast the clock or how rich the functional units, the IPC ceiling is 1. (1.5 marks)
2. **Inability to Exploit ILP** — Programs contain many independent instructions; the scalar design cannot dispatch them in parallel, leaving ALU, FPU, and load/store units idle. (1.5 marks)
3. **Branch Penalty** — Branches resolved late flush fetched instructions. With 20% branch frequency and 2-cycle mispredict penalty, CPI rises to ~1.4. (1 mark)
4. **Data Hazard Stalls** — RAW, WAR, WAW dependencies cannot be re-ordered around; one long-latency load freezes the issue slot. (1 mark)
5. **Functional Unit Underutilization** — Modern dies contain ALU, FPU, branch, and memory units; a scalar pipeline uses only one per cycle. (1 mark)
6. **Memory Wall** — DRAM access (50–100 ns = 100–200 cycles) cannot be overlapped with independent work in a scalar issue model. (1 mark)

### Model Solution to B-A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the effective CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.0 + (0.25)(2) + (0.15)(1) = 1.0 + 0.50 + 0.15 = 1.65
$$

**Step 2 — MIPS rating**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{1.65 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
= \frac{2500}{1.65} \approx 1515.15 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Execution time**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}} = (100 \times 10^{6}) \times 1.65 \times \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.65 \times 10^{8}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.066 \text{ seconds} = 66 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1.65$, MIPS $\approx 1515$, $T_{\text{exec}} = 66$ ms. (7 marks)

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, compare the **scalar pipeline** and the **super scalar pipeline** organizations. Highlight how super scalar overcomes the IPC = 1 limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program has 40% of its instructions that can be parallelized in a 2-wide super scalar issue. Compute the **overall speedup** over a scalar pipeline using **Amdahl's Law**. If the same parallelizable fraction is executed on a **4-wide** issue machine, what is the new speedup? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to B-B (a)

**Block Diagram Comparison** (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram): The scalar pipeline has a **single issue slot** between IF and ID, dispatching one instruction per cycle to a single EX/MEM/WB chain. The super scalar has a **dual issue slot** dispatching two instructions per cycle to **two parallel EX units** and **two memory units** in parallel. (3 marks)

**Overcoming IPC = 1 Limitation:** By providing $n$ issue slots, the super scalar pipeline raises the IPC ceiling to $n$. The issue logic, register file, and bypass network are widened (or replicated) to sustain multiple instructions per cycle. (2 marks)

**Trade-offs:** Increased hardware (more functional units, wider register file ports, more complex hazard detection), higher power dissipation, and diminishing returns due to Amdahl's law. (2 marks)

### Model Solution to B-B (b)

**Step 1 — 2-Wide Super Scalar**

Given $F = 0.40$, $S = 2$:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{2} = \frac{1}{(1-0.40) + \frac{0.40}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.20} = \frac{1}{0.80} = 1.25\times
$$

**Step 2 — 4-Wide Super Scalar**

Given $F = 0.40$, $S = 4$:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{4} = \frac{1}{(1-0.40) + \frac{0.40}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.428\times
$$

**Step 3 — Comment**

Doubling the issue width from 2 to 4 raised speedup only from **1.25×** to **1.43×**, not the theoretical 2×. This is **Amdahl's bottleneck** — the un-enhanced 60% serial portion dominates total execution time. To gain more, one must increase the *parallelizable fraction* $F$ (e.g., via better compiler scheduling or hardware speculation), not merely the issue width. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing CPI with execution time** — they are not the same. CPI is per-instruction; time needs IC × CPI × $T_{\text{clk}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 in CPI** — the ideal CPI of any pipeline is *not* 0; it is **1.0**. Stalls are *added* to 1.0.
> 3. **Misapplying Amdahl's Law** — the un-enhanced fraction is $(1-F)$, **not** $F$.
> 4. **Ignoring units** in MIPS calculation — the $10^6$ divisor is mandatory.
> 5. **Drawing scalar and super scalar as physically identical** — the *issue width* is the structural difference; the board examiner explicitly checks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

- ✅ **Scalar pipeline definition:** 1 instruction issued/completed per cycle in steady state.
- ✅ **Five stages of classic scalar RISC pipeline:** IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB.
- ✅ **Peak scalar IPC = 1**, **CPI floor = 1.0**.
- ✅ **Six limitations:** CPI floor, ILP not exploited, branch penalty, data hazards, functional unit underutilization, memory wall.
- ✅ **Effective CPI formula:** $\text{CPI} = 1.0 + \sum f_i p_i$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$.
- ✅ **CPU time:** $\text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$.
- ✅ **MIPS:** $\dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$.
- ✅ **Super scalar key idea:** Multiple issue slots → multiple parallel functional units → IPC > 1.
- ✅ **Branch penalty contribution:** Branch Frequency × Misprediction Penalty (additive to CPI).
- ✅ **Why 4-wide is not 2× faster than 2-wide:** Amdahl's law caps the gain via the serial fraction.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite exam line:** "Super scalar organization is necessitated by the inability of scalar pipelines to exploit ILP and the presence of a hard CPI floor of 1."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Parallel, Diversified, and Dynamic Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Parallel, Diversified, and Dynamic Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pipeline in Processor Architecture?

A **pipeline** in a digital computer is a technique of overlapping the execution of multiple instructions by partitioning the computation into independent sequential stages. While one instruction is being processed in stage $N$, another can simultaneously be processed in stage $N-1$. This is analogous to an industrial assembly line where the total throughput is dramatically increased without reducing the per-item processing time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A pipeline is a set of data-processing elements arranged in a sequence where the output of one element is the input of the next. The processing of a single instruction is partitioned into multiple sub-operations (stages), and several instructions are concurrently in different stages.*

## 1.2 Basic Concepts of Parallel Pipelines

### Formal Definition
A **Parallel Pipeline** (also called *Array Pipeline* or *Multiple Pipeline*) architecture consists of two or more independent pipelines operating concurrently. The instruction stream is split, and multiple pipelines execute different instructions (or threads) simultaneously, effectively multiplying the throughput compared to a single-pipeline scalar design.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a highway toll booth. A **single pipeline** is one toll booth with 5 stages (card swipe, ticket lift, payment, change return, barrier open) — one car at a time moves through. A **parallel pipeline** is like having **four toll booths side-by-side** — four cars can be processed simultaneously, quadrupling throughput during peak traffic. The bottleneck is now distributed across lanes rather than queued at one booth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Parallel pipelines are characterized by a **degree of parallelism** $k$ (number of parallel lanes). The theoretical maximum speedup is $k$ times, bounded by **Amdahl's Law**.

### Typical Block Structure
- **k identical pipelines**, each with $n$ stages.
- A **dispatch unit** routes ready instructions to free pipelines.
- A **completion unit** reorders results for in-order retirement.

## 1.3 Basic Concepts of Diversified Pipelines

### Formal Definition
A **Diversified Pipeline** uses pipelines with **different stage configurations** or **different functional specializations** to handle distinct classes of operations (e.g., integer arithmetic, floating-point arithmetic, memory load/store, branch, vector). Unlike parallel pipelines where all units are identical, diversified pipelines are heterogeneous — each lane is purpose-built.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a hospital emergency ward. You do **not** send a heart-attack patient and a broken-bone patient to the same general-purpose doctor. Instead, there is a **cardiologist lane**, an **orthopedic lane**, a **pediatric lane**, and a **triage lane**. Each lane (pipeline) is *specialized* — this is **diversification**. Parallel pipelines would be like having four identical general wards; diversified pipelines are like having four specialist wards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Parallel = identical lanes × multiple. Diversified = heterogeneous lanes × multiple.

### Common Diversified Units in Modern CPUs
1. **Integer ALU Pipeline** (fixed-point arithmetic)
2. **Floating-Point Unit (FPU) Pipeline**
3. **Load/Store Pipeline** (memory access)
4. **Branch Pipeline** (control flow)
5. **SIMD / Vector Pipeline**

## 1.4 Basic Concepts of Dynamic Pipelines

### Formal Definition
A **Dynamic Pipeline** is a pipeline whose **structure, configuration, or stage count can be reconfigured at runtime** based on the data being processed, the program phase, or the operating mode. It may merge stages to act as a shorter pipeline (low-latency mode) or split stages to act as a deeper pipeline (high-throughput mode).

### Conceptual Analogy
Consider a modern **automatic washing machine**. When you select "Quick Wash", it merges the wash and rinse stages into one accelerated cycle (short pipeline, low-latency). When you select "Heavy Wash", it splits each stage into pre-soak, deep-wash, and extra-rinse sub-stages (deeper pipeline, high-throughput for delicate quality). The machine *dynamically reconfigures* itself — this is precisely what a dynamic pipeline does in silicon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Dynamic pipelines are characterized by their **configurability factor** — the number of distinct operating modes (typically 2 to 8) and the **reconfiguration latency** (often 1–10 clock cycles).

### Sub-Types of Dynamic Pipelines
| Sub-Type | Behavior | Example Application |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Reconfigurable Stage-Count** | Merges/splits stages | Low-power IoT cores |
| **Data-Dependent Depth** | Bypasses unused stages | Adaptive DSP filters |
| **Clock-Gated Pipeline** | Powers down idle stages | Mobile SoCs |

## 1.5 Comparative Summary of the Three Pipeline Concepts

| Feature | Parallel Pipeline | Diversified Pipeline | Dynamic Pipeline |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Lanes** | Multiple, identical | Multiple, different | Single (or few), reconfigurable |
| **Goal** | Higher throughput | Specialization + throughput | Adaptive performance/power |
| **Control Unit** | Often shared | Often shared (dispatch) | Reconfiguration controller |
| **Best For** | Independent workloads | Heterogeneous instructions | Variable workloads |
| **Complexity** | Medium | High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### Why Use Pipelines at All?
A non-pipelined processor completes one instruction in $T$ time units before starting the next. If we partition $T$ into $n$ equal stages of $\tau = T/n$ each, and overlap them, the first instruction still takes $T$, but every subsequent instruction completes every $\tau$ time units. The **ideal** throughput increase is $\times n$.

### How Parallel Pipelines Operate
1. **Instruction Fetch** — multiple instructions are fetched per cycle.
2. **Dispatch** — the dispatch logic examines each instruction and forwards it to an *available* parallel lane.
3. **Execution** — all $k$ lanes operate concurrently.
4. **Completion** — results are written back (often out-of-order).
5. **Retire** — instructions are retired in program order for precise exceptions.

### How Diversified Pipelines Operate
1. **Decode** — instruction opcode determines its *class*.
2. **Issue** — routed to the appropriate *specialized* lane (e.g., FPU lane for floating-point).
3. **Execute** — each lane executes with its own internal pipeline depth tailored to its operation.
4. **Result collection** — from heterogeneous lanes to a common register file or result bus.

### How Dynamic Pipelines Operate
1. **Mode detection** — monitor instruction mix, branch behavior, or data type.
2. **Reconfiguration decision** — control logic decides optimal stage count/depth.
3. **Stage merge/split** — latches, bypass paths, and clock-gating cells are toggled.
4. **Steady-state operation** — pipeline runs in new configuration until next event.

## 2.2 Performance Metrics — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS** — memorize these; they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on pipelines.

### Core Pipeline Performance Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{pipeline} = \tau = \dfrac{T_{non-pipelined}}{n}$ | Stage time (clock period) | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput}_{ideal} = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{n}{T_{non-pipelined}}$ | Instructions completed per second | instr/s |
| 3 | $\text{Speedup}_{ideal} = n$ | Maximum theoretical speedup | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Speedup}_{actual} = \dfrac{n}{1 + (n-1) \cdot h}$ | Speedup with hazards (h = hazard penalty fraction) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Speedup}}{n} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (n-1) \cdot h}$ | Pipeline efficiency | 0–1 |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput}_{parallel} = \dfrac{k \cdot n}{T_{non-pipelined}}$ | Parallel pipeline throughput ($k$ lanes) | instr/s |
| 7 | $\text{Speedup}_{parallel} = k$ | Parallel pipeline speedup (upper bound) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Speedup}_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | Amdahl's Law (parallel fraction $f$, lanes $k$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Latency}_{dynamic} = n_{active} \cdot \tau_{mode}$ | Latency of dynamic pipeline in given mode | seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Power}_{dynamic} \propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f \cdot \alpha_{active}$ | Active stages contribute $\alpha$ to power | Watts |

### Boundary Conditions & Engineering Rules
- **Upper bound on Speedup:** $\text{Speedup} \leq k$ (number of parallel lanes) or $\leq n$ (depth of pipeline).
- **Efficiency bound:** $0 < \eta \leq 1$.
- **Pipeline bubble cost:** Each hazard introduces $b$ stall cycles → effective throughput = $\dfrac{1}{\tau + b/n}$.
- **Pipeline imbalance:** In diversified pipelines, slowest lane determines dispatch rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **speedup** with **efficiency**. Speedup is *absolute* (e.g., 4× faster); efficiency is *normalized* (e.g., 80%). Both must be quoted with their pipeline depth $n$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Parallel pipelines** are the backbone of superscalar CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen).
- **Diversified pipelines** enable heterogeneous computing (ARM big.LITTLE, Apple SoCs with CPU+GPU+NPU).
- **Dynamic pipelines** power adaptive processors in smartphones (Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity) where power and performance trade-off constantly.

## 2.3 Hazard Classification (Recap for Pipeline Operation)

| Hazard Type | Cause | Mitigation |
|-------------|-------|------------|
| **Structural** | Two instructions need same hardware | Add hardware (parallel lanes) |
| **Data (RAW)** | Read-after-write dependency | Forwarding, stalls |
| **Data (WAR/WAW)** | Write-after-read/write-after-write | Register renaming |
| **Control** | Branch outcomes | Prediction, delay slots |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Speedup of a Pipeline with $n$ Stages

**Starting Premise:**
A non-pipelined processor requires $T$ time units to complete one instruction. Pipelining partitions $T$ into $n$ equal stages.

**Step 1 — Express the non-pipelined execution time for $N$ instructions:**
$$T_{non-pipe} = N \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Express the pipelined stage time:**
$$\tau = \frac{T}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Express the pipelined execution time for $N$ instructions:**
The pipeline must first *fill* (taking $n$ cycles for the first instruction), and then it *drains* one instruction per cycle for the remaining $N-1$ instructions.

$$T_{pipe} = (n + (N - 1)) \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\tau = T/n$ into the expression:**
$$T_{pipe} = \left(n + N - 1\right) \cdot \frac{T}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the speedup ratio:**
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{N \cdot T}{\left(n + N - 1\right) \cdot \frac{T}{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by canceling $T$:**
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{N \cdot n}{n + N - 1}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the asymptotic limit** as $N \to \infty$ (long-running program):
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \text{Speedup} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot n}{n + N - 1} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot N}{N + (n-1)}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the limit** by dividing numerator and denominator by $N$:
$$\text{Speedup}_{ideal} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{n}{1 + \frac{n-1}{N}} = \frac{n}{1 + 0} = n$$

**Conclusion:** The ideal speedup of an $n$-stage pipeline is $n$, achieved for sufficiently large $N$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Parallel Pipelines

**Starting Premise:**
A fraction $f$ of a program's execution time *can* be parallelized across $k$ lanes; the remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is strictly sequential (e.g., I/O, dependencies, dispatch overhead).

**Step 1 — Express the original execution time** (on 1 lane) as:
$$T_1 = 1 \text{ (normalized)}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into parallelizable and sequential parts:**
$$T_1 = (1 - f) + f$$

**Step 3 — Express the new execution time on $k$ lanes:**
The sequential part remains $(1-f)$ — it cannot be sped up.
The parallel part runs $k$ times faster: $\dfrac{f}{k}$.

$$T_k = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Form the speedup ratio:**
$$\text{Speedup}_{Amdahl} = \frac{T_1}{T_k} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- When $f = 0$: $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$ (no benefit).
- When $f = 1$: $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{1/k} = k$ (linear in lanes).
- When $k \to \infty$: $\text{Speedup} \to \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ (the *sequential bottleneck*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **diversification** (specialized lanes) and **dynamic pipelines** are so important — they raise the effective $f$ by making sequential overhead smaller.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem: Comparing Parallel vs. Diversified vs. Dynamic

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A processor executes a benchmark where:
- $40\%$ of instructions are integer ALU (8-stage pipeline),
- $30\%$ of instructions are floating-point (12-stage pipeline),
- $20\%$ of instructions are memory operations (5-stage pipeline),
- $10\%$ of instructions are branch (3-stage pipeline).
Each stage takes $\tau = 1$ ns. The non-pipelined equivalent takes $T = 60$ ns per instruction.

**Part (a):** Compute the ideal speedup using a single unified 12-stage pipeline (static, non-dynamic).
**Part (b):** Compute the speedup if we use a **diversified** multi-lane design with 4 specialized pipelines.
**Part (c):** Compute the speedup if we use a **dynamic** pipeline that adapts to each instruction class.

### Solution to Part (a) — Static Unified Pipeline

Using the formula $T_{pipe} = (n + N - 1) \cdot \tau$ for $N = 1000$ instructions and $n = 12$:

$$T_{pipe} = (12 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 1 \text{ ns} = 1011 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{non-pipe} = 1000 \cdot 60 \text{ ns} = 60{,}000 \text{ ns}$$

$$\text{Speedup}_a = \frac{60{,}000}{1011} \approx 59.35$$

$$\text{Efficiency}_a = \frac{59.35}{12} \approx 4.946 \text{ (or } 494.6\%\text{)}$$

*Wait — efficiency cannot exceed 100%.* The error arises because each instruction class has only a *fraction* of the 60 ns. Recomputing per-class:

- Integer ALU instruction: non-pipe = 20 ns, pipelined = $(8 + N_i - 1)\tau$.
- Etc.

**Correct Recalculation (per-class, then weighted):**

For each instruction class, the non-pipelined time scales with stage count. Assume non-pipe per class = stages × 5 ns:

| Class | Fraction | Stages | Non-pipe T | N | $T_{pipe}$ | Speedup |
|-------|----------|--------|------------|----|------------|---------|
| Integer | 0.4 | 8 | 40 ns | 400 | 407 ns | 9.83 |
| FP | 0.3 | 12 | 60 ns | 300 | 311 ns | 9.65 |
| Memory | 0.2 | 5 | 25 ns | 200 | 204 ns | 9.80 |
| Branch | 0.1 | 3 | 15 ns | 100 | 102 ns | 9.80 |

Weighted speedup (parallel execution across all lanes):
$$\text{Speedup}_{a,weighted} \approx 9.78 \text{ (close to ideal 12, limited by fill/drain)}$$

### Solution to Part (b) — Diversified Pipeline

Each instruction class uses its own dedicated pipeline. The system dispatches all 4 classes **in parallel**. So per cycle, 4 instructions complete (one per lane) instead of 1.

$$\text{Throughput}_{diversified} = \frac{4}{1 \text{ ns}} = 4 \text{ instr/ns}$$

$$\text{Speedup}_b = 4 \times \text{Speedup}_a \approx 4 \times 9.78 \approx 39.1$$

### Solution to Part (c) — Dynamic Pipeline

Assume the dynamic pipeline **reconfigures** between the 4 instruction classes perfectly (zero reconfiguration latency, ideal case). It then behaves identically to the diversified pipeline, but in *one physical lane*.

$$\text{Speedup}_c \approx 39.1 \text{ (in the ideal reconfiguration case)}$$

**Realistic case** with reconfiguration penalty $r = 2$ ns per switch (assume $10\%$ switches):
$$\text{Total penalty} = 0.1 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2 \text{ ns} = 200 \text{ ns}$$
$$\text{Speedup}_c \approx \frac{60{,}000}{1011 + 200} \approx 49.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> The dynamic pipeline offers **lower hardware cost** than the diversified pipeline (one lane instead of four) at the cost of reconfiguration overhead.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Parallel | Diversified | Dynamic |
|--------|----------|-------------|---------|
| Hardware cost | Medium ($k \times$) | High ($k \times$ + specialized) | Low (single reconfigurable lane) |
| Throughput | High ($k \times$) | High (heterogeneous match) | Medium (limited by reconfiguration) |
| Latency | Low (per lane) | Low (per lane) | Variable (mode-dependent) |
| Power | Medium | Medium-High | Low (clock-gating friendly) |
| Best workload | Homogeneous | Mixed instruction types | Variable / adaptive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a parallel pipeline. How does it differ from a diversified pipeline?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **parallel pipeline** consists of two or more *identical* pipelines operating concurrently, with instructions dispatched to whichever pipeline is free. The goal is throughput multiplication (up to $k\times$ speedup, where $k$ is the number of lanes).

A **diversified pipeline** consists of multiple pipelines that are *heterogeneous* — each specialized for a different class of operation (integer, floating-point, memory, branch). The goal is instruction-class matching.

**Key difference (1 mark):** Parallel pipelines have identical lanes; diversified pipelines have specialized lanes.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a dynamic pipeline? Mention any two applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **dynamic pipeline** is a pipeline whose stage structure, depth, or configuration can be altered at runtime based on the workload, data type, or operating mode.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Low-power IoT processors** — merge stages in sleep mode to reduce leakage power.
2. **Adaptive DSP processors** — bypass unused stages for short filters; use full depth for complex transforms.
3. (Bonus) Mobile SoCs like ARM big.LITTLE reconfiguring pipeline depth based on performance demand.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **parallel pipeline** processor. Discuss its merits and demerits. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A non-pipelined processor takes **80 ns** to complete an instruction. It is converted into an **8-stage pipeline**. If **500 instructions** are executed:
  (i) Calculate the speedup.
  (ii) Calculate the efficiency.
  (iii) Comment on the result if **20% of instructions** cause a 2-cycle hazard stall. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

A parallel pipeline consists of **k identical pipelines** working concurrently. The block diagram shows:

- A **common fetch unit** that brings in multiple instructions per cycle.
- A **dispatch unit** that allocates each instruction to a free lane.
- **k parallel lanes**, each with **n identical stages** (e.g., IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB).
- A **completion unit** that retires instructions in program order.

**Merits (3 marks):**
1. **Throughput scaling** — up to $k \times$ improvement.
2. **Fault tolerance** — failure of one lane degrades gracefully.
3. **Lower per-lane complexity** — each lane can run at higher clock.

**Demerits (3 marks):**
1. **Hardware cost** — $k$ times more registers, ALUs, latches.
2. **Dispatch logic** — complex instruction routing and dependency resolution.
3. **Power consumption** — all lanes active even when idle (unless clock-gated).
4. **Diminishing returns** — Amdahl's Law limits speedup due to sequential portions.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $T = 80$ ns, $n = 8$ stages, $N = 500$ instructions.

**Stage time:**
$$\tau = \frac{T}{n} = \frac{80}{8} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**(i) Speedup without hazards:**
$$T_{pipe} = (n + N - 1) \cdot \tau = (8 + 500 - 1) \cdot 10 = 5070 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{non-pipe} = N \cdot T = 500 \cdot 80 = 40{,}000 \text{ ns}$$

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{40{,}000}{5070} \approx 7.89$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{\text{Speedup}}{n} = \frac{7.89}{8} \approx 0.986 = 98.6\%$$

**(iii) With 20% hazard stalls:**
Number of stalled instructions: $0.20 \times 500 = 100$.
Each introduces 2 extra cycles: $100 \times 2 = 200$ extra cycles.

$$T_{pipe,hazard} = 5070 + (200 \times 10) = 5070 + 2000 = 7070 \text{ ns}$$

$$\text{Speedup}_{hazard} = \frac{40{,}000}{7070} \approx 5.66$$

$$\eta_{hazard} = \frac{5.66}{8} \approx 70.7\%$$

**Comment:** Hazards reduce speedup by ~28% and efficiency by ~28 percentage points, illustrating the critical importance of hazard mitigation (forwarding, scheduling, branch prediction) in real pipelines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **diversified pipeline** with suitable examples from modern processors. Compare it with a static single-pipeline design. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A dynamic pipeline can operate in three modes:
  - Mode 1: 4 stages, $\tau_1 = 5$ ns (used 30% of the time)
  - Mode 2: 6 stages, $\tau_2 = 3$ ns (used 50% of the time)
  - Mode 3: 8 stages, $\tau_3 = 2$ ns (used 20% of the time)

  If **1000 instructions** are processed, find the **average throughput** and compare with a fixed 6-stage pipeline. Comment on the trade-off. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **diversified pipeline** uses multiple heterogeneous pipelines, each specialized for an instruction class.

**Examples from modern processors (3 marks):**
1. **Intel Core i9** — has integer execution units, FP/vector units (AVX-512), load/store units, and branch units operating as diversified lanes.
2. **Apple M-series SoC** — CPU (integer + FP), GPU (vector), NPU (matrix), and Media Engine (codec) as diversified functional units.
3. **ARM Cortex-A** — separate integer, NEON SIMD, load/store, and branch pipelines.

**Comparison with static single-pipeline (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Single Pipeline | Diversified Pipeline |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Throughput | Limited by common critical path | Each lane optimized for its class |
| Latency | Variable (worst case for all) | Optimized per class |
| Hardware cost | Lower | Higher (heterogeneous units) |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (same stages for all) | Higher (right tool for the job) |
| Complexity | Low | High (dispatch + multiple RFs) |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Compute cycles per instruction (CPI) in each mode using the formula:**
$$\text{CPI} = 1 + \text{fill/drain penalty per mode}$$

For large $N = 1000$, fill/drain is negligible. So CPI ≈ 1 in each mode, but with different clock periods.

**Mode 1 (4 stages):** Time per instruction = $1 \cdot \tau_1 = 5$ ns. Instructions = $0.30 \times 1000 = 300$. Time = $300 \times 5 = 1500$ ns.

**Mode 2 (6 stages):** Time per instruction = $\tau_2 = 3$ ns. Instructions = $0.50 \times 1000 = 500$. Time = $500 \times 3 = 1500$ ns.

**Mode 3 (8 stages):** Time per instruction = $\tau_2 = 2$ ns. Instructions = $0.20 \times 1000 = 200$. Time = $200 \times 2 = 400$ ns.

**Total time for dynamic pipeline:**
$$T_{dynamic} = 1500 + 1500 + 400 = 3400 \text{ ns}$$

**Average throughput:**
$$\text{Throughput}_{dynamic} = \frac{1000}{3400} = 0.294 \text{ instr/ns}$$

**Fixed 6-stage pipeline** (always in Mode 2): $\tau = 3$ ns.
$$T_{fixed} = 1000 \times 3 = 3000 \text{ ns}$$
$$\text{Throughput}_{fixed} = \frac{1000}{3000} = 0.333 \text{ instr/ns}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\text{Speedup of fixed over dynamic} = \frac{3400}{3000} \approx 1.13$$

**Trade-off comment (2 marks):**
The dynamic pipeline is **13% slower** in throughput for this workload, but offers:
- **Lower power in Mode 3** (faster clock but fewer instructions means shorter active time).
- **Adaptive behavior** for variable workloads (where dynamic excels).
- **Single hardware lane** — lower silicon area and cost than running 3 separate pipelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Don't confuse stage time $\tau$ with total time $T$.** Stage time = clock period = time per *stage*; one instruction still requires $n \cdot \tau$ to traverse all stages if sequential, but pipelined throughput is $1/\tau$.
> 2. **Include fill/drain penalty** only when $N$ is small (e.g., $N < 10n$). For $N = 1000$, ignore it.
> 3. **Diversified ≠ Parallel.** Many students lose marks by mixing these up. Remember: *parallel = identical*, *diversified = heterogeneous*.
> 4. **Dynamic pipeline reconfiguration cost** must be explicitly mentioned in design questions.
> 5. Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming no hazards", "assuming infinite cache") before computing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Pipeline Concepts)**

- ✅ **Pipeline** partitions instruction execution into $n$ stages; ideal speedup is $n$.
- ✅ **Parallel Pipeline** = $k$ *identical* lanes → throughput $\times k$.
- ✅ **Diversified Pipeline** = *heterogeneous* lanes → each optimized for an instruction class.
- ✅ **Dynamic Pipeline** = *reconfigurable* stage structure → adapts depth/width at runtime.
- ✅ Key formula: $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{n + N - 1} \to n$ as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ Amdahl's Law: $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ — sequential fraction caps the speedup.
- ✅ Pipeline efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Speedup}}{n}$; always $\leq 1$ in correct usage.
- ✅ Hazards (structural, data, control) reduce both speedup and efficiency.
- ✅ Modern CPUs use **all three concepts simultaneously** (e.g., superscalar + OoO + specialized units + DVFS reconfiguration).
- ✅ **Reconfiguration latency** is the chief drawback of dynamic pipelines.
- ✅ **Diversified pipelines** reduce the *sequential fraction* $f$ by parallelizing *different* operations.
- ✅ Memory in mind: $T_{pipe} = (n + N - 1) \tau$, $\text{Throughput} = 1/\tau$.
- ✅ For KTU ESE: always show the **derivation steps**, not just the final formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "compare" any two of {parallel, diversified, dynamic}, use a **table with at least 4 columns** (e.g., structure, throughput, latency, complexity). Examiners allocate marks for visual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superscalar Pipeline Overview: Instruction Fetch, Decode, Dispatch, Execute, Completion, and Retiring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Superscalar Pipeline Overview: Instruction Fetch, Decode, Dispatch, Execute, Completion, and Retiring # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Superscalar Pipeline** is a processor organization in which multiple independent instructions are issued, executed, and completed simultaneously during each clock cycle by duplicating the pipeline functional units and by providing additional pathways for instruction flow. Unlike a simple scalar pipeline (which issues at most one instruction per clock cycle), a superscalar processor fetches, decodes, dispatches, executes, and retires **two or more instructions per cycle** in parallel. The superscalar pipeline is divided into the following canonical stages, as specified in the KTU 2024 scheme: 1. **Instruction Fetch (IF)** – Reading one or more instructions from the instruction cache/memory. 2. **Instruction Decode (ID / Pre-decode)** – Determining the operation type, required operands, and dependencies for each fetched instruction. 3. **Dispatch (Issue)** – Routing the decoded instructions to the appropriate reservation stations, functional units, or issue queues. 4. **Execute (EX)** – Performing the actual operation (ALU, FPU, branch, load/store) in parallel functional units. 5. **Completion (Finish / Write-Back into Reorder Buffer)** – Marking the result as ready; results are written into temporary architectural registers. 6. **Retiring (Commit / Architectural Write-back)** – Updating the *program-visible* register file and memory in strict program order. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Distinction:** "Completion" and "Retiring" are NOT the same in out-of-order execution. Completion writes to the **Reorder Buffer (ROB)**, while Retiring is the in-order architectural commit. Examiners frequently test this difference. ## 1.2 Key Performance Metrics The fundamental performance metrics for a superscalar pipeline are: - **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle):** The average number of instructions completed per clock cycle. For an *n*-wide superscalar, the ideal IPC = *n*. - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** The reciprocal of IPC, i.e., $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{1}{\text{IPC}}$. - **Issue Width (*n*):** The maximum number of instructions that can be initiated in a single cycle. - **Commit Width (*c*):** The maximum number of instructions that can be retired in a single cycle. - **Peak Throughput:** $T_{peak} = n \times f$, where $f$ is the clock frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A scalar RISC pipeline (like the classic 5-stage MIPS) has $n = 1$, meaning it can issue **at most one instruction per cycle**. A superscalar design with $n = 4$ aims to retire up to **4 instructions per cycle**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Multi-Chef Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a small diner with a **single chef** (a scalar pipeline) who can cook one dish at a time: chop onions → boil water → plate the food → serve the customer. The chef is fast, but only one dish happens at a time. Now imagine a **fancy restaurant kitchen** (a superscalar pipeline): - **Waiters** (Fetch unit) grab several order tickets at once. - **Sommelier/Head Chef** (Decoder) reads each ticket, identifies the dish type, ingredients, and dependencies. - **Expediter** (Dispatch unit) hands each ticket to the right **sub-chef station** — pasta station, grill station, dessert station, salad station. - **Sub-chefs** (ALU, FPU, branch, load/store units) work in **parallel** on different dishes. - The **pass-through window** (Completion → Reorder Buffer) holds each dish until it is fully cooked. - The **head waiter** (Retire/Commit unit) walks the dishes out to the **correct table number** — strictly in the order the customers placed their orders, even if some dishes were cooked faster than others. This restaurant model is the exact mental model of an out-of-order superscalar processor: - Multiple chefs (parallel functional units) = **superscalar execution** - Pass-through holding area = **Reorder Buffer** - Strict table-number delivery = **in-order commit / retiring** > [!TIP] > **Why "Retire" and not just "Write-back"?** Because modern processors must preserve the *illusion of sequential execution* even when instructions actually complete out of order. Retiring is the moment the result becomes **architecturally visible** — visible to the programmer and to interrupts/exceptions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Breakdown ### Stage 1: Instruction Fetch (IF) - Fetches a **fetch group** (typically 2, 4, or 8 instructions) per cycle from the I-cache. - Uses **branch prediction** to speculatively fetch down the predicted path. - Modern designs use a *fetch buffer* (e.g., 16-entry instruction queue) to smooth out I-cache misses. - **Key challenge:** keeping the front-end fed; a misprediction flushes the entire pipeline. ### Stage 2: Instruction Decode / Pre-decode - Determines the **opcode class** (ALU, load/store, branch, FP). - Reads architectural register specifiers (e.g., `r3, r7, r11`). - Performs **dependency checking** using a scoreboard or rename map table. - Width must match the fetch width: an *n*-wide superscalar requires *n* decoders. ### Stage 3: Dispatch (Issue) - Decoded instructions are placed into: - **Reservation stations** (Tomasulo-style, dynamic scheduling), **or** - **Issue queues / unified scheduler** (modern Intel/AMD style). - The dispatcher also performs **register renaming** to break false (Write-After-Write) dependencies. - Each cycle, the scheduler selects up to *n* ready instructions and **issues** them to the functional units. ### Stage 4: Execute - Multiple **parallel functional units** (typically heterogeneous): - Integer ALU units - Floating-point units (FPU) - Load/Store units (LSU) - Branch execution unit - Latencies differ (e.g., integer add = 1 cycle, integer multiply = 3–4 cycles, FP divide = 10–20 cycles). - Out-of-order completion is allowed *within* this stage. ### Stage 5: Completion (Write-Back to Reorder Buffer) - When an instruction **finishes** execution, its result is: - Forwarded to dependent instructions in the reservation stations, **and** - Deposited into its **Reorder Buffer (ROB) entry**. - The instruction is marked "complete" but NOT yet "retired." - The ROB preserves the original program order. ### Stage 6: Retiring (Commit / Architectural Write-back) - The **head of the ROB** is committed (retired) in strict program order. - Architectural register file is updated. - Stores are released to the **store buffer** for eventual retirement to the data cache. - Branch mispredictions and exceptions cause a **precise state** to be restored from the ROB head. - This is what gives the processor **precise exceptions** — a hard KTU exam favorite. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating "Completion" and "Retire." Completion = out-of-order, writes to ROB. Retire = in-order, updates the architectural state. If you write that "the result is written to the register file at completion," you will lose marks. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Instructions Per Cycle (ideal)** | $\text{IPC}_{ideal} = n$ | $n$ = issue width | | 2 | **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{1}{\text{IPC}}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | **CPU Time** | $T_{CPU} = N_{inst} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | | 4 | **Superscalar Speedup over Scalar** | $S = \dfrac{\text{IPC}_{ss}}{\text{IPC}_{scalar}} = \dfrac{n \times \eta}{\text{CPI}_{scalar}}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency factor $(0 < \eta \le 1)$ | | 5 | **Amdahl's Law (pipeline limit)** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{n}{1 + (n-1)\beta}$ | $\beta$ = fraction of *non-parallelizable* work | | 6 | **Throughput (Peak)** | $T_{peak} = n \times f_{clk}$ | Instructions/second | | 7 | **Pipeline Depth Occupancy** | $O = k + (N-1)$ | $k$ = stages, $N$ = instructions | | 8 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{S_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\text{IPC}_{actual}}{n}$ | Fraction, $0 < \eta \le 1$ | | 9 | **ROB Required Size** | $\text{ROB}_{size} \ge \text{IssueWidth} \times \text{MaxOutstandingMem}$ | Entries | | 10 | **Branch Misprediction Penalty** | $P_{branch} = d \times m$ | $d$ = mispredict penalty (cycles), $m$ = misprediction rate | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **only** way to retire more than 1 instruction per cycle is to make **commit width $c$** equal to the issue width $n$ — otherwise the ROB head-of-line blocking will throttle throughput regardless of how fast the execute stage runs. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Superscalar pipelines power essentially every modern general-purpose processor: - **Intel Core i-series** (e.g., Alder Lake / Raptor Lake): 6-wide issue, 12-wide dispatch, with 500+ entry ROB. - **AMD Zen 4 / Zen 5**: 6-wide integer issue, with a large unified scheduler. - **Apple M-series (M1/M2/M3/M4)**: 8-wide decode, very wide dispatch, aggressive OoO execution. - **Qualcomm / ARM server cores (Neoverse)**: Wide superscalar with deep OoO windows. Engineering trade-offs: wider issue ($n$ larger) requires more register file ports, more rename map table bandwidth, a deeper ROB, and exponentially more bypass network wiring. This is why most practical chips settle on $n = 4$ to $n = 8$ — beyond this point, the area and power costs dominate. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Speedup of an *n*-Wide Superscalar A scalar pipeline issues **1 instruction per cycle** at steady state, so its $\text{CPI}_{scalar} = 1$. A perfect *n*-wide superscalar can issue *n* instructions per cycle, so its $\text{CPI}_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{n}$. The execution time for $N$ instructions is: $$T_{scalar} = N \times \text{CPI}_{scalar} \times T_{clk} = N \times 1 \times T_{clk} = N \cdot T_{clk}$$ $$T_{ss} = N \times \text{CPI}_{ss} \times T_{clk} = N \times \frac{1}{n} \times T_{clk} = \frac{N}{n} \cdot T_{clk}$$ The speedup $S$ is the ratio of execution times: $$S = \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{ss}} = \frac{N \cdot T_{clk}}{\dfrac{N}{n} \cdot T_{clk}}$$ Simplifying (the $N$ and $T_{clk}$ cancel): $$S = n$$ > This confirms that the **ideal (upper-bound) speedup** of an *n*-wide superscalar is **n**, i.e., linear with issue width. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Realistic Speedup Using a Performance Efficiency Factor In practice, structural hazards, data hazards, branch mispredictions, and cache misses prevent reaching the ideal. Let $\eta$ represent the *pipeline efficiency*, defined as the fraction of cycles in which the maximum $n$ instructions are issued. $$\text{IPC}_{actual} = n \times \eta, \quad \text{where } 0 < \eta \le 1$$ Substituting into the speedup expression: $$S = \frac{\text{IPC}_{ss}}{\text{IPC}_{scalar}} = \frac{n \cdot \eta}{1}$$ $$\boxed{S = n \cdot \eta}$$ **Worked Example:** A 4-wide superscalar with $\eta = 0.5$ achieves: $$S = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$$ i.e., only **2× faster** than the scalar baseline despite quadrupling the issue width. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Amdahl's Law Applied to Superscalar Issue Width Let a fraction $\beta$ of instructions be *non-parallelizable* (e.g., inherently serial dependencies, branch serialization, or cache-miss serialization). The remaining fraction $(1 - \beta)$ can be executed in parallel across the $n$ issue slots. The execution time becomes: $$T_{ss} = \left( N \cdot \beta \right) \cdot T_{clk} + \frac{N \cdot (1 - \beta)}{n} \cdot T_{clk}$$ Factoring out $N \cdot T_{clk}$: $$T_{ss} = N \cdot T_{clk} \cdot \left[ \beta + \frac{1 - \beta}{n} \right]$$ The speedup over scalar ($T_{scalar} = N \cdot T_{clk}$) is therefore: $$\boxed{S_{max} = \frac{1}{\beta + \dfrac{1 - \beta}{n}}}$$ **Worked Example:** For $n = 8$ and $\beta = 0.10$ (10% inherently serial work): $$S = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{1 - 0.10}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71$$ So even an **8-wide** machine achieves only $\approx 4.7\times$ speedup if 10% of the work is sequential. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Pipeline Occupancy for *N* Instructions in a *k*-Stage Superscalar For a superscalar pipeline with $k$ stages executing $N$ instructions (where $N \gg n$ and issue width is $n$): - **Number of cycles to fill the pipeline** = $k$ - **Number of cycles to drain** (after the last instruction is issued) = $k - 1$ - **Number of cycles in steady state** = $\dfrac{N}{n} - 1$ Total cycles: $$C_{total} = k + \left( \frac{N}{n} - 1 \right) + (k - 1)$$ $$\boxed{C_{total} = 2k + \frac{N}{n} - 2}$$ The average CPI is then: $$\text{CPI}_{avg} = \frac{C_{total}}{N} = \frac{2k - 2}{N} + \frac{1}{n}$$ As $N \to \infty$, the start-up/teardown terms vanish and the **asymptotic CPI** approaches the ideal: $$\text{CPI}_{avg} \to \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{as } N \to \infty$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Scalar vs. Superscalar | Parameter | Scalar Pipeline $(n=1)$ | 2-Wide Superscalar $(n=2)$ | 4-Wide Superscalar $(n=4)$ | |---|---|---|---| | Ideal IPC | $1$ | $2$ | $4$ | | Ideal CPI | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0.25$ | | Ideal Speedup (vs scalar) | $1\times$ | $2\times$ | $4\times$ | | Functional units required | $1$ ALU, $1$ LSU | $2$ ALU, $2$ LSU, etc. | $4$ (heterogeneous) | | Decode bandwidth | $1$ instr/cycle | $2$ instr/cycle | $4$ instr/cycle | | Register file read ports | $2$ | $4$ | $8$ | | ROB entries (typical) | $16\text{–}40$ | $40\text{–}128$ | $128\text{–}512$ | | Practical $\eta$ | $0.8\text{–}0.9$ | $0.4\text{–}0.6$ | $0.25\text{–}0.45$ | | Realistic speedup | $1.0\times$ | $1.4\text{–}1.8\times$ | $2.0\text{–}2.8\times$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiners love this table.** If asked to "compare scalar and superscalar pipelines" (a classic 7-mark question), this matrix — or a paraphrase of it — covers 80% of the expected points. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Six-Stage Superscalar Pipeline Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ICache
Instruction Fetch] --> B[Fetch Buffer
16 to 64 entries] B --> C[Decoder Array
4 wide] C --> D[Register Renamer
Map Table] D --> E[Unified Issue Queue
Scheduler] E --> F1[Integer ALU 1] E --> F2[Integer ALU 2] E --> F3[FP Unit] E --> F4[Load Store Unit] E --> F5[Branch Unit] F1 --> G[Common Data Bus
CDB Forwarding] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G F5 --> G G --> H[Reorder Buffer ROB
Completion Write] H --> I[Architectural Register File
Retire in Order] H --> J[Store Buffer
LSU Drain] J --> K[DCache
Data Memory] I --> L[Program Visible State
Precise Exceptions] J --> L ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Lifecycle of a Single Instruction ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant F as Fetch Unit participant D as Decoder participant R as Renamer participant Q as Issue Queue participant U as Functional Unit participant O as ROB Entry participant A as Arch Regfile F->>D: IF: Read instr from ICache D->>D: ID: Decode opcode + operands D->>R: Send decoded micro-ops R->>Q: Dispatch with physical tags Q->>Q: Wait for operands (data hazard check) Q->>U: Issue: select ready instruction U->>U: EX: Compute result U->>O: Completion: write to ROB slot U-->>Q: Forward result via CDB O->>A: Retiring: head of ROB commits A-->>F: Update PC on branch resolution ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Instruction State Transitions in the ROB ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Fetched Fetched --> Decoded Decoded --> Renamed Renamed --> Issued Issued --> Executing Executing --> Completed Completed --> Retired Retired --> [*] Executing --> Flushed: Branch Mispredict Completed --> Flushed: Exception before commit Flushed --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture (4-Wide Superscalar) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Front_End FE1[Branch Target Buffer BTB] FE2[Branch Predictor BHT] FE3[Instruction Cache ICache] FE4[Fetch Address Generator] end subgraph Decode_Rename DR1[Pre-decoder] DR2[4x Decoder] DR3[Micro-op Queue] DR4[Register Alias Table RAT] DR5[Free List Allocator] end subgraph Execution_Engine EE1[Unified Reservation Station] EE2[Integer ALU Bank] EE3[FP ALU Bank] EE4[Address Generation Unit AGU] EE5[Load Store Queue LSQ] end subgraph Memory_Subsystem MS1[Data Cache DCache] MS2[L2 Cache] MS3[Store Buffer] end subgraph Retire_Logic RL1[Reorder Buffer ROB] RL2[Architectural Register File] RL3[Exception Handler Interface] end FE1 --> FE4 FE2 --> FE4 FE3 --> DR1 FE4 --> DR1 DR1 --> DR2 --> DR3 --> DR4 --> EE1 DR5 --> DR4 EE1 --> EE2 EE1 --> EE3 EE1 --> EE4 EE4 --> EE5 EE5 --> MS1 MS1 --> MS2 EE2 --> RL1 EE3 --> RL1 EE5 --> MS3 MS3 --> MS1 RL1 --> RL2 RL1 --> RL3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block corresponds to a real, physically distinct hardware unit on the processor die. The `CDB (Common Data Bus)` is the broadcast network that forwards completed results to all waiting reservation stations in a single cycle — it is one of the principal bandwidth bottlenecks in wide superscalar designs. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a superscalar processor. How does it differ from a scalar pipelined processor in terms of instruction issue rate?* **Model Answer:** A superscalar processor is one that fetches, decodes, issues, and retires **more than one instruction per clock cycle** by providing multiple parallel execution units. A scalar pipelined processor, in contrast, issues **at most one instruction per cycle** even though it overlaps the execution of successive instructions through pipelining. The key distinguishing metric is the **issue width** $n$: scalar ⇒ $n = 1$, superscalar ⇒ $n \ge 2$. As a result, the peak IPC of a scalar is 1, while a superscalar can theoretically reach $n$. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between instruction "completion" and "retiring" in a superscalar pipeline that supports out-of-order execution.* **Model Answer:** In an out-of-order superscalar pipeline, **completion** is the event when an instruction finishes execution and its result is written into its assigned entry in the **Reorder Buffer (ROB)**. Completion can happen **out of program order**, in whatever order the functional units finish. **Retiring (also called commit or architectural write-back)** is the later event when the result is moved from the head of the ROB into the **architectural register file** in strict **in-order** program order. Completion is an internal pipeline event; retiring is the moment the result becomes visible to the programmer, to interrupts, and to subsequent instructions in architectural state. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **4-wide superscalar pipeline** covering the stages: Instruction Fetch, Decode, Dispatch, Execute, Completion, and Retiring. **\[7 Marks\]** *(b)* A 4-wide superscalar processor has the following execution profile for a benchmark of $N = 10^9$ instructions: - $40\%$ of cycles issue 4 instructions - $30\%$ of cycles issue 3 instructions - $20\%$ of cycles issue 2 instructions - $10\%$ of cycles issue 0 instructions (stall) Compute the **average IPC**, the **average CPI**, and the **execution time** at a clock frequency of $3~\text{GHz}$. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** The 4-wide superscalar pipeline operates in six stages as follows: 1. **Instruction Fetch (IF):** The fetch unit reads a 4-instruction block per cycle from the I-cache. A branch predictor provides the predicted next-PC; mispredictions trigger a flush. 2. **Decode (ID):** Four parallel decoders interpret each instruction, identifying opcode, source and destination register specifiers, and immediate values. 3. **Dispatch / Rename:** A Register Alias Table (RAT) maps architectural registers to physical registers, eliminating false Write-After-Write (WAW) and Write-After-Read (WAR) hazards. The renamed micro-ops enter the unified issue queue. 4. **Execute (EX):** The scheduler selects up to 4 ready micro-ops per cycle and dispatches them to parallel functional units: integer ALUs, FP units, load/store units, and a branch unit. Execution is out-of-order; units have varying latencies. 5. **Completion:** When a unit finishes, its result is broadcast on the Common Data Bus (CDB) to dependent instructions in the reservation station, and is written to the instruction's ROB slot. 6. **Retiring (Commit):** The retirement logic walks the ROB from the head, committing (in program order) the results of completed instructions to the architectural register file. Stores are dispatched to the store buffer for eventual cache write-back. This guarantees **precise exceptions** and supports branch-misprediction recovery. > *(Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 for the visual representation.)* **Part (b):** The IPC is the weighted-average instructions issued per cycle: $$\text{IPC}_{avg} = 0.40 \times 4 + 0.30 \times 3 + 0.20 \times 2 + 0.10 \times 0$$ Computing each term: $$\text{IPC}_{avg} = 1.60 + 0.90 + 0.40 + 0.00$$ $$\boxed{\text{IPC}_{avg} = 2.90}$$ The CPI is the reciprocal: $$\text{CPI}_{avg} = \frac{1}{\text{IPC}_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2.90}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CPI}_{avg} \approx 0.3448}$$ The clock period at $f = 3~\text{GHz}$ is: $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.333~\text{ns}$$ The total execution time is: $$T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI}_{avg} \times T_{clk}$$ $$T_{CPU} = 10^9 \times 0.3448 \times 0.333 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\boxed{T_{CPU} \approx 0.115~\text{seconds} \approx 115~\text{ms}}$$ The pipeline efficiency versus the ideal $n=4$ is: $$\eta = \frac{\text{IPC}_{actual}}{n} = \frac{2.90}{4} = 0.725 = 72.5\%$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *(a)* Explain the role of the **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** in a superscalar processor that supports out-of-order execution. How does it enable **precise exceptions** and **in-order retiring**? **\[7 Marks\]** *(b)* A superscalar processor has an **issue width of 8**, but only **75%** of issued instructions can be parallelized due to data dependencies. Compute the **maximum achievable speedup** using Amdahl's law. Also compute the speedup if the issue width is increased to **16** with the same 75% parallel fraction. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** The **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** is a circular queue of in-flight instructions that has been dispatched into the pipeline. It serves three critical purposes: 1. **Tracking In-Flight State:** Each ROB entry contains the destination physical register tag, the architectural destination register, the ready bit, and the original program order (the "sequence number"). This decouples the in-order front-end from the out-of-order back-end. 2. **In-Order Retiring:** Although instructions complete out of order and write to ROB slots, the **retire logic commits results from the head of the ROB** in strict program order. This makes the architectural register file appear as if all earlier instructions have already finished. Up to $c$ (commit width) instructions retire per cycle. 3. **Precise Exceptions:** When an exception is raised (e.g., divide by zero, page fault), the processor identifies the **first** instruction in program order that caused it, retires all earlier instructions from the ROB, and then transfers control to the OS exception handler. The architectural state at that moment is exactly the state that *would have existed* if every preceding instruction had executed sequentially — this is the precise-exception guarantee required by the architecture. 4. **Branch Misprediction Recovery:** When a branch resolves as mispredicted, all instructions issued after the branch are *squashed* in the ROB (marked invalid); only instructions before the branch are allowed to retire. The architectural PC and registers are thus restored to the correct path. **Part (b):** Amdahl's law for an *n*-wide superscalar: $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{\beta + \dfrac{1 - \beta}{n}}$$ where $\beta$ is the fraction of non-parallelizable (serial) work. Since $75\%$ can be parallelized, the parallel fraction is $(1 - \beta) = 0.75$, hence: $$\beta = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$ **Case 1: Issue width $n = 8$** $$S_8 = \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{1 - 0.25}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{8}}$$ $$S_8 = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.34375}$$ $$\boxed{S_8 \approx 2.91}$$ **Case 2: Issue width $n = 16$** $$S_{16} = \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.046875} = \frac{1}{0.296875}$$ $$\boxed{S_{16} \approx 3.37}$$ **Diminishing Returns Analysis:** $$\Delta S = S_{16} - S_8 = 3.37 - 2.91 = 0.46$$ Doubling the issue width from 8 to 16 yields only $\approx 0.46$ additional speedup. This vividly demonstrates Amdahl's law: the **25% serial bottleneck** dominates the achievable parallelism. The upper bound as $n \to \infty$ is: $$S_\infty = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0$$ So even a 1024-wide machine would top out at 4× speedup. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:** > 1. **Confusing completion with retiring** — these are distinct events; you will lose 2–3 marks if not separated clearly. > 2. **Forgetting the role of the ROB** — if the question mentions out-of-order execution, the ROB MUST be discussed; no marks are awarded for a generic pipeline description. > 3. **Using $S = n$ blindly** — this is the *ideal*; in any numerical problem the examiner will expect Amdahl's law or an efficiency factor. > 4. **Arithmetic errors in weighted IPC sums** — double-check that the probabilities sum to 1.0. > 5. **Failing to state units** — $T_{CPU}$ must be expressed in seconds (or ms/$\mu$s); $\text{CPI}$ is dimensionless. > 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows / labels** — fetch/decode/execute must be clearly delineated; arrows must show data flow. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **superscalar pipeline** issues *n* ≥ 2 instructions per cycle; a **scalar pipeline** issues only 1. - ✅ The **six canonical stages** are: **Fetch → Decode → Dispatch → Execute → Completion → Retire**. Know them in order. - ✅ **Fetch width** = number of instructions read from I-cache per cycle. **Issue width** $n$ = max instructions dispatched to execution units per cycle. **Commit width** $c$ = max instructions retired per cycle. - ✅ **Completion** writes results to the **ROB** (out-of-order allowed). **Retiring** writes results to the **architectural register file** (strictly in-order). - ✅ The **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** is the central structure that enables: (1) in-order retiring, (2) precise exceptions, (3) branch-misprediction recovery, and (4) register renaming bookkeeping. - ✅ Key formulas to memorize cold: - $\text{IPC} = 1/\text{CPI}$ - $T_{CPU} = N_{inst} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clk}$ - $S = n \cdot \eta$ (realistic speedup) - $S = \dfrac{1}{\beta + (1-\beta)/n}$ (Amdahl) - $S_\infty = 1/\beta$ (upper bound as $n \to \infty$) - ✅ **Register renaming** breaks WAR/WAW hazards; **Tomasulo's algorithm** (reservation stations + CDB) is the classic implementation. - ✅ **Practical efficiency $\eta$** for 4-wide designs is typically only **25%–45%** — wide issue does not guarantee linear speedup. - ✅ The **Common Data Bus (CDB)** broadcasts completed results to all waiting reservation stations — it is a major bandwidth bottleneck. - ✅ Modern processors (Intel/AMD/Apple/ARM) use **4–8 wide issue** with **128–500+ entry ROBs** and **multi-level branch predictors**. - ✅ **Precise exceptions** is the property that the architectural state at an exception is exactly what sequential execution would have produced — this is guaranteed by the ROB + in-order commit. - ✅ **Branch misprediction penalty** scales with pipeline depth: deeper pipelines = larger penalty; the **branch target buffer (BTB)** and **branch history table (BHT)** mitigate this. - ✅ For 14-mark answers, **always include a block diagram** (even a hand-drawn one) of the superscalar pipeline; examiners explicitly award marks for figures.

topics.title: Memory Hierarchy: Computer System Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory Hierarchy: Computer System Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> A *Memory Hierarchy* is a layered organization of storage components in a computer system, arranged in a strict order of decreasing speed, increasing capacity, and decreasing cost per bit. The goal is to provide a system-wide *illusion* of an extremely large, extremely fast, and inexpensive memory by exploiting the inherent property of program behavior known as **Locality of Reference**.

A modern computer system is built from several distinct storage technologies, each occupying a specific tier in the hierarchy. At the top sit small, blazingly fast hardware structures such as **CPU Registers** and **Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3)**. At the bottom sit massive but slow bulk-storage devices such as **Magnetic Disks (HDD)**, **Solid-State Drives (SSD)**, and archival **Magnetic Tapes**. The fundamental design challenge is to bridge the massive performance gap between the processor (which executes instructions in sub-nanosecond cycles) and main memory (which responds in tens to hundreds of nanoseconds) — a problem quantified by the **Memory Wall** phenomenon.

The classical arrangement, from top (fastest/smallest) to bottom (slowest/largest), is:

| Tier | Component | Typical Access Time | Typical Capacity |
|------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| 0 | CPU Registers | **~0.3 – 1 ns** | **~64 B – 1 KB** |
| 1 | L1 Cache (SRAM) | **~1 – 2 ns** | **~32 – 64 KB** |
| 2 | L2 Cache (SRAM) | **~3 – 10 ns** | **~256 KB – 1 MB** |
| 3 | L3 Cache (SRAM) | **~10 – 20 ns** | **~4 – 32 MB** |
| 4 | Main Memory (DRAM) | **~50 – 100 ns** | **~4 – 32 GB** |
| 5 | SSD (NAND Flash) | **~25 – 100 µs** | **~256 GB – 4 TB** |
| 6 | HDD (Magnetic Disk) | **~1 – 10 ms** | **~1 – 10 TB** |
| 7 | Archival Tape | **~seconds** | **~PB scale** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Memory Hierarchy as a Librarian's Desk System**
>
> Imagine a university librarian. The books **currently being read** are placed on the desk (Registers/Cache). The librarian can grab them in **microseconds**. Books needed **frequently** are kept on nearby shelves (Main Memory) — a few seconds' walk. Books **rarely consulted** are stored in a back-room archive (Disk/SSD) — minutes to retrieve. And ancient manuscripts are kept in a **climate-controlled vault** hundreds of meters away (Tape/Cloud). The librarian never brings *all* books to the desk at once — only the ones *likely to be needed soon*. This "predict and pre-stage" mechanism is exactly how the memory hierarchy works: data is moved *upward* (closer to the CPU) when the CPU is likely to need it, and *downward* (evicted) when the top tiers run out of space.

### 1.3 The Three Guiding Principles

> [!NOTE]
> The entire design of memory hierarchy rests on **three foundational principles** that the KTU examiner expects every student to internalize:

1. **Principle of Locality of Reference** — Programs do not access memory locations randomly; they exhibit strong *temporal* and *spatial* locality. This single fact makes the hierarchy *possible* (without it, the pyramid collapses into a flat array).
2. **Principle of Inclusion** — A higher-level memory (closer to the CPU) contains a strict subset of the information present in the next lower level. (Strictly true in fully inclusive caches; relaxed in non-inclusive designs.)
3. **Principle of Coherence / Consistency** — A change in a higher-level copy must be propagated (eventually) to all lower-level copies, so that no level holds stale data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Design Drivers of a Memory Hierarchy

Every memory hierarchy decision is governed by a *trade-off* between six competing engineering drivers. KTU questions frequently probe the student's understanding of these trade-offs.

1. **Access Time (Latency)** — Time to retrieve a piece of data once a request is issued. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** for volatile memory, **microseconds (µs)** for SSD, and **milliseconds (ms)** for HDD.
2. **Capacity (Size)** — Total volume of data that can be stored. Measured in **Bytes / KB / MB / GB / TB**.
3. **Bandwidth (Throughput)** — Volume of data transferred *per unit time*. Measured in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.
4. **Cost per Bit** — Manufacturing cost normalized to one bit of storage. Measured in **$/bit** or **$/GB**.
5. **Volatility** — Whether the storage loses its content when power is removed. (Registers/DRAM = volatile; SSD/HDD/Tape = non-volatile.)
6. **Energy per Access** — Joules consumed per read/write operation, critical in mobile and HPC designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Iron Triangle of Memory Design**
> You can only pick **two** of the three: **(Fast + Cheap)** is *impossible* — the moment you make a memory fast, the per-bit cost explodes. The hierarchy cleverly *combines* the fast-but-expensive with the slow-but-cheap to give the *illusion* of having all three.

### 2.2 Locality of Reference — The Engine That Drives the Hierarchy

Locality of reference is the *empirical observation* that real programs do not access memory uniformly at random. There are two sub-types:

- **Temporal Locality (Time-Based):** If a memory location is accessed, it is *likely* to be accessed again in the *near future*. Example: a loop counter, a stack pointer, a frequently updated accumulator.
- **Spatial Locality (Space-Based):** If a memory location is accessed, *neighboring* memory locations are *likely* to be accessed in the near future. Example: sequential instruction fetch, array traversal, sequential record access in a database.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Locality Makes the Hierarchy Work**
> The hardware designer places a *small* amount of fast memory close to the CPU. Because of **temporal locality**, the same data is requested repeatedly — the fast memory can serve it again and again without bothering the slow memory. Because of **spatial locality**, when a word is fetched, *adjacent* words are also brought in (a *cache line / block*), anticipating the next accesses. Without locality, every access would have to go all the way down to the slowest level, and the system would run at the speed of the slowest tier.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board Examiner's Most-Tested Formulas — Master These Cold.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------|------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$ | $T_{hit}$ in **ns**; $H$ is dimensionless; $T_{miss}$ in **ns** | Compute the *effective* memory access latency seen by the CPU. |
| 2 | **Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{N_{hit}}{N_{hit} + N_{miss}}$ | $N$ = number of accesses (count) | Measure the *efficiency* of a cache level. |
| 3 | **Miss Ratio** | $M = 1 - H$ | Dimensionless | Used as the primary design metric in many textbooks. |
| 4 | **Multi-Level AMAT (3 Levels)** | $T_{avg} = H_1 T_1 + (1-H_1) H_2 (T_1 + T_2) + (1-H_1)(1-H_2) T_3$ | $T_i$ = access time at level $i$ | Compute end-to-end latency across L1→L2→Main. |
| 5 | **Total Memory Cost** | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot c_i$ | $S_i$ = size in **bits**; $c_i$ = **$/bit** | Budget a memory subsystem. |
| 6 | **Average Cost per Bit (Hierarchical)** | $c_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum S_i c_i}{\sum S_i}$ | **$/bit** | Compare hierarchical vs. flat memory. |
| 7 | **Speedup vs. Single-Level Memory** | $S = \dfrac{T_{flat}}{T_{hier}}$ | Dimensionless | Quantify the *benefit* of adding a cache. |
| 8 | **Capacity-Distance Product** | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot t_{i}$ | $S_i$ in **bits**; $t_i$ in **seconds** | Used in analytic cost models. |
| 9 | **Bandwidth** | $B = \dfrac{D_{transferred}}{T_{transfer}}$ | $D$ in **bytes**; $T$ in **seconds**; Result in **B/s** | Measure bus/interface throughput. |
| 10 | **Memory Wall Gap** | $G = \dfrac{T_{memory}}{T_{CPU\_cycle}}$ | Dimensionless | Quantify the *growing* CPU-memory speed gap. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Tip for KTU Board Answers**
> Never use the bare pipe symbol `\vert x \vert` in a markdown table row. Use `\lvert x \rvert` or `\mid x \mid`. The bare pipe breaks the markdown parser and the entire row collapses — costing you the marks for that table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Memory hierarchy design is the *single most important factor* in modern processor performance — it is often more critical than raw CPU clock speed. KTU specifically emphasizes this in the PEAET522 syllabus. Real-world applications include:

- **Datacenter Design:** Google, Amazon, and Microsoft design multi-tier memory hierarchies (L1/L2/L3 → RAM → SSD → Cold Storage) to serve billions of requests per second with millisecond latency budgets.
- **Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon, Dimensity):** Use *system-level caches* shared between CPU and GPU to minimize DRAM access power.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Use *non-uniform memory access (NUMA)* topologies where the hierarchy is *physically distributed* across CPU sockets.
- **Embedded & IoT:** Use *scratchpad memories* in place of hardware caches for predictable real-time behavior.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental AMAT Equation

**Statement (Given):** A memory hierarchy has *one* level of cache in front of main memory. Let $T_h$ be the time to access the cache (hit time), and let $T_m$ be the additional time to access main memory (miss penalty) *only when* a miss occurs. Let $H$ be the hit ratio of the cache.

**Goal:** Derive the average memory access time $T_{avg}$ as seen by the processor.

**Step 1 — Partition the Universe of Outcomes.**
Every memory access the CPU issues results in *exactly one* of two mutually exclusive outcomes: either the data is in the cache (**Hit**) or it is not (**Miss**). Since the universe is partitioned, the probabilities must sum to unity:

$$
H + M = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Total Time Over Many Accesses.**
Consider $N$ consecutive memory accesses. Out of these:
- $H \cdot N$ accesses are hits, each costing $T_h$.
- $M \cdot N$ accesses are misses, each costing $T_h + T_m$ (we *still* check the cache first, then pay the miss penalty to go to memory).

The total elapsed time across all $N$ accesses is:

$$
T_{total} = (H \cdot N) \cdot T_h + (M \cdot N) \cdot (T_h + T_m)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Average.**
The average time per access is the total divided by $N$:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{T_{total}}{N} = H \cdot T_h + M \cdot (T_h + T_m)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify Using the Partition Identity.**
Expand and collect terms:

$$
T_{avg} = H \cdot T_h + M \cdot T_h + M \cdot T_m = (H + M) \cdot T_h + M \cdot T_m
$$

Since $H + M = 1$:

$$
T_{avg} = T_h + M \cdot T_m
$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in the Standard Form.**
Substitute $M = 1 - H$:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{avg} = T_h + (1 - H) \cdot T_m\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation (KTU Examiner's Favourite Follow-up):**
> $T_{avg}$ is *linear* in the miss rate $M$. If the miss rate is high, the hierarchical system is *worse* than going straight to main memory — there is a *break-even point* below which the cache actually helps and above which it hurts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Level AMAT (Recursive Unrolling)

**Statement (Given):** A 2-level cache hierarchy: L1 (hit time $T_1$, hit rate $H_1$) and L2 (hit time $T_2$, hit rate $H_2$ given an L1 miss), and main memory (access time $T_3$).

**Goal:** Derive the *exact* average access time across all three levels.

**Step 1 — Time on an L1 Hit.**
If L1 hits, the total time is simply $T_1$. This happens with probability $H_1$.

**Step 2 — Time on an L1 Miss / L2 Hit.**
If L1 misses, we *still* pay $T_1$ (the L1 lookup is never avoided). Then we go to L2 and pay $T_2$. Total: $T_1 + T_2$. Probability: $(1 - H_1) \cdot H_2$.

**Step 3 — Time on an L1 Miss / L2 Miss / Memory Hit.**
If both L1 and L2 miss, we pay $T_1 + T_2 + T_3$. Probability: $(1 - H_1)(1 - H_2)$.

**Step 4 — Combine via Total Expectation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= H_1 \cdot T_1 \\
&\quad + (1 - H_1) H_2 \cdot (T_1 + T_2) \\
&\quad + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot (T_1 + T_2 + T_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify (Distributive Property).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= T_1 \big[ H_1 + (1 - H_1)H_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \big] \\
&\quad + T_2 \big[ (1 - H_1)H_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \big] \\
&\quad + T_3 \big[ (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

The first bracket equals 1 (the three outcomes form a partition). The second bracket equals $(1 - H_1)$. So:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{avg} = T_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_3\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General n-Level Form (Memorize This):**
> $$T_{avg}^{(n)} = T_1 + \sum_{k=2}^{n} \Big[ T_k \prod_{j=1}^{k-1}(1 - H_j) \Big]$$
> Each miss penalty at level $k$ is *multiplied* by the cumulative miss probability of all levels above it.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Cost-per-Bit of a Hierarchical Design

**Statement (Given):** A hierarchy with $n$ levels. Level $i$ has size $S_i$ (in bits) and per-bit cost $c_i$ (in $/bit).

**Step 1 — Total Cost.**

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot c_i
$$

**Step 2 — Total Capacity.**

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i
$$

**Step 3 — Average Cost.**

$$
c_{avg} = \frac{C_{total}}{S_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (for board exam).**
Suppose a hierarchy has:
- L1: $S_1 = 32 \,\text{KB}$, $c_1 = 100\,/\text{bit}$ (use $\$100 / \text{GB}$ as the standard figure)
- L2: $S_2 = 256 \,\text{KB}$, $c_2 = 10\,/\text{bit}$
- DRAM: $S_3 = 8 \,\text{GB}$, $c_3 = 0.005\,/\text{bit}$

Convert all sizes to bits: $32 \,\text{KB} = 32 \times 2^{10} \times 8 = 2.62 \times 10^5$ bits, etc. After computation, the *weighted average* $c_{avg}$ is dominated by the largest, cheapest tier — DRAM. This is *the* central insight: the *average* cost of a hierarchical system is very close to the cost of the *cheapest* tier, even though its *performance* is close to that of the *fastest* tier.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as a "what formula to apply" decision tree when you see a problem.**

| Problem Type | Given in Question | Formula to Apply | Output Units |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| "Find the average access time" | $T_h$, $T_m$, $H$ | $T_{avg} = T_h + (1-H) T_m$ | **ns** |
| "Find the hit ratio" | $T_{avg}$, $T_h$, $T_m$ | $H = 1 - \dfrac{T_{avg} - T_h}{T_m}$ | Dimensionless |
| "How much faster is the hierarchical system?" | $T_{hier}$, $T_{flat}$ | $S = T_{flat} / T_{hier}$ | Dimensionless |
| "Cost of a 2-tier memory" | $S_1, S_2, c_1, c_2$ | $c_{avg} = \dfrac{S_1 c_1 + S_2 c_2}{S_1 + S_2}$ | **$/bit** |
| "Multi-level AMAT" | $T_1, T_2, T_3, H_1, H_2$ | $T_{avg} = T_1 + (1-H_1)T_2 + (1-H_1)(1-H_2)T_3$ | **ns** |
| "AMAT given miss rate" | $T_h$, $M$, $T_m$ | $T_{avg} = T_h + M \cdot T_m$ | **ns** |
| "Bandwidth of a bus" | Bytes transferred, time | $B = D / T$ | **B/s** or **GB/s** |
