## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — The Classical Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reg[TIER 0: CPU REGISTERS\nFastest, Smallest\n~0.3 ns, ~64 B]
    l1[TIER 1: L1 CACHE SRAM\n~1 ns, ~32 to 64 KB]
    l2[TIER 2: L2 CACHE SRAM\n~5 ns, ~256 KB to 1 MB]
    l3[TIER 3: L3 CACHE SRAM\n~15 ns, ~4 to 32 MB]
    dram[TIER 4: MAIN MEMORY DRAM\n~60 ns, ~4 to 32 GB]
    ssd[TIER 5: SOLID STATE DRIVE\n~50 us, ~256 GB to 4 TB]
    hdd[TIER 6: HARD DISK DRIVE\n~5 ms, ~1 to 10 TB]
    tape[TIER 7: ARCHIVAL TAPE\n~seconds, ~PB scale]

    reg ==> l1
    l1 ==> l2
    l2 ==> l3
    l3 ==> dram
    dram ==> ssd
    ssd ==> hdd
    hdd ==> tape

    reg -.->|SMALLER FASTER EXPENSIVE| reg
    tape -.->|LARGER SLOWER CHEAPER| tape
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The thick arrows (==>) point *downward* in the direction of *decreasing speed* and *increasing capacity*. The dotted self-annotations emphasize the two ends of the trade-off spectrum.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Data Flow During a Memory Access (Hit vs. Miss)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu[CPU CORE\nIssues Memory Request]
    tag[STEP 1: CHECK TAGS\nIn Highest Cache Level]
    hit{STEP 2: IS IT A HIT?}
    serve[STEP 3A: SERVE DATA\nFrom Cache to CPU]
    miss[STEP 3B: CACHE MISS\nPropagate to Next Level]
    lower[STEP 4: FETCH FROM LOWER LEVEL\nBring Block UP and DOWN]
    ref[STEP 5: UPDATE CACHE\nReplace Victim Block if Full]

    cpu --> tag
    tag --> hit
    hit -->|YES| serve
    hit -->|NO| miss
    miss --> lower
    lower --> ref
    ref --> serve
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "explain the working of memory hierarchy," walk the examiner through these five steps in order. This single diagram, properly explained, scores full marks in a 7-mark descriptive question.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Locality of Reference Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    loc[PROGRAM EXECUTION]
    loc --> A[ADDRESS A1 Accessed at Time t]
    loc --> B[ADDRESS A2 Accessed at Time t plus delta]
    loc --> C[ADDRESS A1 Accessed Again at Time t plus 2 delta]

    A -->|TEMPORAL LOCALITY\nSame address, reused later| C
    B -->|SPATIAL LOCALITY\nNear address, used soon| A

    loc --> D[Why this matters:\nCache line of size 64 bytes\nbrings in neighbors automatically]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Block-Level Functional Architecture (CPU-to-Tape Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuUnit[CPU EXECUTION UNIT]
    regFile[REGISTER FILE]
    l1Unit[L1 INSTRUCTION AND DATA CACHE]
    l2Unit[L2 UNIFIED CACHE]
    l3Unit[L3 SHARED LAST LEVEL CACHE]
    mmc[Memory Controller and DRAM]
    pcie[PCIe and NVMe Interface]
    ssdUnit[SSD Storage]
    sata[SATA Interface]
    hddUnit[HDD Storage]
    tapeDrv[Tape Drive and Library]

    cpuUnit <-->|1 cycle| regFile
    regFile <-->|few cycles| l1Unit
    l1Unit <-->|~10 cycles| l2Unit
    l2Unit <-->|~40 cycles| l3Unit
    l3Unit <-->|~200 cycles| mmc
    mmc <-->|DMA and NVMe| pcie
    pcie <-->|high speed| ssdUnit
    mmc <-->|legacy bus| sata
    sata <-->|rotational| hddUnit
    hddUnit -.->|offline archival| tapeDrv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpreting the Cycle Counts:** Each arrow label represents an *order-of-magnitude* jump in access latency. The CPU-to-Register path is **1 cycle**; the CPU-to-DRAM path is **~200 cycles**; the CPU-to-SSD path is **~10,000 cycles**. This is *exactly* why the hierarchy exists — to hide these latencies from the CPU as much as possible.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Reminder:** Each Part-A answer should be 3 to 5 crisp lines. Use bullet points, label formulas, and underline the *key term* in your answer. Examiners reward keyword density.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the term "Memory Hierarchy" and explain why it is essential in a modern computer system.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Memory Hierarchy** is a layered organization of storage components arranged in decreasing order of *speed* and *cost-per-bit* and in increasing order of *capacity*.
- It is essential because there is an enormous speed mismatch (the *memory wall*) between the CPU (sub-nanosecond) and main memory (tens of nanoseconds), and no single memory technology simultaneously offers high speed, large capacity, and low cost.
- The hierarchy exploits the **Principle of Locality of Reference** (temporal and spatial) to present the CPU with the *illusion* of a single very large, very fast, and inexpensive memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between Temporal Locality and Spatial Locality of reference. Give one example for each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Temporal Locality | Spatial Locality |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Recently accessed data is likely to be accessed *again* in the near future. | Data at addresses *near* a recently accessed address is likely to be accessed in the near future. |
| **Time/Space** | Time-based reuse | Space-based reuse |
| **Example 1** | A loop counter `i` updated every iteration. | Sequential access to `arr[0], arr[1], arr[2], ...` |
| **Example 2** | A function's local variable on the stack. | Sequential instruction fetch from consecutive PC addresses. |
| **Hardware Use** | Keeps the same cache line *resident* after a hit. | Fetches an entire *cache line* (e.g., 64 B) on every miss. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and *must* be answered in approximately 5 to 7 pages of the answer booklet. The KTU internal choice allows you to pick ONE of two alternatives (A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEAET522, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the classical memory hierarchy of a computer system. Discuss the *speed-vs-capacity* trade-off and identify the *technology* used at each level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system has an L1 cache with a hit time of **1 ns** and a hit ratio of **0.90**. The miss penalty (to main memory) is **50 ns**. Compute:
   (i) The **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) The **speedup** over a system *without* the cache. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) The *new* hit ratio required to reduce the AMAT to **6 ns** (assuming the same $T_h$ and $T_m$). **(3 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **classical memory hierarchy** is structured as a pyramid with the following tiers (from top to bottom):

1. **Tier 0 — CPU Registers:** Built from high-speed flip-flops fabricated directly on the processor die. Access time ~**0.3 ns**, capacity ~**64 B to 1 KB**, technology = **CMOS flip-flops**, cost ~**1000 × DRAM/GB**.

2. **Tier 1 — L1 Cache (SRAM):** Static RAM cells (6 transistors per bit). Split into separate I-cache and D-cache in modern designs. Access time ~**1 ns**, capacity ~**32–64 KB**, technology = **6T SRAM**.

3. **Tier 2 — L2 Cache (SRAM):** Larger unified cache. Access time ~**3–10 ns**, capacity ~**256 KB – 1 MB**, technology = **SRAM**, sometimes with higher associativity than L1.

4. **Tier 3 — L3 Cache (SRAM):** Last-level cache, often shared across multiple cores. Access time ~**10–20 ns**, capacity ~**4–32 MB**, technology = **SRAM**.

5. **Tier 4 — Main Memory (DRAM):** Dynamic RAM using one transistor + one capacitor per bit. Needs periodic refresh. Access time ~**50–100 ns**, capacity ~**4–32 GB**, technology = **DRAM**.

6. **Tier 5 — SSD (NAND Flash):** Non-volatile, no moving parts. Access time ~**25–100 µs**, capacity ~**256 GB – 4 TB**, technology = **NAND Flash**.

7. **Tier 6 — HDD:** Magnetic spinning platters + moving arm. Access time ~**1–10 ms**, capacity ~**1–10 TB**, technology = **Magnetic Recording**.

8. **Tier 7 — Tape / Optical Archival:** Sequential-access media. Access time ~**seconds**, capacity ~**PB scale**, technology = **Magnetic Tape**.

**Speed-Capacity Trade-off (Conclusion, 2 lines for the 7th mark):**
The trade-off is a strict **inverse relationship**: as we go *up* the pyramid, access time falls by orders of magnitude, but per-bit cost rises and capacity shrinks by orders of magnitude. No single technology delivers *all three* — the hierarchy *combines* them.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Hit time $T_h = 1\,\text{ns}$
- Hit ratio $H = 0.90$, so miss ratio $M = 0.10$
- Miss penalty $T_m = 50\,\text{ns}$

**(i) AMAT Calculation:**

$$
T_{avg} = T_h + (1 - H) \cdot T_m = 1 + (0.10)(50) = 1 + 5 = 6\,\text{ns}
$$

$\Rightarrow \boxed{T_{avg} = 6\,\text{ns}}$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Speedup Over a Cache-less System:**

Without the cache, every access costs $T_m = 50\,\text{ns}$.

$$
S = \frac{T_{flat}}{T_{hier}} = \frac{50}{6} \approx 8.33
$$

$\Rightarrow \boxed{S \approx 8.33\times}$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) New Hit Ratio for $T_{avg} = 6\,\text{ns}$:**

We are told $T_{avg} = 6\,\text{ns}$, $T_h = 1\,\text{ns}$, $T_m = 50\,\text{ns}$, and asked to find the new $H^\prime$.

$$
T_{avg} = T_h + (1 - H^\prime) T_m
$$

$$
6 = 1 + (1 - H^\prime)(50)
$$

$$
5 = 50(1 - H^\prime)
$$

$$
1 - H^\prime = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10
$$

$$
\boxed{H^\prime = 0.90}
$$

$\Rightarrow$ The current hit ratio of **0.90** is *already* the required value. This sub-part tests whether the student understands the AMAT formula can be *inverted* to solve for the hit ratio. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEAET522, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of locality of reference** in detail. Distinguish between *temporal* and *spatial* locality, and explain how the memory hierarchy exploits each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A two-level cache hierarchy has the following parameters:
- L1: $T_1 = 1\,\text{ns}$, $H_1 = 0.95$
- L2: $T_2 = 10\,\text{ns}$, $H_2 = 0.90$ (on L1 miss)
- Main Memory: $T_3 = 100\,\text{ns}$

Compute:
   (i) The **AMAT** of this 2-level system. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) The **AMAT** if L2 were *removed* and the system went directly to main memory on an L1 miss. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) Comment on the *quantitative benefit* of including the L2 cache. **(2 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Principle of Locality of Reference (Definition, 2 marks):**
The principle states that *real programs* do not access memory locations uniformly or randomly; instead, memory accesses exhibit predictable patterns of *clustering* in time and space. The principle is the *empirical foundation* on which the entire memory hierarchy rests.

**Temporal Locality (2 marks):**
- If a memory location is referenced, it is *highly likely* to be referenced again in the *near future*.
- **Hardware exploitation:** A cache keeps a data item in the fast tier even after a hit. Eviction happens only when the fast tier is *full* and a *new* item is demanded. This way, repeated accesses to the same location are served by the cache for free.
- **Examples:** Loop indices, stack variables, accumulator variables in a reduction loop, function-local pointers.

**Spatial Locality (2 marks):**
- If a memory location is referenced, *addresses physically near* it are likely to be referenced soon after.
- **Hardware exploitation:** On every cache miss, the memory controller does *not* fetch a single byte or word — it fetches an entire *cache line / block* (typically **64 bytes** in modern CPUs). The line size is the unit of data transfer between memory and cache.
- **Examples:** Sequential instruction execution, sequential array access, strided access with small stride.

**Why Both Matter (1 mark):**
- *Temporal locality* justifies the *existence* of the cache (we can re-use cached data).
- *Spatial locality* justifies the *size* of the cache line (we can fetch *neighbors* for free).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 1\,\text{ns}$, $H_1 = 0.95$, so $(1 - H_1) = 0.05$
- $T_2 = 10\,\text{ns}$, $H_2 = 0.90$, so $(1 - H_2) = 0.10$
- $T_3 = 100\,\text{ns}$

**(i) AMAT with L2 (using the derived 3-level formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= T_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_3 \\
&= 1 + (0.05)(10) + (0.05)(0.10)(100) \\
&= 1 + 0.5 + 0.5 \\
&= 2.0\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\Rightarrow \boxed{T_{avg} = 2.0\,\text{ns}}$ **(3 marks)**

**(ii) AMAT *without* L2 (single-level formula):**

$$
T_{avg}^{\prime} = T_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_3 = 1 + (0.05)(100) = 6.0\,\text{ns}
$$

$\Rightarrow \boxed{T_{avg}^{\prime} = 6.0\,\text{ns}}$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Quantitative Benefit:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{6.0}{2.0} = 3.0\times
$$

$\Rightarrow$ Including the L2 cache yields a **3× reduction** in average memory access time, *despite* the fact that the L2 only catches 90% of L1 misses. This vividly demonstrates the *amplifying effect* of multi-level caching: a small mid-tier absorbs enough of the L1 misses to keep most requests *far away* from DRAM. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on Memory Hierarchy Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the L1 hit time in a multi-level AMAT.** The CPU *always* pays $T_1$, even on an L1 miss, because the L1 lookup is a prerequisite to knowing it missed. If you write $T_{avg} = H_1 T_1 + (1 - H_1) H_2 T_2 + \dots$, you *will* lose 2 marks. Use the canonical form $T_1 + (1 - H_1) T_2 + \dots$ instead.
> 2. **Confusing hit ratio with miss ratio.** $H$ and $M = 1 - H$ are *not* the same number. A 95% hit ratio means a 5% miss rate, not a 95% miss rate. The error inverts your answer by an order of magnitude.
> 3. **Mixing up time units.** $T_m$ must be in the *same* time unit as $T_h$. If $T_h = 1\,\text{ns}$ and $T_m = 50\,\text{ns}$, the formula is correct. If you convert $T_m$ to **µs** by mistake, the AMAT becomes meaningless. Always declare units *inside* the equation.
> 4. **Forgetting to label the formula with a full name.** The KTU rubric awards a *separate mark* for stating "Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) = $T_h + (1 - H) T_m$." A bare formula with no name costs you the *definition* mark.
> 5. **Omitting the "why" in descriptive questions.** When asked to "explain the memory hierarchy," do not *only* list the levels. Add the **purpose**, the **technology**, and **at least one numerical value** for each level. KTU examiners reserve 2 to 3 marks for "appropriate examples and numerical values."
> 6. **Not mentioning the word "locality" in the introduction.** Almost every memory hierarchy question implicitly tests the locality principle. A student who writes a 7-mark answer without once saying "locality of reference" will *always* lose a mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memory Hierarchy: Computer System Overview**

- [ ] **Memory Hierarchy** = layered storage in decreasing speed / increasing capacity.
- [ ] **The Iron Triangle:** You cannot have *all three* of {fast, large, cheap}. The hierarchy *combines* them to give the *illusion* of all three.
- [ ] **Tiers (Top→Bottom):** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → SSD → HDD → Tape.
- [ ] **Latency jumps (orders of magnitude):** Register ~1 cycle, L1 ~1 ns, DRAM ~60 ns, SSD ~50 µs, HDD ~5 ms, Tape ~seconds.
- [ ] **Two pillars of locality:** *Temporal* (re-use) and *Spatial* (neighbor use).
- [ ] **Temporal Locality →** keeps data resident in cache after a hit.
- [ ] **Spatial Locality →** entire cache *lines* (e.g., 64 B) are moved at once.
- [ ] **Master Formula (Single-Level):** $\;T_{avg} = T_h + (1 - H) T_m\;$.
- [ ] **Master Formula (Multi-Level):** $\;T_{avg} = T_1 + (1 - H_1) T_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) T_3 + \dots$.
- [ ] **Hit Ratio $H$** = Hits / Total Accesses. **Miss Ratio $M$** = $1 - H$.
- [ ] **Cost per Bit (Hierarchical):** $\;c_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum S_i c_i}{\sum S_i}\;$. The average cost of a hierarchy is dominated by the *cheapest* tier.
- [ ] **Speedup vs. Cache-less System:** $\;S = T_{flat} / T_{hier}\;$.
- [ ] **Speedup vs. Single-Level Cache:** $\;S = T_{avg}^{no\,L2} / T_{avg}^{with\,L2}\;$.
- [ ] **Inversion Trick:** To find the *required* hit ratio, substitute the desired $T_{avg}$ and solve for $H$.
- [ ] **Three principles:** (1) Locality, (2) Inclusion, (3) Coherence / Consistency.
- [ ] **Always state units** ($ns$, $\mu s$, $ms$, $/bit$) inside the equation.
- [ ] **Always include $T_1$** in any multi-level AMAT — even on a miss.
- [ ] **Memory Wall:** The ever-widening gap between CPU speed and DRAM speed — the very reason this hierarchy exists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Latency and Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Latency and Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standardized)

In modern processor architecture, **Latency** and **Bandwidth** are the two fundamental performance metrics that govern how quickly and how much data can be moved between the processor, its caches, memory, and I/O subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **LATENCY (L)**: The temporal delay experienced from the initiation of an operation (or transfer request) to the moment its first useful response is available at the destination. It is measured in units of time (ns, µs, ms, or clock cycles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BANDWIDTH (B)**: The maximum rate at which data can be transferred through an interconnect, channel, or functional unit over a sustained period. It is measured in units of data per unit time (MB/s, GB/s, Gb/s, or bytes/cycle).

| Metric | What it Answers | Unit | System Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | "How long until I get the first byte?" | Time (ns, cycles) | Worst-case single-operation delay |
| **Bandwidth** | "How much data can flow per second?" | Data/Time (GB/s) | Steady-state throughput capacity |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a highway connecting City A (CPU) to City B (Memory).**
>
> - **Latency** is the time taken by *one car* to travel from A to B. Even if the highway is wide, a single car still has to traverse the full distance. This is the **per-operation delay**.
> - **Bandwidth** is the *number of lanes* of the highway. More lanes = more cars can travel simultaneously = more data per second.
>
> **Key Insight**: A wider highway (higher bandwidth) does NOT make a single car reach faster; it only allows more cars to reach per second once the pipeline is full.

### Alternative Analogy — Water Pipeline
- **Latency** = Time for the *first drop of water* to exit a pipe after the tap is opened.
- **Bandwidth** = *Diameter of the pipe* — how many drops can flow per second once steady state is reached.

---

## 1.3 Why These Metrics Matter in Modern Processors

In modern multi-core processors, the **Memory Wall** problem arises because CPU clock speeds have grown faster than memory access times. Understanding latency and bandwidth is critical for:

1. Designing efficient **cache hierarchies** (L1, L2, L3, DRAM).
2. Evaluating **interconnects** like NoC (Network-on-Chip), QPI, Infinity Fabric.
3. Optimizing **pipelined execution units** and **superscalar issue**.
4. Sizing **prefetch buffers** and **store-forwarding paths**.
5. Balancing **DRAM channel width** vs **CAS latency** in memory subsystems.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Misconception**: Many students assume "lower latency always implies higher bandwidth." This is **FALSE**. Latency and bandwidth are *orthogonal* properties — a system can have high bandwidth with high latency (e.g., satellite internet) or low bandwidth with low latency (e.g., short serial bus). Designers must optimize both independently.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing Latency (L)

Latency is the sum of all *serial* delays along the data path. For a memory access operation, the total observed latency is:

$$
L_{total} = L_{request} + L_{queue} + L_{transmission} + L_{decode} + L_{response}
$$

Where each component is explained below:

- **$L_{request}$**: Time to issue the request on the bus/interconnect (encoder + arbitration).
- **$L_{queue}$**: Time spent waiting in buffers due to contention with other requests.
- **$L_{transmission}$**: Pure flight time = (Wire Length) / (Speed of Signal Propagation, ~$\tfrac{2}{3}c$ in copper).
- **$L_{decode}$**: Time for the destination to interpret the request and locate the data.
- **$L_{response}$**: Time to send the acknowledgment or data back.

For **DRAM**, latency is typically decomposed into:
$$
L_{DRAM} = t_{CAS} + t_{RCD} + t_{RP}
$$

Where $t_{CAS}$ = Column Access Strobe latency, $t_{RCD}$ = Row-to-Column Delay, $t_{RP}$ = Row Precharge time.

---

## 2.2 Decomposing Bandwidth (B)

Bandwidth is fundamentally the *channel capacity* and is governed by three factors:

$$
B = W \times f \times n
$$

- **$W$**: Width of the data channel (bits per transfer). E.g., DDR5 × 64-bit channel.
- **$f$**: Clock frequency or transfer rate (transfers per second).
- **$n$**: Number of transfers per clock (e.g., DDR = 2, QDR = 4, through pump-doubling).

For **DDR memory**, the effective bandwidth is:
$$
B_{DDR} = 2 \times W \times f_{bus}
$$

The factor of **2** accounts for *Double Data Rate* — data transferred on both rising and falling clock edges.

---

## 2.3 The Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP)** represents the *amount of data that must be "in flight"* in a channel at any instant to fully utilize that channel's bandwidth.

$$
BLP = L \times B
$$

**Why it matters:**
- It defines the **minimum buffer size** required in the receiver (e.g., CPU prefetch unit) to prevent starvation.
- It quantifies the **parallelism potential** of a channel — a high BLP means there is room for many independent requests to be issued.
- It is the *theoretical upper bound* on the data you can extract before the first byte even arrives (useful in pipelined/NoC systems).

---

## 2.4 Throughput vs. Latency

- **Latency** is per-operation (single-shot metric).
- **Throughput** is system-level (sustained rate).
- They are related by the **Pipeline Depth (d)** or **Concurrency (c)**:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{1}{L / c} = \frac{c}{L}
$$

In an *ideal pipeline of d stages*, each stage has latency $L_s$:

$$
\text{Pipeline Throughput} = \frac{1}{L_s} = \frac{d}{L_{total}}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every high-yield formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE in this topic. Pay special attention to units — **mark loss is common when units are not written explicitly**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Latency | $L = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | Time (ns, µs) | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | Basic Bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Data (bytes) / Time (s) | $B > 0$ |
| 3 | DDR Effective BW | $B = 2 \times W \times f$ | W = bits, f = Hz | Factor 2 = DDR; 4 = QDR |
| 4 | Bandwidth–Latency Product | $BLP = L \times B$ | Bytes (or bits) | Independent of workload |
| 5 | Pipeline Throughput | $Th = \dfrac{1}{t_{stage}}$ | Operations per cycle | Ideal (no hazards) |
| 6 | Pipeline Latency | $L_{pipe} = d \times t_{stage}$ | Time (cycles) | d = number of stages |
| 7 | Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{L_{old}}{L_{new}}$ | Dimensionless | $S \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Amdahl's Law | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | f = parallel fraction, n = speedup of parallel part | $0 \leq f \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Little's Law | $N = Th \times L$ | N = items in system | Stable system only |
| 10 | Effective BW with Overhead | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{D}{L + \dfrac{D}{B_{peak}}}$ | bytes/s | $B_{eff} \leq B_{peak}$ |
| 11 | DRAM Row Hit Latency | $L = t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$ | Time (ns) | Page must be open |
| 12 | DRAM Row Miss Latency | $L = t_{RP} + t_{RCD} + t_{CAS}$ | Time (ns) | Requires precharge |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Latency Metric | Bandwidth Metric | Critical Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU ↔ L1 Cache** | $\sim$ 1 ns | $\sim$ 100 GB/s | Low latency is paramount |
| **CPU ↔ DRAM** | $\sim$ 80–120 ns | $\sim$ 25–50 GB/s | Latency-bound for random access |
| **PCIe Gen5 ×16** | $\sim$ 100 ns | $\sim$ 64 GB/s | GPU/SSD communication |
| **DDR5-6400** | CL = 32 cycles | $\sim$ 51.2 GB/s | CL × clock period = absolute latency |
| **NoC (Mesh 8×8)** | Hop count × router delay | Bisection BW | Topology choice |
| **NVMe SSD** | $\sim$ 50 µs (read) | $\sim$ 7 GB/s (sequential) | Latency-bound for random I/O |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth from First Principles

### Setup
Consider a memory channel that transfers a block of $D$ bytes. The channel has a peak (raw) bandwidth $B_{peak}$ and a fixed per-access overhead latency $L_o$ (such as CAS latency or request setup).

**Step 1:** The pure data transfer time (without overhead) is:
$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{B_{peak}}
$$

> **Logic**: If $B_{peak}$ is the rate (bytes/second), then to transfer $D$ bytes we divide the size by the rate.

**Step 2:** The total observed time to complete one transfer including overhead is:
$$
T_{total} = L_o + \frac{D}{B_{peak}}
$$

> **Logic**: The overhead $L_o$ is incurred *once per access*, independent of the block size $D$. It is purely additive (serial) on the critical path.

**Step 3:** The **effective bandwidth** observed by the user is:
$$
B_{eff} = \frac{D}{T_{total}} = \frac{D}{L_o + \dfrac{D}{B_{peak}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $B_{peak}$ to simplify:
$$
B_{eff} = \frac{D \cdot B_{peak}}{B_{peak} \cdot L_o + D}
$$

> **Logic**: This is a rational function of $D$. As $D \to \infty$, the overhead $L_o$ becomes negligible.

**Step 5:** Take the limit as $D \to \infty$ (large block transfer):
$$
\lim_{D \to \infty} B_{eff} = \lim_{D \to \infty} \frac{D \cdot B_{peak}}{B_{peak} \cdot L_o + D} = B_{peak}
$$

> **Logic**: For very large transfers, effective bandwidth approaches peak bandwidth. This is why **streaming large blocks** is so much faster than random small accesses.

**Step 6:** Take the limit as $D \to 0$ (tiny transfer):
$$
\lim_{D \to 0} B_{eff} = \frac{0}{B_{peak} \cdot L_o} = 0
$$

> **Logic**: For very small transfers, the overhead dominates and effective bandwidth collapses. This is the **random-access bottleneck** — the curse of small-granularity workloads.

**Step 7:** The "**crossover block size**" $D_{50}$ where $B_{eff} = \tfrac{1}{2} B_{peak}$ is found by setting:
$$
\frac{D \cdot B_{peak}}{B_{peak} \cdot L_o + D} = \frac{B_{peak}}{2}
$$

Solving:
$$
2D = B_{peak} \cdot L_o + D \implies D_{50} = B_{peak} \cdot L_o = BLP
$$

> **Logic**: The crossover occurs exactly at the **Bandwidth–Latency Product**. This is a beautiful result: BLP is the *minimum* block size needed to extract half the peak bandwidth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law and Bandwidth Parallelism

### Setup
A fraction $f$ of a program can benefit from a bandwidth-enhancing resource (e.g., wider memory bus, more channels). The remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is serial (e.g., instruction control logic). The new resource provides speedup $n$ for the parallel fraction.

**Step 1:** Let the original execution time be $T = 1$ (normalized).

**Step 2:** The serial portion takes time $(1-f) \cdot T = (1-f)$.

**Step 3:** The parallel portion takes time $\tfrac{f}{n} \cdot T = \tfrac{f}{n}$ on the enhanced resource.

**Step 4:** Total new execution time:
$$
T_{new} = (1-f) + \frac{f}{n}
$$

**Step 5:** Speedup ratio:
$$
S = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
$$

**Step 6:** Maximum possible speedup as $n \to \infty$ (infinite bandwidth):
$$
S_{max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

> **Logic**: This is Amdahl's fundamental bound. **No matter how much bandwidth you add, the serial portion $(1-f)$ caps the speedup.** This is the *Bandwidth Diminishing Returns Theorem*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pipeline Throughput and Latency Relationship

### Setup
Consider a 5-stage RISC pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) with clock period $t_{clk} = 2$ ns. Each stage has latency equal to one clock period.

**Step 1:** Pipeline latency for a *single* instruction to complete:
$$
L_{single} = 5 \times t_{clk} = 5 \times 2\;\text{ns} = 10\;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Ideal pipeline throughput (instructions completed per second, assuming no hazards):
$$
Th = \frac{1}{t_{clk}} = \frac{1}{2\;\text{ns}} = 5 \times 10^{8}\;\text{instructions/sec} = 500\;\text{MIPS}
$$

**Step 3:** For $k$ instructions, total time (ignoring hazards) is:
$$
T_k = L_{single} + (k-1) \times t_{clk} = (k + 4) \times t_{clk}
$$

**Step 4:** Average throughput for $k$ instructions:
$$
Th_{avg} = \frac{k}{T_k} = \frac{k}{(k+4) \times t_{clk}}
$$

**Step 5:** As $k \to \infty$, the pipeline drain time becomes negligible:
$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} Th_{avg} = \frac{1}{t_{clk}}
$$

> **Logic**: This shows the central tension: **deeper pipelines reduce $t_{clk}$ (improving throughput) but increase $L_{single}$ (hurting latency for short code)**. This is the *latency-throughput trade-off* central to modern CPU design (e.g., Intel's "Skylake" used deeper pipelines than "Pentium 4").

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem Solving Quick Reference

| Given | Find | Key Equation | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D, T$ | $B$ | $B = D/T$ | Forgetting unit conversion (bits vs bytes) |
| $B, L$ | BLP | $BLP = L \cdot B$ | Mixing seconds with ms |
| $L, B_{peak}$ | $B_{eff}$ | $B_{eff} = D/(L + D/B_{peak})$ | Ignoring the $L$ term for small $D$ |
| $f, n$ | Speedup | $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/n]$ | Using $S = n$ instead of Amdahl's formula |
| $d, t_{clk}$ | Pipeline $L$ | $L = d \cdot t_{clk}$ | Forgetting drain cycles |
| $N, Th$ | $L$ | $L = N/Th$ (Little's Law) | Using non-steady-state values |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Latency vs. Bandwidth — Conceptual Flow Diagram

This diagram illustrates how latency and bandwidth describe different aspects of the same data path. Latency is a *time-axis* metric (single operation), while bandwidth is a *rate-axis* metric (sustained flow).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU_A["CPU CORE"] -->|"REQUEST"| BUS_CTRL["BUS ARBITER"]
    BUS_CTRL -->|"FLIGHT TIME"| MEM["DRAM BANK"]
    MEM -->|"RESPONSE"| CPU_A

    L_META["LATENCY L"] -.->|"measures"| CPU_A
    B_META["BANDWIDTH B"] -.->|"measures"| BUS_CTRL
    BLP_META["BLP = L x B"] -.->|"relates"| MEM

    classDef source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dest fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef metric fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    class CPU_A source
    class MEM dest
    class L_META,B_META,BLP_META metric
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The solid arrows show the **physical data path** (Request → Bus → DRAM → Response).
- The dashed arrows show **what each metric measures**: Latency measures the time experienced at the source; Bandwidth measures the throughput capacity of the bus; BLP bridges them.

---

## 4.2 Memory Hierarchy — Latency/Bandwidth Trade-off

This block diagram maps how latency *increases* and bandwidth *decreases* as we move down the memory hierarchy (the central trade-off in memory system design).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REG["REGISTER FILE
LATENCY 1 cycle
BW 8 to 16 bytes per cycle"]
    L1["L1 CACHE 32 KB
LATENCY 4 cycles
BW 32 bytes per cycle"]
    L2["L2 CACHE 512 KB
LATENCY 12 cycles
BW 32 bytes per cycle"]
    L3["L3 CACHE 8 MB
LATENCY 40 cycles
BW 32 bytes per cycle"]
    DRAM["MAIN DRAM 16 GB
LATENCY 200 cycles
BW 8 to 16 bytes per cycle"]
    SSD["NVMe SSD 1 TB
LATENCY 500000 cycles
BW 1 GB per sec"]
    NET["REMOTE NETWORK
LATENCY 10M cycles
BW 100 MB per sec"]

    REG --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> DRAM --> SSD --> NET

    TRADE["TRADE OFF
L increases
B decreases
cost per byte decreases
capacity increases"]
    TRADE -.-> REG
    TRADE -.-> NET
```

**Engineering Takeaway:** As we move from registers to remote network, **latency increases by ~7 orders of magnitude**, while **bandwidth per access decreases dramatically**. The CPU architect's job is to make the *common case* hit the fastest level.

---

## 4.3 Pipeline Latency vs. Throughput Visualization

This diagram shows how deeper pipelines reduce per-stage latency (improving throughput) but increase total single-instruction latency.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P3["3 STAGE PIPELINE LATENCY 6 cycles THROUGHPUT 1 per 2 cycles"]
        direction LR
        P3_S1["S1"] --> P3_S2["S2"] --> P3_S3["S3"]
    end

    subgraph P6["6 STAGE PIPELINE LATENCY 6 cycles THROUGHPUT 1 per 1 cycle"]
        direction LR
        P6_S1["S1"] --> P6_S2["S2"] --> P6_S3["S3"] --> P6_S4["S4"] --> P6_S5["S5"] --> P6_S6["S6"]
    end

    P3 --> P6
```

> **Reading**: Both pipelines have the **same total latency (6 cycles)** for one instruction. But the 6-stage pipeline has **6× the throughput** because each stage is 1 cycle instead of 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define latency and bandwidth in the context of modern processor architecture. How are they fundamentally different metrics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Latency (L)** is the time elapsed from the initiation of a single operation (such as a memory read or instruction issue) until the first piece of valid output is available at the destination. It is measured in absolute time units (nanoseconds, microseconds) or clock cycles. Latency is a *per-operation* metric and represents the worst-case delay a requester may experience.

**Bandwidth (B)** is the maximum sustained rate at which data can be moved through an interconnect, channel, or processing unit. It is measured in units of data per unit time (e.g., GB/s) and represents the *throughput capacity* of a system.

**Fundamental Difference:** Latency measures **time** for *one* operation (single-shot), while bandwidth measures **rate** of *many* operations (steady-state). They are **orthogonal**: one can have high bandwidth with high latency (satellite link) or low bandwidth with low latency (short on-chip bus). Optimizing for one does not automatically optimize the other.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for orthogonal difference.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`
**What is the Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP)? State its significance in modern processor design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP)** is defined as:
$$
BLP = L \times B
$$

where $L$ is the channel latency (in seconds) and $B$ is the channel bandwidth (in bytes/second). The result is expressed in bytes (or bits) and represents the **amount of data "in flight"** through the channel at any given instant under steady-state operation.

**Significance in Modern Processor Design:**
1. **Buffer Sizing**: It determines the minimum size of prefetch buffers, store queues, and reorder buffers needed to fully utilize the channel without stalls.
2. **Parallelism Potential**: A high BLP means many independent requests can be issued, enabling effective use of pipelined and out-of-order execution.
3. **Workload Optimization**: It informs programmers and compilers about the optimal **granularity** of data accesses — blocks smaller than BLP underutilize bandwidth.
4. **Prefetch Distance**: It defines the prefetch distance in cache hierarchies.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for significance points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`**

**(a) Explain in detail the concepts of latency and bandwidth with suitable examples from processor architecture. Discuss the key trade-offs designers face when optimizing them. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Latency — Definition and Examples:**
Latency is the temporal delay between initiating an operation and receiving its result. In a processor, latency manifests at multiple levels:
- **Instruction Latency**: Number of cycles from issue to result-ready (e.g., FP addition = 4–6 cycles).
- **Cache Latency**: L1 hit = ~1 ns, L2 hit = ~5 ns, DRAM access = ~80–100 ns.
- **Interconnect Latency**: Time for a packet to traverse a NoC router (typically 1–3 cycles per hop).

**2. Bandwidth — Definition and Examples:**
Bandwidth is the data transfer rate. Examples:
- DDR5-6400 memory: ~51.2 GB/s across a 64-bit channel.
- PCIe 5.0 ×16: ~64 GB/s for GPU/CPU communication.
- L1-to-CPU path: ~100 GB/s aggregate.

**3. Key Trade-offs:**
- **Latency vs. Throughput**: Deeper pipelines lower clock period (better throughput) but increase per-instruction latency. Example: Intel Pentium 4 (31-stage pipeline, ~1.5 GHz) vs. Intel Skylake (14-stage, ~5 GHz).
- **Bandwidth vs. Cost**: Wider buses (more pins, more channels) increase bandwidth but increase cost and power.
- **Latency vs. Bandwidth**: Prefetching increases effective bandwidth but can increase latency for the prefetched lines if they are never used (cache pollution).
- **DRAM Timing Trade-off**: Lowering $t_{CAS}$ reduces latency but restricts maximum stable frequency (lower bandwidth).
- **Power-Performance**: Aggressive prefetching (high bandwidth) burns dynamic power without proportional latency benefit.

**4. Conclusion:**
Modern processors balance both metrics using hierarchical caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order execution — none of which is a single-metric solution.

---

**(b) A memory system has a CAS latency of 16 cycles, clock frequency of 3.2 GHz, and a 64-bit wide DDR channel. Calculate: (i) the absolute DRAM access latency, (ii) the peak bandwidth, (iii) the bandwidth–latency product in bytes. If the channel is upgraded to DDR5 with same frequency but 128-bit width, recalculate (ii) and (iii). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- CAS latency: $N_{CAS} = 16$ cycles
- Clock frequency: $f = 3.2$ GHz $= 3.2 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Data width: $W = 64$ bits $= 8$ bytes
- DDR (Double Data Rate): 2 transfers per clock

**Part (i) — Absolute DRAM Access Latency:**
$$
L = \frac{N_{CAS}}{f} = \frac{16}{3.2 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{s} = 5\;\text{ns}
$$

**Part (ii) — Peak Bandwidth (DDR):**
$$
B = 2 \times W \times f = 2 \times 8\;\text{bytes} \times 3.2 \times 10^9\;\text{Hz} = 51.2 \times 10^9\;\text{bytes/s} = 51.2\;\text{GB/s}
$$

**Part (iii) — Bandwidth–Latency Product:**
$$
BLP = L \times B = (5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (51.2 \times 10^9) = 256\;\text{bytes}
$$

**DDR5 Upgrade — New Width: $W' = 128$ bits $= 16$ bytes**

**New Bandwidth:**
$$
B' = 2 \times 16 \times 3.2 \times 10^9 = 102.4\;\text{GB/s}
$$

**New BLP:**
$$
BLP' = (5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (102.4 \times 10^9) = 512\;\text{bytes}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Original DDR | Upgraded DDR5 |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | 5 ns | 5 ns |
| Bandwidth | 51.2 GB/s | 102.4 GB/s |
| BLP | 256 B | 512 B |

**Conclusion:** Doubling the channel width doubled both bandwidth and BLP without affecting latency — a clean demonstration that latency and bandwidth can be tuned independently.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`**

**(a) With suitable diagrams, explain the Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP). How does BLP influence the design of prefetch buffers and the choice of cache line size in modern processors? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of BLP:**
The Bandwidth–Latency Product (BLP) is defined as:
$$
BLP = L \times B
$$

where $L$ is the round-trip latency of the channel and $B$ is the peak bandwidth.

**Diagram Explanation:**

The BLP can be visualized as the **rectangle under the bandwidth curve** during the latency window:
- On a time-vs-data graph, draw a horizontal line at height $B$.
- Mark a window of width $L$ along the time axis.
- The **area of this rectangle** = $L \times B$ = total bytes that can be transmitted in one latency window.

**2. Influence on Prefetch Buffer Design:**
A prefetch buffer must be at least BLP-sized to keep the channel saturated. If smaller, the channel will stall (drain) waiting for new requests.

**Rule of Thumb:**
$$
\text{Prefetch Buffer Size} \geq BLP
$$

**Example:** For DDR5 with $L = 80$ ns, $B = 50$ GB/s:
$$
BLP = 80 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^9 = 4000\;\text{bytes} \approx 4\;\text{KB}
$$
A prefetch buffer of less than 4 KB will throttle the channel.

**3. Influence on Cache Line Size:**
Cache line size should be a multiple of (or comparable to) BLP to ensure efficient use of fetched data. Modern processors use **64-byte cache lines** because:
- BLP of L1↔L2 path is ~32–64 bytes.
- Larger lines reduce tag overhead but increase pollution risk.

**4. Practical Design Insight:**
If BLP >> cache line size, the system is *bandwidth-rich but latency-bound* → use deeper prefetching and out-of-order execution. If BLP << cache line size, the system is *latency-rich but bandwidth-bound* → use wider buses and compression.

---

**(b) Consider a 6-stage pipelined processor with clock period 250 ps. Each instruction transfers 64 bytes of data. Calculate: (i) pipeline latency per instruction, (ii) ideal throughput, (iii) effective bandwidth per instruction, (iv) Bandwidth–Latency Product. If we deepen the pipeline to 10 stages with clock period 150 ps, recalculate all four metrics and comment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Configuration 1: 6-stage pipeline, $t_{clk} = 250$ ps**

**(i) Pipeline Latency (single instruction):**
$$
L_1 = 6 \times 250\;\text{ps} = 1500\;\text{ps} = 1.5\;\text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Ideal Throughput:**
$$
Th_1 = \frac{1}{t_{clk}} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{s}} = 4 \times 10^{9}\;\text{inst/s} = 4\;\text{GIPS}
$$

**(iii) Effective Bandwidth per Instruction:**
$$
B_1 = \frac{D}{L_1} = \frac{64\;\text{bytes}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{s}} = 42.67\;\text{GB/s}
$$

**(iv) Bandwidth–Latency Product:**
$$
BLP_1 = L_1 \times B_1 = (1.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (42.67 \times 10^9) = 64\;\text{bytes}
$$

**Configuration 2: 10-stage pipeline, $t_{clk} = 150$ ps**

**(i) Pipeline Latency:**
$$
L_2 = 10 \times 150\;\text{ps} = 1500\;\text{ps} = 1.5\;\text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Ideal Throughput:**
$$
Th_2 = \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.67 \times 10^{9}\;\text{inst/s} \approx 6.67\;\text{GIPS}
$$

**(iii) Effective Bandwidth per Instruction:**
$$
B_2 = \frac{64}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 42.67\;\text{GB/s}
$$

**(iv) Bandwidth–Latency Product:**
$$
BLP_2 = (1.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (42.67 \times 10^9) = 64\;\text{bytes}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | 6-Stage (250 ps) | 10-Stage (150 ps) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | 1.5 ns | 1.5 ns | No change |
| Throughput | 4 GIPS | 6.67 GIPS | ↑ 1.67× |
| Bandwidth | 42.67 GB/s | 42.67 GB/s | No change |
| BLP | 64 B | 64 B | No change |

**Commentary:**
Deepening the pipeline from 6 to 10 stages **increased throughput by 67%** (from 4 to 6.67 GIPS) without increasing single-instruction latency. This is because the total pipeline latency ($d \times t_{clk}$) remained constant at 1.5 ns in both cases. Bandwidth and BLP are unchanged because they depend on the *total* latency, not the per-stage latency. This illustrates the **latency-throughput decoupling principle** in deep pipelines — the central motivation for high-frequency designs in modern CPUs (e.g., Apple M-series, Intel Core Ultra).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Latency/Bandwidth Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Mixing bits and bytes, or ns and µs. Always write units explicitly. *Lost marks in Dec 2022 ESE: ~15% of students.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the DDR "2×" Factor**: When computing DRAM bandwidth, students often use $B = W \times f$ instead of $B = 2 \times W \times f$. This halves your computed bandwidth — *a guaranteed 1-mark loss*.
>
> 3. **Confusing Latency with Response Time**: Latency is *first-byte arrival*; response time may include queueing. Don't conflate them unless the problem specifies.
>
> 4. **Amdahl's Law Misapplication**: Some students write $S = n$ when only a fraction $f$ is parallelizable. Always use $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/n]$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "Comment" Section**: KTU award 1–2 marks for the qualitative conclusion at the end of a numerical problem. Never end with a number alone — *always interpret the result*.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing Diagrams in Part B**: KTU valuation policy (2024 Scheme) awards **1 free mark** for a relevant labeled diagram in 14-mark answers on architecture topics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Latency and Bandwidth**

- [x] **Latency = time for ONE operation** (single-shot, per-access delay).
- [x] **Bandwidth = rate of data transfer** (sustained throughput).
- [x] They are **orthogonal** — optimizing one does NOT guarantee the other.
- [x] **BLP = L × B** = bytes in flight = minimum prefetch buffer size.
- [x] **DDR Bandwidth Formula**: $B = 2 \times W \times f$ (factor 2 for DDR, 4 for QDR).
- [x] **DRAM Latency = $t_{CAS} + t_{RCD} + t_{RP}$**.
- [x] **Pipeline Throughput = $1/t_{clk}$** (ideal, no hazards).
- [x] **Pipeline Latency = $d \times t_{clk}$** for single instruction.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/n]$ — bandwidth/parallelism has diminishing returns.
- [x] **Little's Law**: $N = Th \times L$ (steady-state systems only).
- [x] **Deeper pipeline → higher throughput, same or higher latency**.
- [x] **Crossover block size for half-peak BW = BLP**.
- [x] **Prefetch buffer size ≥ BLP** to prevent channel starvation.
- [x] **Cache line size ≈ BLP** for optimal utilization.
- [x] **Hierarchical memory trade-off**: ↑ latency, ↓ bandwidth as we go from register → SSD → network.
- [x] **Common confusion**: Lower CL does NOT always mean lower latency — must multiply by clock period.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Hierarchy: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Memory Hierarchy: Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Hierarchy** is a structural organization of computer storage elements arranged in a tiered pyramid based on **access speed**, **cost per bit**, and **storage capacity**, designed to provide the CPU with data at the fastest possible rate while maintaining economic feasibility. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Modern Processor Architecture*, memory hierarchy is defined as a **multi-level storage system** that exploits the **principle of locality** to bridge the ever-widening **performance gap** (often called the *memory wall*) between the high-speed Central Processing Unit (CPU) and the slower, bulk-capacity main memory (DRAM). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates understanding the *need* for hierarchy, the *locality principle* (Temporal & Spatial), and the *quantitative metrics* like Average Memory Access Time (AMAT), Hit Ratio, and Miss Penalty. ### 1.2 The Three Defining Properties of the Hierarchy | Property | Top of Pyramid (Registers/Cache) | Bottom of Pyramid (Magnetic/SSD) | |---|---|---| | **Speed** | ~1 ns (Fastest) | ~5,000,000 ns (Slowest) | | **Cost per bit** | **High** (₹) | **Low** (Paisa) | | **Capacity** | **Bytes – Kilobytes** | **Terabytes** | | **Volatility** | Volatile (SRAM) | Non-Volatile (Disk/SSD) | | **Proximity to CPU** | Closest | Farthest | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Architect's Desk Imagine you are an **architect drafting a skyscraper design**: - **The Drafting Table (Registers/L1 Cache):** Only the single blueprint sheet you are actively drawing on is kept here. You can glance at any line in **less than a second**. - **The Filing Cabinet Beside You (L2/L3 Cache):** Recently referenced reference books, codes, and past similar design sheets. Takes a few seconds to pull out. - **The Office Library Down the Hall (Main Memory – DRAM):** Every standard textbook, resource manual, and code book. Takes a **few minutes of walking** to retrieve. - **The National Archive in Delhi (Magnetic Disk / SSD / Cloud):** Rarely accessed historical records. Requires **days/weeks** of formal requisition. **The core insight:** You *anticipate* that 90% of your work today will reference only a handful of documents, so you keep those *closer*. This is exactly what the memory hierarchy does for a CPU. ### 1.4 The Memory Wall Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Moore's Law** allowed transistor counts to double every ~18 months, exponentially increasing CPU clock speeds. However, **DRAM access latency has historically improved by only ~7% per year**. This divergence is known as the **Memory Wall**. Because of this wall, a CPU spending even **1% of its cycles stalling** for memory can devastate overall throughput. Memory hierarchy is the architectural answer to this physics-constrained bottleneck. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fundamental "Why" — The Von Neumann Bottleneck In the classical **Von Neumann architecture**, the CPU and Memory are connected by a single bus. The speed of this bus becomes the **bottleneck** because: 1. CPU executes an instruction. 2. Fetches data/instruction from memory. 3. Memory is **100x to 1000x slower** than the CPU clock cycle. The **only scalable solution** is to ensure that the CPU *rarely* has to reach the slow main memory — and that is achieved by inserting **intermediate, smaller, faster memories** between the CPU and DRAM. ### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Memory Hierarchy Design The hierarchy is engineered around three foundational pillars: **Pillar 1: Principle of Locality** - **Temporal Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.* (e.g., loop counters, instruction re-fetch in a loop) - **Spatial Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, locations near it are likely to be referenced soon.* (e.g., sequential instruction execution, array traversal) **Pillar 2: Inclusion Property** - Ideally, all data in a higher (faster) level must also be present in all lower (slower) levels. This guarantees that a *hit* at the top means data is found, and a *miss* triggers a search in the next level. **Pillar 3: Coherence Protocols (Foundation for Multi-core)** - When multiple caches hold copies of the same data, the system must enforce **cache coherence** (typically using MESI/MOESI protocols), ensuring every core sees a *consistent* view of memory. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **board-exam critical**. Memorize them verbatim. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hit Rate** | $H = \dfrac{\text{Number of Hits}}{\text{Total CPU Accesses}}$ | Fraction of accesses served by this memory level | | 2 | **Miss Rate** | $M = 1 - H$ | Fraction of accesses that *fall through* to next level | | 3 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + M \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$ | Average time per memory access including miss penalty | | 4 | **Multi-level AMAT (Generalized)** | $T_{\text{access}} = T_{L1} + M_{L1} \cdot (T_{L2} + M_{L2} \cdot (T_{L3} + \dots))$ | Recursive extension to N levels | | 5 | **Miss Penalty** | $T_{\text{miss}} = T_{\text{lower}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$ | Time to access lower level + transfer block back | | 6 | **Access Time Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{T_{\text{slow}}}{T_{\text{fast}}}$ | Speedup factor between consecutive levels | | 7 | **Average Access Cost** | $C_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \cdot S_i)}{\sum S_i}$ | Weighted cost per bit across all levels | | 8 | **Throughput Improvement** | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{no-cache}}}{T_{\text{hierarchy}}}$ | Ratio of un-optimized to optimized access time | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Pitfall Note:** In markdown tables, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is written as `\vert x \vert` (not `|x|`), and conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$ is written as `P(A \mid B)` to avoid breaking KTU's strict markdown rendering rules. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smartphone SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon):** Use 3-level on-die caches to feed Neural Processing Units (NPUs) and GPUs. - **Data Centers (AWS/GCP):** Use memory hierarchies spanning **Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → NVMe SSD → Network Storage**, where the *cloud* acts as the bottom-most "archive." - **Embedded IoT Devices:** Often use a *flat* memory model with no cache to save power, opting instead for **Tightly Coupled Memory (TCM)**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) We derive the single-level AMAT formula from first principles, starting with the definition of *expected access time*. **Step 1 — Define the Random Variable** Let $T$ be a random variable representing the time taken for one memory access. With probability $H$ (the hit rate), the access is served by the fast memory, taking $T_{\text{hit}}$ time. With probability $M = 1 - H$ (the miss rate), the access falls through, taking $T_{\text{miss}}$ time. **Step 2 — Express Expected Value** By definition, the expected (average) value of a discrete random variable is the probability-weighted sum of all outcomes: $$E[T] = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot t_i$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Two Outcomes** There are exactly two outcomes: *hit* and *miss*. Substituting: $$E[T] = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + M \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$$ **Step 4 — Replace Miss Probability** Since $H + M = 1$ (they are complementary events), $M = 1 - H$: $$\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$$ **Step 5 — Expand and Simplify** Distribute the terms: $$\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + T_{\text{miss}} - H \cdot T_{\text{miss}}$$ Group the $H$ terms together: $$\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{miss}} - H \cdot (T_{\text{miss}} - T_{\text{hit}})$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation** The AMAT equals the **miss time**, *reduced* by the savings achieved from the fraction $H$ of accesses that hit early. The product $H \cdot (T_{\text{miss}} - T_{\text{hit}})$ is the **time saved by the cache**. --- ### 3.2 Extension: Two-Level Hierarchy AMAT Derivation For a 2-level system (L1 cache + Main Memory), the access time has **three** outcomes: **Outcome A:** Hit in L1 → time = $T_{L1}$ **Outcome B:** Miss in L1, Hit in L2 → time = $T_{L1} + T_{L2}$ (assume L2 access is the penalty) **Outcome C:** Miss in both, go to RAM → time = $T_{L1} + T_{L2} + T_{\text{RAM}}$ Let: - $H_1$ = Hit rate of L1 - $M_1 = 1 - H_1$ = Miss rate of L1 - $H_2$ = Hit rate of L2 (given we are looking in L2) - $M_2 = 1 - H_2$ = Miss rate of L2 **Compute the weighted sum:** $$\text{AMAT}_{2\text{-level}} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot H_2 \cdot (T_{L1} + T_{L2}) + M_1 \cdot M_2 \cdot (T_{L1} + T_{L2} + T_{\text{RAM}})$$ **Factor out common access time of L1:** $$\text{AMAT}_{2\text{-level}} = T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot \left[ H_2 \cdot T_{L2} + M_2 \cdot (T_{L2} + T_{\text{RAM}}) \right]$$ **Generalize to N levels (Recursive form):** $$T_{\text{effective}} = T_{L1} + M_{L1} \cdot T_{L2} + M_{L1} \cdot M_{L2} \cdot T_{L3} + \dots + \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N-1} M_i \right) \cdot T_{\text{main}}$$ This recursive form is the **foundation** for all multi-level cache analysis questions in KTU exams. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Standard) > **Problem:** A system has an L1 cache with $H_1 = 0.90$, $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, and a main memory with $T_{\text{RAM}} = 100$ ns. Calculate AMAT. **Solution:** $$\text{AMAT} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{\text{RAM}}$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 0.90 \cdot 1 + 0.10 \cdot 100$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 0.9 + 10 = 10.9 \text{ ns}$$ **Insight:** A 10% miss rate slowed the system down by a factor of **10.9x** compared to the raw L1 speed. > **Extended Problem:** Insert an L2 cache with $H_2 = 0.95$ (local), $T_{L2} = 10$ ns. $$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.10 \cdot \left[ 0.95 \cdot 10 + 0.05 \cdot 100 \right]$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.10 \cdot [9.5 + 5] = 1 + 0.10 \cdot 14.5$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 1.45 = 2.45 \text{ ns}$$ **Insight:** Adding L2 cut the effective access time by **77.5%**, demonstrating the *exponential payoff* of hierarchical design. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Memory Level | Scenario | Recommended Placement | Justification | |---|---|---| | Frequently accessed loop variables | **Registers / L1** | Highest temporal locality | | Sequential array traversal | **L1 with prefetch** | Highest spatial locality | | Operating system kernel pages | **L2 / L3** | Moderate reuse, shared across cores | | Cold-start application data | **Main DRAM** | First-time access only | | Log files / historical backups | **SSD / HDD** | Sequential, bulk, low-priority | | Disaster-recovery snapshots | **Cloud / Tape Archive** | Rare access, latency-tolerant | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Mermaid Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB A0["CPU REGISTERS
Size: Bits to Bytes
Latency: 0.3 to 1 ns
Cost: Highest"] A1["L1 CACHE
Size: 16 to 64 KB
Latency: 1 to 3 ns
Cost: Very High"] A2["L2 CACHE
Size: 256 KB to 1 MB
Latency: 5 to 12 ns
Cost: High"] A3["L3 CACHE SHARED
Size: 4 to 64 MB
Latency: 20 to 40 ns
Cost: Moderate"] A4["MAIN MEMORY DRAM
Size: 8 to 128 GB
Latency: 80 to 200 ns
Cost: Low per GB"] A5["NVMe SSD STORAGE
Size: 256 GB to 4 TB
Latency: 50 to 150 us
Cost: Very Low"] A6["HDD MAGNETIC DISK
Size: 1 to 20 TB
Latency: 5 to 15 ms
Cost: Lowest"] A7["CLOUD / TAPE ARCHIVE
Size: Petabytes
Latency: Seconds to Days
Cost: Negligible per GB"] A0 ==> A1 A1 ==> A2 A2 ==> A3 A3 ==> A4 A4 ==> A5 A5 ==> A6 A6 ==> A7 A0 -.->|"INCLUSION PROPERTY:
Lower levels hold everything above"| A7 ``` ### 4.2 Hit / Miss Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["CPU Issues
Memory Request"] --> S2{"Is Data in
L1 Cache?"} S2 -- "YES HIT" --> S3["Return Data to CPU
Time = T_L1"] S2 -- "NO MISS" --> S4{"Is Data in
L2 Cache?"} S4 -- "YES" --> S5["Transfer to L1
Return to CPU
Time = T_L1 + T_L2"] S4 -- "NO" --> S6{"Is Data in
Main Memory?"} S6 -- "YES" --> S7["Transfer Block Up
Return to CPU
Time = T_L1 + T_L2 + T_RAM"] S6 -- "NO PAGE FAULT" --> S8["Fetch from Disk
Time = T_L1 + T_L2 + T_RAM + T_DISK"] S3 --> S9["CPU Continues Execution"] S5 --> S9 S7 --> S9 S8 --> S9 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB B1["CPU CORE
Fetch / Decode / Execute"] B2["L1 INSTRUCTION CACHE I-CACHE"] B3["L1 DATA CACHE D-CACHE"] B4["L2 UNIFIED CACHE
Private per Core"] B5["L3 LAST LEVEL CACHE LLC
Shared Across Cores"] B6["MEMORY CONTROLLER
DDR4 / DDR5 / HBM"] B7["MAIN DRAM BANK
Channels and Ranks"] B8["NVMe CONTROLLER
PCIe Lanes"] B9["SSD NAND FLASH ARRAYS"] B10["FILE SYSTEM VFS LAYER"] B11["NETWORK STACK
NFS or S3"] B1 <--> B2 B1 <--> B3 B2 --> B4 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6 B6 --> B7 B7 --> B8 B8 --> B9 B9 --> B10 B10 --> B11 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The downward arrows in Block Diagram 4.3 represent the **miss path** (data fetched from a slower level). The *coherence protocol* operates laterally between L2 caches of different cores via the shared L3 bus. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Memory Hierarchy. List its key characteristics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Memory hierarchy is the organization of storage elements in a computer system into a hierarchy based on access speed, cost, and capacity, such that the fastest, smallest, and most expensive memory is closest to the CPU and the slowest, largest, and cheapest is farthest away. *Key characteristics (any 2 for 3 marks):* 1. **Decreasing cost per bit** as we move down the hierarchy. 2. **Increasing capacity** as we move down the hierarchy. 3. **Increasing access time** as we move down the hierarchy. 4. **Decreasing frequency of access** by the CPU as we move down. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between Temporal Locality and Spatial Locality with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Temporal Locality | Spatial Locality | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again soon | Data near a recently accessed address is likely to be accessed soon | | **Cause** | Loops, re-execution of code, repeated variable use | Sequential execution, array traversal, instruction fetch pipeline | | **Example** | `sum = sum + arr[i]` — `sum` is accessed in every loop iteration | `arr[0]`, `arr[1]`, `arr[2]` — consecutive array elements are accessed sequentially | | **Exploited by** | Cache line retention, register allocation | Cache block (line) prefetching, wider memory buses | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Memory Hierarchy in detail. Discuss the different levels of memory hierarchy with their access times, capacities, and the technology used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A computer system has a single-level cache with the following specifications: - Cache access time: $T_{L1} = 5$ ns - Main memory access time: $T_{\text{RAM}} = 200$ ns - Cache hit rate: $H_1 = 0.92$ Calculate: (i) The miss rate. (ii) The Average Memory Access Time (AMAT). (iii) The speedup achieved by introducing the cache, compared to a system with no cache. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks The Memory Hierarchy is a tiered arrangement of storage elements in a computer system, designed to balance the trade-off between **speed**, **cost**, and **capacity**. The fundamental motivation is the *Memory Wall* — the growing performance gap between the CPU and DRAM. **Levels of Memory Hierarchy (descending from CPU):** 1. **CPU Registers** — 32 to 64 registers × 64 bits; access time ≈ **0.3–1 ns**; technology = flip-flops on the CPU die; managed by compiler. 2. **L1 Cache (Level 1)** — Size 16 KB to 64 KB per core; access time ≈ **1–3 ns**; technology = **SRAM** (6 transistors per bit); split into I-cache and D-cache (Harvard architecture inside the core). 3. **L2 Cache** — Size 256 KB to 1 MB per core; access time ≈ **5–12 ns**; technology = SRAM; unified (instruction + data). 4. **L3 Cache (Last Level Cache, LLC)** — Size 4 MB to 64 MB shared across cores; access time ≈ **20–40 ns**; technology = SRAM; uses an on-die ring or mesh interconnect. 5. **Main Memory (DRAM)** — Size 8 GB to 128 GB; access time ≈ **80–200 ns**; technology = **DRAM** (1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit); requires periodic refresh. 6. **Secondary Storage (SSD / HDD)** — SSD: 256 GB–4 TB, 50–150 µs; HDD: 1–20 TB, 5–15 ms; technology = NAND flash or magnetic platters. 7. **Tertiary Storage (Cloud / Tape)** — Petabyte scale; latency in seconds; technology = magnetic tape or distributed object stores. **Design Principle:** As we move down the pyramid, *cost per bit decreases* and *capacity increases*, but *access time also increases*. The hierarchy exploits **locality of reference** to ensure that the *most frequently accessed* data resides in the *fastest* memory. --- #### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** - $T_{L1} = 5$ ns - $T_{\text{RAM}} = 200$ ns - $H_1 = 0.92$ **(i) Miss Rate (1 mark):** $$M_1 = 1 - H_1 = 1 - 0.92 = 0.08$$ $$\boxed{M_1 = 0.08 \text{ or } 8\%}$$ **(ii) Average Memory Access Time (3 marks):** $$\text{AMAT} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{\text{RAM}}$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 0.92 \cdot 5 + 0.08 \cdot 200$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 4.6 + 16.0$$ $$\boxed{\text{AMAT} = 20.6 \text{ ns}}$$ **(iii) Speedup (3 marks):** Without cache, every access goes to main memory: $T_{\text{no-cache}} = 200$ ns. With cache, the average is: $T_{\text{with-cache}} = \text{AMAT} = 20.6$ ns. $$\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{no-cache}}}{T_{\text{with-cache}}} = \dfrac{200}{20.6}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Speedup} \approx 9.71 \times}$$ **Interpretation:** The cache provides nearly a **10× speedup**, demonstrating the massive performance benefit of even a single-level memory hierarchy. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Principle of Locality** in detail. How do Temporal and Spatial Locality influence cache design decisions like block size, replacement policy, and write strategy? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A two-level memory system has the following parameters: - L1 cache: $T_{L1} = 2$ ns, hit rate $H_1 = 0.85$ - L2 cache: $T_{L2} = 20$ ns, local hit rate $H_2 = 0.90$ (i.e., for accesses that miss L1) - Main memory: $T_{\text{RAM}} = 500$ ns Calculate: (i) The overall AMAT of the system. (ii) The fraction of total accesses that miss both L1 and L2. (iii) The contribution of L2 to reducing total memory access time. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks The **Principle of Locality** is the empirical observation that programs do not access memory uniformly or randomly. Over any short time window, a program accesses only a small, clustered subset of its entire address space. This principle is the *theoretical justification* for the entire memory hierarchy. **Two Types of Locality:** 1. **Temporal Locality (Time-based reuse):** - *Definition:* If a memory location is accessed, there is a high probability it will be accessed again in the near future. - *Cause:* Loops (`for`, `while`), recursive functions, frequently used variables (loop counters, accumulators). - *Example:* In `for(i=0;i<100;i++) sum += arr[i];`, the variable `sum` is accessed 100 times in quick succession. - *Cache Design Implication:* Cache lines should be **retained** in the cache after the first access (LRU policy preserves temporal locality). Small, fast caches with low associativity exploit temporal locality well. 2. **Spatial Locality (Space-based proximity):** - *Definition:* If a memory location is accessed, neighboring locations are likely to be accessed soon. - *Cause:* Sequential instruction execution, array traversals, struct member access. - *Example:* In `arr[0]`, `arr[1]`, `arr[2]`, the addresses differ by only a few bytes. - *Cache Design Implication:* Cache **block size** (line size) should be large enough to capture a *spatial cluster* — typically 32 to 128 bytes. Prefetching adjacent lines exploits this locality. **Influence on Cache Design:** | Design Decision | Driven by | Trade-off | |---|---|---| | **Block size** (16/32/64/128 B) | Spatial locality | Larger blocks capture more spatial data but increase miss penalty & pollution | | **Cache size** | Both temporal & spatial | Larger caches hold more data but increase access time & cost | | **Associativity** | Temporal locality conflicts | Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases hit time | | **Write-back vs Write-through** | Temporal locality | Write-back exploits temporal reuse of cached writes; write-through is simpler | | **Replacement Policy (LRU/LFU)** | Temporal locality | LRU preserves recently used lines; LFU prioritizes frequently used lines | --- #### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** - $T_{L1} = 2$ ns, $H_1 = 0.85$, $M_1 = 0.15$ - $T_{L2} = 20$ ns, local $H_2 = 0.90$, local $M_2 = 0.10$ - $T_{\text{RAM}} = 500$ ns **(i) Overall AMAT (3 marks):** Using the recursive formula: $$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot \left[ H_2 \cdot T_{L2} + M_2 \cdot T_{\text{RAM}} \right]$$ Substitute values: $$\text{AMAT} = 2 + 0.15 \cdot \left[ 0.90 \cdot 20 + 0.10 \cdot 500 \right]$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 2 + 0.15 \cdot \left[ 18 + 50 \right]$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 2 + 0.15 \cdot 68$$ $$\text{AMAT} = 2 + 10.2$$ $$\boxed{\text{AMAT} = 12.2 \text{ ns}}$$ **(ii) Fraction missing both caches (2 marks):** $$M_1 \cdot M_2 = 0.15 \cdot 0.10 = 0.015$$ $$\boxed{\text{L1 and L2 combined miss rate} = 0.015 \text{ or } 1.5\%}$$ **(iii) L2's contribution to time reduction (2 marks):** Without L2 (only L1 + RAM), the AMAT would be: $$\text{AMAT}_{\text{no L2}} = 2 + 0.15 \cdot 500 = 2 + 75 = 77 \text{ ns}$$ Time saved by L2: $$\Delta T = 77 - 12.2 = 64.8 \text{ ns per access}$$ $$\boxed{\text{L2 reduces AMAT by } 64.8 \text{ ns, an } 84.16\% \text{ improvement}}$$ **Interpretation:** Only 1.5% of accesses reach main memory, yet they account for the *majority* of the remaining time. L2's filtering effect is dramatic. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Memory Hierarchy Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing *local* and *global* hit rates:** In multi-level cache questions, the L2 hit rate is often given as a *local* rate (i.e., $H_2$ = 0.90 of accesses that reach L2). The *global* hit rate is $H_1 + M_1 \cdot H_2$. Using the wrong type yields a wrong AMAT — and **3 marks lost**. > > 2. **Forgetting to include L1 access time in the miss path:** The formula is $\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot (\dots)$, *not* $M_1 \cdot T_{L2}$ alone. Omitting the $T_{L1}$ term loses **2 marks**. > > 3. **Units confusion:** Always state units (ns, µs, ms) in the final answer. The KTU evaluator deducts **0.5 to 1 mark** for unit-less numerical answers. > > 4. **Mixing up Miss Penalty and Miss Rate:** Miss *Rate* is a probability (0 to 1), Miss *Penalty* is a time (ns). They are not interchangeable. Mixing them yields **incorrect formulas**. > > 5. **Not simplifying the AMAT expression:** Always expand and simplify. Writing the formula in unsimplified form is acceptable for partial credit, but a final boxed numerical value is required for **full marks**. > > 6. **Ignoring the inclusion property:** When asked about cache coherence, students often forget to mention that the *inclusion property* may need to be enforced — losing **1 to 2 marks** in essay-type questions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory Hierarchy: Introduction** - **Core Concept:** Memory hierarchy is a tiered storage arrangement that uses faster, smaller, expensive memory near the CPU and slower, larger, cheap memory far from the CPU. - **The Memory Wall:** CPU speed grows ~50%/year, DRAM latency grows ~7%/year — hierarchy is the architectural solution. - **Principle of Locality:** - *Temporal* → reuse over time → drives **cache retention**. - *Spatial* → reuse over address proximity → drives **block size**. - **Key Performance Metric — AMAT:** $$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{L2} + M_1 M_2 \cdot T_{L3} + \dots$$ - **Three Pillars:** Locality, Inclusion, Coherence. - **Hit Rate vs Miss Rate:** $H + M = 1$ (complementary). - **Local vs Global Miss Rate:** Local = within that level; Global = combined miss probability from CPU's perspective. $\text{Global Miss} = M_1 \cdot M_2 \cdot \dots$ - **Speedup Formula:** $\text{Speedup} = T_{\text{baseline}} / T_{\text{improved}}$. - **Hierarchy Levels (Memorize Order):** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → SSD → HDD → Cloud. - **Technology Mapping:** Registers = flip-flops, L1/L2/L3 = **SRAM**, Main = **DRAM**, Storage = **NAND/Magnetic**. - **Cost-Speed-Capacity Triangle:** You can pick any two; the third is sacrificed. This is the *fundamental engineering trade-off* in memory design. - **Five Most Asked Exam Topics:** (1) AMAT calculation; (2) Locality types; (3) Hierarchy diagram; (4) Speedup derivation; (5) Multi-level miss rate analysis. - **Common Units:** L1/L2/L3 in **ns**, DRAM in **ns to µs**, SSD in **µs**, HDD in **ms**, Cloud in **seconds**. > **End of Module 3 — Memory Hierarchy: Introduction Notes** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEAET522 — Modern Processor Architecture.*

topics.title: Levels in Modern Memory Hierarchy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Levels in Modern Memory Hierarchy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Memory Hierarchy** in modern processor architecture refers to the structured, layered organization of storage elements in a computer system, arranged in a strict order of decreasing speed, increasing capacity, and decreasing cost per bit. The primary objective of this hierarchy is to provide a memory subsystem that *appears* as fast as the fastest level (Registers/Cache) to the processor, while *behaving economically* like the largest, slowest level (Secondary Storage), thereby bridging the *processor-memory performance gap* (also called the *Memory Wall*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The memory hierarchy is governed by two fundamental program behaviour properties — **Temporal Locality** (if an address is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again soon) and **Spatial Locality** (if an address is referenced, neighbouring addresses are likely to be referenced soon). These two principles, collectively called the **Principle of Locality**, are the very reason a memory hierarchy *works*.

## 1.2 The Levels in Modern Memory Hierarchy (Top → Bottom)

| Hierarchy Level (Top to Bottom) | Typical Capacity | Typical Latency | Volatile? | Managed By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L0 – CPU Registers** | ~1 KB (64–256 registers) | ~0.1–1 ns | Yes | Compiler / Hardware |
| **L1 Cache (SRAM)** | 32–128 KB per core | ~1–3 ns | Yes | Hardware |
| **L2 Cache (SRAM)** | 256 KB – 1 MB per core | ~4–10 ns | Yes | Hardware |
| **L3 Cache (SRAM, shared)** | 4 MB – 64 MB shared | ~10–30 ns | Yes | Hardware |
| **Main Memory (DRAM)** | 8 GB – 128 GB | ~50–100 ns | Yes | OS / Hardware |
| **Storage (SSD / NVMe)** | 256 GB – 4 TB | ~10–100 µs | No | OS / File System |
| **Remote / Cloud Storage** | Practically unlimited | ~1–100 ms | No | Network Protocol |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Student Desk Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are a student writing an exam.
> 1. The **pen in your hand** = CPU Register (smallest, fastest, used *right now*).
> 2. The **books on your desk** = L1/L2 Cache (frequently referenced).
> 3. The **books in your bag at your feet** = L3 Cache / Main Memory.
> 4. The **locker down the corridor** = SSD/HDD.
> 5. The **central library across town** = Cloud Storage.
>
> You obviously don't go to the library for every formula — you keep the *most useful items closer*. That is exactly how memory hierarchy operates.

## 1.4 The Three Core Design Metrics

At every level, the architect balances three competing metrics — universally known as the **Iron Triangle of Memory Design**:

1. **Speed (Latency / Access Time):** Time to deliver data once requested.
2. **Capacity:** How much data the level can hold.
3. **Cost per bit (₹/bit or $/bit):** Manufacturing expense per stored bit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The golden rule — *Moving UP the hierarchy → Faster, Smaller, Costlier (per bit), Managed by hardware*. Moving DOWN → Slower, Larger, Cheaper.

## 1.5 Why the Hierarchy is Mandatory — The Memory Wall

The processor clock speed has historically scaled faster than DRAM access time. Around the early 2000s, the gap became so severe it was termed the **Memory Wall** (Wulf & McKee, 1995). A modern CPU may execute an instruction every **~0.3 ns**, but a DRAM access takes **~60–100 ns** — meaning the CPU idles for **~200 clock cycles per memory fetch** if no hierarchy is used. The hierarchy minimises this penalty by serving the vast majority of accesses from the upper levels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Hierarchy (Step-by-Step)

When the CPU issues a Load/Store request for address `A`, the following access sequence is followed:

- **Step 1 — Register Check:** The data is already in a CPU register → *0 cycles, proceed.*
- **Step 2 — L1 Lookup:** The L1 controller checks its tags. If a **HIT** occurs, data is forwarded in ~1–3 ns.
- **Step 3 — L2 Lookup (on L1 miss):** The L1 miss triggers an L2 tag lookup. On **HIT**, data is delivered, and a copy is filled into L1 (exploiting **temporal locality**).
- **Step 4 — L3 Lookup (on L2 miss):** Similar to L2, the L3 is checked. On a hit, data is *promoted* up the hierarchy.
- **Step 5 — Main Memory (DRAM) Access (on L3 miss):** A memory controller transaction begins. The fetched **block (cache line, typically 64 bytes)** is filled into L3, L2, and L1 simultaneously — this exploits **spatial locality** (the entire 64-byte line is brought in, not just the requested word).
- **Step 6 — Secondary Storage (on DRAM miss / page fault):** The OS handles a page fault, bringing the page from SSD/HDD, requiring context switches and OS intervention.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \times MP$ | Average Memory Access Time | ns |
| 2 | $Hit\ Ratio\ (H) + Miss\ Ratio\ (M) = 1$ | Probability completeness | dimensionless |
| 3 | $AMAT_{L1} = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times (T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times (T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \times T_{Mem}))$ | Recursive AMAT for 3-level cache | ns |
| 4 | $CPU\ Execution\ Time = (CPU\ Clock\ Cycles + Memory\ Stall\ Cycles) \times Clock\ Cycle\ Time$ | Performance impact | seconds |
| 5 | $Memory\ Stall\ Cycles = (Misses\ per\ Instruction) \times Miss\ Penalty$ | Stall accounting | cycles |
| 6 | $Misses\ per\ Instruction = Memory\ References\ per\ Instruction \times Miss\ Rate$ | MPI derivation | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Bandwidth = \dfrac{Block\ Size}{Transfer\ Time}$ | Throughput | bytes/s |
| 8 | $Cost\ per\ bit\ (effective) = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \times S_i)}{\sum S_i}$ | Hierarchy cost | ₹/bit |

**Where:**
- $T_{hit}$ = access time when data is found in that level
- $MR$ = Miss Rate at that level (0 ≤ MR ≤ 1)
- $MP$ = Miss Penalty = access time of the *next* lower level

## 2.3 Inclusion vs. Exclusion — KTU Favourite Concept

When data is brought from memory on a miss, it is placed in **multiple cache levels**. There are two policies:

- **Inclusive Cache (e.g., Intel's classical design):** Data in L1 is *also* present in L2 and L3. Pro: easy coherence. Con: wasted capacity.
- **Exclusive Cache (e.g., AMD's classical design):** Data in L1 is in L2 or L3, but not both. Pro: higher effective capacity. Con: complex coherence.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Datacentre Optimisation:** Google, AWS engineers use the *AMAT hierarchy* to size their L3 caches and DRAM channels to maximise queries/sec.
- **Edge AI / Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon):** Designers use System-Level Cache (SLC) as an L3-like unified buffer to reduce DRAM traffic and power.
- **GPU Computing (NVIDIA H100):** Use *memory hierarchy* with registers → shared memory → L1/L2 → HBM3 → NVMe, dramatically affecting CUDA kernel performance.
