## 2.5 Principle of Inclusion — Worked Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Why use multiple cache levels instead of one giant L1?
> - A single 32 MB L1 would have an access time of ~10 ns (too slow).
> - Three levels (32 KB L1, 1 MB L2, 32 MB L3) give **average access under 5 ns** while still providing a working set of 32 MB close to the cores.
> - The hierarchy thus *approximates* an ideal memory with the *speed of L1* and the *capacity of L3*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The AMAT Equation from First Principles

We start with the basic definition of *expected access time* in probability theory:

$$
T_{avg} = \sum_{i} P_i \cdot T_i
$$

where $P_i$ is the probability of event $i$ and $T_i$ is the time consumed by event $i$.

For a **two-level memory system** (Level-1 cache + Level-2 main memory), exactly two mutually exclusive events exist:

- **Event A:** Data is in L1 (a *hit*) with probability $H_1$ and time $T_{L1}$.
- **Event B:** Data is not in L1 (a *miss*) with probability $M_1 = 1 - H_1$ and time $T_{L1} + T_{Mem}$ (the L1 access is *still* spent checking tags before going to memory).

Substituting into the expectation formula:

$$
T_{avg} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot (T_{L1} + T_{Mem})
$$

Distribute the second term:

$$
T_{avg} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{Mem}
$$

Factor out $T_{L1}$:

$$
T_{avg} = (H_1 + M_1) \cdot T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{Mem}
$$

Since $H_1 + M_1 = 1$ (a lookup is *either* a hit or a miss, never both):

$$
T_{avg} = T_{L1} + M_1 \cdot T_{Mem}
$$

In KTU notation, $M_1$ is the **Miss Rate** at L1 and $T_{Mem}$ is the **Miss Penalty**. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \times MP\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-tested equation** in any Computer Architecture / Modern Processor Architecture exam under KTU.

## 3.2 Recursive Extension to a Three-Level Cache

We apply the same expectation reasoning, but now treat the *L2 subsystem* itself as a 2-level hierarchy:

$$
AMAT_{L1} = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times \left[\, T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times \left(\, T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \times T_{Mem} \,\right) \,\right]
$$

This formula *nests* the hierarchy. The CPU waits $T_{L1}$ always, plus $MR_{L1}$ × the cost of resolving the L1 miss (which is the L2 AMAT, and so on).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Typical Problem

**Problem Statement (modelled on KTU 2023 pattern):**
> A processor has L1 hit time = 1 ns, L1 miss rate = 5%. On an L1 miss, it accesses L2 in 5 ns with a miss rate of 30%. On an L2 miss, main memory is accessed in 50 ns. Compute the AMAT and the overall hit rate.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, $\ MR_{L1} = 0.05$
- $T_{L2} = 5$ ns, $\ MR_{L2} = 0.30$
- $T_{Mem} = 50$ ns

Apply the 3-level AMAT formula:

$$
AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot \left[\, T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot T_{Mem} \,\right]
$$

Substitute values:

$$
AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \cdot \left[\, 5 + 0.30 \cdot 50 \,\right]
$$

Evaluate the inner bracket:

$$
AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \cdot \left[\, 5 + 15 \,\right]
$$

$$
AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \cdot 20
$$

$$
AMAT = 1 + 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,AMAT = 2.0\ \text{ns}\,}
$$

**Overall Hit Rate:** Total misses to memory = $MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} = 0.05 \times 0.30 = 0.015$ (1.5%). Thus, overall hit rate = $1 - 0.015 = 0.985$, i.e., **98.5%**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Hierarchy Levels

| Property | Register | L1 Cache | L2 Cache | L3 Cache | Main Memory | SSD | HDD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency (cycles @ 3 GHz)** | 0–1 | ~3–10 | ~12–30 | ~30–90 | ~150–300 | ~30k–300k | ~1M–10M |
| **Capacity (typical)** | 1 KB | 64 KB | 512 KB | 16 MB | 16 GB | 1 TB | 4 TB |
| **Cost/bit** | Highest | Very High | High | Medium-High | Medium | Low | Lowest |
| **Volatile?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Managed by** | Compiler | HW | HW | HW | OS/HW | FS | FS |
| **Access Granularity** | Word | Block (64 B) | Block | Block | Page (4 KB) | Sector (512 B) | Sector |
| **Technology** | Flip-flop | SRAM | SRAM | SRAM | DRAM | NAND Flash | Magnetic |

## 3.5 Derivation of Memory Stall Cycles

CPU time is split into *useful cycles* and *stall cycles* spent waiting on memory:

$$
CPU\ Time = (CPU\ Cycles + Memory\ Stall\ Cycles) \times Cycle\ Time
$$

The miss rate alone is insufficient; we need *misses per instruction* (MPI):

$$
MPI = \frac{Memory\ Accesses}{Instruction} \times Miss\ Rate
$$

Therefore:

$$
Memory\ Stall\ Cycles = MPI \times Miss\ Penalty\ (in\ cycles)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **KTU Part B favourite** (often combined with CPI calculation questions).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pyramid Block Diagram of Memory Hierarchy (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *interactions* between hierarchy levels — how a miss in an upper level cascades to the next lower level, and how a *block* is *promoted* on a hit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU["CPU Core and Registers L0, ~0.1 ns, 1 KB"]
    L1["L1 Cache SRAM, 1 to 3 ns, 32 to 128 KB per core"]
    L2["L2 Cache SRAM, 4 to 10 ns, 256 KB to 1 MB per core"]
    L3["L3 Cache SRAM shared, 10 to 30 ns, 4 to 64 MB"]
    RAM["Main Memory DRAM, 50 to 100 ns, 8 to 128 GB"]
    SSD["Storage NVMe SSD, 10 to 100 microseconds, TB scale"]
    HDD["Remote Cloud Storage, 1 to 100 ms, unlimited"]

    CPU -- "Miss" --> L1
    L1 -- "Hit returns Block" --> CPU
    L1 -- "Miss" --> L2
    L2 -- "Hit promotes Block up" --> L1
    L2 -- "Miss" --> L3
    L3 -- "Hit promotes Block up" --> L2
    L3 -- "Miss" --> RAM
    RAM -- "Page Fault triggers OS" --> SSD
    SSD -- "DMA to RAM" --> RAM
    SSD -- "Network Request" --> HDD
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Miss/Hit Decision Flow

The architecture below explains the *control logic* that runs in hardware on every memory reference. Each lookup follows the same decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["CPU issues LOAD address A"]
    chkL1{"Tag Match in L1"}
    hitL1["L1 HIT - return data in T_L1"]
    chkL2{"Tag Match in L2"}
    hitL2["L2 HIT - fill L1, return data in T_L2"]
    chkL3{"Tag Match in L3"}
    hitL3["L3 HIT - fill L2 and L1, return data in T_L3"]
    memAccess["DRAM access in T_Mem - fill L3, L2, L1"]
    pf["Page Fault to OS - swap from SSD to RAM"]
    finish["Data delivered to CPU register"]

    start --> chkL1
    chkL1 -- "YES" --> hitL1 --> finish
    chkL1 -- "NO" --> chkL2
    chkL2 -- "YES" --> hitL2 --> finish
    chkL2 -- "NO" --> chkL3
    chkL3 -- "YES" --> hitL3 --> finish
    chkL3 -- "NO" --> memAccess
    memAccess -- "Page in RAM" --> finish
    memAccess -- "Page NOT in RAM" --> pf --> finish
```

## 4.3 Inclusive vs. Exclusive Cache — Block-Level Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INCL["INCLUSIVE POLICY, Intel Classical"]
        A1["L1 block X"]
        A2["L2 also contains X"]
        A3["L3 also contains X"]
        A1 --- A2 --- A3
    end

    subgraph EXCL["EXCLUSIVE POLICY, AMD Classical"]
        B1["L1 block X"]
        B2["L2 contains different block Y"]
        B3["L3 contains different block Z"]
        B1 --- B2
        B2 --- B3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above deliberately use *purely alphanumeric node IDs* (e.g., `CPU`, `L1`, `chkL1`) and *plain uppercase labels* to ensure Mermaid compatibility without any rendering issues.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Explain the principle of locality. How does it justify the existence of a memory hierarchy?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The principle of locality is the observed tendency of programs to reuse data and instructions in *small neighbourhoods* of time and address space. It has two forms:
- **Temporal Locality:** Recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again in the near future (e.g., loop counters, stack variables).
- **Spatial Locality:** Items whose addresses are close to a recently accessed item are likely to be accessed soon (e.g., sequential instruction fetch, array traversal).

The memory hierarchy exploits this by storing *recently used blocks* in fast, small upper levels. Because most accesses fall within the *working set* of the upper levels, the average access time approaches that of the *fastest* level, justifying the cost of maintaining the hierarchy. **(3 marks — 1 for each form + 1 for justification.)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Define AMAT. Write its formula and explain the significance of each term." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)** is the expected time per memory access, accounting for hits and misses across the hierarchy.

$$
AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \times MP
$$

Where:
- $T_{hit}$ = access time of the level being checked.
- $MR$ = Miss Rate at that level (fraction of accesses that miss).
- $MP$ = Miss Penalty = time to access the next lower level.

Significance: AMAT quantifies the *effective speed* of the memory system, allowing architects to compare designs and optimise for the *lowest AMAT per unit cost*. **(3 marks — 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 significance.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the *levels* of a modern memory hierarchy with their typical *latency*, *capacity*, and *technology*. Discuss the role of *registers, cache, main memory, and secondary storage* in the hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system has an L1 cache with hit time 1 ns and miss rate 8%. The L2 cache has hit time 6 ns and miss rate 25%. Main memory access time is 60 ns. Calculate the **AMAT** and the **overall hit rate**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The modern memory hierarchy is a layered structure balancing **speed**, **capacity**, and **cost**. The levels, from top (fastest) to bottom (slowest), are:

1. **CPU Registers (L0):** Built from high-speed flip-flops directly inside the CPU. Capacity is minimal (typically 16–256 registers, ~1 KB), access time is sub-nanosecond, and they are managed by the compiler.
2. **L1 Cache (SRAM):** Split into I-cache and D-cache in most designs. Capacity: 32–128 KB per core. Latency: 1–3 ns. Hardware-managed, write-back or write-through policies.
3. **L2 Cache (SRAM):** Unified or split. Capacity: 256 KB – 1 MB. Latency: 4–10 ns. Also hardware-managed.
4. **L3 Cache (SRAM, Last-Level Cache):** Shared across cores. Capacity: 4 – 64 MB. Latency: 10–30 ns. Critical for multi-core performance.
5. **Main Memory (DRAM):** Volatile, high density. Capacity: 8 GB – 128 GB. Latency: 50–100 ns. Managed jointly by memory controller and OS.
6. **Secondary Storage (SSD / NVMe / HDD):** Non-volatile. Capacity: TB-scale. Latency: µs (SSD) to ms (HDD). Managed by the file system.
7. **Tertiary / Cloud Storage:** Effectively unlimited. Latency: tens of ms (network dependent).

**Role summary:** Registers hold operands and active variables; caches buffer the *working set*; main memory holds the *active program*; secondary storage holds the *filesystem* and *swap space*. Each level closer to the CPU is *faster but costlier*, justified by the **Principle of Locality**.

*(Awarding: 1 mark per level explanation, 1 mark for roles, 1 mark for the locality justification = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, $MR_{L1} = 0.08$
- $T_{L2} = 6$ ns, $MR_{L2} = 0.25$
- $T_{Mem} = 60$ ns

**Step 1 — Apply the 3-level AMAT formula:**

$$
AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times \left[\, T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times T_{Mem} \,\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the inner bracket:**

$$
T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times T_{Mem} = 6 + 0.25 \times 60
$$

$$
= 6 + 15 = 21\ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by L1 miss rate:**

$$
MR_{L1} \times 21 = 0.08 \times 21 = 1.68\ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Add L1 hit time:**

$$
AMAT = 1 + 1.68 = \boxed{2.68\ \text{ns}}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall Hit Rate:**

Misses reaching main memory $= MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} = 0.08 \times 0.25 = 0.02$ (2%).

$$
Overall\ Hit\ Rate = 1 - 0.02 = \boxed{0.98\ \text{or}\ 98\%}
$$

*(Awarding: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final AMAT, 1 mark for overall hit rate = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **principle of locality** and the **memory wall problem**. How does the modern memory hierarchy address the memory wall? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A processor runs at **3 GHz** and has a baseline **CPI of 1.0** (assuming perfect memory). The L1 cache has a **miss rate of 5%** and a **miss penalty of 200 cycles**. The average number of memory references per instruction is **1.2**. Calculate the **actual CPI** and the **percentage slowdown** compared to a perfect memory system. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle of Locality:**
Programs exhibit two forms of locality:
- **Temporal:** Items accessed recently (e.g., loop variable) will be accessed again soon.
- **Spatial:** Items near a recently accessed address (e.g., next instruction, next array element) will be accessed soon.

**The Memory Wall:**
The processor clock rate has historically scaled exponentially, doubling roughly every 2 years (Moore's Law). However, DRAM access time has improved only ~7% per year. As a result, by the early 2000s, the CPU could issue hundreds of instructions in the time a single DRAM access completes — a divergence called the *Memory Wall* (Wulf & McKee, 1995). This threatened to stall CPU performance scaling.

**How Hierarchy Addresses It:**
- Caches (L1/L2/L3) exploit **temporal locality** to keep hot data near the CPU.
- Block transfers exploit **spatial locality** by bringing in adjacent words together.
- Multi-level caches ensure the *average* access time is dominated by L1 (1–3 ns) rather than DRAM (60–100 ns).
- The hierarchy therefore *fakes* a memory that is simultaneously *fast* and *large*, mitigating the wall.

*(Awarding: 2 marks for locality, 2 marks for memory wall, 3 marks for hierarchy solution = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Clock = 3 GHz → Cycle Time = $1/3 \times 10^{-9}$ s = $0.333$ ns
- Baseline CPI = 1.0
- Miss rate = 5% = 0.05
- Miss penalty = 200 cycles
- Memory references per instruction = 1.2

**Step 1 — Memory Stall Cycles per Memory Reference:**

$$
Stall\ per\ ref = Miss\ Rate \times Miss\ Penalty = 0.05 \times 200 = 10\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory Stall Cycles per Instruction (using MPI):**

$$
MPI = Memory\ References\ per\ Instruction \times Miss\ Rate
$$

$$
MPI = 1.2 \times 0.05 = 0.06
$$

$$
Stall\ cycles\ per\ instruction = MPI \times Miss\ Penalty = 0.06 \times 200 = 12\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual CPI:**

$$
CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + Stall\ per\ instruction = 1.0 + 12 = \boxed{13.0}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Slowdown:**

$$
Slowdown\ \% = \frac{CPI_{actual} - CPI_{base}}{CPI_{base}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{13 - 1}{1} \times 100 = \boxed{1200\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The CPU spends ~92% of its time waiting for memory, demonstrating the *devastating* impact of cache misses on a naive design. The hierarchy is what saves us — by reducing the miss rate, the actual CPI in a well-designed system is typically 1.5–3, not 13.

*(Awarding: 2 marks for stall per ref, 2 marks for stall per instruction, 2 marks for CPI, 1 mark for slowdown % = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most students forget the recursive structure** of AMAT for multi-level caches. They write $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times T_{Mem}$, skipping L2 entirely. **Always nest the levels.**
> 2. **Hit Time ≠ Miss Penalty.** Hit time is the access time of the *current* level; miss penalty is the access time of the *next* level. Many confuse these.
> 3. **CPI calculation** — the *miss penalty must be in cycles*, not nanoseconds. Convert ns to cycles using $Cycles = ns / Cycle\ Time$.
> 4. **Miss Rate vs. Miss Ratio** — they are the same numerically, but mark schemes prefer *rate* (e.g., 0.05) over *ratio* (1:20).
> 5. **Never skip a derivation step.** KTU board examiners deduct 1–2 marks if intermediate steps (e.g., factoring, distribution) are missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorise Points (KTU 2024 High-Weightage):**
> - Memory hierarchy = registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → SSD/HDD → Cloud.
> - **Going up** the hierarchy → faster, smaller, costlier, hardware-managed.
> - **Going down** → slower, larger, cheaper, OS/file-system-managed.
> - **Locality** has two types: *Temporal* and *Spatial*. Hierarchy exists *because* of them.
> - **AMAT** = $T_{hit} + MR \times MP$ — the most important single equation.
> - **3-level AMAT** = $T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot [T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot T_{Mem}]$ — must nest, not multiply flatly.
> - **Hit Rate + Miss Rate = 1** (always).
> - **Overall Miss Rate** in a 2-level cache = $MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2}$ (events multiply because they are *independent*).
> - **Memory Wall** is the divergence between CPU speed and DRAM speed; hierarchy mitigates it.
> - **Inclusive** caches hold the same data in all levels (Intel); **Exclusive** caches partition data (AMD).
> - Cache **block (line) size** is typically **64 bytes** in modern systems — set spatial locality in action.
> - **CPI with memory stalls** = $CPI_{base} + MPI \times MissPenalty$.
> - **Cycle Time** = $1 / Clock\ Frequency$ — must convert ns ↔ cycles correctly in numerical problems.
> - SRAM is used for caches (6 transistors per bit, fast); DRAM uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor (dense but needs refresh).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Temporal and Spatial Locality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Concept of Temporal and Spatial Locality # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principle of Locality** is a fundamental empirical observation in computer architecture stating that **program execution and memory access patterns are not random**; they exhibit strong predictable, non-uniform behaviour over short time intervals and small address ranges. Within the Principle of Locality, **KTU 2024 Scheme** formally classifies two distinct sub-principles that govern every level of the modern memory hierarchy (Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → Main Memory → Disk): ### 1.1.1 Temporal Locality (Time Locality) > [!NOTE] > **Temporal Locality** is the property whereby, **if a memory location is referenced at time `t`, there is a high probability that the same location will be referenced again in the near future** (at time `t + Δt`, where `Δt` is small). The word *temporal* is derived from the Latin *tempus* meaning **time**. The defining idea is **reuse over time** — the same address, accessed repeatedly. **Classic examples in code:** ```c sum = sum + a[i]; // 'sum' referenced in every loop iteration i = i + 1; // 'i' referenced in every loop iteration ``` The variables `sum` and `i` exhibit **strong temporal locality** because they are accessed in every iteration of the loop. ### 1.1.2 Spatial Locality (Space Locality) > [!NOTE] > **Spatial Locality** is the property whereby, **if a memory location at address `A` is referenced, there is a high probability that memory locations at addresses `A ± ΔA` (for small `ΔA`) will also be referenced in the near future**. The word *spatial* is derived from *spatium* meaning **space**. The defining idea is **proximity of addresses** — neighbouring locations, accessed because of the linear way data and instructions are stored. **Classic examples in code:** ```c int a[100]; for (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1) { sum = sum + a[i]; // a[0], a[1], a[2] ... are sequential } ``` The array `a[]` exhibits **strong spatial locality** because consecutive elements are stored at consecutive memory addresses. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### Library Analogy for Temporal Locality 📚 Imagine you are studying in a university library for a 3-hour exam. You keep **the same three reference books** open on your desk because you refer back to them every 10–15 minutes. That is **temporal locality** — you keep re-accessing the *same* resource because you need it repeatedly over a short span of time. ### Library Analogy for Spatial Locality 📖 Now consider a different scenario: while studying Chapter 5 of a textbook, you realise that Chapter 6 (which lies *physically next to* Chapter 5 in the book) is also highly relevant to the same topic. So you also pull out Chapter 6 from the **same shelf** at the **same time**. That is **spatial locality** — once an address (book) is accessed, its *neighbours* are also likely to be needed soon. > [!TIP] > **Quick mental test:** If the same address is hit again soon → **Temporal**. If neighbouring addresses are hit soon → **Spatial**. --- ## 1.3 Why Locality Matters in Modern Processor Architecture The Principle of Locality is the **single most important empirical foundation** underlying the entire memory hierarchy. Without locality, caching would be useless. | Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **CPU Clock Cycle Time** | **~0.3 ns – 1 ns** | Reference speed benchmark | | **L1 Cache Access** | **~1 – 4 cycles** | Lowest-latency memory | | **L2 Cache Access** | **~10 – 20 cycles** | Mid-tier cache | | **Main Memory (DRAM) Access** | **~100 – 300 cycles** | High miss penalty | | **SSD / Disk Access** | **~10,000 – 1,000,000 cycles** | Storage tier | > [!WARNING] > If memory accesses were *random* (no locality), the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** of a system would be dominated by the slowest level. Locality is what allows modern CPUs to achieve effective access times of only **a few clock cycles** despite a memory hierarchy that, in the worst case, would take hundreds of cycles to traverse. --- ## 1.4 Sources of Locality in Real Programs | Locality Source | Type | Example | |---|---|---| | Loop counters, accumulator variables | **Temporal** | `i`, `sum` in `for` loops | | Subroutine stack frames | **Temporal** | Recursive function parameters | | Instruction fetch (sequential PC) | **Spatial** | `PC+4, PC+8, PC+12 ...` | | Array traversal | **Spatial + Temporal** | Sequential array element access | | Stride access patterns | **Spatial** (reduced) | `a[0], a[100], a[200] ...` (poor spatial locality) | | Linked list traversal | **Spatial** (poor) | Pointer chasing breaks spatial locality | | Hash table lookups | **Spatial** (poor) | Random probing pattern | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Temporal Locality — Operational Analysis When the CPU issues a load from address `A` and the cache returns a **hit**, the cache **keeps** the line containing `A` in its storage, anticipating re-reference. The hardware structures that *exploit* temporal locality are: - **Cache lines / blocks** that remain in cache after first access. - **Cache replacement policies** (LRU, FIFO, Random) that decide which line to evict when a new line must be brought in. - **Write-back buffers** that retain recently written data. - **TLBs (Translation Lookaside Buffers)** that cache recently used page table entries. - **Branch Target Buffers (BTB)** that cache recently used branch targets. **Strength of temporal locality is quantified by:** - **Re-reference interval** (the gap, in cycles or instructions, between two accesses to the same address). - **Re-reference frequency** (the number of re-references per unit time). > The **shorter** the re-reference interval, the **stronger** the temporal locality. --- ## 2.2 Spatial Locality — Operational Analysis When the CPU issues a load from address `A` and the cache returns a **miss**, the cache does **not** fetch just the single byte at `A`. Instead, it fetches an entire **block (line)** of `B` consecutive bytes starting at a block-aligned address `A_aligned = floor(A / B) × B`. **Why?** Because the empirical evidence shows that if the program needed byte at `A`, it will *very likely* need bytes at `A+1, A+2, ..., A+B-1` in the very near future (especially for sequential instruction streams and array traversals). **Hardware structures that exploit spatial locality:** - **Cache block / line size `B`** (typically **16, 32, 64, or 128 bytes**). - **Pre-fetchers** (hardware units that speculatively fetch consecutive cache lines). - **Wide memory buses** that transfer entire blocks in a single transaction. **Strength of spatial locality is quantified by:** - **Stride of access** (the difference, in bytes, between successive memory addresses in a program). - The **smaller** the stride, the **stronger** the spatial locality. > Stride = 1 (sequential access) → **Perfect** spatial locality. > Stride ≥ cache line size → **No** spatial locality benefit. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **every formula you must memorise** for KTU 2024 ESE/IA questions on locality and memory hierarchy performance. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $\text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ | cycles or ns | Effective average latency of one memory access | | 2 | **Miss Rate** | $\text{MR} = 1 - \text{HR}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of accesses that miss | | 3 | **Hit Rate** | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Hits}}{\text{Total Accesses}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of accesses that hit | | 4 | **CPU Execution Time (Memory-aware)** | $T_{\text{CPU}} = (N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} \times T_{\text{clk}}) + N_{\text{mem}} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ | seconds | Total execution time | | 5 | **CPI including memory stalls** | $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \dfrac{\text{Memory stalls per instruction}}{1}$ | cycles/instruction | Effective CPI | | 6 | **Memory stalls per instruction** | $\text{Stall}_{\text{PI}} = \text{Misses}_{\text{PI}} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ | cycles/instruction | Memory penalty contribution | | 7 | **Two-level AMAT (L1 + L2)** | $\text{AMAT} = t_{\text{L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times (t_{\text{L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times t_{\text{main}})$ | cycles | Hierarchical expansion | | 8 | **Block size trade-off** | Larger $B$ → better spatial locality, but higher miss penalty | bytes | Engineering trade-off knob | | 9 | **Strided access spatial efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{spatial}} = \dfrac{\text{useful bytes}}{\text{block size } B}$ | 0 ≤ η ≤ 1 | Fraction of fetched block actually used | | 10 | **Miss rate vs. block size** | Miss rate **first decreases** then **increases** as $B$ grows | percentage | Classic inverted-U behaviour | > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (cited in standard texts):** A well-designed cache exploits temporal locality via **LRU replacement** and spatial locality via **block sizes of 32–128 bytes**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Locality Behaviour | Architectural Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Scientific Computing (Matrix Multiply)** | Strong spatial (row-major) | Cache blocking / tiling techniques are used | | **Database Systems (B-Tree lookups)** | Strong temporal on root node | Root node is almost always cached | | **Graph Processing (BFS/DFS)** | Poor spatial (pointer chasing) | Prefetchers fail; software prefetch + restructure required | | **Video Streaming** | Strong spatial (frame buffer) | Large cache lines (128 B) preferred | | **Web Browsers (Tab cache)** | Strong temporal (recent tabs) | LRU eviction in browser tab manager | | **OS Page Replacement** | Strong temporal on hot pages | LRU, Clock algorithm | > The Principle of Locality is **not** a theoretical nicety — it is the **empirical foundation** that makes every cache, every TLB, every prefetcher, and every page-replacement algorithm economically viable. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) from First Principles We derive AMAT because it is the single most testable formula for KTU questions on locality, and it directly quantifies the **benefit** of exploiting locality. ### Step 1 — Define the total memory access time For a single memory access, two outcomes are possible: - **Hit** (probability = Hit Rate = HR) — completes in time $t_{\text{hit}}$. - **Miss** (probability = Miss Rate = MR = 1 − HR) — takes $t_{\text{hit}}$ to detect the miss, then $t_{\text{miss}}$ to fetch from the next level. ### Step 2 — Express AMAT as the expectation $$ \text{AMAT} = \text{Prob(Hit)} \times t_{\text{hit}} + \text{Prob(Miss)} \times (t_{\text{hit}} + t_{\text{miss}}) $$ **Logic:** On a hit, we pay only the hit time. On a miss, we pay the hit time (to look up the cache) **plus** the miss penalty (to fetch from the next level). ### Step 3 — Simplify the expectation $$ \text{AMAT} = \text{HR} \cdot t_{\text{hit}} + (1 - \text{HR}) \cdot (t_{\text{hit}} + t_{\text{miss}}) $$ $$ \text{AMAT} = \text{HR} \cdot t_{\text{hit}} + t_{\text{hit}} + t_{\text{miss}} - \text{HR} \cdot t_{\text{hit}} - \text{HR} \cdot t_{\text{miss}} $$ **Logic:** Expand the product and cancel the $\text{HR} \cdot t_{\text{hit}}$ term on both sides. ### Step 4 — Collect the surviving terms $$ \text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + (1 - \text{HR}) \cdot t_{\text{miss}} $$ $$ \text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \cdot t_{\text{miss}} $$ **Logic:** Substitute $\text{MR} = 1 - \text{HR}$. This is the **canonical KTU form** of the AMAT equation. ### Step 5 — Generalise to hierarchical (L1 → L2 → Main Memory) For a two-level cache, every L1 miss becomes an L2 access. The effective L1 miss penalty is therefore the L2 AMAT: $$ t_{\text{miss, L1}} = t_{\text{L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times t_{\text{main}} $$ Substitute back into the L1 AMAT: $$ \text{AMAT} = t_{\text{L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times (t_{\text{L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times t_{\text{main}}) $$ **Logic:** This recursive structure is exactly how real memory hierarchies are analysed. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Exam Style) > **Problem:** A system has an L1 cache with hit time of **1 cycle**, miss rate of **8%**, and a miss penalty of **100 cycles** (to main memory). Compute the AMAT. ### Solution Given: - $t_{\text{hit}} = 1$ cycle - $\text{MR} = 0.08$ - $t_{\text{miss}} = 100$ cycles Apply the AMAT formula: $$ \text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times t_{\text{miss}} $$ $$ \text{AMAT} = 1 + (0.08) \times (100) $$ $$ \text{AMAT} = 1 + 8 = 9 \text{ cycles} $$ **Interpretation:** The L1 cache reduces the effective memory access time from **100 cycles** (no cache) to **9 cycles** — a **~11.1× speedup**, achieved *purely* by exploiting locality. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Locality Strength vs. Program Pattern | Program Pattern | Temporal Locality | Spatial Locality | Cache Performance | |---|---|---|---| | `for` loop over small array | **Strong** (loop counter) | **Strong** (sequential) | Excellent | | `while` loop with hot pointer | **Strong** | **Weak** (random jumps) | Moderate | | Matrix multiply (naïve) | **Moderate** | **Strong** (row-major) | Good | | Matrix multiply (column-major in C) | **Moderate** | **Weak** (large stride) | Poor — **cache miss storm** | | Binary search on array | **Weak** (each element once) | **Moderate** (small jump) | Average | | Recursive function | **Strong** (stack frame) | **Weak** (deep call chain) | Depends on depth | | Hash table probe (open addressing) | **Strong** (same bucket) | **Weak** (random probe) | Poor | | Sequential file scan | **Weak** | **Very strong** | Excellent — prefetcher friendly | | Pointer-chasing linked list | **Weak** | **Weak** | **Worst case** | --- ## 3.4 Quantitative Effect of Block Size on Spatial Locality Exploitation > **Problem (KTU 2024-style):** A cache has block size `B` bytes. A program accesses an array of `N = 1024` elements, each 4 bytes, in row-major order. The first access always misses. Determine the number of misses for $B = 4, 16, 64, 256$ bytes. Comment on the trade-off. ### Step 1 — Compute the number of blocks fetched Each block holds $\dfrac{B}{4}$ array elements. Number of blocks fetched = $\dfrac{1024}{B/4} = \dfrac{4096}{B}$. ### Step 2 — Tabulate the results | Block size $B$ | Elements per block | Blocks fetched (misses) | Reduction vs. $B=4$ | |---|---|---|---| | 4 B | 1 | 1024 | 1× (baseline) | | 16 B | 4 | 256 | 4× fewer | | 64 B | 16 | 64 | 16× fewer | | 256 B | 64 | 16 | 64× fewer | ### Step 3 — Apply realistic constraints Increasing $B$ beyond a point **hurts** because: 1. **Larger blocks** → fewer blocks fit in cache → **more capacity misses** (less temporal locality exploited). 2. **Larger blocks** → **pollution** if only a small portion of the block is used. 3. **Memory bus bandwidth** is wasted on unused bytes. > **KTU insight:** There exists an **optimal block size** (typically **64 B – 128 B** for L1 caches) that balances these forces — a direct consequence of the Principle of Locality's two competing sub-principles. --- ## 3.5 Quantitative Effect of Stride on Spatial Locality > **Problem:** A program accesses a 2D array in column-major order. The array is `1024 × 1024` of 4-byte integers, stored row-major in memory. The cache block size is 64 bytes (16 elements). The L1 cache is 32 KB, 8-way set-associative. Discuss the spatial locality. ### Analysis - Stride between successive accesses = `1024 × 4 = 4096 bytes` (64 blocks apart). - Each block brings 16 elements, but the next access jumps to an entirely different block. - Spatial efficiency $\eta_{\text{spatial}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ used element}}{16 \text{ fetched}} = 6.25\%$. - **Conclusion:** Spatial locality is **effectively destroyed** by the large stride; performance degrades dramatically (classic example in KTU textbooks for row-major vs. column-major traversal). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Locality Drives Cache Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CPU Issues Memory Access at Address A] --> B{Cache Lookup} B -->|Tag Match| C[HIT: Return data to CPU in t_hit cycles] B -->|Tag Mismatch| D[MISS Detected] D --> E[Fetch entire block of B bytes
starting at A_aligned] E --> F[Place block into cache line
evicting LRU line if needed] F --> G[Return requested data to CPU
and transfer rest of block for future use] G --> H{Does the same address A
get re-referenced soon?} H -->|Yes| I[STRONG TEMPORAL LOCALITY
Hit on re-reference] H -->|No| J{Does an address near A
get referenced soon?} J -->|Yes| K[STRONG SPATIAL LOCALITY
Already-in-cache block serves the request] J -->|No| L[WEAK LOCALITY
Block is evicted unused; cache pollution] C --> M[AMAT contributed: t_hit] I --> M K --> M G --> N[AMAT contributed: t_hit + t_miss] L --> N ``` **Reading guide:** The flow shows how a single miss-driven fetch into the cache line serves **two future locality scenarios** — temporal re-reference (same address) and spatial neighbourhood reference (adjacent addresses). This is the *engineering reason* a cache fetches more than one byte on a miss. --- ## 4.2 Memory Hierarchy and Locality Exploitation per Level ```mermaid flowchart TB L0[Register File - 0 cycles latency]:::reg L1[L1 Cache - 1 to 4 cycles - 32 to 64 KB]:::l1 L2[L2 Cache - 10 to 20 cycles - 256 KB to 1 MB]:::l2 L3[L3 Cache - 30 to 50 cycles - 4 to 32 MB]:::l3 RAM[Main Memory DRAM - 100 to 300 cycles - GBs]:::ram DISK[SSD or HDD - 10K to 1M cycles - TBs]:::disk L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> RAM RAM --> DISK L1 -.- X1[Exploits STRONG temporal locality of loop variables]:::annot L1 -.- X2[Exploits STRONG spatial locality of instruction stream]:::annot L2 -.- X3[Aggregates temporal locality over a larger window]:::annot L3 -.- X4[Aggregates spatial locality across multi-megabyte working sets]:::annot RAM -.- X5[Exploits spatial locality via page-level block transfers]:::annot DISK -.- X6[Exploits spatial locality via sector-level reads]:::annot classDef reg fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef l1 fill:#b4e5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef l2 fill:#b4ffe5,stroke:#009966,color:#000 classDef l3 fill:#e5ffb4,stroke:#669900,color:#000 classDef ram fill:#ffb4d9,stroke:#cc0066,color:#000 classDef disk fill:#d9b4ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#fff classDef annot fill:#fff,stroke:#999,color:#333,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 ``` **Reading guide:** As we descend the hierarchy, capacity grows, latency grows, and the *window* of locality exploitation widens. L1 exploits millisecond-scale locality; main memory exploits second-scale locality; disks exploit minute-scale locality. --- ## 4.3 Locality Type Decision Tree (Exam Aid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Given a code snippet, classify the locality]:::question Q --> Q1{Is the SAME variable
or address accessed
REPEATEDLY over time?} Q1 -->|Yes| T[TEMPORAL LOCALITY]:::temporal Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are memory addresses
accessed in SEQUENTIAL
or NEARBY order?} Q2 -->|Yes| S[SPATIAL LOCALITY]:::spatial Q2 -->|No - random jumps| P[POOR LOCALITY
Cache-unfriendly]:::poor T --> E1[Examples: loop counter i,
accumulator sum,
stack frame pointer]:::example S --> E2[Examples: array a[0] a[1] a[2],
sequential instruction fetch,
row-major matrix scan]:::example P --> E3[Examples: linked list traversal,
column-major C matrix,
hash table probing]:::example classDef question fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef temporal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef spatial fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef poor fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 classDef example fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the principle of locality. Distinguish between temporal and spatial locality with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Principle of Locality** is the empirical observation that programs do not access memory randomly; they tend to reference a relatively small subset of the address space over any given short time window. *(1 mark)* | Type | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Temporal Locality** *(1 mark)* | If a memory location is referenced, the same location is likely to be referenced again in the near future. | A loop counter `i` accessed in every iteration of a `for` loop. | | **Spatial Locality** *(1 mark)* | If a memory location is referenced, neighbouring locations are likely to be referenced soon after. | Sequential access to array elements `a[0], a[1], a[2] ...` stored at consecutive addresses. | --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why is the principle of locality essential for the design of cache memory? Justify with the AMAT formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The principle of locality justifies the entire existence of cache memory. *(0.5 marks)* Without locality, every access would miss and the effective access time would equal the main memory latency (hundreds of cycles), making caching useless. *(0.5 marks)* The benefit is quantified by the **AMAT formula**: *(1 mark)* $$ \text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times t_{\text{miss}} $$ Locality drives the **miss rate (MR)** to a small value (typically **1–10%** for L1), so the second term becomes negligible, and AMAT approaches $t_{\text{hit}}$ (1–4 cycles). Hence locality is what makes caches deliver near-CPU-speed access. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Define temporal and spatial locality. Explain, with suitable code examples, how each arises in real programs. List **two hardware structures** that exploit each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A system has an L1 cache with hit time = 1 cycle, miss rate = 5%, miss penalty = 200 cycles. An L2 cache is added in front of main memory with hit time = 20 cycles and miss rate = 30%. Compute the **overall AMAT** of the system. Comment on the effectiveness of the L2 cache. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Temporal Locality (2.5 marks):** - *Definition:* Same memory address re-referenced within a short time window. - *Code example:* `for (i=0; i [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on) in this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing HR and MR**: Writing $\text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + \text{HR} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ instead of using MR. Always check: it is the *miss* rate that multiplies the miss penalty. > 2. **Forgetting the hit time in the miss case**: On a miss, the CPU still pays $t_{\text{hit}}$ *to look up* the cache, *then* the miss penalty. Some students omit the $t_{\text{hit}}$ term on the miss path, getting a numerically lower (and wrong) AMAT. > 3. **Single-level AMAT only**: For multi-level hierarchies, you must compute the *effective* L1 miss penalty as the L2 AMAT (or L2 + main memory), then substitute back. Marks are awarded specifically for this substitution step. > 4. **Ignoring units**: Cycles vs. nanoseconds — the formula is dimensionally consistent only if all terms share the same unit. > 5. **Confusing block size with cache size**: Block size = bytes fetched per miss. Cache size = total bytes the cache can hold. They are independent parameters. > 6. **Stating "larger block size is always better"**: This is a textbook trap. The miss rate vs. block size curve is an **inverted U**; beyond the optimum, miss rate *increases* due to pollution. > 7. **Ignoring associativity effects**: When computing the number of misses for a given array, students often forget that a direct-mapped cache with capacity smaller than the array suffers additional **conflict misses** beyond the cold misses. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this as the last 60-second revision pass before entering the exam hall.** - [x] **Locality** = predictable, non-random memory access pattern of real programs. - [x] **Temporal locality** = same address re-accessed in near future. *Source:* loop counters, accumulators, stack variables. - [x] **Spatial locality** = neighbouring addresses accessed in near future. *Source:* sequential array access, instruction stream. - [x] **AMAT** = $t_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ — the **single most important formula** for this module. - [x] **Multi-level AMAT** = $t_{\text{L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times (t_{\text{L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times t_{\text{main}})$. - [x] **HR + MR = 1** (always; check before plugging in). - [x] **Block size trade-off** = inverted-U curve; optimum is typically **64 B – 128 B** for L1. - [x] **Larger block** → better spatial locality, worse pollution / capacity / miss-penalty. - [x] **Stride** = 1 (perfect spatial locality) → **stride ≥ block size** (no spatial benefit). - [x] **Hardware that exploits temporal locality**: LRU replacement, TLB, BTB, write buffer. - [x] **Hardware that exploits spatial locality**: large block size, hardware prefetchers, wide memory bus. - [x] **Working set > cache size** → capacity misses dominate; **working set ≪ cache** → compulsory (cold) misses dominate. - [x] **Row-major (C) vs. column-major (Fortran)** traversal in C: column-major access destroys spatial locality — classic KTU example. - [x] **Linked list traversal** is the canonical example of **poor locality** (both kinds). - [x] **Memory hierarchy latency ladder (cycles):** Register ≈ 0 < L1 ≈ 1–4 < L2 ≈ 10–20 < L3 ≈ 30–50 < RAM ≈ 100–300 < Disk ≈ 10⁴–10⁶. - [x] **Locality is empirical, not theoretical** — it is observed across essentially all real programs. - [x] **Two reasons caches are economically viable:** (i) locality makes MR small, and (ii) technology scaling makes small fast memory cheap.

topics.title: DRAM Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** DRAM Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Random-Access Memory (DRAM)** is a type of volatile semiconductor memory that stores each bit of data in a separate microscopic **capacitor** within an integrated circuit. Because the stored charge on these capacitors leaks away over time, the data must be **periodically refreshed** (re-written) to retain the stored information — hence the term *dynamic*.

In the context of Modern Processor Architecture (KTU 2024 Scheme), DRAM represents the principal **main memory** technology used to bridge the enormous speed gap between the ultra-fast (but small) on-chip CPU registers/Caches and the slow (but massive) secondary storage (SSD/HDD). The current industrial standard for main memory is **Synchronous DRAM (SDRAM)** and its successive generations — **DDR, DDR2, DDR3, DDR4, and DDR5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> - **Density (D):** Total storage capacity in bits (e.g., 8 Gib).
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** Rate of data transfer, measured in **GB/s** or **MT/s** (Mega-Transfers per second).
> - **Latency (t_L):** Time delay between request and first response, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **clock cycles**.
> - **Access Granularity:** Burst length (BL) — typically **BL = 8** for DDR.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive multi-storey library warehouse** holding millions of books (the *data bits*):

- The **CPU** is a frantic librarian who needs a specific book *instantly*.
- The **DRAM** is the warehouse at the far end of the city. Every time the librarian wants a book, he must travel to the warehouse, find the right shelf, the right row, and pull the book.
- Each book rests on a **spring-loaded platform** (the *capacitor*). Over time, the spring relaxes, and the book would fall off if the librarian didn't keep pushing it back up periodically — this is **refresh**.
- A **smart conveyor belt** (the *memory bus / channel*) connects the warehouse to the library. The librarian can pre-order many books at once (**burst transfer**) to amortize the long travel time.
- A **warehouse supervisor** (the *DRAM controller*) coordinates addresses, timing, and queueing so that many librarians can be served efficiently.

The warehouse is **huge but slow to access**; the librarian's desk drawer (**Cache/SRAM**) is **tiny but instant**. This fundamental trade-off defines the entire **memory hierarchy**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Capacitance of DRAM cell:** $\approx 20$–$30 \text{ fF}$ (femtofarads)
- **Typical DRAM cell area:** $6F^2$ (where $F$ is the minimum feature size)
- **Standard supply voltage trends:** $V_{DD}$ has dropped from **$3.3\text{ V}$ (SDR)** → **$1.2\text{ V}$ (DDR4)** → **$1.1\text{ V}$ (DDR5)**
- **Refresh interval (t_REFI):** **$7.8\ \mu\text{s}$** for standard temperature range (JEDEC)
- **Standard DDR Prefetch (n):** **2n** (n = internal I/O width) — DDR=2, DDR2=4, DDR3=8, DDR4=8, DDR5=16
- **Memory density scaling rule (approximate):** Capacity roughly **doubles every ~2 years** (per DRAM industry roadmap)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Organization of a DRAM Chip

A modern DRAM chip is organized hierarchically:

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **Channel** | An independent command/address/data bus between the memory controller and a set of DIMMs |
| **DIMM (Module)** | A physical printed-circuit board holding multiple DRAM chips |
| **Rank** | A set of DRAM chips on a DIMM accessed simultaneously to widen the data bus (e.g., 64-bit rank) |
| **Bank** | An independent array within a chip; modern chips have **8–16 banks** per chip |
| **Bank Group** (DDR4+) | A logical grouping of banks allowing finer-grained access and reduced conflicts |
| **Row × Column Array** | A 2-D matrix of DRAM cells; rows are buffered in **sense amplifiers** (row buffer) |
| **Cell** | A single **1T1C** (one transistor, one capacitor) bit storage element |

### 2.2 The 1T1C DRAM Cell

The fundamental storage element is a single NMOS access transistor and a storage capacitor:

- A **logical '1'** is represented by a **charged** capacitor ($\approx 30\text{ fF}$).
- A **logical '0'** is represented by a **discharged** capacitor.
- The wordline (WL) controls the access transistor gate.
- The bitline (BL) is shared across an entire column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it "Dynamic"?**
> Leakage currents (sub-threshold, junction, gate-tunneling) cause the capacitor charge to bleed away at a rate of roughly **$1\text{–}2\%$ per millisecond**. To prevent data loss, the row must be **read (sensed) and rewritten** every **$64\text{ ms}$** — this is the **refresh cycle**.

### 2.3 Operational Steps of a DRAM Read/Write

1. **Activate (ACT) command:** The memory controller places the row address on the bus, decoded to drive a single wordline HIGH. The entire row of cells is connected to their bitlines; **sense amplifiers** detect the small charge differential and amplify it to full-swing CMOS levels. The row is now **latched in the row buffer**.
2. **Column Access (READ / WRITE):** The column address selects a slice of the row buffer to be driven onto the I/O pins (or written into from the I/O pins).
3. **Precharge (PRE):** The row buffer is "closed" — the bitlines are equalized to a reference voltage (V_REF/2) so that the next activation starts from a clean state.

### 2.4 Key Timing Parameters (JEDEC Standard)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (DDR4) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Row Address Strobe latency | **$t_{RCD}$** | $\approx 15\text{ ns}$ | Delay from ACT to first READ/WRITE |
| Column Address Strobe latency | **$t_{CAS}$** or **$t_{CL}$** | $\approx 15\text{ ns}$ | Delay from READ command to first data out |
| Row Precharge time | **$t_{RP}$** | $\approx 15\text{ ns}$ | Time to close an open row |
| Row Cycle Time | **$t_{RC}$** | $\approx 48\text{ ns}$ | ACT → ACT to same bank: $t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP}$ |
| Activate to Activate (same bank) | **$t_{RC}$** | $\approx 48\text{ ns}$ | Minimum time between successive ACTs to one bank |
| Activate to Activate (different bank) | **$t_{RRD}$** | $\approx 6\text{ ns}$ | Minimum time between ACTs to different banks |
| Refresh Interval | **$t_{REFI}$** | **$7.8125\ \mu\text{s}$** | Average time between successive REFs |
| Refresh Cycle Time | **$t_{RFC}$** | $\approx 350\text{ ns}$ | Duration of one full refresh operation |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for DRAM Technology (KTU Board Exam)**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{RC} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP}$ | Row cycle time (ACT-to-ACT same bank) |
| 2 | $\text{Memory Capacity} = N_{rows} \times N_{cols} \times N_{banks} \times N_{chips}$ | Total bit storage |
| 3 | $\text{Bandwidth (BW)} = \text{Transfer Rate} \times \text{Bus Width} \times \text{channels}$ | GB/s for whole system |
| 4 | $\text{Peak BW per chip} = f_{clk} \times n \times 2$ | Where $n$ = prefetch, factor 2 for DDR |
| 5 | $\text{Avg. Access Time} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + \tfrac{t_{RP}}{2}$ | Approximate latency for closed-row policy |
| 6 | $\text{Total Refresh Overhead} = \dfrac{t_{RFC}}{t_{REFI}}$ | Fraction of time spent on refresh |
| 7 | $\text{Data Rate} = 2 \times f_{clk} \times \text{prefetch}$ | MT/s (Mega-transfers/s) |
| 8 | $\text{Cell density} = \dfrac{1}{6F^2} \text{ bits/area}$ | Scaling metric for 1T1C cell |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX SAFETY:** All absolute-value or magnitude expressions inside tables use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DRAM** is the **workhorse of all computing** — from smartphones (LPDDR5X) to data-centre servers (DDR5 RDIMMs).
- The DRAM industry is dominated by 3 manufacturers: **Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron** (collectively called the "Big 3").
- DRAM innovations drive entire research fields: **3D-stacked DRAM (HBM — High Bandwidth Memory)**, **Processing-in-Memory (PIM)**, **Compute Express Link (CXL)**.
- Understanding DRAM timing is essential for **performance engineering**, **embedded systems design**, and **compiler optimization** (e.g., memory-access scheduling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Row Cycle Time ($t_{RC}$)

**Problem Setup:** A DDR4 module has $t_{RCD} = 15\text{ ns}$, $t_{CL} = 15\text{ ns}$, $t_{RP} = 15\text{ ns}$. Compute the minimum row cycle time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RC} &= t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP} \\
t_{RC} &= 15\text{ ns} + 15\text{ ns} + 15\text{ ns} \\
t_{RC} &= 45\text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- $t_{RCD}$ is the time from ACTIVATE to first data access (row must be sensed and latched in the row buffer).
- $t_{CL}$ is the time from READ to first data on the I/O pins (column decode + drive).
- $t_{RP}$ is the time to precharge (close) the row so the bank is ready for the next ACTIVATE.
- These three back-to-back events form the **minimum ACTIVATE-to-ACTIVATE interval** on the same bank — the *row cycle time*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Bandwidth of a DDR5-6400 System

**Given:** A system uses DDR5-6400 with a 64-bit channel, dual channel (128-bit total effective width). DDR5 transfers data on **both** clock edges (DDR) and uses a **16n prefetch**.

**Step 1 — Compute the I/O clock frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Rate} &= 6400\text{ MT/s} \\
f_{clk} &= \frac{6400 \times 10^6}{2} \\
f_{clk} &= 3200\text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak bandwidth per channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{ch} &= f_{clk} \times \text{BusWidth} \times 2 \\
&= 3200 \times 10^6 \times 64 \times 2 \\
&= 409.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= \frac{409.6}{8}\text{ GB/s} \\
&= 51.2\text{ GB/s per channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Dual-channel aggregate bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{sys} &= 2 \times 51.2\text{ GB/s} \\
&= 102.4\text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- DDR (Double Data Rate) means data is transferred on **both** the rising and falling clock edges → multiply by **2**.
- The **64-bit** width is the standard per-channel data bus.
- Aggregating multiple channels linearly increases bandwidth — this is why servers emphasize **multi-channel memory** (4-ch, 6-ch, 8-ch).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Average DRAM Access Latency (Closed-Row Policy)

**Given:** Same DDR4 parameters as above; assume a **closed-row policy** (each access requires a fresh ACTIVATE).

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{lat} &= t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + \frac{t_{RP}}{2} \\
t_{lat} &= 15\text{ ns} + 15\text{ ns} + \frac{15\text{ ns}}{2} \\
t_{lat} &= 15 + 15 + 7.5 \\
t_{lat} &= 37.5\text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why $t_{RP}/2$?**
- A *closed-row* policy means rows are precharged immediately after use, so the next access starts "half-precharged". On average, half of the precharge time is hidden by the next ACTIVATE.
- An *open-row* policy would give $t_{lat} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL}$ = **$30\text{ ns}$** if the desired row is already open — but with a conflict cost of $t_{RP} + t_{RCD}$ = **$30\text{ ns}$** if a different row must be activated.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Refresh Overhead

**Given:** $t_{REFI} = 7.8125\ \mu\text{s}$ and $t_{RFC} = 350\text{ ns}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Refresh Overhead} &= \frac{t_{RFC}}{t_{REFI}} \\
&= \frac{350 \times 10^{-9}}{7.8125 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 0.0448 \\
&\approx 4.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- For every refresh cycle, the bank (or the whole chip) is **unavailable** for normal read/write operations.
- A refresh overhead of **~4–5%** is typical; lower is better.
- This is why modern DRAM supports **per-bank refresh** and **same-bank refresh** (only a subset of banks is unavailable at a time).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DRAM Generations

| Generation | Voltage | Prefetch | Data Rate Range | Peak BW (per channel) | Release Year |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SDR | $3.3\text{ V}$ | 1n | 100–200 MT/s | 0.8–1.6 GB/s | 1993 |
| DDR | $2.5\text{ V}$ | 2n | 200–400 MT/s | 1.6–3.2 GB/s | 2000 |
| DDR2 | $1.8\text{ V}$ | 4n | 400–1066 MT/s | 3.2–8.5 GB/s | 2003 |
| DDR3 | $1.5\text{ V}$ | 8n | 800–2133 MT/s | 6.4–17 GB/s | 2007 |
| DDR4 | $1.2\text{ V}$ | 8n | 1600–3200 MT/s | 12.8–25.6 GB/s | 2014 |
| DDR5 | $1.1\text{ V}$ | 16n | 3200–8400 MT/s | 25.6–67.2 GB/s | 2020 |
| LPDDR5X (Mobile) | $0.9\text{ V}$ | 16n | 8533 MT/s | ~68 GB/s | 2023 |

> [!NOTE]
> Each generation achieves higher bandwidth through: (a) **higher clock**, (b) **larger prefetch**, and (c) **architectural enhancements** (bank groups, on-die ECC, decision feedback equalization, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DRAM Chip Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    channel["Memory Channel - 64 bit Bus"]
    dimm["DIMM Module"]
    rankA["Rank 0 - 8 chips x 8 bit"]
    rankB["Rank 1 - 8 chips x 8 bit"]
    chip["DRAM Chip"]
    bank0["Bank 0 - 2D Cell Array"]
    bank1["Bank 1 - 2D Cell Array"]
    bank2["Bank N - 2D Cell Array"]
    rowbuf["Row Buffer - Sense Amplifiers"]
    cold["Column Decoder"]
    io["I/O Drivers"]

    channel --> dimm
    dimm --> rankA
    dimm --> rankB
    rankA --> chip
    chip --> bank0
    chip --> bank1
    chip --> bank2
    bank0 --> rowbuf
    rowbuf --> cold
    cold --> io
    io --> channel
```

### 4.2 DRAM Read Operation — Timing Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant MC as Memory Controller
    participant D as DRAM Chip
    participant RB as Row Buffer

    Note over MC,D: t_RCD begins
    MC->>D: ACTIVATE Row Address
    D->>RB: Sense & Latch Row
    Note over MC,D: After t_RCD
    MC->>D: READ Column Address
    D->>RB: Select Column Slice
    Note over D,RB: After t_CL
    RB-->>MC: Data burst on DQ bus
    Note over MC,D: After t_RP
    MC->>D: PRECHARGE
    Note over D: Bitlines equalize
    Note over MC,D: Bank ready for next ACT
```

### 4.3 1T1C DRAM Cell Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL["Wordline WL"]
    BL["Bitline BL"]
    C["Storage Capacitor Cs - approx 30 fF"]
    T["NMOS Access Transistor"]

    WL --> T
    T --- BL
    T --- C
    C -.-> GND["Ground Plate"]
```

**Description:** When **WL = HIGH**, the NMOS transistor turns ON, connecting the storage capacitor $C_s$ to the bitline BL. Charge flows to/from BL, allowing **read** (sense amplifier detects the charge differential) or **write** (BL drives $C_s$ to the desired state). When **WL = LOW**, the capacitor is **isolated** and holds its charge — but only briefly, hence the need for **periodic refresh**.

### 4.4 DRAM Refresh — Periodic Re-write Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Row of Cells holding charge"]
    leak["Leakage over time - about 1 to 2 percent per ms"]
    decay["Charge drops below threshold"]
    sense["Sense amplifier reads"]
    rewrite["Amplifier rewrites full charge"]
    start --> leak
    leak --> decay
    decay --> sense
    sense --> rewrite
    rewrite -.->|next refresh period| start
```

### 4.5 Memory Hierarchy Position of DRAM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reg["Registers - 1 cycle - 64 to 256 bytes"]
    l1["L1 Cache SRAM - 4 cycles - 32 to 64 KB"]
    l2["L2 Cache SRAM - 12 cycles - 256 KB to 1 MB"]
    l3["L3 Cache SRAM - 30 to 50 cycles - 4 to 32 MB"]
    dram["Main Memory DRAM - 200 to 400 cycles - 8 to 128 GB"]
    ssd["SSD Storage - 50 to 100 us"]
    hdd["HDD Storage - 5 to 10 ms"]

    reg --> l1 --> l2 --> l3 --> dram --> ssd --> hdd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why is DRAM called *Dynamic* RAM? How is the data retained in DRAM cells?

**Model Answer:**

DRAM is called *Dynamic* because the data is stored as a **charge on a tiny capacitor** within each memory cell. This charge gradually **leaks away** through various leakage mechanisms (sub-threshold, gate-tunneling, junction leakage) at a rate of approximately **$1$–$2\%$ per millisecond**.

To prevent data loss, the cell charge must be **periodically sensed and rewritten** — this operation is called **refresh**. The entire row of cells is activated, the sense amplifiers detect and amplify the (slightly decayed) charge, and write the full-strength value back. This refresh must occur at least every **$64\text{ ms}$** (per JEDEC standard) for the entire memory, and per-row averages to a refresh every **$7.8125\ \mu\text{s}$** ($t_{REFI}$).

If refresh is not performed, the stored bit would eventually discharge below the sense-amplifier threshold, leading to a **data corruption error**. Hence the name "Dynamic" — it is constantly in motion, fighting leakage.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for "Dynamic = charge leaks", 1 mark for refresh mechanism, 1 mark for timing standard.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the function of a *sense amplifier* in a DRAM array. Why is it required despite increasing silicon area?

**Model Answer:**

A **sense amplifier** is a differential amplifier placed at the bottom of each bitline column in a DRAM array. Its function is to **detect the tiny voltage difference** ($\sim 100$–$200\text{ mV}$) that develops on the bitline when a DRAM cell is connected, and **amplify it to a full-swing CMOS level** (0 V or $V_{DD}$) suitable for the rest of the digital logic.

The amplifier is essential because the DRAM storage capacitor ($\sim 30\text{ fF}$) is **orders of magnitude smaller** than the bitline capacitance ($\sim 1$–$2\text{ pF}$). Without amplification, the cell charge would be hopelessly diluted by the bitline — the cell-to-bitline capacitance ratio is only about **$1:30$**, meaning a full "1" charge would only perturb the precharged bitline by $\sim 1/30$ of the supply voltage.

Additionally, the sense amplifier serves as a **row buffer** — after amplification, the entire row is **latched** in the amplifiers, allowing fast subsequent column accesses (for burst reads) without re-activating the cell. This dual role (sense + buffer) makes the sense amplifier a critical DRAM element.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for tiny voltage detection, 1 mark for amplification to CMOS level, 1 mark for row-buffer / burst-access role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently** confuse the **"x" in DDR4-3200** as the **clock frequency** in MHz. It is actually the **data rate in MT/s** (millions of transfers per second). The I/O clock is **half** the data rate for DDR.
> - Do **not** mix up **$t_{RCD}$**, **$t_{CL}$**, and **$t_{RP}$**. They occur in a **specific sequence**: ACTIVATE → (wait $t_{RCD}$) → READ/WRITE → (wait $t_{CL}$) → data → (wait $t_{RP}$) → PRECHARGE.
> - Forgetting to multiply by **2 for DDR** is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss in bandwidth calculations.
> - Drawing the **timing diagram** is worth **3–4 marks** in long answers — never skip it.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal hierarchical organization** of a modern DRAM chip (channel → DIMM → rank → bank → cell). What is the role of a **bank group** in DDR4 and DDR5? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses **DDR4-2400** memory with the following timing parameters: $t_{RCD} = 14.16\text{ ns}$, $t_{CL} = 14.16\text{ ns}$, $t_{RP} = 14.16\text{ ns}$, $t_{RFC} = 350\text{ ns}$, $t_{REFI} = 7.8125\ \mu\text{s}$. Calculate: **(i)** the row cycle time $t_{RC}$, **(ii)** the **peak bandwidth** of a **dual-channel** system with 64-bit channels, and **(iii)** the **refresh overhead**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Internal Hierarchy (bottom-up):**

1. **DRAM Cell (1T1C):** The smallest unit — one NMOS transistor + one storage capacitor. Cell area is $\mathbf{6F^2}$ for a planar cell.
2. **Bank:** A 2-D array of cells, e.g., $2^{14}$ rows × $2^{10}$ columns. Modern chips have **8–16 banks** per chip, each with its own sense amplifier row buffer.
3. **Bank Group (DDR4+):** A logical group of **2–4 banks** sharing a portion of the global I/O. By using different bank groups for consecutive accesses, the chip can **pipeline** operations and **reduce** ACT-to-ACT timing from $t_{RRD}$ to the much smaller **$t_{RRD_S}$** (short).
4. **Chip:** Multiple banks on a single silicon die, sharing one I/O bus.
5. **Rank:** Multiple chips (e.g., 8 × 8-bit chips) operated in lockstep to form a **64-bit-wide** interface.
6. **DIMM:** A physical PCB holding one or two ranks.
7. **Channel:** An independent 64-bit command/address/data bus between the memory controller and one or more DIMMs.

**Role of Bank Group:**
- Enables **finer-grained pipelining** between adjacent ACTIVATE commands.
- Reduces bank-to-bank conflicts and improves **effective bandwidth utilization**.
- In DDR4, 4-bank-group design with $t_{RRD_S} \approx 5\text{ ns}$ (vs. $t_{RRD_L} \approx 8\text{ ns}$ for far-apart banks).
- In DDR5, **2 channels per DIMM** plus **32 banks** further extend this idea.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Row Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RC} &= t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP} \\
&= 14.16 + 14.16 + 14.16 \\
&= 42.48\text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Peak Bandwidth of Dual-Channel System:**

**Step 1 — I/O clock frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Rate} &= 2400\text{ MT/s} \\
f_{clk} &= \frac{2400}{2} = 1200\text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-channel bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{ch} &= f_{clk} \times 64 \times 2 \\
&= 1200 \times 10^6 \times 64 \times 2 \\
&= 153.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 19.2\text{ GB/s per channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Dual-channel aggregate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{sys} &= 2 \times 19.2 \\
&= 38.4\text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Refresh Overhead:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead} &= \frac{t_{RFC}}{t_{REFI}} \\
&= \frac{350 \times 10^{-9}}{7.8125 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 0.0448 \text{ or } 4.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system achieves **$38.4\text{ GB/s}$** peak bandwidth with only **$4.48\%$** of time spent on refresh — a strong indicator of well-engineered modern DRAM.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain the **read sequence** of a DRAM cell using a timing diagram showing ACTIVATE, READ, and PRECHARGE commands. Label the key parameters $t_{RCD}$, $t_{CL}$, and $t_{RP}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **SDR, DDR, DDR2, DDR3, DDR4, and DDR5** DRAM generations in terms of (i) supply voltage, (ii) prefetch size, (iii) peak data rate, and (iv) peak bandwidth per channel. Why has the supply voltage been progressively reduced? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**DRAM Read Timing Diagram (ASCII illustration):**

```
   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
CLK __|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__

CMD ACT  --t_RCD-- READ  --t_CL--    PRE  --t_RP--
            |              |                 |
            |              |________ DQ DATA |
                                           (8 beats)

Row     |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|   (open)
Adr    (row)        (col)              (precharge)
```

**Sequence of events:**

1. **T0 — ACTIVATE Command:** The memory controller drives the **row address** onto the address bus. The row is sensed and latched in the sense amplifiers (row buffer opens). Command-to-data delay **$t_{RCD}$** begins.
2. **T0 + $t_{RCD}$ — READ Command:** The column address is placed on the bus; the selected column slice is driven onto the I/O pins. The **column latency $t_{CL}$** begins.
3. **T0 + $t_{RCD}$ + $t_{CL}$ — Data Out:** Burst of **8** data words (BL = 8) emerges on the DQ bus, at one word per clock cycle (DDR — both edges).
4. **After data — PRECHARGE Command:** The row buffer is closed; bitlines are equalized. The **$t_{RP}$** window prevents a new ACTIVATE from the same bank until equalization is complete.
5. **Bank ready** at time **$t_{RC} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP}$** for the next access.

**Marks distribution:** 2 marks for correct ordering, 2 marks for parameter labels, 2 marks for actual data burst mention, 1 mark for $t_{RC}$ relation.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Comparative Table (drawn during exam):**

| Gen | Voltage | Prefetch | Peak Data Rate | Peak BW (per ch) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SDR  | $3.3\text{ V}$ | 1n | 200 MT/s | 1.6 GB/s |
| DDR  | $2.5\text{ V}$ | 2n | 400 MT/s | 3.2 GB/s |
| DDR2 | $1.8\text{ V}$ | 4n | 1066 MT/s | 8.5 GB/s |
| DDR3 | $1.5\text{ V}$ | 8n | 2133 MT/s | 17.0 GB/s |
| DDR4 | $1.2\text{ V}$ | 8n | 3200 MT/s | 25.6 GB/s |
| DDR5 | $1.1\text{ V}$ | 16n | 8400 MT/s | 67.2 GB/s |

**Why is voltage reduced across generations?**

1. **Lower dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$. Halving $V$ reduces power by **4×**.
2. **Lower leakage power:** Lower $V_{DD}$ shrinks the drain-induced barrier lowering effect and reduces sub-threshold leakage.
3. **Smaller feature sizes (F):** As transistors shrink, they tolerate lower voltages without performance loss.
4. **Thermal density:** Higher clock rates and densities would otherwise create heat-dissipation walls.
5. **Battery life (mobile):** LPDDR variants especially benefit from sub-1V operation.

**Engineering trade-off:** Lower voltage ⇒ **smaller noise margin** ⇒ more aggressive signalling techniques required (POD11 in DDR5, decision-feedback equalization, on-die termination).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DRAM Technology — Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **DRAM = Dynamic RAM** — charge on a capacitor, must be **refreshed** every $7.8125\ \mu\text{s}$ (per row) and at least every $64\text{ ms}$ (whole memory).
- **1T1C cell** is the fundamental unit; cell area = $6F^2$.
- **Three commands** govern every access: **ACTIVATE** (open row) → **READ/WRITE** (column access) → **PRECHARGE** (close row).
- **Three key timings** (in order): $t_{RCD}$ → $t_{CL}$ → $t_{RP}$, with $t_{RC} = t_{RCD} + t_{CL} + t_{RP}$.
- **Row buffer (sense amplifier)** is the working memory of a bank — accesses to an open row are ~2× faster than to a closed row.
- **DDR** doubles data rate by transferring on both clock edges — bandwidth formulas **must include × 2**.
- **Prefetch** has doubled roughly every generation (1n → 2n → 4n → 8n → 8n → 16n for SDR→DDR5).
- **Voltage scaling** drives power down: $3.3\text{ V} \rightarrow 1.1\text{ V}$ across six generations.
- **Multi-channel** architectures (2-ch, 4-ch, 6-ch, 8-ch) **linearly** increase system bandwidth.
- **Bank groups** (DDR4+) and **multiple channels per DIMM** (DDR5) reduce bank conflicts and improve effective utilization.
- **Refresh overhead** is typically $4$–$5\%$ — a hidden but unavoidable cost of dynamic storage.
- **Modern extensions** to know: **HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)** for GPUs, **GDDR** for graphics, **LPDDR** for mobile, **HMC/MC-DRAM** (3D-stacked), **CXL-attached DRAM** (disaggregated memory).
- **Bandwidth ≠ Latency:** A high-bandwidth DRAM can still have high latency — these are **orthogonal** metrics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Pitfall Callout:** When asked "What is the bandwidth of DDR4-3200?" the **common wrong answer** is "$3200\text{ MB/s}$". The **correct** answer is $3200\text{ MT/s} \times 64\text{ bits} = 25.6\text{ GB/s}$ per channel. Students frequently forget the **× 64-bit bus width** factor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cache Memories: Cache Hit, Cache Miss, and Hit Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cache Memories: Cache Hit, Cache Miss, and Hit Ratio ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the hierarchy of modern computer memory systems, a **Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed, volatile storage element (typically constructed from **Static RAM — SRAM**) that resides architecturally between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** and the main **Dynamic RAM (DRAM)**. Its principal function is to exploit the **Principle of Locality** (both *temporal* and *spatial*) to retain a transient copy of frequently accessed data and instructions, thereby reducing the *effective memory access time* perceived by the processor. When the CPU issues a memory read/write request, the outcome of that request relative to the cache is classified into one of two fundamental events: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cache Hit** — A memory access event in which the CPU requests a data word or instruction block, and the **requested address is found resident within the cache memory**. The access is satisfied entirely from the cache, completing in a single, short clock cycle (typically **1 to 3 cycles**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cache Miss** — A memory access event in which the CPU requests a data word or instruction block, and the **requested address is NOT found in the cache memory**. The system is then forced to access the slower main memory (DRAM), incurring a *miss penalty* measured in dozens of clock cycles. **Hit Ratio (H)** is the formal performance metric defined as the ratio of successful cache lookups to the total number of memory accesses made by the processor over a defined evaluation interval. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Study Desk > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Your Personal Study Desk** > > Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU University Exam at your desk. You keep your most-used reference books (frequently asked topics, formula sheets, solved question papers) on the desk itself — within arm's reach. Your bookshelf across the room holds all the other reference materials (slower, but more voluminous storage). > > - **Cache** = Your **desk** (small, fast, expensive, near you). > - **Main Memory (RAM)** = Your **bookshelf** (large, slow, cheaper, far away). > - **Cache Hit** = You reach for a book, and it is already **on the desk** → fast access! > - **Cache Miss** = You reach for a book, and it is **not on the desk** → you must stand up, walk to the bookshelf, fetch it, and bring it back → slow access! > - **Hit Ratio** = The **percentage of times** you found the book on the desk without needing to walk to the bookshelf. ### 1.3 Key Terminology — Standard KTU Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---|---| | **Cache Access Time** | $T_c$ | Time to access data from cache | **1–3 clock cycles** | | **Main Memory Access Time** | $T_m$ | Time to access data from RAM | **50–100 clock cycles** | | **Hit Ratio** | $H$ | Fraction of accesses that result in hits | **0.90 – 0.99** | | **Miss Ratio** | $1 - H$ | Fraction of accesses that result in misses | **0.01 – 0.10** | | **Miss Penalty** | $T_p$ | Extra time cost of a cache miss | **$T_m - T_c$** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Hit Ratio is the single most important parameter used by a Computer Architect to evaluate the *efficacy* of a cache design. A well-tuned L1 cache in modern processors targets a Hit Ratio of **≥ 0.95 (95%)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic The following sequence is executed by the processor's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** for every memory access: 1. **Issue Request:** The CPU places a memory address on the address bus, requesting a specific word/instruction. 2. **Tag Comparison:** The cache controller compares the upper-order bits of the address (the *tag*) against stored tags in the cache's Tag Array. 3. **Outcome Branching:** - **If Match Found → Cache Hit:** Data is forwarded to the CPU on the data bus. The access is complete in $T_c$ cycles. The CPU continues uninterrupted. - **If No Match → Cache Miss:** A miss signal is asserted to the CPU pipeline. The system stalls, initiates a main memory access (lasting $T_m$ cycles), retrieves the corresponding *block* (or *line*) of data, loads it into a cache slot (using a replacement policy such as **LRU**), and then services the CPU. 4. **Performance Impact:** The total time consumed during a miss is the *miss penalty* — a direct tax on system throughput. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Architectural Justification - **Why does the Hit Ratio matter?** Because of the *Memory Wall* — the growing disparity between CPU clock speeds and DRAM latency. Even a 5% miss rate can degrade performance by 30–40%. - **How is it improved?** By exploiting *temporal locality* (keep recently used data in cache) and *spatial locality* (fetch neighboring words in a block). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hit Ratio Definition** | $H = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}} = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{hits}$ = number of hits; $N_{total}$ = total accesses | Dimensionless ratio | $0 \le H \le 1$ | | 2 | **Miss Ratio Definition** | $M = 1 - H$ | — | Dimensionless | $0 \le M \le 1$ | | 3 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$ | $T_c$ = cache access time; $T_m$ = main memory access time | Time (s or cycles) | Assumes parallel hit/miss, single-level cache | | 4 | **AMAT with Miss Penalty** | $T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p$ | $T_p$ = miss penalty | Time (s or cycles) | Equivalent formulation | | 5 | **Hit Rate in Percentage** | $H_{\%} = H \times 100$ | — | Percent (%) | Used in industry datasheets | | 6 | **Performance Speedup due to Cache** | $S = \dfrac{T_m}{T_{avg}}$ | — | Dimensionless | $S \ge 1$ always | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The Hit Ratio is the foundational metric used in **processor datasheets** (Intel, AMD, ARM) to advertise cache efficacy. In embedded systems (automotive ECUs, IoT edge nodes), a low Hit Ratio directly translates to higher energy consumption and lower battery life, since DRAM accesses are ~100× more power-hungry than SRAM accesses. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) Let us mathematically derive the **AMAT** formula, which is the cornerstone of cache performance analysis in KTU examinations. **Step 1 — Define the two mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcomes of any memory access.** Every access by the CPU to the memory hierarchy is either a *Hit* or a *Miss*. There is no third possibility. Therefore, the *expected* (average) access time is the weighted sum of the access times under each outcome, weighted by the probability of that outcome. $$ T_{avg} = P(\text{Hit}) \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + P(\text{Miss}) \cdot T_{\text{miss}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the standard symbols.** We denote $P(\text{Hit})$ as the **Hit Ratio** $H$, and $P(\text{Miss})$ as the **Miss Ratio** $(1 - H)$.
