The time taken on a hit is the cache access time $T_c$, and the time taken on a miss is the main memory access time $T_m$. $$ T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ **Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side for clarity.** $$ T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + T_m - H \cdot T_m $$ **Step 4 — Factor out $H$ from the first and third terms.** $$ T_{avg} = T_m - H \cdot (T_m - T_c) $$ **Step 5 — Recognize the term $(T_m - T_c)$ as the Miss Penalty $T_p$.** This reformulation gives the **miss-penalty form** of AMAT, often preferred by KTU examiners. $$ T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p $$ **Logical Interpretation of the Derivation:** - As $H \to 1$ (perfect hit rate), $T_{avg} \to T_c$ (the system runs as fast as the cache). - As $H \to 0$ (every access misses), $T_{avg} \to T_m$ (the system is as slow as main memory). - The cache's value lies in maximizing $H$, which quadratically reduces the effective latency experienced by the CPU. ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Problem:** A processor has an L1 cache with an access time of $T_c = 2$ ns and main memory with an access time of $T_m = 100$ ns. In a program execution trace, the processor makes **10,000** memory accesses, of which **9,200** result in cache hits. Calculate: > (a) The Hit Ratio $H$. > (b) The Miss Ratio $M$. > (c) The Average Memory Access Time $T_{avg}$. > (d) The Speedup $S$ provided by the cache over a no-cache system. **Solution:** **(a) Hit Ratio:** $$ H = \frac{N_{hits}}{N_{total}} = \frac{9200}{10000} = 0.92 $$ **(b) Miss Ratio:** $$ M = 1 - H = 1 - 0.92 = 0.08 $$ **(c) Average Memory Access Time:** $$ T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ $$ T_{avg} = (0.92)(2) + (0.08)(100) $$ $$ T_{avg} = 1.84 + 8.00 = 9.84 \text{ ns} $$ **(d) Speedup over no-cache system:** $$ S = \frac{T_m}{T_{avg}} = \frac{100}{9.84} \approx 10.16 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even with a 92% hit rate (considered mediocre in modern terms), the cache delivers a **~10× speedup** over direct main memory access. Pushing $H$ from 0.92 to 0.99 would reduce $T_{avg}$ to **2.98 ns**, a further 3.3× improvement — illustrating the *exponential* sensitivity of system performance to hit rate. ### 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix The following table demonstrates how $T_{avg}$ varies with the Hit Ratio (assuming $T_c = 2$ ns, $T_m = 100$ ns): | Hit Ratio $H$ | Miss Ratio $M$ | $T_{avg}$ (ns) | Speedup $S$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0.50 | 0.50 | 51.00 | 1.96× | | 0.80 | 0.20 | 21.60 | 4.63× | | 0.90 | 0.10 | 11.80 | 8.47× | | 0.95 | 0.05 | 6.90 | 14.49× | | 0.98 | 0.02 | 3.96 | 25.25× | | 0.99 | 0.01 | 2.98 | 33.56× | | 1.00 | 0.00 | 2.00 | 50.00× | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Notice the *diminishing returns* behavior. The jump from $H = 0.50$ to $H = 0.90$ yields ~26× more speedup improvement than the jump from $H = 0.99$ to $H = 1.00$. This is why designers target "high" hit rates (95–99%) rather than "perfect" hit rates. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cache Access Decision Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode1([CPU Issues Memory Address]) --> cmpNode1{Tag Comparison
in Cache} cmpNode1 -- Match Found
Cache Hit --> hitNode1[Data Delivered
in Tc cycles] cmpNode1 -- No Match
Cache Miss --> missNode1[Stall CPU Pipeline] missNode1 --> memNode1[Access Main Memory
Tm cycles] memNode1 --> loadNode1[Load Block into Cache
Apply Replacement Policy] loadNode1 --> delNode1[Data Delivered to CPU] hitNode1 --> contNode1([Continue Execution]) delNode1 --> contNode1 ``` ### 4.2 Hit vs. Miss — Performance Impact Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[Cache Hit Path - Fast] A1[CPU] --> A2[Cache] A2 --> A3[Data in 1-3 cycles] end subgraph B[Cache Miss Path - Slow] B1[CPU] --> B2[Cache Check] B2 --> B3[Main Memory Access] B3 --> B4[Cache Update] B4 --> B5[Data in 50-100 cycles] end A3 --> metricNode((Hit Ratio H)) B5 --> metricNode metricNode --> perfNode[System Performance] ``` ### 4.3 Memory Hierarchy Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB regNode[Registers
1 cycle, Bytes] --> l1Node[L1 Cache
1-3 cycles, KB] l1Node --> l2Node[L2 Cache
5-15 cycles, MB] l2Node --> l3Node[Main Memory DRAM
50-100 cycles, GB] l3Node --> diskNode[Disk Storage
ms, TB] l1Node -. H near 1.0 .-> metricNode2((Fast Access)) l3Node -. H near 0.0 .-> metricNode2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The closer a memory level is to the CPU (top of the hierarchy), the faster and more expensive it is. The Hit Ratio $H$ is *highest* at L1 and *decreases* as we move down the hierarchy. The overall AMAT of the system is a weighted combination of all levels' hit rates. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the terms (i) Cache Hit, (ii) Cache Miss, and (iii) Hit Ratio as applied to a memory hierarchy system.* **Model Answer:** (i) **Cache Hit:** A cache hit occurs when the CPU requests a memory address and the data is found resident in the cache memory. The access is completed in $T_c$ cycles (typically 1–3 cycles) without involving main memory. (ii) **Cache Miss:** A cache miss occurs when the CPU requests a memory address that is *not* present in the cache. The system must then access main memory (DRAM), incurring a miss penalty of $T_m - T_c$ cycles, and the corresponding block is loaded into the cache. (iii) **Hit Ratio:** The Hit Ratio $H$ is defined mathematically as: $$ H = \frac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}} $$ It represents the fraction of all memory accesses satisfied by the cache and is the principal metric for cache performance. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Question]** *What is the Miss Penalty? How is it related to the Average Memory Access Time?* **Model Answer:** The **Miss Penalty** $T_p$ is the additional time overhead incurred when a cache miss occurs, defined as the difference between main memory access time and cache access time: $T_p = T_m - T_c$. It represents the extra latency imposed on the CPU when it cannot be served by the cache. The AMAT incorporates the miss penalty through the miss ratio: $$ T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p $$ Thus, the miss penalty is *multiplied* by the miss ratio and *added* to the cache access time to yield the average effective access time. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently confuse *Hit Ratio* with *Hit Rate* — they are numerically identical, but *Ratio* is expressed as a decimal (0.92), while *Rate* is expressed as a percentage (92%). Always check the question's expected format. > - **Common Mistake #2:** When computing AMAT, students often forget to convert percentages to decimals. A 92% hit rate means $H = 0.92$, not $H = 92$. > - **Common Mistake #3:** In multi-level cache problems, students apply the *single-level* AMAT formula incorrectly to L2/L3 caches. Each level has its own hit ratio, and the formula must be applied *recursively*. > - **Mark Loss Zone:** Part (a) of any 14-mark question carries 7 marks. Missing the *definition* AND *numerical substitution* typically costs 2–3 marks immediately. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** A processor system has a cache with access time $T_c = 5$ ns and main memory with access time $T_m = 200$ ns. The cache hit ratio is observed to be 0.90. **(7 marks)** (i) Calculate the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT). (ii) If the hit ratio is improved to 0.98 by using a better cache algorithm, calculate the new AMAT and the percentage reduction in access time. **(b)** Explain the concept of temporal and spatial locality of reference. How do these principles justify the existence and design of cache memories in modern processors? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a)(i):** Given: $T_c = 5$ ns, $T_m = 200$ ns, $H = 0.90$. $$ T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ $$ T_{avg} = (0.90)(5) + (0.10)(200) $$ $$ T_{avg} = 4.5 + 20 = 24.5 \text{ ns} $$ **∴ AMAT = 24.5 ns** *(3 marks)* **Part (a)(ii):** New hit ratio: $H_{new} = 0.98$, so $(1 - H_{new}) = 0.02$. $$ T_{avg,new} = (0.98)(5) + (0.02)(200) $$ $$ T_{avg,new} = 4.9 + 4.0 = 8.9 \text{ ns} $$ **Percentage reduction:** $$ \text{Reduction \%} = \frac{T_{avg} - T_{avg,new}}{T_{avg}} \times 100 $$ $$ = \frac{24.5 - 8.9}{24.5} \times 100 = \frac{15.6}{24.5} \times 100 \approx 63.67\% $$ **∴ New AMAT = 8.9 ns, with a 63.67% reduction.** *(4 marks)* **Part (b):** **Temporal Locality** is the principle that *if a memory location is accessed now, it is highly likely to be accessed again in the near future*. Example: loop counters, accumulator variables, and stack pointers in iterative code. **Spatial Locality** is the principle that *if a memory location is accessed, neighboring locations (with adjacent addresses) are likely to be accessed soon*. Example: sequential execution of instructions, array traversals. **Justification for Cache Design:** 1. When the CPU fetches an instruction at address $X$, *temporal locality* suggests $X$ will be re-used → the cache retains it for fast re-access. 2. When a program accesses an array element $A[i]$, *spatial locality* suggests $A[i+1], A[i+2], \dots$ will follow → the cache fetches a *block* of consecutive bytes (typically 64 bytes) in a single transfer, satisfying several future accesses. 3. Together, these principles ensure a high Hit Ratio (typically 0.95+), validating the cost-effectiveness of the cache. *(7 marks for Part b)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample]** **(a)** Derive the formula for Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) in a single-level cache system. A system has a hit ratio of 0.85, $T_c = 3$ ns, and $T_m = 120$ ns. Calculate AMAT. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the memory hierarchy in a modern computer system. Discuss the role of cache memory in bridging the processor-memory speed gap. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** *Step 1:* Every memory access is either a Hit (probability $H$) or a Miss (probability $1-H$). *Step 2:* On a Hit, time taken is $T_c$. On a Miss, time taken is $T_m$. *Step 3:* The expected (average) time is: $$ T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m $$ *Step 4:* Rewriting: $$ T_{avg} = T_m - H(T_m - T_c) = T_c + (1 - H) T_p $$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given: $H = 0.85$, $T_c = 3$ ns, $T_m = 120$ ns. $$ T_{avg} = (0.85)(3) + (0.15)(120) $$ $$ T_{avg} = 2.55 + 18.00 = 20.55 \text{ ns} $$ **∴ AMAT = 20.55 ns** *(7 marks)* **Part (b) — Memory Hierarchy Block Diagram:** The memory hierarchy from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest: | Level | Component | Typical Size | Access Time | |---|---|---|---| | L0 | CPU Registers | Bytes–KB | ~1 cycle | | L1 | L1 Cache (SRAM) | 32–128 KB | 1–3 cycles | | L2 | L2 Cache (SRAM) | 256 KB – 8 MB | 5–15 cycles | | L3 | L3 Cache (SRAM, shared) | 8–64 MB | 20–50 cycles | | L4 | Main Memory (DRAM) | 4–64 GB | 50–100 cycles | | L5 | SSD/HDD | 256 GB – 4 TB | μs–ms | **Role of Cache in Bridging the Speed Gap:** The CPU operates at GHz frequencies, issuing memory requests every ~1 ns. Main memory (DRAM) responds in ~100 ns — a 100× mismatch. Placing a small, fast SRAM cache between them exploits locality to *amortize* this slow access. Even with a 95% hit rate, the system perceives an AMAT of ~7 ns instead of 100 ns, effectively closing the speed gap. *(7 marks)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:** - ⭐ **Cache Hit** = Address found in cache → fast access ($T_c$). - ⭐ **Cache Miss** = Address NOT in cache → slow access ($T_m$ + cache update). - ⭐ **Hit Ratio** $H = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{total}}$, always in range **[0, 1]**. - ⭐ **Miss Ratio** = $1 - H$. - ⭐ **Master Formula:** $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$. - ⭐ **Alternative Form:** $T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p$, where $T_p = T_m - T_c$. - ⭐ **Hit Rate in %:** Multiply $H$ by 100. E.g., $H = 0.92$ → Hit Rate = 92%. - ⭐ **Speedup:** $S = T_m / T_{avg}$ — measures cache benefit over no-cache system. - ⭐ **Locality Principles:** Temporal (re-use) + Spatial (near-neighbor) → drive cache design. - ⭐ **Exponential Sensitivity:** Small improvements in $H$ near 0.99 yield large AMAT reductions. - ⭐ **Always convert** percentage hit rates to decimals before substituting into AMAT formula. - ⭐ **State units clearly** in numerical answers (ns, μs, or cycles) — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

topics.title: Types of Cache Mapping: Direct, Associative, and Set Associative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Cache Mapping: Direct, Associative, and Set Associative

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cache Mapping (also called Address Mapping or Placement Policy)** is the algorithmic mechanism used by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) and cache controller hardware to determine the *exact location* in the cache memory where a particular block (or line) of data fetched from the main memory (RAM) will be stored. It defines the correspondence between the main memory address space and the limited, much smaller cache memory address space.

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus classifies cache mapping into three principal categories:

1. **Direct Mapping** — A *one-to-one* deterministic placement policy.
2. **Fully Associative Mapping** — A *one-to-many* flexible placement policy.
3. **Set Associative Mapping** — A *hybrid n-to-one grouped* placement policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners consistently test the *address partitioning* (Tag + Index + Offset fields) and the *hardware search mechanism* (comparator logic) for each mapping type. Mastery of these two aspects guarantees marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Shelf" Intuition

Imagine a massive central library (the **Main Memory**) with millions of books, and you have only a small 4-drawer cabinet on your desk (the **Cache**). When you pull a book from the library, into which drawer will you place it?

- **Direct Mapping (The Strict Drawer Rule):** "All books whose ISBN ends in `1` go to Drawer 1, those ending in `2` go to Drawer 2, etc." Simple to organize and quick to find, but two frequently needed books (e.g., ISBN `1` and `11`) will constantly *evict* each other.
- **Fully Associative (The Free Roaming Rule):** "Drop the book into *any* empty drawer; you'll scan every drawer's label to find it later." Maximum flexibility, but searching is slow and the filing system is expensive to build.
- **Set Associative (The Themed Drawer Rule):** "Group drawers by subject (Math, Physics, etc.) — within that subject's drawer-set, the book can go in any drawer." Balanced trade-off between speed and flexibility. *This is what real CPUs use.*

> [!NOTE]
> A **Tag** in cache mapping is essentially a unique identifier stored alongside the data — analogous to writing the full ISBN of the book on a sticky note attached to the front of the drawer. When the CPU asks "is my desired book here?", it compares the requested ISBN against the sticky notes — a mismatch means a *cache miss* even if the drawer index is correct.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- Cache size is always a power of **2** (in Bytes).
- Block size (line size) is always a power of **2** (typically 16, 32, 64, or **128 Bytes**).
- The unit of transfer between cache and main memory is one **Block** (or **Line**).
- Performance is primarily measured by **Hit Ratio (H)**, where $H = \frac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Memory Accesses}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Address Partitioning (The Heart of Cache Mapping)

For any physical memory address, the bits are partitioned into three fields. The *width* of each field changes depending on the mapping strategy.

| Mapping Type | Address Format (MSB → LSB) | Partitioning Logic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct** | `TAG + LINE_INDEX + BLOCK_OFFSET` | Determines the *single unique* line where the block may live. |
| **Fully Associative** | `TAG + BLOCK_OFFSET` | No index exists; the *Tag* must be searched everywhere. |
| **Set Associative (k-way)** | `TAG + SET_INDEX + BLOCK_OFFSET` | Determines the *set* where the block may live; the *Tag* is searched within that set's k lines. |

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown of Each Mapping Type

#### 2.2.1 Direct Mapping
- **How it works:** Main memory block `i` is mapped to cache line `(i mod N)`, where `N` = total number of cache lines.
- **Hardware Logic:** A single comparator suffices. The incoming `LINE_INDEX` acts as a direct pointer (like a hash table with a single bucket).
- **Pros:** Fastest lookup, simplest hardware (cheapest).
- **Cons:** High *conflict misses* — if two popular blocks hash to the same index, they thrash.
- **Replacement Policy:** None needed — a new block **unconditionally** overwrites the old one at its mandated index.

#### 2.2.2 Fully Associative Mapping
- **How it works:** A memory block can be placed in **any** of the `N` cache lines.
- **Hardware Logic:** Requires `N` parallel comparators (one per line) that compare the incoming `TAG` against all stored tags simultaneously. This is a **Content-Addressable Memory (CAM)**.
- **Pros:** Zero conflict misses, lowest miss rate of the three.
- **Cons:** Hardware cost scales with `N` — for a 64KB cache with 4-byte words, you need **16,384** comparators — practically infeasible for large caches.
- **Replacement Policy:** Required (e.g., **LRU**, **FIFO**, or **Random**).

#### 2.2.3 Set Associative Mapping (k-way)
- **How it works:** The cache is divided into `S` sets, each holding `k` lines. Memory block `i` maps to set `(i mod S)`, but within that set, it can occupy any of the `k` lines.
- **Hardware Logic:** Only `k` comparators are needed per set (one set is active at a time based on `SET_INDEX`).
- **Pros:** Excellent compromise — reduces conflict misses while keeping hardware tractable.
- **Cons:** Slightly slower than direct mapping.
- **Real-world Use:** Modern L1/L2/L3 caches in Intel/AMD/ARM processors are almost universally **8-way** or **16-way** set associative.
- **Special Cases:** `k = 1` reduces to Direct Mapping; `k = N` reduces to Fully Associative.

> [!NOTE]
> The most important conceptual takeaway: **Set Associative = Direct Mapping at the Set level + Fully Associative within each Set.** This is why it inherits the strengths of both.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used below:**
> - $M$ = Total Main Memory size (in Bytes or Words)
> - $C$ = Total Cache size
> - $B$ = Block size (Line size)
> - $N$ = Number of cache lines $= C / B$
> - $S$ = Number of sets
> - $k$ = Associativity (ways per set)
> - $w$ = Address word length (in bits)
> - $s$ = Set index bits, $r$ = Line index bits, $b$ = Block offset bits, $t$ = Tag bits

| # | Parameter | Direct Mapping | Fully Associative | Set Associative (k-way) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Offset bits ($b$) | $b = \log_2 B$ | $b = \log_2 B$ | $b = \log_2 B$ |
| 2 | Index bits | $r = \log_2 N$ | None (0 bits) | $s = \log_2 S$ |
| 3 | Tag bits | $t = w - r - b$ | $t = w - b$ | $t = w - s - b$ |
| 4 | Number of lines | $N = C / B$ | $N = C / B$ | $N = S \times k$ |
| 5 | Number of comparators | **1** | $N$ | $k$ (per set) |
| 6 | Replacement logic | Not required | Required (LRU/Random) | Required within set |
| 7 | Address formula | $\text{Line} = \text{Block} \bmod N$ | $\text{Line} = \text{Any free line}$ | $\text{Set} = \text{Block} \bmod S$ |
| 8 | Hit Ratio (typical) | Lowest | Highest | Mid-to-High |
| 9 | Hardware cost | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Moderate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cache mapping is the bedrock of high-performance computing. Engineers designing CPUs, GPUs, and SoCs must choose the *associativity* and *block size* based on the workload:

- **Databases & Servers (OLTP workloads):** Benefit from **high associativity** (16-way or higher) because access patterns are highly random and prone to conflict.
- **Gaming CPUs:** Use **8-way L1 caches** with smaller block sizes for low-latency instruction streaming.
- **Embedded MCUs (ARM Cortex-M):** Often use **direct-mapped** caches to minimize silicon area and power consumption.
- **DSP & Streaming Chips:** Use **fully associative or highly associative** caches to handle predictable streams with minimal misses.

Understanding this trade-off — speed vs. area vs. miss rate — is a direct application of the **Iron Triangle of Computer Architecture** taught across KTU's Computer Engineering curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Problem — Worked Example (Covers All 3 Mappings)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Consider a system with:
> - Main Memory size $M = 16$ KB (Kilobytes)
> - Cache size $C = 1$ KB
> - Block size $B = 4$ Bytes
> - CPU address word length $w = 14$ bits

**Compute** the number of address bits for `Tag`, `Index`/`Set Index`, and `Offset` for *Direct*, *Fully Associative*, and *4-way Set Associative* mappings.

#### 3.1.1 Preliminary Derivation (Common to all three)

**Step 1:** Calculate the Block Offset bits ($b$).
$$ b = \log_2 B = \log_2 4 = 2 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2:** Calculate the total number of cache lines ($N$).
$$ N = \frac{C}{B} = \frac{1 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ B}} = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ lines} $$

**Step 3:** Verify address width covers the whole memory.
$$ M = 2^w \Rightarrow 16 \text{ KB} = 2^{14} \text{ Bytes} \quad \checkmark \text{ (since } 2^{14} = 16384 = 16 \text{ KB)} $$

---

#### 3.1.2 Case A — Direct Mapping

**Step 4A:** Calculate the Line Index bits ($r$).
$$ r = \log_2 N = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 5A:** Calculate the Tag bits ($t$).
$$ t = w - r - b = 14 - 8 - 2 = 4 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Partitioning (Direct):** `Tag (4) + Line Index (8) + Block Offset (2)`

**Step 6A (Sanity check):** Sum of all field widths must equal $w$.
$$ 4 + 8 + 2 = 14 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 7A (Placement Rule):** For a memory block number $i$,
$$ \text{Cache Line Number} = i \bmod 256 $$

---

#### 3.1.3 Case B — Fully Associative Mapping

**Step 4B:** Since every block can go *anywhere*, the Index field is **eliminated** (0 bits).

**Step 5B:** Calculate the Tag bits ($t$).
$$ t = w - b = 14 - 2 = 12 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Partitioning (Fully Associative):** `Tag (12) + Block Offset (2)`

**Step 6B (Sanity check):**
$$ 12 + 0 + 2 = 14 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 7B (Placement Rule):** A block can be placed in *any* of the 256 lines; the 12-bit tag must be compared against all 256 stored tags simultaneously (requiring 256 comparators).

---

#### 3.1.4 Case C — 4-way Set Associative Mapping

**Step 4C:** Calculate the number of sets ($S$).
$$ S = \frac{N}{k} = \frac{256}{4} = 64 \text{ sets} $$

**Step 5C:** Calculate the Set Index bits ($s$).
$$ s = \log_2 S = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 6C:** Calculate the Tag bits ($t$).
$$ t = w - s - b = 14 - 6 - 2 = 6 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Partitioning (4-way Set Associative):** `Tag (6) + Set Index (6) + Block Offset (2)`

**Step 7C (Sanity check):**
$$ 6 + 6 + 2 = 14 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 8C (Placement Rule):** For a memory block number $i$,
$$ \text{Set Number} = i \bmod 64 $$

Within that set, the block can be loaded into any of the 4 available lines (searched via 4 parallel comparators).

---

### 3.2 Comparative Summary Table for the Worked Example

| Mapping Type | Tag | Index | Offset | Comparators |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Direct | **4** | **8** | **2** | **1** |
| Fully Associative | **12** | **0** | **2** | **256** |
| 4-way Set Associative | **6** | **6** | **2** | **4** |

### 3.3 AMAT (Average Memory Access Time) — Performance Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> AMAT is frequently asked as a follow-up in KTU problems linking mapping theory with performance metrics.

**Formula (Hierarchical Memory Model):**
$$ \text{AMAT} = T_{\text{cache}} + (\text{Miss Rate}) \times T_{\text{main memory penalty}} $$

**Step-by-Step Computation Example:**
Let:
- Cache access time $T_c = 10$ ns
- Main memory access time $T_m = 100$ ns
- Miss rate for Direct Mapping $= 8\%$
- Miss rate for Fully Associative $= 3\%$

**Direct Mapping AMAT:**
$$ \text{AMAT}_{\text{direct}} = 10 + (0.08)(100) = 10 + 8 = 18 \text{ ns} $$

**Fully Associative AMAT:**
$$ \text{AMAT}_{\text{fully}} = 10 + (0.03)(100) = 10 + 3 = 13 \text{ ns} $$

**Performance Gain (Direct → Fully Associative):**
$$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{18}{13} \approx 1.38 \times \text{ faster} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reality Check:** While fully associative yields a 38% theoretical speedup, the hardware cost (256 comparators vs. 1) is rarely justified. **8-way set associative** typically delivers ~95% of fully associative's performance at a fraction of the cost — this is exactly why KTU examiners test these trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Address Partitioning — Visual Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIRECT ["DIRECT MAPPING ADDRESS FORMAT"]
        direction LR
        D1["TAG: t bits"]:::tag --> D2["LINE INDEX: r bits"]:::idx --> D3["BLOCK OFFSET: b bits"]:::off
    end

    subgraph FULLY ["FULLY ASSOCIATIVE ADDRESS FORMAT"]
        direction LR
        F1["TAG: t bits"]:::tag --> F2["NO INDEX FIELD: 0 bits"]:::none --> F3["BLOCK OFFSET: b bits"]:::off
    end

    subgraph SETA ["SET ASSOCIATIVE k-way ADDRESS FORMAT"]
        direction LR
        S1["TAG: t bits"]:::tag --> S2["SET INDEX: s bits"]:::idx --> S3["BLOCK OFFSET: b bits"]:::off
    end

    classDef tag fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef idx fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef off fill:#14532d,stroke:#4ade80,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef none fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Direct Mapping Hardware Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Issues Address w bits"]:::cpu --> PART["Address Partitioner"]:::part
    PART -->|"Offset b bits"| OFFSET["Word Selector within Block"]:::logic
    PART -->|"Line Index r bits"| DEC["Cache Line Decoder"]:::logic
    PART -->|"Tag t bits"| CMP["Tag Comparator"]:::cmp
    CACHE["Cache Memory N lines"]:::mem --> CMP
    CMP -->|Match| HIT["CACHE HIT Output Data"]:::hit
    CMP -->|Mismatch| MISS["CACHE MISS Fetch from RAM"]:::miss
    MISS -.->|Update Line| CACHE

    classDef cpu fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef part fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#713f12,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cmp fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mem fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hit fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef miss fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Set Associative k-way Search Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU2["CPU Issues Address"]:::cpu --> PART2["Address Partitioner"]:::part
    PART2 -->|"Offset b"| OFF2["Byte/Word Selector"]:::logic
    PART2 -->|"Set Index s"| DEC2["Set Decoder"]:::logic
    PART2 -->|"Tag t"| CMPARR["k Parallel Comparators"]:::cmp

    DEC2 --> SET0["Set 0: k lines + k tags"]:::mem
    DEC2 --> SET1["Set 1: k lines + k tags"]:::mem
    DEC2 --> SETN["Set S-1: k lines + k tags"]:::mem

    SET0 --> CMPARR
    SET1 --> CMPARR
    SETN --> CMPARR

    CMPARR -->|One Match| HIT2["HIT Select that line"]:::hit
    CMPARR -->|No Match| MISS2["MISS Apply LRU Replacement"]:::miss
    MISS2 -.->|Evict + Load| SET0 & SET1 & SETN

    classDef cpu fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef part fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#713f12,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cmp fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#f87171,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mem fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hit fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef miss fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Block Placement Logic — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["New Memory Block Arrives"]:::start --> Q1{"Mapping Type?"}:::q

    Q1 -->|"Direct"| A["Compute i mod N → Single Fixed Line"]:::logicA
    A --> A2["Overwrite existing block unconditionally"]:::logicA
    A2 --> ENDX["Done — No replacement policy"]:::end

    Q1 -->|"Fully Associative"| B["Search all N lines for empty or victim"]:::logicB
    B --> B2["Apply LRU / FIFO / Random to pick victim"]:::logicB
    B2 --> B3["Load block into chosen line"]:::logicB
    B3 --> ENDY["Done — 1 comparator per line required"]:::end

    Q1 -->|"Set Associative k-way"| C["Compute i mod S → Target Set"]:::logicC
    C --> C2["Search only k lines inside that set"]:::logicC
    C2 --> C3{"Set full?"}:::q
    C3 -->|No| C4["Load into first empty line"]:::logicC
    C3 -->|Yes| C5["Apply LRU within set to evict"]:::logicC
    C5 --> ENDZ["Done — k comparators required"]:::end
    C4 --> ENDZ

    classDef start fill:#312e81,stroke:#a5b4fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef q fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef logicA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#93c5fd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef logicB fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef logicC fill:#581c87,stroke:#d8b4fe,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef end fill:#374151,stroke:#d1d5db,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **Direct Mapping** and **Associative Mapping** in cache memory.

**Model Answer:**
Direct Mapping places each main memory block into one specific cache line (Block `i` → Line `i mod N`), requiring only **1 comparator** and no replacement policy. Associative Mapping allows a block to be placed in **any** cache line, requiring **N comparators** (parallel tag matching) and a replacement policy (LRU/FIFO). Direct is faster but suffers from conflict misses; Associative has minimum misses but is hardware-expensive.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**Q2.** What is a **Tag** in the context of cache mapping? Why is it needed?

**Model Answer:**
A Tag is a portion of the physical memory address (the upper bits) stored alongside each cache line. It is needed because multiple main memory blocks may map to the *same* cache line (or set). The Tag uniquely identifies *which* memory block currently occupies that line. During a lookup, the incoming Tag is compared with the stored Tag — a match indicates a Hit; a mismatch indicates a Miss (despite a correct index).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, ESE Pattern: Internal Choice Provided]**

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks) — Compute-Heavy**

**(a)** A system has a main memory of **64 KB** and a cache of **2 KB**. The block size is **8 bytes**. Compute the address partitioning (Tag, Index, Offset) for:
   (i) Direct Mapping  [3 marks]
   (ii) Fully Associative Mapping  [2 marks]
   (iii) 2-way Set Associative Mapping  [2 marks]

**(b)** If the cache access time is **20 ns** and main memory access time is **200 ns**, compute the **AMAT** for a program with hit ratio **0.92** in direct-mapped mode.  [7 marks]

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Solution to (a)(i) — Direct Mapping:**

**Step 1:** Compute address word length ($w$).
$$ w = \log_2(64 \text{ KB}) = \log_2(2^{16}) = 16 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2:** Compute Block Offset bits ($b$).
$$ b = \log_2(8) = 3 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3:** Compute number of cache lines ($N$).
$$ N = \frac{C}{B} = \frac{2 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ B}} = \frac{2048}{8} = 256 \text{ lines} $$

**Step 4:** Compute Line Index bits ($r$).
$$ r = \log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 5:** Compute Tag bits ($t$).
$$ t = w - r - b = 16 - 8 - 3 = 5 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Format:** `Tag(5) + Line Index(8) + Block Offset(3)`

---

**Solution to (a)(ii) — Fully Associative Mapping:**

**Step 1:** No index field; only Tag and Offset.
$$ \text{Index bits} = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Tag bits:
$$ t = w - b = 16 - 3 = 13 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Format:** `Tag(13) + Block Offset(3)`

---

**Solution to (a)(iii) — 2-way Set Associative Mapping:**

**Step 1:** Number of sets.
$$ S = \frac{N}{k} = \frac{256}{2} = 128 \text{ sets} $$

**Step 2:** Set Index bits.
$$ s = \log_2(128) = 7 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3:** Tag bits.
$$ t = w - s - b = 16 - 7 - 3 = 6 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Format:** `Tag(6) + Set Index(7) + Block Offset(3)`

---

**Solution to (b) — AMAT Computation:**

**Given:**
- $T_c = 20$ ns (cache access time)
- $T_m = 200$ ns (main memory access time)
- Hit ratio $H = 0.92 \Rightarrow$ Miss rate $= 1 - 0.92 = 0.08$

**AMAT Formula:**
$$ \text{AMAT} = T_c + (\text{Miss Rate}) \times T_m $$

**Substituting values:**
$$ \text{AMAT} = 20 + (0.08)(200) $$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**
$$ \text{AMAT} = 20 + 16 = 36 \text{ ns} $$

**Conclusion:** The average memory access time for this system is **36 nanoseconds**.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Concept-Heavy Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of cache mapping** with neat diagrams of the address formats. Compare them on the basis of **hit ratio**, **hardware cost**, and **replacement policy**.  [7 marks]

**(b)** A computer uses a **4-way set associative cache** of total size **32 KB** with a **block size of 16 bytes**. The CPU generates **24-bit addresses**.
   (i) Find the number of sets.  [2 marks]
   (ii) Compute the number of Tag, Index, and Offset bits.  [3 marks]
   (iii) How many comparators are required?  [2 marks]

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Solution to (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Model answer structured exactly as KTU evaluators expect: Definition → Diagram → Comparison Table.*

**1. Direct Mapping:**
Each main memory block `i` maps to exactly one cache line given by `i mod N`. The address is partitioned into **Tag | Line Index | Block Offset**. Hardware is minimal (1 comparator), but it has the **lowest hit ratio** due to forced conflict misses. No replacement policy is required.

**2. Fully Associative Mapping:**
A block can reside in **any** cache line. The address contains **Tag | Block Offset** (no index). All tags are compared in parallel using a **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)**. It has the **highest hit ratio** and the **highest hardware cost** (N comparators). A replacement policy like LRU is mandatory.

**3. Set Associative Mapping (k-way):**
Cache is divided into `S` sets of `k` lines each. A block maps to set `i mod S` and can occupy any of the `k` lines in that set. Address: **Tag | Set Index | Block Offset**. It achieves a **near-optimal hit ratio** (close to fully associative) at a **moderate hardware cost** (`k` comparators). LRU is applied *within* a set.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Direct | Fully Associative | Set Associative |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Hit Ratio | Lowest | **Highest** | High |
| Hardware Cost | **Lowest** (1 comparator) | Highest (N comparators) | Moderate (k comparators) |
| Replacement Policy | Not needed | Required | Required (within set) |
| Speed | **Fastest** | Slowest | Moderate |
| Conflict Misses | **Maximum** | **Zero** | Few |

**Conclusion:** Set associative mapping is the *industry-standard compromise* and is used in virtually all modern processors.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 (i):** Calculate the number of lines and sets.
$$ N = \frac{C}{B} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \frac{32768}{16} = 2048 \text{ lines} $$

$$ S = \frac{N}{k} = \frac{2048}{4} = 512 \text{ sets} $$

**Step 2 (ii):** Compute all bit-fields.
$$ w = 24 \text{ bits} $$

$$ b = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits} $$

$$ s = \log_2(512) = 9 \text{ bits} $$

$$ t = w - s - b = 24 - 9 - 4 = 11 \text{ bits} $$

**Address Format:** `Tag(11) + Set Index(9) + Block Offset(4)`

**Step 3 (iii):** Comparators required.
$$ \text{Comparators} = k = 4 \text{ (one per way within a set)} $$

**Verification:** $11 + 9 + 4 = 24$ bits ✓

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Wrong tag computation:** Many students compute $t$ using $w - r - b$ *only* for direct mapping, but forget to substitute $s$ (set bits) instead of $r$ in set-associative problems.
> 2. **Unit confusion in $B$ and $C$:** Mixing KB and Bytes silently. Always convert to the same unit *first* (e.g., $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ B}$).
> 3. **Forgetting comparator count:** Direct = **1**; Fully Associative = **N**; Set Associative = **k**. Examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for this.
> 4. **Missing replacement policy mention:** Direct mapping has *no* replacement; Fully and Set Associative *do* — this is a compulsory 1-mark point.
> 5. **AMAT formula misuse:** Some students write $\text{AMAT} = H \times T_c + (1-H) \times T_m$ and add $T_c$ again, double-counting. Use exactly **one** canonical form.
> 6. **Skipping the sanity check** ($t + r + b = w$): Showing this verification earns the "presentation" mark that KTU examiners grant for neat work.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cache mapping = a placement policy** defining where each main memory block is stored in the cache.
- **Direct Mapping:** `Block i → Cache Line (i mod N)`. **Pros:** Fastest, cheapest. **Cons:** Worst hit ratio. **Comparators:** 1.
- **Fully Associative Mapping:** Block can go *anywhere*. **Pros:** Best hit ratio. **Cons:** N comparators (CAM). **Replacement:** Mandatory (LRU).
- **Set Associative (k-way):** Block goes to set `(i mod S)`, then any of the k lines within. **Pros:** Near-optimal balance. **Cons:** k comparators. **Real-world default** (8-way, 16-way).
- **Address field formulas (must memorize):**
  - $b = \log_2 B$
  - $r = \log_2 N$ (Direct)
  - $s = \log_2 S$ (Set Associative)
  - $t = w - r - b$ or $t = w - s - b$
  - Always verify: $t + \text{index} + b = w$
- **AMAT Formula:** $\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \times T_m$
- **Sanity-check ritual:** Add all field widths; must equal $w$. Do this on *every* problem.
- **Block size is fixed** in all three mappings — only the index/tag split changes.
- **K-way Set Associative = Direct at set level + Fully Associative within set.** (Highest-yield one-liner for KTU viva.)
- **Real CPUs use Set Associative** — remember this to justify answers in design-type questions.
- **AMAT** is the performance metric that links mapping *theory* with mapping *practice* — always quote it in conclusion-style questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Memory Systems: Demand Paging and Memory Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory Systems: Demand Paging and Memory Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Virtual Memory — Formal Academic Definition

**Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique that provides an idealized abstraction of the storage resources available to a program, by decoupling logical (virtual) addresses used by processes from physical (real) addresses in main memory (RAM). It allows execution of processes that may not be completely resident in physical memory by using **secondary storage (disk)** as an extension of main memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Virtual memory is a hardware/software subsystem that permits the execution of processes whose logical address space is larger than the available physical memory, by automatically moving portions (pages or segments) between main memory and secondary storage while preserving program correctness and isolation.

### 1.2 Demand Paging — The Core Concept

**Demand Paging** is a specific implementation strategy of virtual memory in which pages of a process are loaded into physical memory **only when they are demanded (referenced)** by the executing process, rather than loading the entire process upfront. A **page fault** (or demand miss) is the hardware-generated exception that occurs when a referenced page is not currently present in main memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lazy Loading Principle:** "Don't load a page until it is absolutely required." This minimizes I/O, reduces startup latency, and allows the system to run more processes concurrently.

### 1.3 Memory Protection — The Security & Isolation Layer

**Memory Protection** refers to the set of hardware and software mechanisms that prevent unauthorized, accidental, or erroneous access to memory regions belonging to other processes, the operating system kernel, or protected system areas. Common protection primitives include:

- **Base / Limit Registers** (segment-level protection)
- **Protection Bits** within Page Table Entries (PTE) — Valid, Read/Write, Execute, User/Supervisor
- **Ring Levels / Privilege Levels** (e.g., x86 Ring 0 to Ring 3)
- **Memory Management Unit (MMU) Hardware Enforcement**

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Library Book Analogy — Why Virtual Memory is Like a Massive Library Desk**
>
> Imagine you are a researcher with a tiny desk (your **physical memory / RAM**) but an enormous personal locker (your **virtual address space**) containing 10,000 books. You cannot keep all books on your desk at once. The librarian's catalog is your **page table**, mapping each book title (virtual address) to a shelf location (physical address or "out for retrieval" notice).
>
> 1. When you pick up a book title to read — this is a **memory reference (logical address generation)**.
> 2. The librarian checks the catalog. If the book is on your desk, you get it instantly — **TLB hit / page hit**.
> 3. If the book is in your locker but not on the desk, the librarian fetches it and places it on the desk — **page fault handling (disk I/O)**.
> 4. If you try to access a book belonging to another researcher, the librarian stops you — this is **memory protection violation**.
>
> Your desk is small, but you have the *illusion* of working with the entire library — the essence of virtual memory.

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Physical memory access time ($T_{mem}$) | **10 – 100 ns** |
| TLB access time ($T_{TLB}$) | **1 – 10 ns** |
| Disk access time (page fault service) | **1 – 10 ms (≈ 10⁶ ns)** |
| Page size (typical) | **4 KB – 64 KB** |
| Effective Access Time (EAT) target | **Close to $T_{mem}$** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Demand Paging — Operational Logic

The demand paging mechanism operates in a strict, deterministic sequence:

- **Step 1 — Address Generation:** CPU generates a logical (virtual) address comprising a *Virtual Page Number (VPN)* and an *offset* within the page.
- **Step 2 — MMU Translation:** The Memory Management Unit consults the page table using the VPN.
- **Step 3 — Validation Check:** The MMU inspects the **Valid/Invalid (V/I) bit** in the corresponding Page Table Entry (PTE).
  - *If Valid bit = 1:* Frame number is extracted; physical address is formed by concatenating the frame number with the offset.
  - *If Valid bit = 0:* A **page fault (trap to OS)** is raised.
- **Step 4 — Page Fault Service (OS routine):**
  1. Check if the reference is legal (protection check).
  2. Locate a free frame in physical memory; if none, invoke a **page replacement algorithm** (e.g., LRU, FIFO, Clock).
  3. Issue disk I/O to read the demanded page from swap space into the chosen frame.
  4. Update the PTE with the new frame number and set Valid bit = 1.
  5. Restart the faulting instruction.
- **Step 5 — Resume Execution:** CPU re-executes the instruction, which now completes successfully (assuming no further faults).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the instruction is restarted, not resumed:** Because the fault may have occurred *before* the instruction modified destination registers or memory. Resuming could lead to inconsistent state. This is a common KTU viva/ESE question.

### 2.2 Memory Protection — Mechanism Deep Dive

Memory protection in a paged system is implemented at the PTE level and enforced by the MMU hardware. Each Page Table Entry contains:

- **Valid (V) Bit:** `1` = page in physical memory; `0` = page absent or invalid.
- **Read / Write (R/W) Bit:** `0` = read-only; `1` = read/write.
- **User / Supervisor (U/S) Bit:** `0` = kernel-mode only; `1` = user-mode accessible.
- **Execute (X) Bit:** `1` = executable; `0` = data only (prevents code injection).
- **Dirty (D) Bit:** `1` = page modified; useful for write-back policies.
- **Reference (A) Bit:** `1` = page accessed; useful for LRU approximation.

Protection violations trigger **segmentation faults (SIGSEGV)** on Unix/Linux or **access violations** on Windows.

> [!NOTE]
> **The MMU is the hardware gatekeeper.** It inspects protection bits on *every* memory reference. If the access is illegal, it aborts the access and raises an exception — this is the *only* reliable protection mechanism; software checks alone are insufficient for security.

### 2.3 Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) — The Performance Accelerator

A TLB is a small, fully-associative, hardware cache that stores recent **VPN → PPN** translations. It is the cornerstone of high-performance demand paging.

- **TLB Hit:** Translation is available in TLB → no page table walk required.
- **TLB Miss:** Page table must be walked in physical memory to fetch the PTE.
- **TLB Flush:** Occurs on context switch (in non-tagged TLBs), which can degrade performance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value — never the raw pipe `|`.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $EAT$ | $(1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault}$ | Basic Effective Access Time (no TLB) | ns |
| $EAT_{TLB}$ | $h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{mem}) + p \cdot T_{fault}$ | EAT with TLB | ns |
| $AS$ (Amdahl-style speedup) | $T_{mem} / EAT$ | Memory access speedup factor | dimensionless |
| $P_{size}$ | $2^{offset\_bits}$ | Page size in bytes | bytes |
| $VPN$ | $\lfloor V\_addr / P_{size} \rfloor$ | Virtual page number | integer |
| $offset$ | $V\_addr \mod P_{size}$ | Byte offset within page | integer |
| $P_{fault\_rate}$ | $N_{faults} / N_{refs}$ | Probability of page fault | 0 to 1 |
| $T_{fault}$ | $T_{service} + T_{transfer} + T_{restart}$ | Page fault service time | ns |
| $TLB\_hit\_ratio$ | $h = N_{hits} / (N_{hits} + N_{misses})$ | TLB effectiveness | 0 to 1 |
| $MPN$ | $Memory\_pages / Page\_size$ | Mapping granularity | pages |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Linux uses demand paging with the **Clock-Pro** algorithm; Windows uses a variant of the working-set model.
- **Embedded Systems:** Memory-constrained IoT devices rely on demand paging to run complex applications on tiny RAM.
- **Database Systems:** Buffer pool managers in PostgreSQL/MySQL use page-level demand loading analogous to OS demand paging.
- **Security:** Memory protection via page table bits is the foundation of process isolation, sandboxing, and modern exploit mitigations (DEP/NX bit, ASLR, KPTI).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Access Time (EAT) Without TLB

We derive the average memory access time considering only the two cases: page hit and page fault.

**Step 1 — Define the random variables.**
Let $p$ be the page fault rate (probability of a page fault per memory reference), and $T_{mem}$ be the access time to physical memory in nanoseconds. Let $T_{fault}$ be the total time to service a page fault, including disk I/O, kernel overhead, and instruction restart.

**Step 2 — Express the time per reference as a weighted sum.**
A memory reference either hits in memory (probability $1 - p$) or faults (probability $p$). By the law of total expectation:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{hit} + p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the hit time.**
When a page is present in physical memory, the only cost is one memory access, so $T_{hit} = T_{mem}$:

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand for clarity.**
This is the canonical KTU formula:

$$
EAT = T_{mem} - p \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

$$
EAT = T_{mem} + p \cdot (T_{fault} - T_{mem})
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
Since $T_{fault} \gg T_{mem}$ (typically $10^6$ ns vs. $100$ ns), even a tiny $p$ dramatically increases $EAT$. For example, if $p = 0.001$ and $T_{fault} = 1$ ms, then $EAT \approx 1000$ ns, which is **10× slower** than a no-fault access. This is why minimizing $p$ is the central goal of virtual memory design.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — EAT With TLB

**Step 1 — Identify three regimes.**
With a TLB, the access can result in:
- **TLB hit** (probability $h$): one TLB access + one memory access.
- **TLB miss, page hit** (probability $(1-h)(1-p)$): one TLB access + two memory accesses (page table walk + data).
- **TLB miss, page fault** (probability $(1-h) \cdot p$): TLB + page table walk + page fault service.

**Step 2 — Write the EAT expansion.**

$$
EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot (1-p) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by assuming $T_{TLB} \ll T_{mem}$ and small $p$.**
For typical workloads where $p$ is negligible:

$$
EAT \approx h \cdot T_{mem} + (1-h) \cdot 2 \cdot T_{mem}
$$

**Step 4 — Further simplify.**

$$
EAT = T_{mem} \cdot (2 - h)
$$

**Step 5 — Quantify the impact.**
A TLB hit ratio of $h = 0.98$ yields $EAT = T_{mem} \cdot 1.02$, i.e., only 2% overhead — proving why a high TLB hit rate is critical.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Protection Violation Probability

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
In a system with $N$ processes, each occupying $F$ frames of memory, what is the probability of a protection violation given uniform random page access?

**Step 2 — Define the protected area.**
Suppose the OS reserves $R$ frames for its own use. The probability that a process accesses one of its own frames is:

$$
P_{legal} = \frac{F}{F + R}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the violation probability.**

$$
P_{violation} = 1 - P_{legal} = \frac{R}{F + R}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for realistic workload locality.**
With locality, effective access distribution is non-uniform. Using an exponential locality model $P(access) \propto e^{-\lambda d}$, the effective violation probability is bounded by:

$$
P_{violation}^{eff} \leq \frac{R}{F + R} \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot M})
$$

where $M$ is the memory access locality window. This is rarely computed in KTU exams but illustrates the concept.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These problems are KTU-style — solve them yourself using the formulas above and verify the answers.

**Problem A — EAT Computation (No TLB)**
Given: $T_{mem} = 100$ ns, $T_{fault} = 5$ ms = $5 \times 10^6$ ns, $p = 0.0001$.

Substitute into the EAT formula:

$$
EAT = (1 - 0.0001) \cdot 100 + 0.0001 \cdot (5 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
EAT = 0.9999 \cdot 100 + 500
$$

$$
EAT = 99.99 + 500 = 599.99 \text{ ns} \approx 600 \text{ ns}
$$

**Conclusion:** The page fault, although rare (1 in 10,000), inflates EAT by **6×**.

**Problem B — EAT With TLB**
Given: $h = 0.95$, $T_{TLB} = 5$ ns, $T_{mem} = 100$ ns, $T_{fault} = 5$ ms, $p = 0.0001$.

$$
EAT = 0.95 \cdot (5 + 100) + 0.05 \cdot 0.9999 \cdot (5 + 200) + 0.05 \cdot 0.0001 \cdot 5 \times 10^6
$$

$$
EAT = 0.95 \cdot 105 + 0.049995 \cdot 205 + 0.000005 \cdot 5 \times 10^6
$$

$$
EAT = 99.75 + 10.249 + 25
$$

$$
EAT = 135 \text{ ns}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 95% TLB hit rate keeps EAT close to physical memory latency despite occasional page faults.

### 3.5 Detailed Address Translation Walkthrough

**Given:** 32-bit virtual address, 4 KB page size (12-bit offset), single-level page table.

**Step 1 — Compute offset bits.**

$$
offset\_bits = \log_2(4096) = 12
$$

**Step 2 — Compute VPN bits.**

$$
VPN\_bits = 32 - 12 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Extract components from virtual address `0x0000_3F25`.**
Convert to binary: `0000 0000 0000 0000 0011 1111 0010 0101`.
- Lower 12 bits (offset): `0x025` = `0000 0010 0101` → decimal `37`.
- Upper 20 bits (VPN): `0x0003F` = decimal `63`.

**Step 4 — Page table lookup.**
Page table entry 63 contains: `PPN = 0x00005`, `V = 1`, `R/W = 1`, `U/S = 1`.

**Step 5 — Form physical address.**

$$
P\_addr = (PPN \ll 12) \mid offset
$$

$$
P\_addr = (0x00005 \ll 12) + 37
$$

$$
P\_addr = 0x05000 + 0x025 = 0x05025
$$

**Step 6 — Protection check.**
If the CPU is in user mode and $U/S = 0$, the access is denied and a protection fault is raised.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Demand Paging Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Generates Virtual Address] --> B[MMU Splits Address into VPN and Offset]
    B --> C[Check TLB for VPN]
    C --> D{TLB Hit?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Extract PPN from TLB]
    D -- No --> F[Walk Page Table in Memory]
    F --> G{Valid Bit Set?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update TLB and Extract PPN]
    G -- No --> I[Page Fault Raised to OS]
    I --> J[Check Protection Bits]
    J --> K{Legal Access?}
    K -- No --> L[Protection Fault Process Terminated]
    K -- Yes --> M[Locate Free Frame or Evict Page]
    M --> N[Issue Disk I/O to Load Page]
    N --> O[Update PTE and TLB]
    O --> P[Restart Faulting Instruction]
    H --> Q[Form Physical Address]
    E --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Access Physical Memory]
    R --> S[Return Data to CPU]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PTE[Page Table Entry Layout]
        P1[Valid Bit V]
        P2[Read Write Bit RW]
        P3[User Supervisor Bit US]
        P4[Execute Bit X]
        P5[Dirty Bit D]
        P6[Reference Bit A]
        P7[Frame Number F]
    end
    CPU[CPU with Privilege Level] --> MMU[MMU Hardware]
    MMU --> PTE
    PTE --> Check{Check Protection}
    Check -- Pass --> Access[Memory Access Granted]
    Check -- Fail --> Fault[Protection Fault]
    Fault --> OS[OS Kills Process or Traps to Kernel]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Two-Level Page Table (Hierarchical Translation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VA[32-bit Virtual Address] --> Split[Split into 10 10 12 bits]
    Split --> P1[Level 1 Index PDIR]
    Split --> P2[Level 2 Index PTBL]
    Split --> P3[Offset 12 bits]
    P1 --> PD[Page Directory]
    PD --> PDE[Page Directory Entry PDE]
    PDE --> PT[Page Table PT]
    P2 --> PT
    PT --> PTE[Page Table Entry PTE]
    PTE --> PFN[Physical Frame Number]
    PFN --> Combine[Combine with Offset]
    P3 --> Combine
    Combine --> PA[32-bit Physical Address]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — TLB and Page Table Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU2[CPU] --> VA2[Virtual Address]
    VA2 --> TLBCheck{TLB Lookup}
    TLBCheck -- Hit --> Fast[PPN from TLB]
    TLBCheck -- Miss --> PTWalk[Page Table Walk in Memory]
    PTWalk --> TLBUpdate[Update TLB]
    TLBUpdate --> Fast
    Fast --> PA2[Physical Address to Memory]
    PA2 --> Data2[Data Returned]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q1. Define demand paging. How does it differ from simple paging?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Demand paging is a virtual memory technique in which pages of a process are loaded into physical memory **only when they are first referenced (demanded)** during execution, rather than loading the entire process at once. In **simple (eager) paging**, the entire process is brought into memory at load time. In **demand paging**, a *page fault* is raised when a referenced page is not in memory, and the OS loads the missing page from secondary storage. This lazy approach reduces I/O, supports larger programs, and enables multiprogramming.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **Q2. List any three memory protection mechanisms used in modern processors.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Base and Limit Registers:** Define the legal address range for a process; MMU checks every reference.
> 2. **Protection Bits in PTE:** Valid, Read/Write, Execute, and User/Supervisor bits enforce per-page access control.
> 3. **Privilege Levels / Rings:** Multi-level protection (e.g., x86 Ring 0–3) separates kernel and user code.
> *(Additional acceptable answers: TLB ASIDs for isolation, NX/XD bit, segmentation with descriptors, ASLR.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "$EAT = p \cdot T_{fault}$" forgetting the $(1-p) \cdot T_{mem}$ term. Always include the hit case. (Lose 2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Mixing up VPN and PPN during address translation. Show *both* decimal and hex representations.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "memory protection is done by the OS" — the **MMU hardware** enforces it; the OS only configures the page table. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to account for **TLB miss penalty** in EAT calculations when a TLB is present.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not restarting the faulting instruction — students often write "resume" instead of "restart" (this is critical for KTU).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the demand paging mechanism with a neat diagram. Discuss the steps taken by the OS on a page fault.

**(b) [7 marks]** A system has $T_{mem} = 200$ ns, $T_{TLB} = 10$ ns, $T_{fault} = 8$ ms, TLB hit ratio $h = 0.90$, and page fault rate $p = 0.0002$. Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Demand Paging Mechanism (7 marks)**

Demand paging loads pages on demand. The MMU maintains a **Valid bit** in each PTE. On a memory reference:

1. The MMU extracts the VPN and consults the page table.
2. If the **Valid bit = 1**, the page is in memory → physical address is formed → data is fetched.
3. If the **Valid bit = 0**, a **page fault** trap is raised to the OS.

**OS Page Fault Service Routine (4 sub-steps for 4 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Validity Check:** The OS verifies the reference is legal (protection check). If illegal, the process is terminated (segmentation fault).
- **Step 2 — Free Frame Search:** The OS locates a free frame; if none, invokes a **page replacement algorithm** (FIFO, LRU, Clock).
- **Step 3 — Disk I/O:** The OS schedules disk I/O to read the demanded page from the swap device into the chosen frame. The requesting process is blocked.
- **Step 4 — Update and Restart:** The PTE is updated with the new frame number, the Valid bit is set, the TLB is updated, and the faulting instruction is **restarted** from the beginning.

*(Refer to Mermaid Section 4.1 for the flow diagram; 2 marks for diagram and 1 mark for correct terminologies.)*

**(b) EAT Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $T_{mem} = 200$ ns
- $T_{TLB} = 10$ ns
- $T_{fault} = 8$ ms = $8 \times 10^6$ ns
- $h = 0.90$ (TLB hit ratio)
- $p = 0.0002$ (page fault rate)

**Formula:**

$$
EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot (1-p) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot p \cdot T_{fault}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
EAT = 0.90 \cdot (10 + 200) + 0.10 \cdot 0.9998 \cdot (10 + 400) + 0.10 \cdot 0.0002 \cdot 8 \times 10^6
$$

**Compute each term:**

$$
Term\_1 = 0.90 \cdot 210 = 189 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
Term\_2 = 0.10 \cdot 0.9998 \cdot 410 = 40.99 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
Term\_3 = 0.10 \cdot 0.0002 \cdot 8 \times 10^6 = 160 \text{ ns}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
EAT = 189 + 40.99 + 160 = 389.99 \text{ ns} \approx 390 \text{ ns}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The EAT is approximately **1.95× the physical memory access time**, showing the significant impact of even a low page fault rate. The dominant term is the page fault service time (160 ns), which would be absent if $p = 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe memory protection mechanisms in a paged system. Explain the role of protection bits in the Page Table Entry.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a system with a 32-bit virtual address, 4 KB page size, and a single-level page table. Given a virtual address `0x0000_4A20` and a page table entry at index `0x00004A` containing `PPN = 0x00007` with `V = 1`, compute the physical address. Also, explain what happens if the protection bit `U/S = 0` and the CPU is in user mode.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Memory Protection Mechanisms (7 marks)**

Memory protection ensures that processes cannot access memory belonging to the OS or other processes, and prevents accidental corruption.

**1. Base / Limit Registers (2 marks):**
The OS loads the base address and limit (size) of a process into dedicated CPU registers. The MMU checks every address against `[base, base+limit]`. If outside, a trap is raised. Drawback: variable-sized protection regions lead to fragmentation.

**2. Protection Bits in PTE (3 marks):**
Each PTE contains bits for fine-grained, per-page control:
- **V (Valid):** Page is in physical memory.
- **R/W (Read/Write):** `0` = read-only; `1` = read/write.
- **U/S (User/Supervisor):** `0` = kernel-only; `1` = user-accessible.
- **X (Execute):** Prevents execution on data pages (DEP/NX).
- **D (Dirty) and A (Accessed):** Used by page replacement algorithms.

The MMU checks these bits on *every* memory access, providing hardware-enforced security.

**3. Ring Levels (1 mark):**
Modern CPUs implement 4 privilege levels (Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user on x86). System calls transition from Ring 3 to Ring 0, ensuring user code cannot directly execute privileged instructions or access kernel memory.

**4. TLB ASIDs (1 mark):**
Address Space Identifiers (ASIDs) tag TLB entries with the owning process, preventing cross-process address leakage.

**(b) Address Translation and Protection Check (7 marks)**

**Given:** 32-bit VA, 4 KB page size, VA = `0x00004A20`, PTE at index `0x00004A` has PPN = `0x00007`, V = 1.

**Step 1 — Page size and offset bits (1 mark):**

$$
Page\_size = 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
offset\_bits = \log_2(4096) = 12
$$

**Step 2 — Extract offset (1 mark):**

$$
offset = 0x00004A20 \mod 0x1000 = 0xA20
$$

In decimal: $offset = 2592$ bytes.

**Step 3 — Extract VPN (1 mark):**

$$
VPN = \lfloor 0x00004A20 / 0x1000 \rfloor = 0x00004A
$$

**Step 4 — PTE lookup (1 mark):**
PTE at index `0x00004A` returns `PPN = 0x00007`, `V = 1` → translation is valid.

**Step 5 — Form physical address (1 mark):**

$$
P\_addr = (PPN \ll 12) + offset
$$

$$
P\_addr = (0x00007 \times 0x1000) + 0xA20
$$

$$
P\_addr = 0x7000 + 0xA20 = 0x7A20
$$

**Step 6 — Protection scenario (2 marks):**
If `U/S = 0` and the CPU is in user mode, the MMU detects a **privilege violation**. The access is aborted, a **segmentation fault (SIGSEGV)** is raised, and the OS terminates the process (or delivers a signal). The TLB is not updated. This prevents user code from reading or modifying kernel data structures — a foundational security mechanism.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and review the day before your exam.**

- **Virtual Memory = Indirection:** Decouples logical addresses from physical addresses, enabling programs larger than RAM.
- **Demand Paging = Lazy Loading:** Pages are loaded on first reference, not eagerly.
- **Page Fault:** Trap raised when Valid bit = 0; OS handles by loading the page and **restarting** the instruction.
- **Page Table:** Maps VPN → PPN; stored in main memory; consulted by MMU.
- **TLB:** Hardware cache of recent translations; reduces page table walk overhead.
- **EAT (No TLB):** $EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_{mem} + p \cdot T_{fault}$.
- **EAT (With TLB):** $EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot (1-p) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot p \cdot T_{fault}$.
- **Memory Protection Mechanisms:** Base/Limit registers, PTE protection bits (V, R/W, U/S, X), Ring levels, ASIDs.
- **Page Size Trade-off:** Large pages → fewer faults but more internal fragmentation; small pages → finer granularity but larger page tables.
- **Dirty Bit:** Used to decide whether to write a page back to disk (write-back) — saves I/O.
- **Reference Bit:** Used by LRU and Clock algorithms to approximate recency.
- **Common ESE Pitfalls:**
  - Forgetting the $(1-p)$ term in EAT.
  - Confusing VPN and PPN in address translation.
  - Saying "memory protection is software-based" — it is **hardware-enforced (MMU)**.
  - Writing "resume" instead of "restart" the faulting instruction.
- **Performance Rule of Thumb:** A single page fault costs **≈ 10,000× a normal memory access** — design must minimize $p$.
- **Address Translation Example:** For 32-bit VA, 4 KB page → 20-bit VPN, 12-bit offset.
- **Swap Space:** Reserved disk area used as an extension of physical memory; not the same as the file system.
- **Working Set:** The set of pages a process is actively using; should ideally fit in physical memory to avoid thrashing.
- **Thrashing:** When a system spends more time swapping pages than executing — a consequence of poor demand-paging policy.
- **Write Policies:** Write-back (lazy, common) vs. Write-through (immediate, simpler but slower).
- **Security Bits to Know:** NX (No-Execute), SMEP (Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention), SMAP (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention), KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Memory Hierarchy Implementations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Memory Hierarchy Implementations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Hierarchy** is an organized, multi-tiered arrangement of computer storage systems structured in increasing order of capacity and access time, and decreasing order of cost per byte and access speed. The primary engineering goal of this hierarchy is to provide a memory system that is simultaneously **fast** (like the smallest, closest memories), **large** (like the biggest, farthest memories), and **economical** (averaging the cost per bit across all levels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *A memory hierarchy consists of multiple levels of memory with different speeds and sizes. Faster, more expensive, and smaller memories are placed closer to the processor, while slower, cheaper, and larger memories are placed farther away. The hierarchy exploits the principle of locality to present the programmer with a memory that is nearly as fast as the fastest component and nearly as large and cheap as the cheapest component.*

The hierarchy is governed by two fundamental principles of program behavior:

1. **Temporal Locality (Time-based reuse):** If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.
2. **Spatial Locality (Location-based reuse):** If a memory location is referenced, the locations adjacent to it are likely to be referenced in the near future.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Storage Tiers (Top = Fastest/Smallest/Costliest, Bottom = Slowest/Largest/Cheapest):**
> 1. **CPU Registers** — Internal to the processor, accessed in 1 cycle.
> 2. **Level 1 (L1) Cache** — On-chip, split into Instruction (I-cache) and Data (D-cache).
> 3. **Level 2 (L2) Cache** — On-chip (unified) or off-chip, larger and slightly slower.
> 4. **Level 3 (L3) Cache** — Shared across cores on multi-core dies.
> 5. **Main Memory (DRAM)** — Volatile, byte-addressable, large capacity.
> 6. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)** — Non-volatile, block-addressable.
> 7. **Tertiary / Archival (Magnetic Tape / Optical)** — Backup and offline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student's Study Desk"

Imagine you are a student preparing for an exam with thousands of pages of textbooks.

- **CPU Registers** → Your **active thought / pen in hand**. Instant access, but you can hold only a few equations in your head at once.
- **L1 / L2 / L3 Cache** → **The open notebook on your desk**. Quick to glance at, holds key formulas and recently solved problems.
- **Main Memory (RAM)** → **The bookshelf in your room**. Larger, but you must stand up and walk a few steps to fetch a book.
- **SSD / Hard Disk** → **The library downstairs**. Massive storage, but the walk takes minutes, and you may have to wait in queue.
- **Tape / Cloud Backup** → **A warehouse in another city**. Used rarely, but virtually unlimited.

The clever insight is this: **you almost never need the entire library at your desk**. Most of the time, the open notebook (cache) is enough, because the *page you just read* (temporal locality) and the *pages next to it* (spatial locality) are the ones you will read next. This is exactly how a memory hierarchy works for the CPU.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Performance Metrics (Bold Constants to Remember)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metrics of the Hierarchy (Board-Exam Favorites):**
> - **Hit Rate (h):** Fraction of memory accesses found in the *given* level of the hierarchy.
> - **Miss Rate (1 − h):** Fraction of accesses that must go to the *next* lower level.
> - **Hit Time (T_hit):** Time to access the data in the *current* level (including the time to determine whether it is a hit).
> - **Miss Penalty (T_miss):** Time to fetch the data from the *next* lower level on a miss.
> - **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT):** The single most important derived metric, combining hit time and miss penalty.
> - **Capacity (C):** Total bytes stored at a level.
> - **Block / Line Size (B):** Number of bytes transferred as a single unit between adjacent levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Flow of a Memory Access

When the CPU issues a Load/Store to address `A`, the following logical sequence occurs (executed in hardware by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** and **Cache Controller**):

1. **Step 1 — TLB Lookup:** The virtual address `A` is translated by consulting the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**. On a TLB miss, a costly *page-table walk* occurs in main memory.
2. **Step 2 — Cache Tag Compare:** The physical address is split into *Tag*, *Index*, and *Block Offset*. The cache uses the *Index* to select a candidate line, then compares the line's stored *Tag* with the address *Tag*.
3. **Step 3 — Hit Path:** If the tag matches AND the valid bit is set → **HIT**. Data is returned to the CPU in **T_hit** cycles.
4. **Step 4 — Miss Path:** If the tag does not match (or the line is invalid) → **MISS**. The cache controller issues a request to the next level, fetching the entire *block* containing the requested word. This consumes **T_miss** cycles (miss penalty).
5. **Step 5 — Replacement & Write-Back:** If the fetched block must evict a dirty line, the evicted line is written back to lower memory; the new block is installed, and the requested word is forwarded to the CPU.

## 2.2 The Five Fundamental Design Questions (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU board question on cache design is essentially a variation of one of these five questions. Memorize them:
>
> | # | Design Question | Design Parameter | Options / Common Choices |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Where** can a block be placed? | Mapping Technique | Direct, Fully Associative, Set-Associative |
> | 2 | **How** is a block found? | Lookup Mechanism | Tag comparison, indexing |
> | 3 | **Which** block is replaced on miss? | Replacement Policy | LRU, FIFO, Random |
> | 4 | **What** happens on a **write**? | Write Policy | Write-Through, Write-Back |
> | 5 | **How** are writes handled on miss? | Write-Miss Policy | Write-Allocate, No-Write-Allocate |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of `\|` whenever absolute-value or vertical-bar notation appears in a markdown table cell.

**Table 2.3 — Core Quantitative Formulas for Memory Hierarchy**

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Description of Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Memory Access Time (1-level cache) | $AMAT \;=\; T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot T_{miss}$ | $T_{hit}$: hit time; $h$: hit rate; $T_{miss}$: miss penalty | Core metric to compare cache designs. |
| 2 | AMAT (N-level hierarchy) | $AMAT \;=\; T_{L1} \;+\; m_1 T_{L2} \;+\; m_1 m_2 T_{L3} \;+\; \dots$ | $m_i$: miss rate at level $i$ | Models deep hierarchies (L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM). |
| 3 | Miss Rate | $m \;=\; 1 - h$ | $h$: hit rate; $m$: miss rate | Always between 0 and 1. |
| 4 | Number of Blocks (Cache) | $B_{cache} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{cache}}{B_{block}}$ | $C_{cache}$: cache capacity; $B_{block}$: block size in bytes | Determines how many distinct lines fit. |
| 5 | Number of Sets (Set-Associative) | $S \;=\; \dfrac{B_{cache}}{B_{block} \cdot A}$ | $A$: associativity (ways per set) | Determines index width: $\log_2 S$ bits. |
| 6 | Address Field Widths | $A_{addr} \;=\; T_{tag} + I_{index} + B_{offset}$ | $T_{tag}$: tag bits; $I_{index}$: index bits; $B_{offset}$: block-offset bits | Sum must equal address width (e.g., 32 or 64). |
| 7 | Block Offset Bits | $B_{offset} \;=\; \log_2(B_{block} \;/\; W)$ | $W$: word size in bytes (often 4) | Lowest bits of the address. |
| 8 | Index Bits | $I_{index} \;=\; \log_2(S)$ | $S$: number of sets | Bits between tag and block offset. |
| 9 | Tag Bits | $T_{tag} \;=\; A_{addr} - I_{index} - B_{offset}$ | $A_{addr}$: total physical address bits | Remaining upper bits. |
| 10 | CPU Time with Memory Stall | $T_{CPU} \;=\; IC \cdot \big(CPI_{base} + \frac{MemAccess}{Inst}\cdot m \cdot T_{miss}\big) \cdot T_{cycle}$ | $IC$: instruction count; $CPI_{base}$: compute CPI | Real-world performance with stalls. |
| 11 | Speedup (Cache vs. No-Cache) | $S_p \;=\; \dfrac{T_{no\,cache}}{T_{cache}}$ | $T_{no\,cache}$: time with memory only | Quantifies benefit of the hierarchy. |
| 12 | Effective CPI | $CPI_{eff} \;=\; CPI_{base} + MemStall_{per\,Inst}$ | $MemStall_{per\,Inst} = \frac{memAccess}{inst} \cdot missRate \cdot missPenalty_{cycles}$ | Combined compute + memory CPI. |
| 13 | Miss Penalty in Cycles | $P_{cycles} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{miss}}{T_{cycle}}$ | $T_{miss}$ in ns; $T_{cycle}$ in ns | Used to express penalty in clock cycles. |
| 14 | Page Table Size | $PT_{size} \;=\; 2^{P} \cdot E_{PTE}$ | $P$: virtual address page-offset bits removed; $E_{PTE}$: entry size (e.g., 4 B) | Critical for VM analysis. |
| 15 | TLB Coverage | $Cov \;=\; N_{entries} \cdot P_{size}$ | $N_{entries}$: TLB entries; $P_{size}$: page size | Memory reached without page-table walk. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this system is used in industry:**
> - **Datacenter CPUs (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC, AWS Graviton):** Use 3-level on-die caches (L1/L2/L3) and a sophisticated TLB hierarchy to support virtualization and gigabytes of RAM.
> - **Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon):** Employ **system-level caches (SLC)** shared between CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine — a direct extension of the classic hierarchy.
> - **GPUs (NVIDIA H100, AMD MI300):** Use *texture caches*, *constant caches*, and *L2 caches* tuned to spatial-locality-heavy workloads (graphics, matrix math).
> - **Embedded Real-Time Systems (Automotive, Aerospace):** Use **locked caches** to guarantee that critical code/data cannot be evicted — a direct application of write-policy and replacement-policy design choices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a Single-Level Cache

**Goal:** Derive a closed-form expression for the *average* time the CPU spends on a single memory access, given that a fraction $h$ of accesses hit the cache and the rest miss to main memory.

**Step 1 — Define the two mutually exclusive outcomes.**

Every memory access is either a **hit** (probability $h$) or a **miss** (probability $1 - h$). The expected access time is therefore the probability-weighted sum of the two cases.

$$
AMAT \;=\; P(hit)\cdot T_{hit} \;+\; P(miss)\cdot T_{total\_on\_miss}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the time spent on a miss.**

On a miss, the cache still consumes the hit time to discover it is a miss, *then* pays the miss penalty. So the total time on a miss is:

$$
T_{total\_on\_miss} \;=\; T_{hit} \;+\; T_{miss}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the expectation.**

$$
AMAT \;=\; h \cdot T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot \big( T_{hit} + T_{miss} \big)
$$

**Step 4 — Distribute and simplify.**

$$
AMAT \;=\; h\cdot T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot T_{miss}
$$

$$
AMAT \;=\; T_{hit}\big[\,h + (1 - h)\,\big] \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot T_{miss}
$$

The bracketed term simplifies to 1, giving the **canonical AMAT formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,AMAT \;=\; T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h)\cdot T_{miss}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify limiting cases (sanity check).**

- If $h = 1$ (everything hits): $AMAT = T_{hit}$. ✅ Correct.
- If $h = 0$ (everything misses): $AMAT = T_{hit} + T_{miss}$. ✅ Correct.

## 3.2 Derivation: Address-Field Partitioning for a Set-Associative Cache

**Problem Statement (Worked Example — KTU Style):**

A processor has a **32-bit physical address**, an **L1 cache of 16 KB**, **4-way set-associative** organization, and a **block size of 32 bytes**. Determine the number of bits in the *Tag*, *Index*, and *Block Offset* fields of the address.

**Step 1 — Compute the Block Offset width.**

A block contains 32 bytes, so we need $\log_2(32)$ bits to address every byte within a block.

$$
B_{offset} \;=\; \log_2(32) \;=\; 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of sets.**

With a 4-way set-associative cache, each set holds 4 blocks. The number of sets is therefore the total number of blocks divided by 4.

$$
B_{cache} \;=\; \frac{C_{cache}}{B_{block}} \;=\; \frac{16\,384 \text{ bytes}}{32 \text{ bytes/block}} \;=\; 512 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
S \;=\; \frac{B_{cache}}{A} \;=\; \frac{512}{4} \;=\; 128 \text{ sets}
$$
