**Step 3 — Compute the Index width.**

$$
I_{index} \;=\; \log_2(128) \;=\; 7 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Tag width.**

$$
T_{tag} \;=\; A_{addr} - I_{index} - B_{offset} \;=\; 32 - 7 - 5 \;=\; 20 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the total.**

$$
20 + 7 + 5 \;=\; 32 \text{ bits} \quad\checkmark
$$

**Final Address Partition Diagram:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Tag: }20\text{ bits}}_{\text{stored in cache line}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Index: }7\text{ bits}}_{\text{selects set}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Block Offset: }5\text{ bits}}_{\text{selects byte}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: CPU Execution Time with Cache

**Step 1 — Start with the basic CPU-time equation.**

$$
T_{CPU} \;=\; IC \cdot CPI \cdot T_{cycle}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the CPI into compute and memory-stall parts.**

$$
CPI \;=\; CPI_{base} + CPI_{memory\_stall}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the memory-stall CPI.**

For every instruction, the CPU issues on average $MemAccess/Inst$ memory references. Each reference stalls for $m \cdot P_{miss}$ cycles (where $P_{miss}$ is the miss penalty in cycles and $m$ is the miss rate).

$$
CPI_{memory\_stall} \;=\; \frac{MemAccess}{Inst} \cdot m \cdot P_{miss}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{CPU} \;=\; IC \cdot \big( CPI_{base} + \tfrac{MemAccess}{Inst}\cdot m \cdot P_{miss} \big) \cdot T_{cycle}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Examination Grade

**Question:** A system has a 2-level cache. L1 has $T_{hit} = 1$ ns, hit rate $h_1 = 0.90$. L2 has $T_{hit} = 10$ ns, hit rate (of accesses reaching L2) $h_2 = 0.95$. Main memory access time is $T_{RAM} = 100$ ns. Compute the AMAT seen by the processor.

**Step 1 — Identify the per-level miss rates.**

$$
m_1 \;=\; 1 - h_1 \;=\; 1 - 0.90 \;=\; 0.10
$$

$$
m_2 \;=\; 1 - h_2 \;=\; 1 - 0.95 \;=\; 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Write the multi-level AMAT formula.**

$$
AMAT \;=\; T_{L1} + m_1 \cdot T_{L2\_total} + m_1\cdot m_2 \cdot T_{RAM}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the time to access L2 (including L1 hit-detection overhead).**

$$
T_{L2\_total} \;=\; T_{L1} + T_{L2} \;=\; 1 + 10 \;=\; 11 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$
AMAT \;=\; 1 \;+\; 0.10 \cdot 11 \;+\; 0.10 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 100
$$

$$
AMAT \;=\; 1 + 1.1 + 0.5 \;=\; 2.6 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Without the L1 cache, every access would take at least 11 ns. With L1, the average is **2.6 ns — a $\approx 4.2\times$ speedup**. This quantitatively demonstrates why the hierarchy is designed as it is.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cache Mapping Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison matrix is a **must-memorize** table for KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 problems. It is a high-frequency 7-mark exam question.

**Table 3.5 — Mapping-Technique Trade-offs**

| Criterion | Direct-Mapped | Fully Associative | Set-Associative (k-way) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mapping Rule** | Block $B$ → Line $(B \bmod N)$ | Block $B$ → any of $N$ lines | Block $B$ → Set $(B \bmod S)$, any of $k$ ways |
| **Hardware Cost** | **Lowest** (1 comparator) | **Highest** ($N$ comparators) | Moderate ($k$ comparators) |
| **Lookup Speed** | **Fastest** (1 cycle typical) | Slowest (parallel compare) | Moderate (k parallel compares) |
| **Conflict Misses** | **Maximum** (thrashing) | **Zero** | Reduced by factor of $k$ |
| **Capacity Misses** | Same | Same | Same |
| **Replacement Logic** | Trivial (evict that line) | Complex (LRU, etc.) | Per-set LRU |
| **Typical Associativity $k$** | 1 | $N$ | 2, 4, 8, 16 |
| **Used In** | L1 instruction caches | TLB, small critical caches | L1 / L2 unified caches (industry default) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `cpuA`, `ramB`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
> - No markdown formatting (`**`, `*`) inside quoted node labels — only clean uppercase text.

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of a memory reference from the CPU down to secondary storage, illustrating how data is shuttled between levels and which level holds the *current working set*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuA["CPU CORE"]:::top
    regA["REGISTER FILE\n32 TO 64 ENTRIES\n1 CYCLE"]:::l1
    l1A["L1 INSTRUCTION CACHE\nL1 DATA CACHE\n32 KB SPLIT\n2 TO 4 CYCLES"]:::l1
    l2A["L2 UNIFIED CACHE\n256 KB TO 1 MB\n8 TO 12 CYCLES"]:::l2
    l3A["L3 SHARED CACHE\n4 MB TO 64 MB\n30 TO 50 CYCLES"]:::l3
    mmA["MAIN MEMORY DRAM\n4 GB TO 1 TB\n200 TO 300 CYCLES"]:::ram
    ssdA["SSD OR HDD\n256 GB TO 16 TB\n50K TO 500K CYCLES"]:::disk
    tapeA["ARCHIVAL TAPE OR CLOUD\nPB SCALE\nMILLIONS OF CYCLES"]:::tape

    cpuA -- "LOAD STORE" --> regA
    regA -- "L1 MISS" --> l1A
    l1A -- "L2 MISS" --> l2A
    l2A -- "L3 MISS" --> l3A
    l3A -- "RAM MISS" --> mmA
    mmA -- "PAGE FAULT" --> ssdA
    ssdA -- "ARCHIVE PULL" --> tapeA

    classDef top fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l2 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l3 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ram fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef disk fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tape fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every downward arrow represents a *miss path* (the next, slower level is consulted).
- Every upward arrow would represent a *write-back* of evicted dirty data.
- The cost per byte decreases dramatically as we go down; the access time increases dramatically.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Direct-Mapped Cache Organization

This diagram shows how a **32-bit address** is decomposed and routed to a single cache line in a direct-mapped cache. The index selects exactly one line, and the tag is compared to determine hit or miss.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrA["32 BIT PHYSICAL ADDRESS"]:::addr
    tagF["TAG FIELD\n20 BITS"]:::tag
    idxF["INDEX FIELD\n7 BITS"]:::idx
    offF["BLOCK OFFSET\n5 BITS"]:::off

    addrA --> tagF
    addrA --> idxF
    addrA --> offF

    idxF --> muxA["MUX\n128 TO 1"]:::mux

    tagA["STORED TAG IN LINE"]:::tagstore
    validA["VALID BIT"]:::valid
    dataA["DATA 32 BYTES"]:::data

    tagA --> cmpA["TAG COMPARATOR"]:::cmp
    validA --> cmpA

    tagF --> cmpA

    cmpA -- "MATCH AND VALID" --> hitA["HIT SIGNAL\nTO CPU"]:::hit
    cmpA -- "MISMATCH" --> missA["MISS SIGNAL\nFETCH FROM NEXT LEVEL"]:::miss

    offF --> muxA
    muxA --> dataA
    dataA -- "WORD SELECTED" --> hitA

    classDef addr fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tag fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef idx fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef off fill:#98fb98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tagstore fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef valid fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cmp fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef hit fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef miss fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — k-way Set-Associative Cache Organization

This diagram illustrates the more general **k-way set-associative** structure: the index selects a *set* of $k$ candidate lines, and **all $k$ tags are compared in parallel**. A hit occurs if any one comparator matches.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpub["CPU ADDRESS"]:::addr
    idxb["INDEX"]:::idx
    tagb["INCOMING TAG"]:::tag
    offb["OFFSET"]:::off

    setMux["SET SELECTOR\nS WAYS"]:::mux

    w1["WAY 0\nTAG STORE 0"]:::way
    w2["WAY 1\nTAG STORE 1"]:::way
    w3["WAY 2\nTAG STORE 2"]:::way
    wk["WAY K-1\nTAG STORE K-1"]:::way

    cmp1["COMPARATOR 0"]:::cmp
    cmp2["COMPARATOR 1"]:::cmp
    cmp3["COMPARATOR 2"]:::cmp
    cmpk["COMPARATOR K-1"]:::cmp

    enc["ENCODER AND\nMUX"]:::mux
    out["DATA WORD"]:::data

    cpub --> idxb
    cpub --> tagb
    cpub --> offb

    idxb --> setMux
    setMux --> w1
    setMux --> w2
    setMux --> w3
    setMux --> wk

    w1 --> cmp1
    w2 --> cmp2
    w3 --> cmp3
    wk --> cmpk

    tagb --> cmp1
    tagb --> cmp2
    tagb --> cmp3
    tagb --> cmpk

    cmp1 --> enc
    cmp2 --> enc
    cmp3 --> enc
    cmpk --> enc

    enc -- "SELECTED WAY AND BYTE" --> out
    offb --> enc

    classDef addr fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tag fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef idx fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef off fill:#98fb98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef way fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cmp fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Virtual-to-Physical Address Translation Flow

This block diagram traces the path of a **virtual address** as it moves from the CPU through the TLB, page table, and finally to the L1 cache. This is a *high-frequency* KTU 2024 exam concept for Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuC["CPU ISSUES VIRTUAL ADDRESS"]:::addr
    tlbC["TLB LOOKUP\nCOMPARE VPN AGAINST\nTLB ENTRIES"]:::tlb
    ptwC["PAGE TABLE WALK\nIN MAIN MEMORY\nON TLB MISS"]:::pt
    phyC["PHYSICAL ADDRESS\nCONSTRUCTED FROM PFN\nPLUS OFFSET"]:::phy
    l1C["L1 CACHE LOOKUP\nUSING TAG AND INDEX"]:::l1
    dataC["DATA RETURNED TO CPU"]:::data

    cpuC -- "VIRTUAL PAGE NUMBER" --> tlbC
    tlbC -- "TLB HIT" --> phyC
    tlbC -- "TLB MISS" --> ptwC
    ptwC --> phyC
    phyC --> l1C
    l1C -- "CACHE HIT" --> dataC

    classDef addr fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tlb fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pt fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef phy fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Read Before You Write):**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the Hit Time ($T_{hit}$) inside $T_{miss\_total}$** — a one-cycle hit-time is paid *even on a miss*. Marks are routinely docked for writing $AMAT = h \cdot T_{hit} + (1-h)\cdot T_{miss}$ instead of the correct $AMAT = T_{hit} + (1-h)\cdot T_{miss}$. This is the **#1 mark-loss reason** in AMAT problems.
> 2. **Mixing up the address field directions.** Tag is the *upper* bits, index is the *middle* bits, block offset is the *lower* bits. Students often place offset on top — instant 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract the offset when computing the number of cache lines/sets.** Always compute $\log_2$ of the *line count*, not the *byte count*.
> 4. **Conflating "miss rate" with "miss penalty."** Miss rate is a *fraction* (0 to 1); miss penalty is a *time* (ns or cycles). They are NOT interchangeable.
> 5. **Not stating units.** "T_hit = 1" earns 0 marks; "T_hit = 1 ns" earns full marks. KTU examiners are strict about units.
> 6. **Skipping the AMAT sanity check** at the end of a derivation. Always verify the two boundary cases (h=1, h=0). Examiners reward this with 1 bonus mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define memory hierarchy. State the principles of locality that make it work. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A *memory hierarchy* is a structural organization of computer storage into multiple levels — registers, L1/L2/L3 caches, main memory (DRAM), and secondary storage (SSD/HDD/tape) — arranged so that smaller, faster, costlier memories sit closer to the CPU and larger, slower, cheaper memories sit farther away. The goal is to deliver an effective memory that combines the **speed of the topmost level** with the **capacity of the bottommost level**, at a moderate cost.

The two locality principles that make this work are:

1. **Temporal Locality:** A memory location accessed now is likely to be accessed again in the near future. Hence keeping recently used data in a small, fast cache is profitable.
2. **Spatial Locality:** Memory locations adjacent to a recently accessed one are likely to be accessed soon. Hence transferring data in *blocks/lines* (not single words) reduces future misses.

*(Examiner: full 3 marks for both principles explicitly stated and tied to the hierarchy.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between write-through and write-back cache policies. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Write-Through | Write-Back |
|---|---|---|
| Write action | Data is written **simultaneously** to the cache line and to the next lower level (e.g., main memory). | Data is written **only to the cache line**. The lower level is updated **later**, when the line is evicted. |
| Dirty bit required? | **No** — main memory is always up-to-date. | **Yes** — a *dirty/modified* bit tracks whether a line must be written back on eviction. |
| Memory traffic | **High** — every store goes to RAM. | **Low** — multiple stores to the same line coalesce. |
| Consistency | Simple; ideal for DMA / multi-core coherence. | Harder; requires explicit cache-coherence protocol (e.g., MESI). |
| Performance | Slower for write-heavy workloads. | Faster for write-heavy workloads. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Format (Strict 2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question is internally optional. The student must answer **one** of the two. Each is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A (a).** Explain the three cache mapping techniques — direct-mapped, fully associative, and set-associative — with neat diagrams. Compare their hit rates, hardware cost, and conflict-miss behavior. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Direct-Mapped Cache:**
Each main-memory block maps to *exactly one* cache line determined by the formula:
$$\text{Line} \;=\; (\text{Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Lines})$$
The address is split into *Tag*, *Index*, and *Block Offset*. The index selects the line; the tag is compared with the line's stored tag. A match yields a hit. **Hardware cost is minimal** (1 comparator, 1 MUX). However, *conflict misses* are high because two frequently used blocks that map to the same line will continually thrash each other.

**2. Fully Associative Cache:**
A block can be placed in *any* cache line. On a lookup, the incoming tag is compared in *parallel* against the tags of all lines. A match in any line produces a hit. **Hardware cost is maximum** ($N$ comparators for $N$ lines). Conflict misses are **zero** because no two blocks are forced to share a line. Replacement is by LRU/FIFO/Random. Used in small, critical structures such as the TLB.

**3. Set-Associative Cache (k-way):**
A compromise. The cache is organized as $S$ sets, each holding $k$ lines (ways). A block maps to set $S = (\text{Block Number}) \bmod S$, and may be placed in *any of the $k$ ways* within that set. Lookup compares the incoming tag with the $k$ tags of the selected set in parallel. **Hardware cost is moderate** ($k$ comparators per set). Conflict misses are reduced by a factor of $k$ compared to direct-mapped.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Direct | Fully Associative | Set-Associative (k-way) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware | 1 comparator | $N$ comparators | $k$ comparators |
| Conflict Misses | Highest | Zero | Reduced by $k$ |
| Lookup Speed | 1 cycle | Slow (parallel) | $k$-wide compare |
| Industry Use | Rare in L1 (low power) | TLB, small critical caches | Default in modern L1/L2 |

*(Examiner: 3 marks for direct, 2 marks for fully associative, 2 marks for comparison — full 7 marks if all three are covered with diagrams.)*

---

**A (b).** A 32-bit address machine has a **direct-mapped cache** of **64 KB capacity** with a **block size of 16 bytes**. For a memory reference to address `0x3F4A2C18`, determine (i) the Tag, Index, and Block Offset field widths, and (ii) the specific tag, index, and offset values for the given address. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute Block Offset bits.**
$$B_{offset} = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total number of blocks (and hence cache lines).**
$$B_{cache} = \frac{64\,KB}{16\,B} = 4096 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Index bits.**
$$I_{index} = \log_2(4096) = 12 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Tag bits.**
$$T_{tag} = 32 - 12 - 4 = 16 \text{ bits}$$

**(i) Field widths:** Tag = **16 bits**, Index = **12 bits**, Block Offset = **4 bits**.

**Step 5 — Convert `0x3F4A2C18` to binary.**

`0x3F4A2C18` in hex → binary (32 bits):
```
3   F    4   A    2   C    1   8
0011 1111 0100 1010 0010 1100 0001 1000
```

**Step 6 — Slice the binary into the three fields.**

- **Block Offset** (lower 4 bits): `1000` → Decimal **8**.
- **Index** (next 12 bits): `1100 0010 0010` → `110000100010` → Decimal ****1554** (or 0xC22 in hex).
- **Tag** (upper 16 bits): `0011 1111 0100 1010` → `0011111101001010` → Decimal **0x3F4A** = **16202**.

**Verification:** `16 + 12 + 4 = 32` ✅

*(Examiner: 2 marks for field-width derivation, 3 marks for correct hex-to-binary conversion, 2 marks for correct field slicing and final decimal values. Always show the binary explicitly.)*

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**B (a).** A two-level cache system has the following parameters:

- **L1 cache:** $T_{hit} = 1$ ns, hit rate $h_1 = 0.92$
- **L2 cache:** $T_{hit} = 8$ ns, hit rate of accesses reaching L2 is $h_2 = 0.90$
- **Main memory:** $T_{RAM} = 100$ ns

Calculate the **AMAT** seen by the processor. If a designer proposes to *remove the L2 cache* and connect the L1 directly to RAM, what is the new AMAT? By what factor does the original hierarchy improve performance? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the per-level miss rates.**
$$m_1 = 1 - h_1 = 1 - 0.92 = 0.08$$
$$m_2 = 1 - h_2 = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Write the multi-level AMAT expression.**
$$AMAT = T_{L1} + m_1 \cdot T_{L2\_total} + m_1 \cdot m_2 \cdot T_{RAM}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total L2 access time (including L1 hit detection).**
$$T_{L2\_total} = T_{L1} + T_{L2} = 1 + 8 = 9 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the numbers.**
$$AMAT_{hier} = 1 + 0.08 \cdot 9 + 0.08 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 100$$
$$AMAT_{hier} = 1 + 0.72 + 0.80 = 2.52 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the AMAT without L2 (L1 directly to RAM).**
On an L1 miss, the penalty is $T_{L1} + T_{RAM} = 1 + 100 = 101$ ns.
$$AMAT_{noL2} = T_{L1} + m_1 \cdot (T_{L1} + T_{RAM}) = 1 + 0.08 \cdot 101 = 1 + 8.08 = 9.08 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the speedup.**
$$S_p = \frac{AMAT_{noL2}}{AMAT_{hier}} = \frac{9.08}{2.52} \approx 3.60 \times$$

**Conclusion:** The 2-level hierarchy provides a **~3.6× speedup** over a single-level design. This justifies the cost and silicon area of an L2 cache.

*(Examiner: 3 marks for AMAT formula, 2 marks for numerical computation, 2 marks for the comparison and the speedup factor. Missing the $T_{L1}$ inside the miss penalty is the most common error.)*

---

**B (b).** Explain the **five fundamental design questions** for any memory hierarchy. For each, list the two or three most common alternative design choices, and indicate which choice is most commonly used in modern desktop CPUs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five design questions (coined by **Patterson and Hennessy** in their canonical textbook *Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach*) must be answered for *every* level of the hierarchy:

**1. Block Placement — *Where can a block be placed?***
- (i) Direct-Mapped — block maps to exactly one line.
- (ii) Fully Associative — block can go in any line.
- (iii) Set-Associative — block can go in any line of a specific set.
- **Modern choice:** **Set-associative** (typically 4-way to 16-way) for L1/L2/L3 data caches; **fully associative** for TLBs.

**2. Block Identification — *How is a block found?***
- (i) Tag comparison (mandatory for any associative cache).
- (ii) Indexing (for direct-mapped and set-associative).
- (iii) Sometimes combined with a *CAM* (Content-Addressable Memory) for fast parallel search.
- **Modern choice:** **Tag comparison on a candidate set** selected by indexing — this is the heart of every set-associative cache.

**3. Block Replacement — *Which block is evicted on a miss?***
- (i) LRU (Least Recently Used) — best in theory, expensive in hardware for $k > 4$.
- (ii) FIFO (First-In First-Out) — cheaper than LRU.
- (iii) Random — simplest, surprisingly competitive.
- **Modern choice:** **Approximated LRU (pseudo-LRU)** for L1/L2; **random or tree-based pseudo-LRU** for higher associativity.

**4. Write Strategy — *What happens on a write hit?***
- (i) Write-Through — write propagates immediately to next level.
- (ii) Write-Back — write stays in cache; dirty bit set; written back on eviction.
- **Modern choice:** **Write-Back** for performance, paired with a coherence protocol (MESI/MOESI) for multi-core correctness.

**5. Write-Miss Policy — *What happens on a write miss?***
- (i) Write-Allocate — fetch the block on miss, then write into it.
- (ii) No-Write-Allocate — bypass the cache and write directly to the next level.
- **Modern choice:** **Write-Allocate with Write-Back** is the default pairing in modern CPUs.

*(Examiner: 1 mark per question (5 marks) + 2 marks for naming the modern default for each. Always state the modern choice explicitly.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist the night before the exam. Every bullet is a potential 1- or 2-mark sub-question.**

**Foundational Concepts**
- ☐ Memory hierarchy = multiple storage tiers arranged by speed/capacity/cost trade-offs.
- ☐ *Temporal locality* → reuse of the same address; *Spatial locality* → reuse of nearby addresses.
- ☐ Top of hierarchy is fast/small/costly; bottom is slow/large/cheap.

**Core Quantitative Formulas (Commit to memory)**
- ☐ $AMAT = T_{hit} + (1 - h)\cdot T_{miss}$ (single level).
- ☐ $AMAT = T_{L1} + m_1 T_{L2} + m_1 m_2 T_{RAM} + \dots$ (multi-level).
- ☐ $T_{CPU} = IC \cdot (CPI_{base} + \frac{MemAccess}{Inst} \cdot m \cdot P_{miss}) \cdot T_{cycle}$.
- ☐ Block Offset = $\log_2$(block size in bytes).
- ☐ Index = $\log_2$(number of sets).
- ☐ Tag = address bits − index bits − block-offset bits.

**Cache Mapping Techniques (High-Frequency Exam Topic)**
- ☐ *Direct-mapped:* 1 comparator, fastest, most conflict misses. Formula: line = (block number) mod (lines).
- ☐ *Fully associative:* $N$ comparators, zero conflict misses, slow, used in TLBs.
- ☐ *Set-associative (k-way):* $k$ comparators per set, best balance; default for L1/L2.

**The Five Design Questions (Patterson-Hennessy)**
- ☐ Q1 Placement → direct / fully / set-associative.
- ☐ Q2 Identification → tag/index lookup.
- ☐ Q3 Replacement → LRU / FIFO / Random.
- ☐ Q4 Write-Hit → Write-Through / Write-Back.
- ☐ Q5 Write-Miss → Write-Allocate / No-Write-Allocate.

**Virtual Memory & TLB**
- ☐ Virtual address = VPN + Offset; Physical address = PFN + Offset.
- ☐ TLB = hardware cache for page-table entries; miss → page-table walk in RAM.
- ☐ Multi-level page tables reduce memory overhead (e.g., x86-64 uses 4 levels).

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam**
- ☐ Do NOT write $AMAT = h T_{hit} + (1-h) T_{miss}$ — this drops the $T_{hit}$ paid on a miss. **Always** add $T_{hit}$ outside.
- ☐ Do NOT confuse miss rate (fraction) with miss penalty (time).
- ☐ Do NOT forget units (ns, cycles, bytes).
- ☐ Do NOT place the block offset in the upper bits of the address.
- ☐ Do NOT confuse "number of sets" with "number of lines" in set-associative caches.
- ☐ Do verify boundary cases (h=1, h=0) at the end of any AMAT derivation.

**Quick Numerical Examples for Self-Testing**
- ☐ 16 KB cache, 32 B block, 4-way → 128 sets → 7-bit index, 5-bit offset, 20-bit tag (32-bit address).
- ☐ $h = 0.95$, $T_{hit} = 1$ ns, $T_{miss} = 100$ ns → $AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \cdot 100 = 6$ ns.
- ☐ 64 MB cache, 16 B block, direct-mapped → 22-bit index, 4-bit offset, 6-bit tag (32-bit address).
- ☐ A 3-level hierarchy: $AMAT = T_{L1} + m_1 T_{L2} + m_1 m_2 T_{L3} + m_1 m_2 m_3 T_{RAM}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread-Level Parallelism: Introduction to Thread Level Parallelism and Multiprocessor Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Thread-Level Parallelism: Introduction to Thread Level Parallelism and Multiprocessor Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)** is a form of parallelism in computer architecture where multiple **threads of execution** (independent streams of instructions) are executed concurrently across one or more processor cores, exploiting the simultaneity of operations at the thread or process granularity rather than within a single instruction stream. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (Modern Processor Architecture, PEAET522), TLP represents a fundamental shift from *Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)* — which exploits parallelism *within* a single thread — to a model where the **hardware simultaneously services multiple threads of control**. The IEEE and Hennessy-Patterson canonical definition states: > *"TLP exists when a computer system can execute two or more separate threads of control concurrently, typically through the use of multiple processors, multiple cores, or hardware multithreading mechanisms."* A **Multiprocessor System** is defined as a computer system containing two or more **processing elements (PEs)** — typically Central Processing Units (CPUs) or cores — that communicate and cooperate to solve a common problem by sharing access to a common memory space or by exchanging messages through an interconnection network. Mathematically, the availability of $N$ independent execution contexts in a system enables a *theoretical* maximum speedup of $N\times$ over a single-threaded baseline, governed by Amdahl's Law. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Hospital Analogy Imagine a single **doctor (single-core CPU)** treating patients one at a time. Even if the doctor is brilliant (high clock speed, deep pipeline), the *waiting room* is overwhelmed. - **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** is like the doctor learning to check the patient's pulse, temperature, and blood pressure **simultaneously** in one visit. The doctor is faster per patient, but still sees patients one after another. - **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)** is like hiring **multiple doctors (cores/threads)** in the same hospital. Each doctor treats a different patient at the *same time*. The total throughput of patients treated per hour multiplies. - **Multiprocessor Systems** are like building an **entire hospital network** — multiple hospitals (processors), a centralized record room (shared memory) or distributed clinics that phone each other (message passing), and ambulances that shuttle patients (interconnection network). When the doctors (cores) all share the same centralized record room (memory), it is a **Shared-Memory Multiprocessor (SMP)**. When each doctor keeps their own records and must call colleagues to share information, it is a **Distributed-Memory Multiprocessor**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology The following standard metrics are universally used in TLP analysis and must be memorized: - **Speedup ($S$)**: Ratio of single-processor execution time to parallel execution time. $S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N}$ - **Efficiency ($E$)**: Speedup divided by number of processors. $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$ - **Throughput**: Number of tasks completed per unit time. - **Latency**: Time to complete a single task from start to finish. - **Parallelism Granularity**: Size of independent work units (fine vs. coarse). - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**: Critical metric reduced by parallel execution. - **MIPS / FLOPS**: Throughput benchmarks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEAET522 Module 4):** The primary focus is on understanding *why* TLP is necessary after the limits of ILP, the taxonomy of multiprocessor systems (UMA, NUMA, COMA, NORMA), the organization of symmetric multiprocessors, and the architectural support required (interconnection networks, memory consistency, cache coherence). These topics are **frequently asked** in Part A and Part B questions. ## 1.4 Motivation: Why TLP After ILP? The performance gains achievable through **Instruction-Level Parallelism** are inherently limited. The fundamental reasons driving the industry toward TLP are: 1. **The Power Wall**: Increasing clock frequency beyond ~4 GHz causes exponential power dissipation ($P \propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$), making single-core frequency scaling economically unviable. 2. **The ILP Wall**: Studies show that even with infinite hardware resources, the *average* ILP available in general-purpose programs is only 3–5 instructions per cycle. 3. **The Memory Wall**: Latency to main memory grows while processor speed grows, limiting single-thread performance. 4. **The Complexity Wall**: Deeper pipelines and wider issue logic increase hardware complexity quadratically while performance gains become logarithmic. These physical and architectural ceilings forced the multi-core revolution of 2005 onward, where manufacturers placed **multiple simple cores** on a single die rather than building one impossibly complex core. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Parallelism: A Hierarchical View Computer architecture parallelism can be classified along two orthogonal axes: ### 2.1.1 Flynn's Taxonomy (1966) — The Most Critical KTU Topic Flynn's Taxonomy classifies computer systems by the number of **instruction streams** and **data streams** they process concurrently: | Class | Instruction Streams | Data Streams | Description | Example | |:------|:-------------------:|:------------:|:------------|:--------| | **SISD** | Single | Single | Classical Von-Neumann uniprocessor | Intel 486, older desktops | | **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | Single instruction acts on many data points simultaneously | GPU shader cores, SSE/AVX, Vector processors (Cray-1) | | **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Multiple instructions process the same data (rarely used) | Fault-tolerant TMR systems, systolic arrays | | **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Independent processors execute different instructions on different data | Multiprocessors, Multicomputers, Multi-core | > [!NOTE] > **TLP is fundamentally MIMD.** All multiprocessor and multi-threaded systems fall into the MIMD category. This is a **guaranteed question** in KTU exams. ### 2.1.2 MIMD Sub-Classification (Crucial for KTU) MIMD systems are further classified by their **memory organization** and **interconnection**: 1. **Shared-Memory MIMD (Multiprocessors)** - **UMA (Uniform Memory Access)**: All processors access any memory location in equal time. Example: Standard Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP). - **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)**: Local memory is faster than remote memory. Example: AMD Opteron, modern multi-socket servers. - **COMA (Cache-Only Memory Architecture)**: Specialized NUMA where local memory acts as a large cache. 2. **Distributed-Memory MIMD (Multicomputers)** - **NORMA (No Remote Memory Access)**: Each node has private memory; communication via message passing. Example: Clusters, supercomputers (early Beowulf). ## 2.2 Multiprocessor System Architecture — A Deeper Look A **shared-memory multiprocessor** can be further categorized by how processors and memory are physically organized: | Architecture Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages | |:------------------|:------------|:-----------|:--------------| | **Centralized Shared Memory (SMP/UMA)** | Multiple cores share a single main memory through a bus or crossbar | Simple programming model (uniform memory), easy cache coherence | Limited scalability (bus bandwidth bottleneck) | | **Distributed Shared Memory (NUMA)** | Memory is physically distributed to processors but logically shared | Highly scalable, reduces memory contention | Complex programming, non-uniform latencies | | **Distributed Memory (Cluster)** | Each processor has private memory; communicates via network | Massive scalability, fault isolation | Hardest programming model (explicit message passing) | ## 2.3 Threading Models Within a Single Processor Beyond multi-core, TLP can be exploited within a single processor via **hardware multithreading**: 1. **Coarse-Grained Multithreading**: Switches to a different thread only on costly stalls (cache miss). Issue slot utilization is high but switching overhead exists. 2. **Fine-Grained Multithreading**: Switches threads every cycle in a round-robin manner, hiding pipeline stalls completely. Example: Tera MTA, Sun Niagara. 3. **Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)**: All hardware contexts issue instructions in the *same* cycle to different execution units. Example: Intel Hyper-Threading. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Point to Remember:** SMT is *not* the same as multi-core. A quad-core SMT processor can issue instructions from **8 logical threads** simultaneously. Multi-core replicates the entire core; SMT duplicates only the *architectural state* (registers, PC) within a single core. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table is a **must-memorize** summary of every formula, condition, and metric in this module. Practice solving problems using these. | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units | Boundary Conditions | |:------------------|:------------------------|:-------------------|:-------------------| | Speedup | $S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N}$ | Ratio of single-processor time to $N$-processor time (unitless) | $S_N \le N$ in practice | | Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{S_N}{N}$ | Fraction of theoretical speedup achieved | $0 \le E \le 1$ | | Amdahl's Law | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors | As $N \to \infty$, $S \to \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | | Gustafson's Law | $S = N - f(N-1)$ | Scaled speedup (problem size grows with $N$) | Linear in $N$ | | Karp-Flatt Metric | $f_K = \dfrac{1/S - 1/N}{1 - 1/N}$ | Identifies inherently serial fraction | $0 \le f_K \le 1$ | | Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{\text{Tasks}}{\text{Time}}$ | Tasks per second | Higher is better | | Memory Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\text{Time}}$ | GB/s | Critical for shared memory systems | | Cache Coherence Misses | $\text{Coh. Misses} = \text{True Sharing} + \text{False Sharing}$ | Invalidation-based misses | False sharing wastes bandwidth | | MTTF (Multiprocessor) | $\text{MTTF}_{sys} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}_{comp}}{N \cdot (1-(\text{Availability})}$ | Reliability metric | Falls as $N$ grows | | CPI in Multiprocessor | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + \text{CPI}_{\text{stall}}$ | Stalls include coherence, contention | Must be minimized | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Exam Scripts:** When writing Amdahl's Law, use parentheses clearly. The fraction $\dfrac{f}{N}$ is the **execution time of the parallel portion** on $N$ processors. The $(1-f)$ is the **unavoidable serial portion**. Students frequently lose marks by forgetting the denominator $N$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility TLP and multiprocessor systems are foundational to nearly every modern computing domain: - **Data Centers & Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP)**: NUMA-based multi-socket servers run thousands of VMs. - **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**: Weather prediction, molecular dynamics, AI training — all run on MIMD clusters. - **Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)**: Heterogeneous multi-core (big.LITTLE) with shared memory. - **Embedded Real-Time Systems**: Automotive ECUs, avionics use MIMD lockstep configurations for safety. - **Databases & Web Servers**: Multi-threading enables concurrent transaction processing with 10,000+ user sessions. - **AI/Deep Learning**: Tensor parallelism and pipeline parallelism across GPU/CPU multiprocessors. > [!NOTE] > The trade-off is consistent: as $N$ (number of processors/threads) increases, **programming complexity**, **hardware cost**, **power consumption**, and **synchronization overhead** also increase. The *art* of computer architecture is finding the optimal balance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive derivations** with every algebraic transition written explicitly. No steps are skipped. ## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Full Derivation) Amdahl's Law quantifies the theoretical speedup limit of a parallel system based on the portion of a program that can be parallelized. ### 3.1.1 Defining the Variables Let: - $T_1$ = Total execution time on a single processor (baseline). - $f$ = Fraction of the program that can be parallelized. So $0 \le f \le 1$. - $(1-f)$ = Fraction that **must** remain serial. - $N$ = Number of processors available. ### 3.1.2 The Time Decomposition The total execution time on a single processor splits into a serial and a parallel portion: $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} \\ T_1 &= (1-f) \cdot T_1 + f \cdot T_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The first equation is by definition. The second equation applies the fact that the *serial portion* takes $(1-f)$ of $T_1$, and the *parallel portion* takes $f$ of $T_1$. ### 3.1.3 Time on $N$ Processors When $N$ processors are used: - The serial portion still takes the full $(1-f) \cdot T_1$ time (no parallelization possible). - The parallel portion is now split across $N$ processors, so its time becomes $\dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$. Therefore: $$ T_N = (1-f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N} $$ ### 3.1.4 Deriving the Speedup Formula Speedup is defined as $S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N}$. Substituting $T_N$: $$ S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{(1-f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}} $$ Factor out $T_1$ from the denominator (since it is common to both terms): $$ S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 \cdot \left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}\right]} $$ Cancel $T_1$ from numerator and denominator: $$ S_N = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ **This is Amdahl's Law.** The result is a unitless scalar representing how many times faster the parallel system is. ### 3.1.5 Taking the Infinite-Processor Limit A crucial special case occurs when $N \to \infty$ (an unbounded number of processors). Taking the limit: $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S_N = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ As $N \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{f}{N} \to 0$, leaving: $$ S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} $$ **Interpretation:** No matter how many processors you add, the speedup is *bounded* by the reciprocal of the serial fraction. If only 5% of a program is serial ($f = 0.95$), the maximum possible speedup is: $$ S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.95} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20 $$ Even with **infinite** processors, the program can only run 20 times faster. ## 3.2 Derivation of Efficiency Efficiency is defined as the *useful* speedup achieved per processor: $$ E = \dfrac{S_N}{N} $$ Substituting Amdahl's Law: $$ E = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot \left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}\right]} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-f) + f} $$ As $N \to \infty$, $E \to 0$. This means **adding more processors beyond a point gives diminishing returns**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A program requires $T_1 = 100$ seconds on a single processor. The serial fraction is $f_{\text{serial}} = 0.20$. Calculate the speedup, efficiency, and execution time on $N = 4$, $N = 8$, and $N = 16$ processors. ### Step 1: Identify the parallel fraction $f_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$ ### Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law for $N = 4$ $$ S_4 = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.40} = 2.5 $$ The execution time becomes: $$ T_4 = \dfrac{T_1}{S_4} = \dfrac{100}{2.5} = 40 \text{ seconds} $$ Efficiency: $$ E_4 = \dfrac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 = 62.5\% $$ ### Step 3: Apply Amdahl's Law for $N = 8$ $$ S_8 = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \dfrac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33 $$ The execution time: $$ T_8 = \dfrac{100}{3.33} \approx 30 \text{ seconds} $$ Efficiency: $$ E_8 = \dfrac{3.33}{8} \approx 0.4167 = 41.67\% $$ ### Step 4: Apply Amdahl's Law for $N = 16$ $$ S_{16} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{16}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4 $$ The execution time: $$ T_{16} = \dfrac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ seconds} $$ Efficiency: $$ E_{16} = \dfrac{4}{16} = 0.25 = 25\% $$ ### Step 5: Compute the Infinite-Processor Limit $$ S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5 $$ > [!NOTE] > Notice how doubling the processors from 4 to 8 increased speedup from 2.5x to only 3.33x. This is the **diminishing returns** effect of Amdahl's Law, which is **guaranteed** to appear in KTU numerical questions. ## 3.4 Speedup Analytical Matrix The following matrix summarizes the results of the worked example — useful for KTU exam revision: | $N$ | Serial Portion $(1-f)T_1$ | Parallel Portion $\dfrac{fT_1}{N}$ | $T_N$ (seconds) | Speedup $S_N$ | Efficiency $E$ | |:---:|:------------------------:|:-----------------------------------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:| | 1 | $20$ | $80$ | $100$ | $1.00$ | $100\%$ | | 2 | $20$ | $40$ | $60$ | $1.67$ | $83.3\%$ | | 4 | $20$ | $20$ | $40$ | $2.50$ | $62.5\%$ | | 8 | $20$ | $10$ | $30$ | $3.33$ | $41.7\%$ | | 16 | $20$ | $5$ | $25$ | $4.00$ | $25.0\%$ | | $\infty$ | $20$ | $0$ | $20$ | $5.00$ | $0\%$ | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP). How does it differ from Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)? **Model Answer:** > **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)** is a form of parallelism where multiple *independent threads of execution* are processed concurrently by the hardware, typically through multiple cores, multiple processors, or hardware multithreading. **Differences:** | Aspect | ILP | TLP | |:-------|:----|:----| | Granularity | Within a single thread | Across multiple threads | | Exploits | Independent instructions in a single stream | Independent threads/processes | | Hardware | Pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order | Multi-core, SMT, multiprocessors | | Limit | ~3–5 instructions/cycle (program dependent) | Scalability bounded by Amdahl's Law | | Example | Pentium 4 pipeline | Intel Core i7 with 4 cores + Hyper-Threading | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip (3-mark question):** You must state the definition clearly (1 mark), state the granularity difference (1 mark), and provide a concrete example (1 mark). --- ### Question 2: List and briefly explain the four classes of Flynn's Taxonomy. **Model Answer:** Flynn's Taxonomy classifies computer architectures based on the number of concurrent **instruction** and **data** streams: 1. **SISD (Single Instruction, Single Data):** Traditional sequential computer. One instruction stream operates on one data item at a time. Example: Older uniprocessors. 2. **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data):** A single instruction operates on multiple data items simultaneously. Example: Vector processors, GPU shader cores, Intel SSE/AVX. 3. **MISD (Multiple Instruction, Single Data):** Multiple instructions operate on the same data item concurrently. Rare; used in fault-tolerant systems and systolic arrays. 4. **MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data):** Multiple processors execute different instructions on different data simultaneously. Example: All modern multi-core processors and multiprocessor systems. > [!NOTE] > All multiprocessor systems with TLP fall under **MIMD**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the classification of multiprocessor systems based on memory organization with neat diagrams. Discuss UMA, NUMA, and NORMA architectures in detail. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** A multiprocessor system can be classified by the way processors access memory. The two main categories are **Shared-Memory** and **Distributed-Memory**. **2. UMA (Uniform Memory Access) — Symmetric Multiprocessor (2 marks):** - All processors share a **single physical memory** connected via a common bus, crossbar, or on-chip interconnect. - Access time to any memory location is **identical** (uniform) for all processors. - Advantages: Simple programming model (programmers see a single memory address space), easy cache coherence. - Disadvantages: The shared bus becomes a bottleneck; hence UMA systems are limited to a small number of processors (typically 4–16). - Example: Early Intel Xeon dual-socket servers, AMD dual-core processors. **3. NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) (2 marks):** - Memory is **physically distributed** to processor nodes but **logically shared** (single address space). - Access time to *local* memory is faster than to *remote* memory attached to another node. - Advantages: Highly scalable (supports dozens of processors), reduces contention. - Disadvantages: Performance depends on data placement; programmers must consider locality for efficiency. - Example: Modern multi-socket servers (AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon Scalable), high-end desktop CPUs (Threadripper). **4. NORMA (No Remote Memory Access) — Distributed Memory (1.5 marks):** - Each processor has its **own private memory**; no global address space exists. - Communication is via explicit **message passing** over a network. - Advantages: Massively scalable, fault isolation. - Disadvantages: Most difficult programming model (MPI required); no hardware cache coherence. - Example: Beowulf clusters, supercomputers (Top500 systems). **5. Comparison Table (0.5 mark):** | Feature | UMA | NUMA | NORMA | |:--------|:----|:-----||:------| | Memory | Shared | Distributed but shared | Private per node | | Access Time | Uniform | Non-uniform | Local only | | Programming | Easy | Moderate | Hard (MPI) | | Scalability | Low | High | Very high | | Coherence | Hardware | Hardware | Software (none) | **Diagram (additional clarity, can be drawn in exam):** ``` UMA: [P0][P1][P2][P3] ----[BUS]---- [SHARED MEMORY] NUMA: [P0-P1-M0] <---> [P2-P3-M1] (Interconnect) NORMA: [P0-M0] <--net--> [P1-M1] <--net--> [P2-M2] ``` --- #### (b) State and derive Amdahl's Law. A program has a parallel fraction of 0.85. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable with 8 processors. What happens as the number of processors approaches infinity? **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Amdahl's Law Statement (1 mark):** Amdahl's Law states that the *theoretical speedup* of a parallel program is limited by its *serial (non-parallelizable) fraction*. The formula is: $$ S_N = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ where $f$ is the parallelizable fraction and $N$ is the number of processors. **2. Derivation (3 marks):** Let $T_1$ be the execution time on a single processor. The execution time decomposes as: $$ T_1 = (1-f) \cdot T_1 + f \cdot T_1 $$ The serial portion $(1-f)T_1$ cannot be parallelized. The parallel portion $f \cdot T_1$ is split across $N$ processors, taking $\dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$ time. Therefore, the total time on $N$ processors is: $$ T_N = (1-f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N} $$ Speedup is $S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N}$. Substituting and cancelling $T_1$: $$ S_N = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ **3. Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** Given $f = 0.85$ and $N = 8$: $$ S_8 = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.15 + 0.10625} = \dfrac{1}{0.25625} \approx 3.90 $$ **4. Infinite-Processor Limit (1 mark):** As $N \to \infty$, $\dfrac{f}{N} \to 0$, so: $$ S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67 $$ **Conclusion:** With 8 processors, the maximum speedup is approximately **3.90×**. Even with infinite processors, the speedup is **capped at 6.67×** due to the 15% serial portion. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) What is a multiprocessor system? Explain the various hardware approaches to multithreading, including coarse-grained, fine-grained, and simultaneous multithreading (SMT). Compare them with multi-core architectures. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition of Multiprocessor (1 mark):** A multiprocessor system is a computer system containing two or more processing elements (CPUs or cores) that work cooperatively, sharing resources such as memory and I/O, to execute multiple threads/processes simultaneously. **2. Coarse-Grained Multithreading (1.5 marks):** - The processor switches to a different thread **only when the current thread stalls** on a costly event (e.g., L2 cache miss, pipeline flush). - Between stalls, all issue slots are used by the current thread. - **Advantage:** Simple hardware; no per-cycle switching overhead. - **Disadvantage:** Wastes cycles during short stalls; cannot hide stalls within a single thread's pipeline bubbles. - **Example:** IBM Power5 (in coarse mode). **3. Fine-Grained Multithreading (1.5 marks):** - The processor switches threads **every cycle** in a round-robin fashion. - Eliminates pipeline bubbles because the stalls of one thread are hidden by the instructions of another. - **Advantage:** Maximum throughput; no pipeline ever stalls. - **Disadvantage:** Single-thread latency increases because each thread issues fewer instructions per unit time. - **Example:** Tera MTA, Sun UltraSPARC T1 (Niagara). **4. Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) (2 marks):** - All hardware thread contexts can issue instructions in the **same clock cycle** to different execution units. - The processor appears as $N$ logical CPUs to the OS, but is a single physical core with replicated **architectural state** (register files, program counters). - **Advantage:** Highest resource utilization; can dynamically allocate execution units to threads with available ILP. - **Disadvantage:** Complex scheduling; shared execution resources can cause contention. - **Example:** Intel Hyper-Threading (2-way SMT on most Core i7/i9), IBM Power8 (8-way SMT). **5. Comparison with Multi-Core (1 mark):** | Feature | Multi-Core | SMT | |:--------|:-----------|:----| | Replicated resources | Entire core | Architectural state only | | Issue width | Per core | Combined across threads | | OS view | $N$ CPUs | $N$ logical CPUs ($1$ physical) | | Combined | Multi-core + SMT (e.g., 4-core/8-thread Core i7) | --- #### (b) Explain the concept of hardware multithreading in modern processors. How does Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) differ from symmetric multiprocessing (SMP)? Discuss with a diagram showing the architecture of an SMT-enabled core. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Concept of Hardware Multithreading (2 marks):** Hardware multithreading allows a single physical processor to maintain the context of *multiple threads simultaneously* and to switch between them (or run them concurrently) without OS intervention. This improves **throughput** and **resource utilization** by filling execution slots that would otherwise be idle due to stalls in a single thread. **2. SMP vs SMT — Key Differences (3 marks):** | Aspect | SMP (Multi-Core) | SMT | |:-------|:----------------|:----| | Physical cores | Multiple | One | | Execution units | Per core | Shared across threads | | Register files | Per core | Replicated per thread | | Cache hierarchy | Per core (sometimes shared L3) | Shared across threads in the core | | OS view | $N$ processors | $N$ logical processors | | Power/Cost | High (more silicon) | Low (only state duplicated) | | Performance isolation | Strong (cores independent) | Weak (threads compete for units) | | Example | AMD Ryzen 9 (16 cores) | Intel Hyper-Threading (2 threads per core) | **3. SMT-Enabled Core Diagram (2 marks):** ``` +----------------------------------+ | SMT-ENABLED CORE | | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | | | T0 PC | | T1 PC | | T2 PC | | <- Program Counters | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | | | T0 RF | | T1 RF | | T2 RF | | <- Register Files (replicated) | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | | INSTRUCTION QUEUE | | | | | +-----------------------------+ | | | SHARED EXEC UNITS | | | | INT_ALU FP_ALU LSU BPU | | | +-----------------------------+ | | | | | +-----------------------------+ | | | SHARED L1 CACHE / L2 | | | +-----------------------------+ | +----------------------------------+ ``` **4. Why SMT is Beneficial (additional 1 mark):** Modern superscalar processors have many execution units (e.g., 6-wide issue in Intel Skylake). A single thread rarely fills all units due to dependencies and stalls. SMT allows multiple threads to *combine* their available ILP, achieving 15–30% additional throughput in most workloads compared to a single-threaded core. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing TLP with ILP:** Students frequently write *"TLP is achieved by pipelining."* This is **incorrect** — pipelining is an ILP technique. TLP requires **multiple threads of control**, not multiple stages of one instruction. > > 2. **SMT ≠ Multi-Core:** A common error is to claim that *"Intel i7 has 8 cores because it has 8 threads."* The i7 has 4 physical cores and 4 logical threads (2-way SMT). Always clarify *physical cores* vs *logical processors*. > > 3. **Amdahl's Law sign errors:** Many students write $(1+f)$ instead of $(1-f)$. The serial portion is $1-f$, **not** $1+f$. This single error invalidates the entire derivation. > > 4. **Missing the infinite-processor limit:** A 14-mark Amdahl's question almost always requires the $N \to \infty$ limit. Forgetting to state $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ costs at least 1–2 marks. > > 5. **Confusing UMA with NUMA:** Both are shared-memory systems. The key difference is that UMA has *uniform* access time, while NUMA has *non-uniform* access time. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks in Part B. > > 6. **No diagram in memory organization question:** Part B questions on UMA/NUMA *require* a neat labeled diagram. Omitting it can cost 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Thread-Level Parallelism:** - **TLP** = Parallelism across multiple *threads*; **ILP** = Parallelism *within* a single thread. - **Multiprocessor** = System with 2+ processing elements cooperating on a common problem. - **Flynn's Taxonomy** has 4 classes: **SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD**. All multiprocessors are **MIMD**. - **MIMD** is further divided into **Shared-Memory (Multiprocessors)** and **Distributed-Memory (Multicomputers/Clusters)**. - **UMA** = Uniform Memory Access (e.g., standard SMP, easy to program, limited scalability). - **NUMA** = Non-Uniform Memory Access (e.g., modern multi-socket, scalable, locality-aware). - **NORMA** = No Remote Memory Access (e.g., clusters, message-passing via MPI). - **Multithreading Types**: Coarse-grained (switch on stall), Fine-grained (switch every cycle), **SMT** (issue from multiple threads in same cycle). - **SMT** duplicates *architectural state only*; **Multi-core** duplicates the *entire core*. - **Amdahl's Law**: $S_N = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$. As $N \to \infty$, $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$. - **Efficiency**: $E = \dfrac{S_N}{N}$, decreases as $N$ increases. - **Speedup is bounded** by the serial fraction; doubling processors gives diminishing returns. - **Cache Coherence Problem** is the central challenge in shared-memory multiprocessors (addressed via MESI/MOESI protocols — covered in subsequent topics). - **Interconnection Networks** for multiprocessors include **shared bus**, **crossbar switch**, **multistage networks** (Omega, Butterfly), and **Network-on-Chip (NoC)**. - **Modern CPUs** (Intel i7, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series) combine **multi-core** + **SMT** + **shared L3 cache** in NUMA-like organizations. - **Hardware Metrics**: Speedup, Efficiency, Throughput, Latency, CPI, MIPS, Memory Bandwidth. - **The Power Wall, ILP Wall, Memory Wall** are the three forces that drove the multi-core revolution. - **Gustafson's Law** $S = N - f(N-1)$ is a *scaled speedup* alternative to Amdahl's Law, used when problem size grows with $N$.

topics.title: Basic Architecture of Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor and Distributed Memory Multiprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Architecture of Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor and Distributed Memory Multiprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **multiprocessor** is a computer system comprising two or more processors (Central Processing Units) that cooperate by sharing a common main memory and communicating via shared variables in memory. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, multiprocessors are broadly classified into two fundamental architectural classes based on the **physical organisation of the main memory** and the **mechanism of inter-processor communication**.

### 1.1.1 Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor (SMP / UMA)

A **Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor**, formally known as a **Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP)** or **Uniform Memory Access (UMA)** architecture, is a parallel computing architecture in which **multiple identical processors** are interconnected to a **single, physically centralised main memory** module through a shared bus, crossbar switch, or common interconnection fabric. All processors:

- Share the **same physical address space**.
- Have **equal (uniform) memory access latency** to every memory location, regardless of which processor initiates the request.
- Communicate through **load/store operations** on shared memory locations.
- Typically maintain private L1/L2 caches, requiring a **hardware cache coherence protocol** (MESI, MOESI, Dragon) to maintain data consistency.

### 1.1.2 Distributed Memory Multiprocessor

A **Distributed Memory Multiprocessor** is a parallel architecture in which **each processor possesses its own private, locally-attached physical memory**, and processors cooperate by **explicitly exchanging messages** over a high-speed interconnection network. This category includes:

- **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)** systems, where logically shared memory is physically distributed.
- **Message-Passing Clusters** and **Massively Parallel Processors (MPP)**, where the address spaces are entirely private.
- The defining property is that **memory access latency is non-uniform**: accessing local memory is fast, while accessing remote memory (either via hardware directory in NUMA or via explicit messages in clusters) takes significantly longer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification hinges on the **physical location of memory**, not merely on whether the address space is logically shared. A NUMA system may *logically* present a shared address space but is *physically* a distributed memory system — and must be classified accordingly.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Centralised Shared Memory = The Whiteboard Office
> Imagine a small office of 4 engineers working around **one giant whiteboard** mounted on a common wall. Every engineer can read or erase any part of the whiteboard. When Engineer A writes an equation, all other engineers can immediately see it (uniform access). However, the whiteboard has **limited physical space** — only one engineer can write on it at a time, and the team must coordinate using a "marker token" (the bus/arbiter) to prevent chaos. This is exactly how a **centralised shared memory multiprocessor** works: a single shared resource (the whiteboard = main memory) with a contention point (the marker = the shared bus).

### Distributed Memory Multiprocessor = The Branch Office Network
> Now picture a multinational company with 50 branch offices across the world. **Each branch has its own private filing cabinet** (local memory) and works independently. To share data, branches must **send a courier with a sealed envelope** (message passing) through a postal service (interconnection network). Asking the local cabinet takes seconds; asking a distant branch's cabinet takes hours. This is the **distributed memory multiprocessor**: private, fast local storage; expensive, slow remote access; and explicit message-passing as the only means of inter-processor communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Memory Architecture:**
> - *Centralised Shared Memory* → One big memory, many processors, **uniform** access time, **shared variables** for communication.
> - *Distributed Memory* → Memory co-located with each processor, **non-uniform** access time, **message passing** for communication.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Metric | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Access Latency** | Time from issuing a load to receiving data | ~50–100 ns (local); ~200–500 ns (remote NUMA) |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | Bytes transferred per second from memory | ~10–50 GB/s (SMP bus); ~100+ GB/s (crossbar) |
| **Scalability Limit** | Maximum number of processors before performance degrades | 8–32 for bus-based SMP; 1000s for distributed |
| **Cache Coherence Overhead** | Extra traffic for maintaining coherent views | 20–40% of bus traffic in SMPs |
| **Message Latency** | Time to send a message between two nodes | 1–10 μs (cluster); 100–500 ns (NUMA) |

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024)

| CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | Coverage in This Topic |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Analyse the architecture of centralised and distributed memory multiprocessors | **Primary** |
| **CO5** | Evaluate scalability, coherence, and communication trade-offs | **Secondary** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bloom's Taxonomy Level (KTU):** This topic maps to **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyse)** cognitive levels under the Revised Bloom's framework adopted by KTU 2024. Students are expected to *apply* architectural concepts to design problems and *analyse* the performance trade-offs between the two classes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor — Structural Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Topology
A typical SMP system consists of **P processors**, each with its own private L1 (and often L2) cache, all connected to a **single shared bus** or **crossbar interconnection** that provides access to a **common DRAM bank**. The defining invariant is:

> **Single Physical Memory, Single Address Space, Multiple Processors.**

### 2.1.2 Operational Pipeline
1. **Processor issues a memory request** (load or store) for a physical address $A$.
2. The request travels over the **shared interconnect** (bus/switch) to the memory controller.
3. The **memory controller** resolves the request, fetches or updates the data, and routes the response back to the requesting processor.
4. **Cache controllers** on every other processor's cache check whether they have a copy of line $A$. If yes, they must act on coherence messages (Invalidate, Update, Snoop).
5. The requesting processor **retires the instruction** upon receiving the data.

### 2.1.3 The Coherence Bottleneck
Because every cache miss traverses the **shared interconnect**, the bus becomes a **single physical bottleneck**. As $P$ (the number of processors) increases, the **per-processor available bandwidth** decreases approximately as:

$$B_{per\_proc} = \frac{B_{total}}{P \cdot \alpha}$$

where $\alpha \geq 1$ is the **contention factor** capturing coherence-induced extra traffic. For large $P$, $B_{per\_proc} \to 0$, limiting scalability.

### 2.1.4 Why "Symmetric"?
All processors are **peers** — any processor can initiate a memory transaction, run the OS kernel, and handle interrupts. There is no master/slave distinction at the hardware level. The OS scheduler may impose asymmetry, but the *hardware* is symmetric.

### 2.1.5 The Cache Coherence Imperative
When two cores cache the same memory line:
- Core 0 reads $X$ (value 10) → caches it in state **S (Shared)**.
- Core 1 writes $X$ (value 20) → its store must **invalidate** or **update** Core 0's copy.
- Without this, Core 0 would continue to use the stale value 10, violating coherence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Concept:** The **MESI protocol** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) is the de-facto coherence protocol in modern SMP systems (Intel Core, AMD Zen, ARM Cortex-A clusters). Its state diagram is a frequently-asked exam topic.

---

## 2.2 Distributed Memory Multiprocessor — Structural Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Topology
A distributed memory system consists of **P independent nodes**, each containing:
- One (or more) **processor(s)** with private L1/L2 caches.
- A **local main memory** (private DRAM).
- A **network interface controller (NIC)** connecting to the **interconnection network**.

No memory is physically shared at the hardware level. Data sharing happens via two mechanisms depending on subclass:

| Subclass | Address Space | Communication Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Distributed (Cluster / MPP)** | **Private** per node | **Message passing** (MPI, PVM) |
| **NUMA** | **Logically shared**, physically distributed | **Load/store to remote line** via directory protocol |

### 2.2.2 Operational Pipeline (Message Passing)
1. Processor P1 wants to send data $D$ to Processor P2.
2. P1 executes a **send instruction** — data is copied from local memory to a **network buffer** in the NIC.
3. The NIC injects a **packet** (containing $D$ and routing headers) into the **interconnection network**.
4. The network routes the packet hop-by-hop to P2's NIC.
5. P2's NIC **DMA-transfers** the data into P2's local memory.
6. P2 receives an **interrupt** or polls a flag indicating data arrival.

### 2.2.3 The Non-Uniform Access Property
For NUMA, the access time for memory location $X$ on remote node $N_k$ from a processor on node $N_i$ is:

$$T_{access}(i, k) = T_{local} + (k-1) \cdot T_{hop} + T_{directory}$$

where:
- $T_{local}$ = local memory access time.
- $T_{hop}$ = per-hop network traversal time.
- $T_{directory}$ = directory lookup overhead for coherence.
- $k$ = number of network hops between nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NUMA scales better than UMA:** Adding a new node adds its local memory bandwidth and a small amount of network traffic. The aggregate memory bandwidth grows roughly linearly with $P$, unlike SMP where it stays constant.

### 2.2.4 The Programming Model Challenge
- **SMP:** Write a sequential-looking program; use **threads** and **mutexes/locks**. Compiler/hardware handles the rest.
- **Distributed:** Explicitly **partition data** across nodes, **send/receive messages** (e.g., `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`), and **synchronise** using barriers or message acknowledgments. Much more programming effort.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **UMA (SMP) Per-Processor Bandwidth** | $B_{pp} = \frac{B_{bus}}{P \cdot \alpha}$ | Decreases with $P$ |
| 2 | **NUMA Remote Access Time** | $T_{remote} = T_{local} + (h-1) T_{hop} + T_{dir}$ | $h$ = number of hops |
| 3 | **Amdahl's Law (Universal)** | $S(P) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{P}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction, ceiling: $\frac{1}{f}$ |
| 4 | **Speedup Upper Bound (SMP)** | $S_{max} = \frac{P \cdot B_{proc}}{B_{bus}}$ | When memory-bound |
| 5 | **Message Latency (Distributed)** | $T_{msg} = T_{startup} + \frac{N}{B_{net}}$ | $N$ = bytes, $B_{net}$ = network bandwidth |
| 6 | **Coherence Traffic Overhead** | $\alpha = 1 + \rho$ | $\rho$ = invalidation/update ratio |
| 7 | **NUMA Locality Benefit** | $R = \frac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}}$ | Typically $R \in [1.5, 5]$ |
| 8 | **Scalability Threshold (SMP)** | $P_{crit} \approx \frac{B_{bus}}{B_{proc} \cdot U}$ | $U$ = utilisation per proc |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Formula Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly apply Amdahl's Law to a memory-bandwidth bottleneck. Remember: Amdahl's Law is for **computational serial fraction**, while the bandwidth formula $B_{pp} = B_{bus}/(P \alpha)$ is for **memory contention**. The two are independent and both can limit performance simultaneously.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Data Centers (Cloud Servers):** Modern hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) deploy **NUMA-aware SMP nodes** — each server is a 2-socket or 4-socket SMP, and racks of servers form a distributed cluster. Understanding both classes is essential for cloud performance engineering.

2. **HPC & Scientific Computing:** Supercomputers (Frontier, Fugaku) are **distributed memory clusters of SMPs** — a *hybrid* of both architectures. MPI handles inter-node message passing; OpenMP handles intra-node threading.

3. **Embedded Multicore SoCs:** Smartphones use ARM's **big.LITTLE** or **DynamIQ** architectures, which are essentially **centralised shared memory SMPs** with coherence across heterogeneous cores.

4. **Edge Computing / IoT:** Sensor networks are typically **distributed memory** with wireless message passing — the same architectural principles apply at miniature scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SMP Memory Bandwidth Contention Model

**Problem Statement:** Consider an SMP with $P$ processors connected to a single shared bus of peak bandwidth $B_{bus}$ = 1 GB/s. Each processor issues memory requests at a rate $\lambda$ (requests/sec), and a fraction $\rho$ of requests generate coherence invalidations on the bus. Derive the per-processor effective bandwidth as a function of $P$.

### Step 1 — Define the variables.
Let:
- $B_{bus}$ = total bus bandwidth (bytes/sec).
- $\lambda$ = per-processor request rate (requests/sec).
- $S_{req}$ = average size of each memory transaction (bytes).
- $\rho$ = fraction of transactions that are coherence-induced (i.e., the request travels on the bus *in addition* to the normal data transfer).

### Step 2 — Compute the load offered by a single processor.
The traffic a single processor places on the bus is:

$$T_{proc} = \lambda \cdot S_{req} \cdot (1 + \rho)$$

The factor $(1 + \rho)$ accounts for the data transfer *plus* the average coherence overhead.

### Step 3 — Sum the load across all $P$ processors.
Assuming independent request streams, the total offered bus load is:

$$T_{total} = P \cdot \lambda \cdot S_{req} \cdot (1 + \rho)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the bandwidth saturation constraint.
The bus can carry at most $B_{bus}$ bytes/sec. For the system to remain stable (no queue overflow):

$$P \cdot \lambda \cdot S_{req} \cdot (1 + \rho) \leq B_{bus}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the maximum sustainable request rate.
Rearranging the inequality for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{B_{bus}}{P \cdot S_{req} \cdot (1 + \rho)}$$

### Step 6 — Derive the per-processor effective bandwidth.
The per-processor effective bandwidth is $\lambda_{max} \cdot S_{req}$:

$$B_{per\_proc} = \lambda_{max} \cdot S_{req} = \frac{B_{bus}}{P \cdot (1 + \rho)}$$

### Step 7 — Identify the scalability ceiling.
Letting $P \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{P \to \infty} B_{per\_proc} = 0$$

This proves that **bus-based SMP cannot scale indefinitely** — bandwidth per processor vanishes as $P$ grows. This is the fundamental reason SMP architectures are eventually replaced by distributed memory at large scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plug-in example:** If $B_{bus} = 1$ GB/s, $P = 8$, $\rho = 0.25$, then $B_{per\_proc} = 1 / (8 \cdot 1.25) = 0.1$ GB/s = 100 MB/s per processor. Doubling $P$ to 16 halves it to 50 MB/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NUMA Remote Access Latency for a Hypercube Network

**Problem Statement:** A distributed memory multiprocessor uses a **$d$-dimensional hypercube** network. Each node has local memory access time $T_{local}$ = 50 ns, and each network hop takes $T_{hop}$ = 20 ns. Directory lookup adds $T_{dir}$ = 30 ns per remote access. Derive the average remote access time for a uniform random access pattern.

### Step 1 — Recall hypercube topology.
In a $d$-dimensional hypercube with $P = 2^d$ nodes, the number of hops between any two distinct nodes is the **Hamming distance** of their binary addresses, ranging from 1 to $d$.

### Step 2 — Compute the average Hamming distance.
For two uniformly random $d$-bit addresses, each bit differs with probability $\frac{1}{2}$, and bit differences are independent. The expected Hamming distance is:

$$E[h] = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \frac{1}{2} = \frac{d}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $d = \log_2 P$.

$$E[h] = \frac{\log_2 P}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the average remote access time.

$$T_{remote} = T_{local} + E[h] \cdot T_{hop} + T_{dir}$$

$$T_{remote} = T_{local} + \frac{\log_2 P}{2} \cdot T_{hop} + T_{dir}$$

### Step 5 — Plug in numerical values for $P = 16$ (i.e., $d = 4$).

$$E[h] = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ hops}$$

$$T_{remote} = 50 + 2 \cdot 20 + 30 = 50 + 40 + 30 = 120 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 6 — Compare with local access.

$$\text{Ratio } R = \frac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.4 \times$$

This quantifies the **NUMA penalty**: a remote access is 2.4× slower than a local access on a 16-node hypercube.

### Step 7 — Project to $P = 1024$ ($d = 10$).

$$E[h] = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ hops}$$

$$T_{remote} = 50 + 5 \cdot 20 + 30 = 180 \text{ ns}$$

$$R = \frac{180}{50} = 3.6 \times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** As the system scales, the **average** NUMA penalty grows logarithmically, not linearly. This is why hypercube and torus topologies (logarithmic diameter) are preferred over rings or buses (linear diameter) for large-scale distributed systems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Centralised Shared Memory (SMP / UMA) | Distributed Memory | NUMA (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory Location** | Single central module | Local to each node | Distributed but logically shared |
| **Address Space** | Single shared | Multiple private | Single shared (logically) |
| **Access Time** | **Uniform** (constant) | **Non-uniform** (message-dependent) | **Non-uniform** (hop-dependent) |
| **Communication** | Shared variables (loads/stores) | **Message passing** (MPI, PVM) | Shared variables via directory |
| **Coherence** | **Hardware bus-snooping** (MESI) | Not needed (private) | **Hardware directory** |
| **Programming** | Threads + locks (easier) | Explicit messages (harder) | Threads + affinity hints |
| **Scalability** | Poor (8–32 typical) | Excellent (1000s of nodes) | Good (64–256 typical) |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited by shared bus/switch | Scales linearly with $P$ | Scales sub-linearly |
| **Latency** | Low and predictable | High and variable | Medium and variable |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor (single memory = SPOF) | High (node failures isolated) | Moderate |
| **Cost per Node** | Higher (shared infrastructure) | Lower (commodity parts) | Moderate |
| **Examples** | Intel Xeon servers, AMD EPYC, Mac M-series | Beowulf clusters, TOP500 MPPs | SGI Origin, AMD EPYC dual-socket, Intel multi-socket |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The comparison table above is the most likely **"Compare and Contrast"** question format. Memorise the **six most important distinctions**: (1) memory location, (2) access uniformity, (3) communication mechanism, (4) coherence protocol, (5) scalability, and (6) programming model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor (Bus-Based SMP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC0["Processor P0"]
        CPU0["CPU Core 0"]
        L10["L1 Cache"]
        L20["L2 Cache"]
    end
    subgraph PROC1["Processor P1"]
        CPU1["CPU Core 1"]
        L11["L1 Cache"]
        L21["L2 Cache"]
    end
    subgraph PROC2["Processor P2"]
        CPU2["CPU Core 2"]
        L12["L1 Cache"]
        L22["L2 Cache"]
    end
    subgraph PROC3["Processor P3"]
        CPU3["CPU Core 3"]
        L13["L1 Cache"]
        L23["L2 Cache"]
    end
    BUS["Shared System Bus\nArbitrator + Coherence Engine"]
    MEM["Centralised Main Memory\nSingle DRAM Bank"]
    CPU0 --> L10 --> L20
    CPU1 --> L11 --> L21
    CPU2 --> L12 --> L22
    CPU3 --> L13 --> L23
    L20 --> BUS
    L21 --> BUS
    L22 --> BUS
    L23 --> BUS
    BUS <--> MEM
    BUS -.Snoop.-> L20
    BUS -.Snoop.-> L21
    BUS -.Snoop.-> L22
    BUS -.Snoop.-> L23
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All four processor-cache modules connect to a single shared bus. Dashed arrows represent **snooping** — every cache controller monitors bus traffic to maintain coherence via the MESI protocol.

---

## 4.2 Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor (Crossbar-Based SMP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0["P0 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P1["P1 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P2["P2 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P3["P3 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P4["P4 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P5["P5 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P6["P6 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    P7["P7 with L1/L2 Cache"]
    XBAR["Crossbar Switch Fabric\nP x M Full Connectivity"]
    M0["Memory Bank 0"]
    M1["Memory Bank 1"]
    M2["Memory Bank 2"]
    M3["Memory Bank 3"]
    P0 --- XBAR
    P1 --- XBAR
    P2 --- XBAR
    P3 --- XBAR
    P4 --- XBAR
    P5 --- XBAR
    P6 --- XBAR
    P7 --- XBAR
    XBAR --- M0
    XBAR --- M1
    XBAR --- M2
    XBAR --- M3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A crossbar switch enables **simultaneous** P-to-M connections, eliminating the single-bus bottleneck at the cost of $O(P \cdot M)$ switch complexity. Memory is still **physically centralised** even though banked.

---

## 4.3 Distributed Memory Multiprocessor (Cluster / MPP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N0["Node 0"]
        CP0["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM0["Local Memory\nPrivate Address Space"]
        NIC0["Network Interface"]
    end
    subgraph N1["Node 1"]
        CP1["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM1["Local Memory\nPrivate Address Space"]
        NIC1["Network Interface"]
    end
    subgraph N2["Node 2"]
        CP2["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM2["Local Memory\nPrivate Address Space"]
        NIC2["Network Interface"]
    end
    subgraph N3["Node 3"]
        CP3["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM3["Local Memory\nPrivate Address Space"]
        NIC3["Network Interface"]
    end
    NET["Interconnection Network\nEthernet / Infiniband / Torus / Fat-Tree"]
    CP0 --- MEM0
    CP0 --- NIC0
    CP1 --- MEM1
    CP1 --- NIC1
    CP2 --- MEM2
    CP2 --- NIC2
    CP3 --- MEM3
    CP3 --- NIC3
    NIC0 --- NET
    NIC1 --- NET
    NIC2 --- NET
    NIC3 --- NET
    NET -.Message Passing.-> NIC0
    NET -.Message Passing.-> NIC1
    NET -.Message Passing.-> NIC2
    NET -.Message Passing.-> NIC3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each node owns its private memory and CPU. The only way to share data is via **explicit message passing** across the network. No hardware coherence exists because address spaces are private.

---

## 4.4 NUMA Distributed Shared Memory (with Directory Coherence)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph N0["Node 0 NUMA"]
        CPU0["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM0["Local Memory\nPart of Shared Address Space"]
        DIR0["Directory Controller\nTracks remote sharers"]
    end
    subgraph N1["Node 1 NUMA"]
        CPU1["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM1["Local Memory\nPart of Shared Address Space"]
        DIR1["Directory Controller"]
    end
```
