```mermaid
    subgraph N2["Node 2 NUMA"]
        CPU2["CPU + L1/L2 Cache"]
        MEM2["Local Memory\nPart of Shared Address Space"]
        DIR2["Directory Controller"]
    end
    NW["High-Speed Interconnect\nHypercube / Torus / Mesh"]
    CPU0 --- MEM0
    CPU0 --- DIR0
    CPU1 --- MEM1
    CPU1 --- DIR1
    CPU2 --- MEM2
    CPU2 --- DIR2
    DIR0 <--> NW
    DIR1 <--> NW
    DIR2 <--> NW
    NW -.Remote Load/Store.-> DIR0
    NW -.Remote Load/Store.-> DIR1
    NW -.Remote Load/Store.-> DIR2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Memory is **physically distributed** across nodes, but the system presents a **logically shared address space** to software. Coherence is maintained via **distributed directory controllers** (not bus snooping) that track which nodes cache each line.

---

## 4.5 Unified Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SMA["Shared Memory Architecture"]
    DMA["Distributed Memory Architecture"]
    UMA["Centralised SMP / UMA\nBus or Crossbar\nSingle Memory\nHardware Snooping Coherence\nEasy Programming\nLimited Scalability 8 to 32"]
    NUMA["Distributed Shared Memory / NUMA\nInterconnection Network\nMemory Banked Per Node\nDirectory Based Coherence\nModerate Programming Effort\nScalability 64 to 256"]
    MPP["Pure Distributed / Cluster / MPP\nInterconnection Network\nPrivate Local Memory\nNo Hardware Coherence\nMessage Passing MPI\nMassive Scalability 1000 plus"]
    SMA --> UMA
    SMA --> NUMA
    DMA --> NUMA
    DMA --> MPP
```

> **Reading the diagram:** NUMA sits at the **intersection** of shared and distributed memory — it is *logically* shared (programmer's view) but *physically* distributed (hardware reality). Pure distributed clusters (MPP) are the most scalable but hardest to program.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between centralised shared memory multiprocessors and distributed memory multiprocessors with respect to memory organisation, access time uniformity, and communication mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralised Shared Memory (SMP) | Distributed Memory |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Organisation** | Single centralised main memory shared by all processors | Memory is physically distributed; each node has its own local memory |
| **Access Time Uniformity** | **Uniform** — every processor takes the same time to access any memory location (UMA) | **Non-uniform** — local access is fast, remote access is slower (NUMA / message-passing) |
| **Communication Mechanism** | Implicit, via **shared variables** (load/store instructions) | Explicit, via **message passing** (send/receive primitives) |

*(1 mark for each correct contrasting aspect. Award full 3 marks if all three are clearly contrasted.)*

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the cache coherence problem? Why does it arise specifically in centralised shared memory multiprocessors and not in pure distributed memory systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cache coherence problem** (1 mark) is the challenge of ensuring that all processor caches maintain a **consistent view of shared memory**, so that no processor reads a **stale value** after another processor has updated that memory location.

It arises in **centralised shared memory multiprocessors** (1 mark) because all processors share a **single address space** and can cache the same memory line simultaneously in private caches; without coordination, a write by one processor would not be visible to the others.

It does **not** arise in **pure distributed memory systems** (1 mark) because each node's memory is **private** — there is no shared data cached in multiple nodes' caches, so no coherence is required at the hardware level.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic architecture of a centralised shared memory multiprocessor (SMP)**. Discuss the role of the shared bus, cache coherence, and the memory bandwidth bottleneck in limiting scalability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **basic architecture of a distributed memory multiprocessor**. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages compared to centralised shared memory, with specific reference to scalability, programming complexity, and communication latency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Centralised Shared Memory Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
A Centralised Shared Memory Multiprocessor consists of $P$ processors ($P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{P-1}$), each with its private **L1 cache** (and optionally L2 cache). All processors connect via a **shared system bus** (or crossbar switch) to a **single centralised main memory** unit. The bus also carries **snooping signals** for cache coherence. A block diagram should clearly show: P processors → caches → shared bus → main memory, with snooping arrows from the bus back to each cache controller.

**Step 2 — Role of the Shared Bus (1 mark):**
The shared bus is the **sole communication medium** between processors and memory. It is arbitrated to allow only one transaction at a time, and it serves as the **broadcast medium** for coherence messages (invalidations, updates). All processors see every transaction.

**Step 3 — Cache Coherence (2 marks):**
Because multiple private caches may hold copies of the same memory line, the system must enforce coherence using a protocol like **MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid)**:
- **M**: Line is modified in this cache (only copy, dirty).
- **E**: Line is exclusive (only copy, clean).
- **S**: Line is shared (other caches may also have it).
- **I**: Line is invalid.

When a processor writes to a line in state S, it issues a **Bus Invalidate**; all other caches with the line transition to I. The writer transitions to M.

**Step 4 — Memory Bandwidth Bottleneck (2 marks):**
The shared bus has a **finite bandwidth $B_{bus}$**. Per-processor effective bandwidth decreases as $P$ increases:

$$B_{per\_proc} = \frac{B_{bus}}{P \cdot (1 + \rho)}$$

where $\rho$ is the coherence-induced extra traffic fraction. As $P \to \infty$, $B_{per\_proc} \to 0$, making the system **memory-bandwidth bound**. This is the fundamental scalability limit of bus-based SMP, typically capping useful processors at 8–32.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Distributed Memory Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
A distributed memory multiprocessor consists of $P$ independent **nodes**. Each node contains a processor, private L1/L2 caches, **local main memory**, and a **network interface controller (NIC)**. Nodes are interconnected by a **high-speed network** (torus, hypercube, fat-tree, Infiniband). The defining property is that **no memory is shared at the hardware level**.

**Step 2 — Communication via Message Passing (2 marks):**
Processors communicate by **explicitly sending and receiving messages**:
- **Send**: Processor copies data from local memory to NIC buffer; NIC injects a packet into the network.
- **Receive**: Remote NIC DMA-transfers the packet into the recipient's local memory; recipient is notified by interrupt or polling.
- Standard APIs: **MPI (Message Passing Interface)**, PVM, GASNet.

**Step 3 — Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Scalability**: Adding a node adds its local memory bandwidth; total bandwidth scales near-linearly with $P$.
- **Fault tolerance**: Failure of one node does not crash the whole system.
- **Cost**: Built from commodity parts; no expensive shared infrastructure.
- **Memory capacity**: Aggregate memory grows with $P$.

**Step 4 — Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Programming complexity**: Programmer must explicitly partition data and orchestrate communication.
- **Communication latency**: Message round-trips are orders of magnitude slower than shared-memory loads ($\mu$s vs. ns).
- **No transparent shared memory**: Hard to port shared-memory code; data must be replicated or redistributed.
- **Load balancing difficulty**: Uneven work distribution causes severe slowdown.

---

### Question B-B. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **UMA (Symmetric Multiprocessor)** and **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)** architectures in terms of memory organisation, access latency, coherence mechanism, and scalability. Use diagrams to support your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distributed memory multiprocessor uses a **4-dimensional hypercube** network. Each node has local memory access time $T_{local}$ = 60 ns, and each network hop takes $T_{hop}$ = 15 ns. The directory lookup adds $T_{dir}$ = 25 ns per remote access. Compute:
- (i) The number of nodes in the system.
- (ii) The **average** remote access time for a uniform random access pattern.
- (iii) The **worst-case** remote access time.
- (iv) The **NUMA penalty ratio** $R = T_{remote\_avg} / T_{local}$.
- (v) Comment on the scalability implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — UMA vs NUMA Comparison (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | UMA (Centralised SMP) | NUMA (Distributed Shared Memory) |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Organisation** | Single centralised memory | Memory banked and distributed across nodes |
| **Access Latency** | **Uniform** for all locations | **Non-uniform** — local fast, remote slow |
| **Coherence Mechanism** | **Bus snooping** (MESI) — simple, low latency | **Directory-based** — scalable, higher latency |
| **Interconnect** | Shared bus or crossbar | High-speed network (mesh, torus, hypercube) |
| **Scalability** | Poor: 8–32 processors | Good: 64–512 processors |
| **Programming Model** | Threads + shared variables | Threads + memory affinity hints |
| **Hardware Cost** | High for large $P$ (bus saturates) | Moderate, scales better |
| **Example** | Quad-core laptop CPU | Intel multi-socket servers, AMD EPYC dual-socket |

**Diagrams (2 marks):**
Provide the SMP bus-based diagram and the NUMA directory-based diagram (see SECTION_4 above).

**Key Insight (1 mark):**
NUMA is the **bridge** between SMP and pure distributed memory. It preserves the **shared address space** of SMP for programming ease, but distributes the **physical memory** to scale bandwidth.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Hypercube Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Network: 4-dimensional hypercube, so $d = 4$.
- $T_{local} = 60$ ns.
- $T_{hop} = 15$ ns.
- $T_{dir} = 25$ ns.

**Step 1 — Number of Nodes (1 mark):**

$$P = 2^d = 2^4 = 16 \text{ nodes}$$

**Step 2 — Average Number of Hops (1 mark):**

For a $d$-dimensional hypercube, the average Hamming distance between two uniform random nodes is:

$$E[h] = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ hops}$$

**Step 3 — Average Remote Access Time (2 marks):**

$$T_{remote\_avg} = T_{local} + E[h] \cdot T_{hop} + T_{dir}$$

$$T_{remote\_avg} = 60 + 2 \cdot 15 + 25 = 60 + 30 + 25 = 115 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Remote Access Time (1 mark):**
The worst case is when the two nodes are diametrically opposite, with Hamming distance $h = d = 4$:

$$T_{remote\_worst} = 60 + 4 \cdot 15 + 25 = 60 + 60 + 25 = 145 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — NUMA Penalty Ratio (1 mark):**

$$R = \frac{T_{remote\_avg}}{T_{local}} = \frac{115}{60} \approx 1.92$$

**Step 6 — Scalability Comment (1 mark):**
The NUMA penalty is only $1.92\times$ for a 16-node system, which is very manageable. However, if we extend to $d = 10$ ($P = 1024$ nodes), $E[h] = 5$ and $R = (60 + 5 \cdot 15 + 25)/60 = 160/60 \approx 2.67\times$. The penalty grows **logarithmically**, not linearly, which is why hypercube/torus topologies are scalable. Pure local-memory placement by the OS scheduler is therefore critical for NUMA performance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "shared address space" with "shared physical memory."** Many students write that NUMA has "shared memory" — *logically* yes, but *physically* it is distributed. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention cache coherence protocols by name.** Writing "coherence is maintained" without naming **MESI, MOESI, MSI, or directory protocol** loses 1–2 marks. Always name the protocol.
>
> 3. **Confusing the bandwidth formula.** The formula $B_{per\_proc} = B_{bus}/(P \alpha)$ is for **SMP contention**, not for distributed memory (where bandwidth *increases* with $P$). **(-1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts.** A 7-mark question in KTU 2024 scheme typically expects at least **one labelled diagram**. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up UMA with NUMA in NUMA problems.** When a problem says "remote access," students often apply UMA's uniform latency. Always check whether the system is UMA or NUMA before plugging in values. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Not stating units in numerical answers.** Writing "average time = 115" without "ns" is considered incomplete in KTU valuation. **(-0.5 mark)**
>
> 7. **Writing Amdahl's Law in memory architecture questions.** Amdahl's Law concerns **computational serial fraction**, not memory bandwidth. Use it only when the question is about *computational* parallelisation. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centralised vs Distributed Memory Multiprocessors**

### Centralised Shared Memory (SMP / UMA)
- [x] **Single** physical memory module shared by **all** processors.
- [x] **Uniform** Memory Access time (UMA) — every location is equally far.
- [x] Communication via **shared variables** — `load`/`store` instructions.
- [x] Requires **hardware cache coherence** — typically **MESI** protocol via **bus snooping**.
- [x] Interconnect: **shared bus** (limited) or **crossbar switch** (scalable but expensive).
- [x] **Scalability limited** to ~8–32 processors due to bandwidth bottleneck.
- [x] Programming model: **threads + locks/semaphores** (easiest model).
- [x] Examples: Multicore CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Zen), small servers.

### Distributed Memory Multiprocessor
- [x] **Each node has its own private local memory**; no shared physical memory.
- [x] **Non-uniform** access time (NUMA) or pure message passing (clusters).
- [x] Communication via **explicit message passing** (MPI, PVM).
- [x] **No hardware coherence** needed for pure clusters (private address spaces).
- [x] NUMA variant uses **directory-based coherence** to present a shared logical view.
- [x] Interconnect: **high-speed network** — torus, hypercube, fat-tree, Infiniband.
- [x] **Scales to thousands of nodes** (TOP500 supercomputers).
- [x] Programming model: harder — requires **data partitioning** and explicit communication.
- [x] Examples: Beowulf clusters, MPPs, HPC supercomputers (Frontier, Fugaku).

### Key Formulae to Memorise
- [x] **SMP bandwidth:** $B_{per\_proc} = \dfrac{B_{bus}}{P \cdot (1 + \rho)}$
- [x] **NUMA latency:** $T_{remote} = T_{local} + h \cdot T_{hop} + T_{dir}$
- [x] **Hypercube average hops:** $E[h] = \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{\log_2 P}{2}$
- [x] **NUMA penalty ratio:** $R = \dfrac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}}$
- [x] **Message latency:** $T_{msg} = T_{startup} + \dfrac{N}{B_{net}}$

### Must-Know Terminology (One-Liners)
- [x] **UMA** = Uniform Memory Access = SMP = Centralised Shared Memory.
- [x] **NUMA** = Non-Uniform Memory Access = Distributed memory + shared address space + directory coherence.
- [x] **MPP** = Massively Parallel Processor = Pure distributed memory + message passing.
- [x] **MESI** = Modified / Exclusive / Shared / Invalid — the SMP coherence protocol.
- [x] **Snooping** = broadcast-based coherence (cheap but unscalable).
- [x] **Directory** = lookup-based coherence (scalable but more complex).
- [x] **Coherence** = same memory location, same value, same time.
- [x] **Consistency** = same memory location, same value, in some defined order.

### Comparative Snapshot for Quick Recall

| **Pick SMP when...** | **Pick Distributed when...** |
|---|---|
| Workload is **threaded**, small to medium scale | Workload is **data-parallel**, massive scale |
| Latency-critical, cache-friendly | Throughput-critical, embarrassingly parallel |
| Easier programming preferred | Performance-per-watt and scalability critical |
| Coarse-grained locking is acceptable | MPI / PGAS programming expertise available |
| Budget allows high-end shared infrastructure | Commodity hardware acceptable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"If the memory is in one box, it's SMP. If the memory is in many boxes, it's distributed. If it logically looks shared but is physically in many boxes, it's NUMA. The bus is the bottleneck; the directory is the saviour; the message is the messenger."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Cache Coherence Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Cache Coherence Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cache Coherence Problem** is a fundamental challenge in shared-memory multiprocessor systems where each processor maintains its own local cache of main memory contents. The problem arises when **two or more cached copies of the same memory location exist** in different private caches, and one processor performs a write operation to its local copy, causing the other cached copies (and main memory) to become **stale or inconsistent**.

Formally, a memory system is defined to be *coherent* if it satisfies all three of the following invariants (Goodman's formal definition):

1. **Write Propagation (Update Propagation):** A write to a memory location by processor $P_i$ must eventually become visible to all other processors $P_j$ (where $j \neq i$) that have a cached copy of that location.
2. **Transaction Serialization:** Writes to the *same* memory location must be observed in the *same order* by all processors. That is, if two writes $W_1$ and $W_2$ to address $A$ are issued in that order by any processor, every other processor must observe $W_1$ before $W_2$.
3. **Read Consistency (Coherence):** A read by processor $P_i$ from address $A$ following a write by $P_i$ to $A$ must return the value written by $P_i$ (program order preservation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The cache coherence problem is a **high-priority Module 4 topic** under the unit *Memory Hierarchy in Multi-Core Systems*. Questions are frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) as direct definitions and in Part B (14 marks) combined with coherence protocols (MESI / MSI) and directory-based schemes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shared Whiteboard Problem**

Imagine a classroom with **5 students**, each holding a personal **photocopy** of the same shared whiteboard. A new announcement is written on the original whiteboard (main memory).

- Student A **modifies his personal photocopy** (write to local cache) to reflect the new note.
- Students B, C, D, and E are still holding **outdated photocopies** that contradict the original whiteboard and Student A's copy.

Now, when the teacher asks, *"What is the current announcement?"*, students will give **conflicting answers** depending on which photocopy they read. The **cache coherence problem** is precisely this situation in silicon — multiple caches holding inconsistent copies of the same memory line.

The **Cache Coherence Protocol** is the set of classroom rules (e.g., *"raise your hand before you change your copy, others must destroy or update theirs"*) that enforce **eventual consistency** among all copies.

### 1.3 Why the Problem Exists — Root Causes

| Cause | Explanation | Engineering Reality |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Cache Replication** | Each core has a private L1/L2 cache holding line copies | Modern CPUs have $4$–$128$ cores |
| **Write-Back Policy** | Writes update only the cache, not main memory | Reduces memory traffic, but defers propagation |
| **Independent Cache Controllers** | Each cache acts autonomously without global coordination | Hardware scalability limit reached at $\approx 8$ cores for snooping |

### 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics / Terminology

- **Line (Block):** Unit of cache transfer, typically **32 B, 64 B, or 128 B**.
- **Coherence Granularity:** Size of the smallest addressable coherence unit — usually a **cache line**.
- **Coherence Point:** The logical entity (bus / directory) that orders and broadcasts coherence transactions.
- **False Sharing:** Performance degradation when unrelated variables reside in the *same* cache line, causing unnecessary invalidations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Coherence Lifecycle

The cache coherence problem unfolds through a structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Read Sharing (Initial State):** Multiple caches load the *same* line in the **Shared (S)** state. The line is consistent in all caches and main memory.
- **Step 2 — Write Request:** One processor (say $P_1$) intends to write to the line. It issues a **BusRdX** (Bus Read with Exclusive intent) transaction.
- **Step 3 — Coherence Action:** The coherence controller (snooper or directory) invalidates or updates all *other* cached copies.
- **Step 4 — Exclusive Ownership:** $P_1$ transitions the line to the **Modified (M)** state and performs the write locally.
- **Step 5 — Propagation:** When another processor $P_2$ later requests the line, $P_1$ supplies the **updated data** (write-back) and the system regains coherence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Step 3 is the *heart* of the solution. Without invalidating other copies, the next read by $P_2$ would return stale data, silently corrupting program semantics. Step 4 ensures $P_1$ has uncontested ownership so subsequent writes don't need repeated broadcasts.

### 2.2 Coherence vs. Consistency — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Cache Coherence | Memory Consistency |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Scope** | A *single* memory location | The *order* of operations across multiple locations |
| **Granularity** | Per-cache-line | Per-instruction / per-thread |
| **Guarantee** | All processors see the *same* value of one word at a time | All processors see memory operations in a globally agreed order |
| **Example Mechanism** | MESI, MSI, MOESI | Sequential Consistency (SC), TSO, Weak Ordering, Release Consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Many students confuse *coherence* with *consistency*. Memorize the line: **"Coherence is about a single address; Consistency is about the order of all addresses."**

### 2.3 Classes of Coherence Protocols

| Class | Mechanism | Scalability | Latency | KTU Reference |
|-------|-----------|-------------|---------|---------------|
| **Snooping (Bus-Based)** | All caches monitor a shared broadcast medium (bus / ring) | Poor beyond $\sim 8$ cores | Low (single-hop) | AMD Opteron, Intel Core i7 (early) |
| **Directory-Based** | Centralized directory tracks sharing list per line | Excellent ($\geq 64$ cores) | Higher (multi-hop) | Intel Xeon Phi, SGI Origin |
| **Token-Based** | Tokens circulated; coherence violation = missing token | Theoretical interest | Variable | Research |
| **Hybrid / Hierarchical** | Snooping within cluster, directory across clusters | Modern data centers | Balanced | AMD EPYC, recent Intel |

### 2.4 Write-Update vs. Write-Invalidate Trade-off

| Strategy | Action on write | Traffic Pattern | Best For |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|----------|
| **Write-Invalidate** | Mark all other copies as Invalid; only one cache holds Modified copy | **Low traffic** if reads dominate | Most workloads |
| **Write-Update** | Push the new value to all other caches immediately | **High traffic** if writes dominate | Rare; producer-consumer with broadcast |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Variables | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $\text{AMAT} = H_t + M_r \cdot P_{miss} \cdot (H_{mem} + H_{bus})$ | $H_t$ = cache hit time, $M_r$ = miss rate, $H_{mem}$ = memory hit time, $H_{bus}$ = bus arbitration | ns / cycles |
| 2 | **Coherence Miss Rate** | $M_{coh} = \dfrac{\text{Invalidation-induced misses}}{\text{Total memory references}}$ | Ratio of coherence misses to total refs | dimensionless |
| 3 | **False Sharing Penalty** | $T_{fs} = N_{cores} \cdot (t_{inv} + t_{reload})$ | $N_{cores}$ = contending cores, $t_{inv}$ = invalidation latency, $t_{reload}$ = reload latency | seconds |
| 4 | **Sharing Set Size (Directory)** | $S_{share} = \vert \{ P_j \mid j \neq i, \text{line } A \in \text{Cache}_j \} \vert$ | Number of caches with a copy | cores |
| 5 | **Snooping Bandwidth** | $B_{snoop} = N_{cores} \cdot f_{bus} \cdot W_{txn}$ | $f_{bus}$ = bus frequency, $W_{txn}$ = txn width | bits/s |
| 6 | **Directory Memory Overhead** | $O_{dir} = N_{lines} \cdot (\log_2 N_{cores} + \text{state bits})$ | Bits per line for sharing vector | bits |
| 7 | **State Count (MESI)** | $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$ | $M, E, S, I$ | states |
| 8 | **Coherence Traffic Ratio** | $\rho_{coh} = \dfrac{T_{snoop}}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{T_{snoop}}{T_{data} + T_{snoop}}$ | $T_{snoop}$ = coherence transactions, $T_{data}$ = data transfers | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute value bars inside LaTeX tables** to avoid breaking markdown column separators. Never use the literal `|` character in math expressions within tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multi-core CPUs** (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series) implement MESI/MOESI in hardware at L1/L2.
- **GPU Coherent Interconnects** (NVIDIA NVLink, AMD Infinity Fabric) extend coherence across chiplets.
- **HPC Systems** (Cray, Fujitsu Fugaku) rely on directory coherence for hundreds of thousands of cores.
- **Cloud KV-stores** (Redis, Memcached) implement *software-emulated* coherence for distributed caches, drawing directly on these hardware principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantitative False Sharing Penalty

We derive the performance penalty incurred when $N$ cores write to *different* variables that happen to reside in the *same* cache line (false sharing).

**Given:**
- $N = 4$ cores write concurrently.
- Each write causes an invalidation and reload on the other cores.
- Invalidation latency $t_{inv} = 40$ cycles.
- Reload latency $t_{reload} = 100$ cycles (L1 miss to L2 hit).

**Step 1:** Total penalty per write is the sum of invalidation and reload overhead on *remote* cores.

$$
T_{penalty} = t_{inv} + t_{reload}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values into the expression above.

$$
T_{penalty} = 40 + 100 = 140 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3:** When $N$ cores participate, each core experiences this penalty per write attempt. The aggregate stall time per contended line is the product of the per-core penalty and the number of contending cores.

$$
T_{fs} = N_{cores} \cdot T_{penalty}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $N_{cores} = 4$ and $T_{penalty} = 140$ cycles into the aggregate equation.

$$
T_{fs} = 4 \cdot 140 = 560 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5:** Express as a percentage slowdown assuming a useful computation time of $T_{useful} = 1000$ cycles per shared-line access.

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \dfrac{T_{useful} + T_{fs}}{T_{useful}} - 1
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the values into the slowdown expression.

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \dfrac{1000 + 560}{1000} - 1 = \dfrac{1560}{1000} - 1 = 0.56
$$

**Step 7:** Convert the ratio to a percentage to state the final result.

$$
\boxed{\text{Slowdown} = 56\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though no two cores share a *logical* variable, the cache-line aliasing causes a **56% performance loss**. This is precisely why production code pads structures to cache-line boundaries (`alignas(64)` in C++, `__declspec(align(64))` in MSVC).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Directory Memory Overhead

We compute the storage cost of a directory entry for a system with $N_{cores}$ and $N_{lines}$ main-memory lines.

**Step 1:** Each line needs an entry tracking which caches hold a copy (a *sharing vector*) plus state bits.

$$
O_{entry} = \log_2 N_{cores} + S_{state}
$$

**Step 2:** For MESI, $S_{state} = 2$ bits. Substitute $N_{cores} = 64$ into the formula.

$$
O_{entry} = \log_2 64 + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8 \text{ bits/line}
$$

**Step 3:** Total memory required for the directory scales linearly with the number of main-memory lines.

$$
O_{dir,total} = N_{lines} \cdot O_{entry}
$$

**Step 4:** For $N_{lines} = 2^{32}$ (4 GB system at 64 B/line).

$$
N_{lines} = \dfrac{2^{32} \text{ B}}{64 \text{ B}} = 2^{26} = 67{,}108{,}864 \text{ lines}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply the number of lines by the per-entry overhead to get the total directory memory.

$$
O_{dir,total} = 2^{26} \cdot 8 = 2^{29} \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 6:** Express as a percentage of the 4 GB main memory to show overhead magnitude.

$$
\text{Overhead} = \dfrac{64 \text{ MB}}{4 \text{ GB}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{64}{4096} \times 100\% = 1.5625\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Directory overhead is small ($\sim 1.6\%$), but grows as $\log_2 N_{cores}$. Beyond $N_{cores} = 1024$, designers switch to *compressed* or *hierarchical* directory schemes to control cost.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coherence-Induced AMAT Penalty

We extend the classic AMAT formula to account for coherence traffic in a $4$-core system.

**Step 1:** Start from the baseline AMAT formula.

$$
\text{AMAT}_{base} = H_t + M_r \cdot H_{mem}
$$

**Step 2:** Augment with a coherence miss term $M_{coh}$ (misses due to invalidations) having penalty $H_{coh}$.

$$
\text{AMAT}_{coh} = H_t + M_r \cdot H_{mem} + M_{coh} \cdot H_{coh}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values: $H_t = 1$ ns, $M_r = 0.05$, $H_{mem} = 50$ ns, $M_{coh} = 0.02$, $H_{coh} = 80$ ns.

$$
\text{AMAT}_{coh} = 1 + (0.05 \cdot 50) + (0.02 \cdot 80)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the two multiplication terms independently.

$$
= 1 + 2.5 + 1.6
$$

**Step 5:** Sum the three terms to obtain the final augmented AMAT.

$$
\boxed{\text{AMAT}_{coh} = 5.1 \text{ ns}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coherence contribution adds **1.6 ns (~31% of total AMAT)** — non-trivial, justifying hardware protocol design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — State Transition Behavior (MESI)

| Current State | Event | Next State | Bus Action | Reasoning |
|---------------|-------|------------|------------|-----------|
| **Invalid (I)** | PrRd | Shared (S) | BusRd | Load line, other caches may also have copy |
| **Invalid (I)** | PrWr | Modified (M) | BusRdX | Exclusive write, invalidate all others |
| **Shared (S)** | PrRd | Shared (S) | — | Read-only, no traffic |
| **Shared (S)** | PrWr | Modified (M) | BusRdX / BusUpgr | Invalidate others to gain exclusivity |
| **Exclusive (E)** | PrRd | Exclusive (E) | — | Still clean & exclusive |
| **Exclusive (E)** | PrWr | Modified (M) | — | Silent transition (no bus action) |
| **Modified (M)** | PrRd (other) | Shared (S) | Flush | Write-back to memory, share line |
| **Modified (M)** | PrWr (other) | Invalid (I) | Flush + BusRdX | Snoop conflict, surrender ownership |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — False Sharing vs. True Sharing

| Property | True Sharing | False Sharing |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Logical Variable** | Same variable accessed | Different variables |
| **Cache Line** | Same line | Same line (unintentional) |
| **Coherence Traffic** | Necessary | **Wasted** |
| **Performance Impact** | Inherent to algorithm | Eliminated by padding / alignment |
| **Example** | Counter incremented by all threads | Adjacent struct fields updated by different threads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coherence in a Multi-Core System

This block diagram illustrates how cache coherence traffic flows between cores, their private caches, the coherence controller, and main memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["CORE P1"] --> C1["L1 CACHE P1"]
    P2["CORE P2"] --> C2["L1 CACHE P2"]
    P3["CORE P3"] --> C3["L1 CACHE P3"]
    P4["CORE P4"] --> C4["L1 CACHE P4"]
    C1 --> BUS["SHARED COHERENCE BUS"]
    C2 --> BUS
    C3 --> BUS
    C4 --> BUS
    BUS --> CC["COHERENCE CONTROLLER SNOOPER OR DIRECTORY"]
    CC --> MM["MAIN MEMORY DRAM"]
    CC --> REP["REPLIES BUSRD BUSRDX INVALIDATE FLUSH"]
    BUS --> SNOOP1["SNOOP FILTER P1"]
    BUS --> SNOOP2["SNOOP FILTER P2"]
    BUS --> SNOOP3["SNOOP FILTER P3"]
    BUS --> SNOOP4["SNOOP FILTER P4"]
    SNOOP1 -.monitor.-> C1
    SNOOP2 -.monitor.-> C2
    SNOOP3 -.monitor.-> C3
    SNOOP4 -.monitor.-> C4
```

### 4.2 State Transition Flow — MESI Protocol

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stI : Cache Reset
    stI : INVALID
    stM : MODIFIED
    stE : EXCLUSIVE
    stS : SHARED
    stI --> stS : PrRd BusRd
    stI --> stM : PrWr BusRdX
    stS --> stM : PrWr BusRdX
    stE --> stM : PrWr silent
    stM --> stS : Snoop PrRd Flush
    stM --> stI : Snoop PrWr Flush BusRdX
    stE --> stS : Snoop PrRd
    stE --> stI : Snoop PrWr
    stS --> stI : Snoop Invalidate
```

### 4.3 Coherence Protocol Decision Flowchart — Write Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Core Issues Write to Line X"] --> B{"Current State of X"}
    B -->|Invalid| C["BusRdX on Bus"]
    B -->|Shared| D["BusUpgr or BusRdX"]
    B -->|Exclusive| E["Silent Transition to M"]
    B -->|Modified| F["Already Owner Write Locally"]
    C --> G["Other Caches Invalidate X"]
    D --> G
    G --> H["State becomes Modified"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I["Write Completes Coherent"]
```

### 4.4 Snooping vs. Directory — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SN["SNOOPING BASED"]
        SA["Core 1"] --> SB["Bus"]
        SC["Core 2"] --> SB
        SD["Core N"] --> SB
        SB --> SM["Main Memory"]
    end
    subgraph DR["DIRECTORY BASED"]
        DA["Core 1"] --> DB["Network"]
        DC["Core 2"] --> DB
        DD["Core N"] --> DB
        DB --> DE["Directory Controller"]
        DE --> DF["Main Memory"]
        DE -.tracks.-> DG["Sharing List per Line"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the snooping diagram, every cache monitors the bus (broadcast model). In the directory diagram, requests are point-to-point via the network, and the directory maintains a *sharing list* per line (e.g., *{Core 2, Core 5}*). The directory scales; the bus does not.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define the cache coherence problem. List any two conditions a memory system must satisfy to be considered coherent.**

**Model Answer:**

The **cache coherence problem** arises in shared-memory multiprocessor systems when multiple private caches hold copies of the same memory line, and a write to one copy leaves other copies (and main memory) inconsistent.

A memory system is coherent if it satisfies:

1. **Write Propagation:** A write by processor $P_i$ to address $A$ must eventually be visible to all other processors $P_j$ ($j \neq i$) that have a copy of $A$.
2. **Transaction Serialization:** Writes to the same location are observed in the same order by all processors.

*(Third condition — Read Consistency — may also be cited for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between true sharing and false sharing in the context of cache coherence.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | True Sharing | False Sharing |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Variable | Multiple cores access the *same* variable | Multiple cores access *different* variables |
| Cache Line | Same line (intended) | Same line (unintended aliasing) |
| Coherence Traffic | Necessary for correctness | Wasted; eliminated by padding |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **cache coherence problem** in detail with a suitable example. Discuss the three conditions that must be satisfied for a memory system to be coherent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **snooping-based** and **directory-based** coherence protocols in terms of scalability, latency, hardware overhead, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

The cache coherence problem arises when **two or more processors** in a shared-memory multiprocessor hold **cached copies of the same memory location**, and one of them updates its copy without informing the others. Consequently, when another processor reads its local copy, it observes a **stale (inconsistent) value**, violating program correctness.

**Example:**

- $P_1$ and $P_2$ both cache the variable $X$ (initially $X = 10$) from main memory.
- $P_1$ executes `X = X + 5` locally, so its cache now has $X = 15$.
- $P_2$ reads its cached $X = 10$ — **stale data**.
- Main memory still holds $X = 10$.

For a memory system to be **coherent**, three conditions must hold:

1. **Write Propagation:** $P_1$'s write of $X = 15$ must eventually reach $P_2$'s cache (or be served from $P_1$ on demand).
2. **Transaction Serialization:** If two writes to $X$ occur, every processor sees them in the same global order.
3. **Read Consistency:** $P_1$ reading $X$ after its own write must get $15$.

These are enforced by **hardware coherence protocols** (MESI, MOESI, directory-based schemes).

##### Model Solution — (b)

| Parameter | Snooping | Directory |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Mechanism** | Broadcast on shared bus; caches snoop | Point-to-point messages; directory tracks sharing |
| **Scalability** | Limited ($\sim 8$ cores); bus becomes bottleneck | Excellent ($64+$ cores, used in HPC) |
| **Latency** | Single-hop broadcast — low | Multi-hop lookup — higher |
| **Hardware Overhead** | Snoop logic per cache; bus arbitration | Directory memory ($\sim 1{-}3\%$ of main memory) |
| **Typical Use** | Small/medium multicore (Intel Core i7, Apple M1 L1) | Large-scale servers (Intel Xeon Phi, SGI Origin) |
| **Power** | High (every cache monitors every txn) | Lower (targeted messaging) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Bus failure = total loss | Directory can be replicated |

**Conclusion:** Snooping is favored for low-latency, small-scale systems; directory is mandatory when scalability beyond a few cores is required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MESI protocol** state diagram. List the bus transactions required for each transition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **false sharing**? Explain with an example how it degrades performance. Suggest a method to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

The **MESI protocol** is a write-invalidate cache coherence protocol with four states:

- **M — Modified:** The cache line is dirty (modified), exclusive to this cache, and main memory is stale.
- **E — Exclusive:** The cache line is clean, exclusive to this cache, and matches main memory.
- **S — Shared:** The cache line is clean and may be present in multiple caches.
- **I — Invalid:** The cache line is not present or is unusable.

**State Transition Table:**

| From → To | Trigger | Bus Transaction |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| I → S | PrRd | BusRd |
| I → M | PrWr | BusRdX |
| S → M | PrWr | BusRdX / BusUpgr |
| E → M | PrWr | None (silent) |
| M → S | Snoop PrRd | Flush (write-back) |
| M → I | Snoop PrWr | Flush + BusRdX |
| E → S | Snoop PrRd | Flush |
| S → I | Snoop Invalidate | None (passive) |

**Diagram (textual):**

```
            PrRd                 PrWr                PrWr (silent)
   I ───────────────▶ S ─────────────────▶ M ◀────────────── E
   ▲                  ▲                  │                  │
   │                  │                  │ Snoop PrRd       │ Snoop PrRd
   │ Snoop Invalidate │ Snoop            ▼                  ▼
   │                  │ PrRd             S ◀────────── E (via Flush)
   │                  │                               
   └──────────────────┴─────────────────────────────── (via various)
```

The key insight is that **E → M is silent** (no bus traffic), giving MESI an efficiency edge over MSI when a cache has sole clean ownership.

##### Model Solution — (b)

**False sharing** occurs when **two or more unrelated variables reside in the same cache line**, and **different processors** write to those variables independently. The coherence protocol sees line-level activity and **invalidates other caches unnecessarily**, causing severe slowdowns despite no logical data dependency.

**Example in C:**

```c
struct {
    int counter_A;   // written by Thread 0
    int counter_B;   // written by Thread 1
} shared;
```

If `int` is $4$ bytes and the cache line is $64$ bytes, both `counter_A` and `counter_B` lie in the *same* line. Every write by Thread 0 invalidates Thread 1's cache (and vice versa), generating **ping-pong invalidations**.

**Performance Degradation:** With $N=4$ threads, a workload taking $1$ second may balloon to $5{-}10$ seconds due to invalidation storms.

**Mitigation: Padding / Alignment**

```c
struct {
    int counter_A;
    char pad[60];    // pad to 64-byte boundary
    int counter_B;
} __attribute__((aligned(64)));
```

This places each counter in its **own cache line**, eliminating false sharing. Modern C++ uses `alignas(64)`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Coherence with Consistency.** Coherence is *per-address*; consistency is *globally across all addresses*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Omitting the "Why".** Simply stating *"MESI has four states"* without explaining **when each transition occurs and which bus transaction is generated** loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the Example.** A coherence answer *without* an illustrative example (stale-value scenario) is considered incomplete — lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting Write-Back Action.** When transitioning M → S, students often forget to mention **flushing the dirty data** to memory. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Treating False Sharing as True Sharing.** Clearly mark the distinction using a worked C-struct example.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cache Coherence Problem** = stale-data issue when multiple caches hold copies of one memory line.
- **Three Coherence Conditions:** Write Propagation, Transaction Serialization, Read Consistency.
- **Coherence vs. Consistency:** Per-line vs. global-order.
- **Protocol Classes:** Snooping (broadcast, scales poorly) vs. Directory (scalable, $\sim 1{-}3\%$ memory overhead).
- **MESI States:** Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid — **E → M is silent**, an efficiency win.
- **Write-Invalidate** is preferred over **Write-Update** for typical workloads (less traffic).
- **False Sharing** = different variables, same line, different cores → wasted invalidations.
- **Mitigation:** Cache-line alignment / padding (`alignas(64)`, `__attribute__((aligned))`).
- **AMAT Augmentation:** Add a coherence-miss term $M_{coh} \cdot H_{coh}$ to AMAT for multi-core analysis.
- **Directory Cost Formula:** $O_{dir,total} = N_{lines} \cdot (\log_2 N_{cores} + S_{state})$.
- **Exam Rule of Thumb:** Always pair a *definition* with a *numerical example or diagram* for full marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Schemes for Enforcing Coherence: Snooping Coherence Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Schemes for Enforcing Coherence: Snooping Coherence Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cache Coherence Problem

In a **shared-memory multiprocessor**, each processor typically has its own private cache. When two or more caches hold a copy of the same memory block, and one processor updates its copy, the other caches become **stale** (hold obsolete data). This contradiction of values is called the **Cache Coherence Problem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A memory system is **coherent** if the results of any execution of a program are such that:
> 1. **Write Propagation (Single-Writer, Multiple-Reader Invariant)** — A value written by processor **Pᵢ** must eventually become visible to processor **Pⱼ** if **Pⱼ** reads the location and there is no intervening write.
> 2. **Transaction Serialization (Write Serialization Invariant)** — All writes to the same memory location are observed in the **same total order** by every processor.

If even one of these two properties is violated, the memory system is **non-coherent**.

## 1.2 Why Coherence is Required

| Symptom | Root Cause | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Stale data in remote caches | No broadcast/invalidation on write | Incorrect program results |
| Inconsistent view of memory | No serialization of writes | Non-deterministic behavior in parallel programs |
| Lost updates | Writes silently overwritten | Race conditions, debugging nightmares |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU parlance, this is often called the **"multiple copies problem"** — the system must track *every* cached copy of a block, even though it is stored in physically separate SRAM arrays.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Office Whiteboard

Imagine **4 colleagues** sharing a wall-mounted whiteboard (the **main memory**). Each has a **personal notebook** (private cache).

- Suppose Anu writes "Meeting at 5 PM" in her notebook.
- If Binu, Chris, and Deepa do not know, they may still walk in at 4 PM.
- The whiteboard itself is correct, but the **distributed copies disagree**.

To fix this, the office adopts a **Snooping Scheme**:
- Whenever Anu updates her notebook, she **shouts on the office intercom**: "Erase your copy of the date 5 PM!"
- Everyone **listens (snoops)** to the intercom and invalidates their copy.
- Later, if Binu needs the date, he asks the whiteboard (main memory) for a fresh copy.

This is exactly what a **Snooping Coherence Protocol** does on a shared interconnect (typically a bus). The "shout" is a **bus transaction** (BusRdX / Invalidate); the "listening" is the **snoop controller** in every cache.

## 1.4 Two Architectural Families for Coherence

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus explicitly groups coherence schemes into two categories:**

1. **Snooping Protocols** (broadcast-based, this module's focus)
2. **Directory-Based Protocols** (centralized tracking, covered in Module 5)

The choice depends on **scale**: Snooping works well for small-to-medium systems (≤ 16–32 cores) where the interconnect can carry broadcast traffic; directory-based scales to hundreds of cores.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 System Model for Snooping

Every private cache has a **snoop controller** (often a simple finite state machine) that **monitors every transaction** appearing on the shared broadcast bus. The processor–cache pair is *not* the only master; the bus is shared media with arbitration.

**Operational Steps of a Snooping Protocol:**

1. **Transaction Issuance** — When processor Pᵢ performs a load/store to block X, its cache controller issues a *bus transaction* (e.g., `BusRd`, `BusRdX`, `Invalidate`, `Flush`).
2. **Bus Arbitration** — A central arbiter grants the bus to one transaction at a time.
3. **Snoop Phase** — *Every* other cache controller observes the transaction and checks if it has a copy of block X.
4. **Response** — Snooping caches either supply data, signal shared presence, or invalidate their copy.
5. **Completion** — The issuing processor either retries, completes, or marks the block in a new coherence state.
6. **Write-Back (if needed)** — A cache with the block in **Modified** state must `Flush` the block to memory on eviction.

## 2.2 Write-Update vs. Write-Invalidate

These are the **two fundamental write-handling policies** within snooping:

| Property | Write-Invalidate (WI) | Write-Update (WU) |
|---|---|---|
| Action on write | Broadcast **Invalidate** to all other caches | Broadcast new **data** to all other caches |
| Bus traffic | **One** transaction per write, then on first remote read | **One** transaction **per write** (continuous) |
| Local cache state after write | **Modified / Exclusive** | **Modified** (others become Shared) |
| Latency for writer | Low (no data transfer needed) | Higher (must arbitrate for bus) |
| Latency for readers | Pay miss penalty on next read | No miss on next read (already updated) |
| Scalability | Better (less bus usage in write-heavy loops) | Worse (writes dominate) |
| Used in practice | **Yes — most real systems (MESI, MOESI)** | Dragon protocol (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Modern processors universally adopt **Write-Invalidate** because writes in typical workloads *cluster* in time (temporal locality) but are *not* read immediately by all processors, so updates would be wasted bandwidth.

## 2.3 Snoop Consistency: The Two Sides of a Transaction

Each bus transaction has **two viewpoints** that must be kept consistent:

- **Issued by Pᵢ (Request side):** "I want block X in state S" (e.g., `BusRd` request).
- **Observed by Pⱼ, Pₖ (Snoop side):** "Pᵢ requested X — do I have it? In what state? Must I invalidate?"

This dual role of the bus is what gives the protocol its name: every controller is both a **master** (occasionally) and a **snoop** (continuously).

## 2.4 Sources of Cache Misses — The 3C + Coherence Model

The classic **3C model** (Hill, Smith) is extended to **4C** when coherence is added:

$$
\text{Total Misses} = \text{Compulsory} + \text{Capacity} + \text{Conflict} + \text{Coherence}
$$

**Coherence Misses are subdivided into two categories:**

- **True Sharing Misses** — Misses caused by a *legitimate* sharing of a data word that migrates between processors. Pᵢ writes a word; Pⱼ later reads it and must fetch the new value.

- **False Sharing Misses** — Misses caused by *unrelated* data words that happen to reside in the same cache block. Pᵢ writes word W₁, Pⱼ writes word W₂ (both in same block); the block "ping-pongs" between caches, but **no data is actually shared**.

$$
\text{Coherence Misses} = \text{True Sharing} + \text{False Sharing}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** False sharing is a *software* problem solvable by **data layout / padding / alignment**. True sharing is fundamental and reflects actual data dependencies. KTU often asks students to compute the false-sharing probability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Notation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{fs} = \dfrac{B-1}{B}$ | $B$ = block size (words) | dimensionless | Probability two independent accesses pick *different* words in the same block |
| 2 | $E[\text{fs misses per block}] = n(n-1) \cdot \dfrac{B-1}{B^2}$ | $n$ = processors, $B$ = words/block | misses/block | Expected false-sharing rate for $n$ concurrent writers |
| 3 | $AMAT = h + m \cdot p$ | $h$ = hit time, $m$ = miss rate, $p$ = miss penalty | cycles | Average Memory Access Time (3C/4C) |
| 4 | $\text{Miss Rate} = \text{Compulsory} + \text{Capacity} + \text{Conflict} + \text{Coherence}$ | rates per reference | fraction | 4C decomposition |
| 5 | $\text{Coherence Miss Rate} = \text{True Sharing} + \text{False Sharing}$ | fraction | fraction | Snoop-specific |
| 6 | Bus utilization (saturating) $U = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{cycle}} \to 1$ | time | unitless | Determines if snooping scales |
| 7 | $\text{Ratio}_{fs} = \dfrac{\text{False Sharing Misses}}{\text{Total Coherence Misses}}$ | ratio | $\vert x \vert \le 1$ | Measure of "wasted" invalidations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Validation Warning:** The probability formula $P_{fs} = (B-1)/B$ assumes **uniform random** access to any word in the block. With sequential or skewed access patterns, the analytical result must be adjusted; the KTU exam expects the uniform-random assumption unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: False-Sharing Probability Between Two Processors

**Setup.** A cache block contains $B$ words. Two independent processors P₁ and P₂ each access exactly one word of this block during a given time window. Accesses are **uniformly random** over the $B$ words. We want the probability that they pick *different* words, causing an invalidation ping-pong with no real data sharing.

**Step 1 — Sample space.**
Each processor picks one of $B$ words. The combined sample space has size $B^2$ ordered pairs $(w_1, w_2)$.

**Step 2 — Count of equal-word pairs.**
The number of pairs where both pick the *same* word is $B$ (one for each word).

**Step 3 — Count of unequal-word pairs.**
The number of pairs where they pick *different* words is $B^2 - B$.

**Step 4 — Compute probability.**

$$
P_{fs} = \frac{\text{Unequal pairs}}{\text{Total pairs}} = \frac{B^2 - B}{B^2} = \frac{B(B-1)}{B^2} = \frac{B-1}{B}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify boundary cases.**
- If $B = 1$ (one word/block): $P_{fs} = 0$ — correct, there is no "other word" to false-share with.
- If $B \to \infty$: $P_{fs} \to 1$ — any two random accesses will almost surely differ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Insight:** Designers combat false sharing by **padding** shared data structures (e.g., in OpenMP, `aligned` clauses, or per-thread counters in C++ `alignas(64)`). A cache line is typically **64 bytes** or **128 bytes** in modern x86/ARM systems, so padding wastes memory but eliminates ping-pong.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected False-Sharing Misses per Block with $n$ Processors

**Setup.** Extend to $n$ processors, each writing one word of the block, uniform random. We want the expected number of *false-sharing* events.

**Step 1 — Define a "false-sharing event".**
A false-sharing event for the pair $(P_i, P_j)$ occurs iff $P_i$ and $P_j$ pick *different* words. The number of such pairs is $C(n, 2) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

**Step 2 — Linearity of expectation.**
The expected total false-sharing events per block across all pairs is:

$$
E[\text{fs events}] = \binom{n}{2} \cdot P_{fs} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \cdot \frac{B-1}{B}
$$

**Step 3 — Alternative formulation per pair (used in KTU problems).**
Often the KTU exam asks for the **per-pair** rate:

$$
E_{pair} = n(n-1) \cdot \frac{B-1}{B^2}
$$

This counts ordered pairs rather than unordered, and normalizes by total $B^2$ ordered pair possibilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $n(n-1)/2$ vs. $n(n-1)$. Read the question: "*ordered pairs*" → use $n(n-1)$; "*unordered pairs*" → use $n(n-1)/2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Snoop Protocol Performance — Bus-Bandwidth Bound

**Setup.** A single shared bus of bandwidth $\beta$ (transactions/second) services $N$ processors. Each processor issues an average of $\lambda$ coherence transactions per second. Determine the maximum $N$ before saturation.

**Step 1 — Total demand.**
Total transactions per second on the bus:

$$
\Lambda_{total} = N \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Saturation condition.**
The bus saturates when demand equals capacity:

$$
N \cdot \lambda = \beta
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum number of processors.**

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\beta}{\lambda} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Practical adjustment.**
In real systems, only a fraction $f$ of bus cycles are usable (rest lost to arbitration, idle gaps, retransmits). So:

$$
N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{f \cdot \beta}{\lambda} \right\rfloor, \quad 0 < f \le 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** This is precisely why **directory-based protocols** (Module 5) replace broadcast snooping at large scale — broadcast demand scales as $O(N)$ and the bus saturates quickly. Snooping is fundamentally limited by the *single shared broadcast medium*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 3C + Coherence Decomposition (Worked Example)

**KTU-Style Problem.** A 4-core processor runs a parallel workload. The following per-1000-instructions miss rates are measured:

- Compulsory: **5** per 1000
- Capacity: **8** per 1000
- Conflict: **2** per 1000
- Coherence: **10** per 1000
  - True sharing: **4** per 1000
  - False sharing: **6** per 1000

**Step 1 — Total miss rate.**

$$
m_{total} = 5 + 8 + 2 + 10 = 25 \text{ misses per 1000 instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — True sharing as fraction of coherence misses.**

$$
f_{ts} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40 \quad (40\%)
$$

**Step 3 — False sharing as fraction of coherence misses.**

$$
f_{fs} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.60 \quad (60\%)
$$

**Step 4 — AMAT computation.**
Assume hit time $h = 1$ cycle and miss penalty $p = 100$ cycles:

$$
AMAT = h + m_{total} \cdot p = 1 + \frac{25}{1000} \cdot 100 = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — What if we eliminate false sharing?**
After padding to remove false sharing, coherence misses drop from 10 to 4. New total:

$$
m'_{total} = 5 + 8 + 2 + 4 = 19 \text{ per 1000}
$$

$$
AMAT' = 1 + \frac{19}{1000} \cdot 100 = 2.9 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Performance gain:**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{AMAT}} = \frac{3.5}{2.9} \approx 1.21 \text{ (i.e., 21% faster)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This calculation is a classic KTU exam ask** — students must demonstrate that **software-level fixes (padding) can improve hardware-level performance**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Snooping Multiprocessor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CP1["Core 1 Private L1"]
        P1["CPU Core 1"]
        C1["L1 Cache Controller + Snoop Engine"]
        T1["Tag Array incl State Bits M E S I"]
    end

    subgraph CP2["Core 2 Private L1"]
        P2["CPU Core 2"]
        C2["L1 Cache Controller + Snoop Engine"]
        T2["Tag Array incl State Bits M E S I"]
    end

    subgraph CP3["Core 3 Private L1"]
        P3["CPU Core 3"]
        C3["L1 Cache Controller + Snoop Engine"]
        T3["Tag Array incl State Bits M E S I"]
    end

    subgraph CP4["Core 4 Private L1"]
        P4["CPU Core 4"]
        C4["L1 Cache Controller + Snoop Engine"]
        T4["Tag Array incl State Bits M E S I"]
    end

    BUS["Shared Broadcast Bus Snooping Interconnect"]
    ARB["Central Bus Arbiter"]
    MEM["Main Memory Shared"]

    P1 <--> C1
    P2 <--> C2
    P3 <--> C3
    P4 <--> C4

    C1 <--> T1
    C2 <--> T2
    C3 <--> T3
    C4 <--> T4

    C1 -.Snoops all bus traffic.-> BUS
    C2 -.Snoops all bus traffic.-> BUS
    C3 -.Snoops all bus traffic.-> BUS
    C4 -.Snoops all bus traffic.-> BUS

    BUS --- ARB
    BUS --- MEM

    ARB -.Grants bus to one requestor at a time.-> BUS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Students:** Every cache controller is *bidirectionally* connected to the bus — it both **drives** bus transactions (when its own processor misses or writes) and **listens** to transactions issued by others. The arbiter serializes all transactions to maintain a single global order — this is the hardware foundation of the **write-serialization** property.

---

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram: MESI Snooping Protocol

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state "Modified" as stM
    state "Exclusive" as stE
    state "Shared" as stS
    state "Invalid" as stI

    [*] --> stI

    stI --> stE: PrRd BusRd and no other cache has block
    stI --> stS: PrRd BusRd and other caches have block Shared
    stI --> stM: PrWr BusRdX upgrade path write allocate

    stE --> stM: PrWr write hit silent transition
    stE --> stS: BusRd snoop by another cache supplies Shared
    stE --> stI: Flush or replacement Clean Evict

    stS --> stM: PrWr BusRdX Invalidate other caches
    stS --> stS: BusRd snoop by another cache remains Shared
    stS --> stI: Flush or replacement Clean Evict

    stM --> stS: BusRd snoop Flush data to memory then Shared
    stM --> stI: BusRdX snoop Flush data to memory then Invalidate
    stM --> stM: PrWr or PrRd silent local access
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Invariants of MESI:**
> 1. **Modified** is the *only* state where a cache may supply data that **differs from memory**. On eviction, the cache must `Flush` to memory.
> 2. **Exclusive** is a clever optimization: the block is *clean and unique* in the local cache. A local write is a **silent transition** ($E \to M$) with **no bus transaction** — saving invalidation traffic.
> 3. **Shared** implies the memory copy is *up-to-date*; multiple caches may hold clean copies.
> 4. **Invalid** simply means the block is not present.

---

## 4.3 Flowchart: Snoop Controller Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Bus Transaction Detected on Shared Bus"]
    DEC1{"Is address match in local tag array?"}
    NOACT["No action: ignore transaction"]
    DEC2{"What is current coherence state?"}
    ISHARED["State equals Shared S: provide data if needed and stay Shared or transition"]
    IEXCL["State equals Exclusive E: assert Shared on bus and transition to S"]
    IMOD["State equals Modified M: assert data on bus and Flush to memory then transition to S"]
    IINV["State equals Invalid I: no action taken"]
    FLUSH["Flush Write Back Modified block to memory"]
    INV["Invalidate local copy transition to I"]
    FIN["End snoop cycle return to idle"]

    START --> DEC1
    DEC1 -- No --> NOACT --> FIN
    DEC1 -- Yes --> DEC2
    DEC2 -- Shared --> ISHARED --> FIN
    DEC2 -- Exclusive --> IEXCL --> FIN
    DEC2 -- Modified --> IMOD --> FLUSH --> FIN
    DEC2 -- Invalid --> IINV --> FIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Students often forget the **assert data + flush** sequence for the Modified state. The snoop controller must (1) supply the correct (newer-than-memory) data to the requester, and (2) write the block back to memory because it is being downgraded to Shared/Invalid.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Write-Invalidate vs Write-Update Traffic Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WISeq["Write Invalidate Sequence Time T0 T1 T2"]
        A1["T0: P1 reads X. BusRd. All caches S."]
        A2["T1: P1 writes X. BusRdX Invalidate. P1 now M. Others go to I."]
        A3["T2: P2 reads X. BusRd. P1 Flushes supplies data. P1 downgrades to S. P2 fills S."]
    end

    subgraph WUSeq["Write Update Sequence Time T0 T1 T2"]
        B1["T0: P1 reads X. BusRd. All caches S."]
        B2["T1: P1 writes X. BusUpdate. P1 stays M. P2 P3 update their copies to S with new data."]
        B3["T2: P2 reads X. Cache hit in S. No bus traffic."]
    end

    WISeq ~~~ WUSeq
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Write-Invalidate defers data transfer until a remote *read* actually occurs (lazy); Write-Update performs the transfer immediately (eager). For workloads where writes are followed by remote reads, both are equivalent in cost; for write-heavy loops with no remote reads, **Invalidate is strictly better**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Write-Invalidate and Write-Update snooping protocols. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Write-Invalidate:** When a processor writes to a cached block, it broadcasts an `Invalidate` (or `BusRdX`) signal on the bus. All other caches with a copy transition to the **Invalid** state. Future reads by those processors will incur a cache miss and fetch updated data.
- **Write-Update:** When a processor writes, the **new data value itself** is broadcast on the bus. All other caches update their local copies in place, remaining in the **Shared** state.
- **Advantage — Write-Invalidate:** Generates **less bus traffic** in workloads with high write temporal locality, because only one bus transaction is required per write (not per remote read).
- **Advantage — Write-Update:** Remote processors that read the data soon after the write suffer **no cache miss**, since their copy is already updated.

*(Full marks awarded for: 1 mark for each protocol description, 1 mark for the two advantages — 3 total.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define false sharing. How does it differ from true sharing in a multiprocessor system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **False sharing** occurs when two or more processors access *different* data words that happen to reside in the **same cache block**, causing unnecessary block invalidations and misses even though no actual data is being shared.
- **True sharing** occurs when two or more processors access the **same data word** (or overlapping bytes), and the block must legitimately migrate between caches to keep values consistent.
- The key distinction: true sharing reflects *real data dependencies* (unavoidable for correctness); false sharing reflects *accidental co-location* (avoidable via software padding/alignment).
- **Example:** P₁ increments `counter_a`, P₂ increments `counter_b`; both in the same 64-byte block → block ping-pongs (false sharing).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | 14 Marks | Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a) Explain the MSI snooping protocol with a neat state transition diagram. Discuss the state transitions for the following events: (i) Processor Read, (ii) Processor Write, (iii) Bus Read, (iv) Bus Read Exclusive / Invalidate. [7 Marks]**

**(b) A 4-processor shared-memory multiprocessor uses a snooping coherence protocol. A cache block contains 8 words. All four processors simultaneously access (read-then-write) one randomly chosen word from the same block. Calculate:**
   1. **The probability of false sharing in a single block.**
   2. **The expected number of false-sharing events per block.**
   3. **The expected number of *true sharing* events per block.**
   4. **The ratio of false-sharing to true-sharing events.**
   **[7 Marks]**

---

#### (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**MSI States:**

- **Modified (M):** Block is present only in this cache, and the local copy is **dirty** (differs from memory). Cache must supply data on any other cache's request.
- **Shared (S):** Block is present in this and possibly other caches. The local copy is **clean** (matches memory).
- **Invalid (I):** Block is not present in this cache (or is present but marked unusable).

**State Transition Table for MSI (Processor & Bus Events):**

| Current State | Event | Next State | Bus Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | PrRd | S | BusRd (memory or another cache supplies) |
| I | PrWr | M | BusRdX (Invalidate others, get exclusive) |
| S | PrRd | S | None (silent hit) |
| S | PrWr | M | BusRdX (Invalidate others, upgrade) |
| M | PrRd | M | None (silent hit) |
| M | PrWr | M | None (silent hit) |
| S | BusRd (snoop) | S | Assert Shared signal on bus |
| S | BusRdX (snoop) | I | Invalidate local copy |
| S | Flush (snoop) | I | Discard local copy (memory now updated) |
| M | BusRd (snoop) | S | Write back block, supply data to requester |
| M | BusRdX (snoop) | I | Write back block, invalidate local copy |

**Mark Allocation:**
- State definitions — 1 mark
- Processor event transitions (PrRd, PrWr) — 2 marks
- Bus event transitions (BusRd, BusRdX, Flush) — 2 marks
- Neat state diagram — 1 mark
- Explanation of write-back/flush — 1 mark

---

#### (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $n = 4$ processors, $B = 8$ words per block. Each processor chooses a word **uniformly at random** and reads-then-writes it.

**(1) Probability of false sharing for a single pair of processors:**

For 2 processors choosing from $B = 8$ words independently, the probability of picking *different* words is:

$$
P_{fs}^{(2)} = \frac{B-1}{B} = \frac{8-1}{8} = \frac{7}{8} = 0.875
$$

**(2) Expected number of false-sharing events per block:**

The number of unordered processor pairs is:

$$
\binom{n}{2} = \binom{4}{2} = 6
$$

By linearity of expectation, the expected number of false-sharing events is:

$$
E[\text{fs events}] = \binom{n}{2} \cdot P_{fs}^{(2)} = 6 \cdot \frac{7}{8} = \frac{42}{8} = 5.25
$$

**(3) Expected number of true-sharing events per block:**

A true-sharing event for a pair $(P_i, P_j)$ occurs iff they pick the *same* word. The probability of that is:

$$
P_{ts}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

By linearity of expectation:

$$
E[\text{ts events}] = \binom{n}{2} \cdot P_{ts}^{(2)} = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75
$$

**Verification:** Total expected events $= 5.25 + 0.75 = 6.00 = \binom{4}{2}$ ✓ (every pair produces exactly one event, either true or false).

**(4) Ratio of false-sharing to true-sharing events:**

$$
\text{Ratio}_{fs \mid ts} = \frac{5.25}{0.75} = 7.0
$$

Or, equivalently, the false-sharing fraction of all coherence events:

$$
f_{fs} = \frac{5.25}{6.00} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A staggering **87.5%** of coherence invalidations in this scenario are *wasted* on false sharing. Software padding to separate the four counter variables into distinct cache lines would eliminate nearly all of this overhead — a 7× reduction in coherence traffic.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Probability formula and value (1 mark) — 1
- Pair count and false-sharing expectation (2 marks) — 2
- True sharing computation and verification (2 marks) — 2
- Ratio interpretation and engineering insight (2 marks) — 2

---

### Question B-B
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | 14 Marks | Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a) Explain the three key invariants that a coherent memory system must maintain. Describe how the MESI protocol (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) enforces them with special emphasis on the role of the *Exclusive* state. [7 Marks]**

**(b) A 2-processor SMP has a shared bus of bandwidth $\beta = 10^6$ transactions/second. Each processor issues coherence transactions at an average rate $\lambda = 10^4$ transactions/second. Compute:**
   1. **Total bus demand.**
   2. **Maximum number of processors before bus saturation, assuming 100% utilization.**
   3. **The same if only 60% of bus cycles are usable (the rest lost to arbitration overhead).**
   4. **Comment on the implications for scaling beyond this point using a snooping protocol.**
   **[7 Marks]**

---

#### (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Three Invariants of a Coherent Memory System:**

1. **Write Propagation Invariant:** A value written by processor Pᵢ to address X must eventually become visible to processor Pⱼ if Pⱼ reads X and no intervening write has occurred.
2. **Write Serialization Invariant:** Writes to the same memory location are observed in the **same total order** by every processor (no reordering of writes to a single address).
3. **No Invalid-Read Anomaly:** A processor must never read a stale value that contradicts a more recent write it has already observed.

**How MESI Enforces These:**

- **Write Propagation:** When a processor performs a `PrWr` to a block in state E or S, it issues `BusRdX` (Invalidate). All snooping caches with a copy transition to **I**, ensuring they will fetch the new value on their next read.
- **Write Serialization:** The shared bus arbiter grants the bus to **one transaction at a time**, creating a globally visible order. All caches observe the same transaction order on the bus, enforcing serialization.
- **No Invalid-Read Anomaly:** A cache supplying data on a `BusRd` snoop is **required** to also flush that data to memory, so subsequent remote reads (cache miss) will see the correct value.

**Special Role of the *Exclusive* State:**

- The **E** state captures the case where a block is **clean and unique** in one cache. Memory is up-to-date, but no other cache has a copy.
- **Optimization:** A processor writing to an **E**-state block performs a **silent transition** to **M** — **no bus transaction is required**, because no other cache has a copy to invalidate.
- **Contrast with MSI:** In MSI, an `S → M` write would require a `BusRdX` invalidate (even though no one has the block). The E state **saves bus bandwidth** for the common "first-touch write" pattern.
- **Detection:** When a `BusRd` returns data with **no Shared signal asserted** by any snoop, the requesting cache knows it is the unique holder and may mark the block as **E** (not just S).

**Mark Allocation:** Invariants (3 × 1 mark = 3), MESI enforcement mechanism (2 marks), Exclusive state explanation (2 marks).

---

#### (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\beta = 10^6$ transactions/sec, $\lambda = 10^4$ transactions/sec per processor.

**(1) Total bus demand for the 2-processor system:**

$$
\Lambda_{total} = N \cdot \lambda = 2 \cdot 10^4 = 2 \times 10^4 \text{ transactions/second}
$$

**(2) Maximum processors at 100% bus utilization:**

Setting $N \cdot \lambda = \beta$:

$$
N_{max} = \frac{\beta}{\lambda} = \frac{10^6}{10^4} = 100 \text{ processors}
$$

**(3) Maximum processors with only 60% usable bus cycles:**

Effective bandwidth is $f \cdot \beta = 0.6 \times 10^6 = 6 \times 10^5$ transactions/second.

$$
N'_{max} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^6}{10^4} = 60 \text{ processors}
$$

**(4) Implications for scaling:**

- A snooping protocol's scalability is **fundamentally bounded** by the shared broadcast bus.
- Beyond $N = 60$ (or $N = 100$ ideally), the bus saturates and coherence transactions queue indefinitely, destroying performance.
- The **bandwidth demand scales as $O(N)$** because every write must be broadcast to all caches.
- **Solution:** Replace snooping with a **directory-based protocol** (Module 5) for systems exceeding ~32–64 cores. The directory keeps a per-block list of sharers, avoiding global broadcast.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Part (1) computation — 1 mark
- Part (2) computation — 2 marks
- Part (3) with $f = 0.6$ — 2 marks
- Part (4) qualitative discussion of scaling — 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing PrRd/BusRd:** A *processor read miss* triggers a *bus read* — students often mix up which side initiates and which side responds.
> 2. **Forgetting the Flush on Modified snoop:** When state is **M** and another cache issues `BusRd`, the snoop controller must (a) supply data, (b) write back to memory, and (c) transition to **S** — skipping any of these three steps costs a mark.
> 3. **E state misnomer:** Some students treat E as identical to S. E is *clean* AND *unique*; S may be shared. The E→M silent transition is the entire point of having E.
> 4. **Coherence miss formula errors:** Writing $P_{fs} = (B-1)/B$ but then using $B$ in words without converting to bytes — a block of 32 bytes = 8 words (assuming 4-byte words). Always state units explicitly.
> 5. **Serialization vs. Coherence confusion:** *Coherence* is about a *single address*; *Consistency* (separate topic) is about *different addresses*. Don't conflate them in answers.
> 6. **Bus saturation arithmetic:** Forgetting to apply the utilization factor $f$ in scalability problems is the most common numerical error in KTU papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Snooping Coherence):**

- ✅ **Coherence Problem** = Multiple cached copies of the same block disagree after a write.
- ✅ **Two Invariants** = Write Propagation + Transaction Serialization.
- ✅ **Snooping** = All cache controllers observe every bus transaction; works for small-scale systems.
- ✅ **Bus Arbiter** = Single point of serialization; only one transaction at a time → enforces write ordering.
- ✅ **Write-Invalidate** is preferred over Write-Update in real systems (less traffic for write-clustered workloads).
- ✅ **MSI States:** Modified (dirty, unique), Shared (clean, possibly shared), Invalid (not present).
- ✅ **MESI States:** Adds **Exclusive** (clean, unique) → enables silent E→M write transition.
- ✅ **MOESI States:** Adds **Owned** (dirty, possibly shared) → avoids write-back to memory on every downgrade.
- ✅ **Modified cache must `Flush`** when downgraded to S or evicted — this is the *only* way memory learns of a write.
- ✅ **Coherence Misses** = True Sharing + False Sharing (subcategory of the 4C model).
- ✅ **False Sharing** = Different processors access *different words* in the same block; fixable with software padding.
- ✅ **True Sharing** = Different processors access the *same word*; reflects real data dependency.
- ✅ **Probability formulas:** $P_{fs} = (B-1)/B$ for two uniform-random accesses; $E[\text{fs}] = \binom{n}{2} \cdot P_{fs}$ for $n$ processors.
- ✅ **Bus saturation:** $N_{max} = f\beta/\lambda$; snooping scales as $O(N)$ and breaks down past ~32–64 cores.
- ✅ **Snoop-side responsibility:** Supply data + Flush on Modified snoop; Assert Shared on S snoop; Invalidate on BusRdX snoop.
- ✅ **Snooping vs. Directory:** Snooping uses broadcast (small-scale); Directory uses per-block sharer list (large-scale — covered in Module 5).
- ✅ **KTU favourite sub-questions:** state transition tables, false-sharing probability computations, M/E state optimization, bus-bandwidth bound derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Distributed Shared Memory and Directory-Based Coherence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Distributed Shared Memory and Directory-Based Coherence # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** is a parallel programming abstraction in which physically distributed memory modules across multiple nodes are presented to the programmer as a single, unified, globally shared logical address space. Each node has its own private cache and local memory, but coherence is maintained through hardware or software protocols so that any processor can access any memory location with the consistency semantics of a shared-memory multiprocessor.
