In essence, DSM is a hybrid design that combines the **scalability of message-passing systems** (where each node owns its own memory) with the **programming ease of shared-memory multiprocessors** (where a single address space is globally visible). ### Key Properties of DSM - **Single Physical Address Space:** Every memory location has a globally unique address. - **Distributed Physical Memory:** The memory is physically partitioned across nodes. - **Cache Coherence Required:** Since multiple nodes may cache the same line, a coherence protocol is mandatory. - **Location Transparency:** Programmer does not need to know *where* the data physically resides. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Cloud Whiteboard":** > Imagine a large office where 8 employees work in separate rooms. Each room has a personal notebook (local cache) and a personal filing cabinet (local memory). There is also a **central electronic whiteboard** (shared memory) visible to all. When employee A in Room 1 writes something on the whiteboard, employees in Room 2 and Room 3 must eventually see the same update. The "whiteboard synchronization rule" is the **coherence protocol** — it dictates *when* the others must refresh their notebooks. DSM is exactly this: a centralized *logical* view backed by *distributed* physical storage with strict update rules. ### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Block (Cache Line) Size:** Typically **64 Bytes** in modern systems. - **Memory Access Time (Local):** ~**50–100 ns** (DRAM). - **Remote Access Latency (DSM):** ~**200–1000 ns** (inter-node network hop + remote cache). - **Coherence Directory Size:** Proportional to (Memory Size / Block Size) × (Nodes). ## 1.2 Why Directory-Based Coherence? In small-scale multiprocessors (≤ 8–16 cores), **bus-based snooping protocols** work well: every cache broadcasts its transactions on a shared bus, and all other caches "snoop" to maintain coherence. However, as the system scales to **tens or hundreds of nodes**, broadcasting becomes infeasible because: - The shared bus becomes a **bottleneck** (single point of contention). - Broadcasting does not **scale** — bandwidth grows as O(N²) where N = number of nodes. - Energy per coherence transaction grows linearly with N. The solution is **Directory-Based Coherence**, in which a directory acts as a centralized bookkeeping structure that tracks **which caches hold a copy of each memory block**, and routes coherence messages only to the relevant nodes — avoiding broadcast entirely. ### Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Directory-Based Coherence** is a cache coherence protocol in which a directory entry (kept at the home node of each memory block) maintains a presence vector listing all nodes that currently cache the block. Coherence actions (invalidations, updates, data forwarding) are sent **only to the nodes in the presence vector**, eliminating the need for broadcast. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Library Borrowing Register":** > Instead of yelling across the library every time you borrow a book (snooping/broadcast), the librarian maintains a **register (directory)** for each book. The register lists the names of all people who currently have the book. If a new person wants it, the librarian looks at the register and contacts only those listed people — not everyone in the library. This is **directory-based coherence**: precise, targeted, scalable. ### Standard Metrics in Directory Protocols - **Directory Entry Size:** O(P) bits per block, where **P = number of processors** (presence vector). - **Network Traffic per Coherence Action:** O(P) in worst case (e.g., invalidation to all sharers). - **Directory Storage Overhead:** (Memory Size / Block Size) × P bits. --- # SECTION 1 END # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Motivation for DSM Modern multiprocessors fall into three architectural categories: | Architecture | Memory Model | Communication | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **UMA (Uniform Memory Access)** | Single shared memory | Shared bus / interconnect | Small SMP servers | | **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)** | Distributed but shared | Directory-based coherence | Modern multi-socket servers | | **Message Passing** | Private memory per node | Explicit send/receive | HPC clusters | DSM is essentially the **software/hardware realization of NUMA** programming models. ## 2.2 Directory Organization For each memory block, the directory stores the following: 1. **State field** — indicates current coherence state (e.g., Uncached, Shared, Modified, Exclusive). 2. **Owner pointer** — identifies which node currently holds the block in **Modified** state (if any). 3. **Presence vector / Sharer list** — a bit-vector of length P indicating which nodes have a valid copy. ### Common Directory States (Full-Map Directory) | State | Meaning | Presence Vector | |---|---|---| | **Uncached (U)** | No node has a copy. | All zeros | | **Shared (S)** | One or more nodes have a shared (read-only) copy. | Lists all sharers | | **Modified (M)** | Exactly one node has the block in Modified state. | Owner = single node | ## 2.3 Directory-Based Coherence Protocol — Operation Steps ### Step 1: Read Miss 1. Requesting node sends **Read** message to the **home node** of the block. 2. Home node consults the directory entry. 3. If state = **Uncached** or **Shared**: directory updates presence vector to include requester; home supplies data (or points requester to owner if state = Shared with data at another node). 4. If state = **Modified**: home forwards request to the **owner**; owner sends data to requester and changes its state to Shared; directory updates state to Shared and adds requester to presence vector. ### Step 2: Write Miss (Invalidate Protocol) 1. Requesting node sends **Write** message to home node. 2. Home node consults directory. 3. If state = **Uncached** or **Modified (and requester is owner)**: home grants Exclusive access; requester obtains block in Modified state. 4. If state = **Shared**: home sends **Invalidate** messages to **all nodes in the presence vector**; after acks are received, directory state changes to Modified, requester becomes owner, presence vector is cleared except for the new owner. ### Step 3: Write Hit (already in Modified state) - No coherence action required — silent transition. ### Step 4: Replacement (Eviction) by a Sharer - The evicting node sends a **Cache Flush / Write-Back** message to home if the block is Modified. - Directory updates its state and presence vector accordingly. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes all critical parameters and equations for DSM and Directory-Based Coherence. Master these for KTU 2024 ESE preparation. | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Expression | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | Directory Storage Overhead | $\text{MemSize} \div \text{BlockSize} \times P$ | Bits required for full-map directory (P = nodes) | | Presence Vector Size per Entry | $P \text{ bits}$ | One bit per possible sharer | | Total Directory Memory | $D = \dfrac{M}{B} \cdot (P + \log_2 P + k)$ | $M$ = total memory, $B$ = block size, $k$ = state bits | | Network Traffic per Invalidation | $O(P)$ | Worst-case message count | | Average Memory Access Time (NUMA) | $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{local} + (1-h) \cdot T_{remote}$ | $h$ = local access hit rate | | Coherence Miss Rate | $\text{MR}_{coh} = 1 - \text{Hit Rate}_{coh}$ | Fraction of accesses needing coherence action | | Scalability Limit (Snooping) | $N \le 16$ (approx.) | Broadcast bus bandwidth saturation point | | Bandwidth of Bus (Snooping) | $BW_{bus} = f_{bus} \times W$ | $f_{bus}$ = bus frequency, $W$ = bus width in bytes | | Protocol Actions per Write Miss (Shared → Modified) | $2P + 1$ messages | 1 Read, P Invalidates, P Acks (worst case) | ## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Directory-based coherence is the **backbone of all modern multi-core and multi-socket systems**: - Intel's **QPI / UPI** and AMD's **Infinity Fabric** use directory-based coherence for multi-socket servers. - **CCIX (Cache Coherent Interconnect for Accelerators)** extends directory coherence to heterogeneous computing. - **NVIDIA NVLink-C2C** in Grace Hopper employs similar principles for CPU–GPU coherence. Without directory coherence, scaling beyond a handful of cores would be impossible. --- # SECTION 2 END # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Directory Storage Overhead The goal is to compute the total memory required to store a full-map directory for a multiprocessor system. **Given:** - Total physical memory $M$ bytes - Block (cache line) size $B$ bytes - Number of processor nodes $P$ - State field width $k$ bits (e.g., 2 bits for 3 states) **Step 1 — Number of Memory Blocks:** The number of distinct memory blocks that can be cached is the total memory divided by the block size. $$ N_{blocks} = \dfrac{M}{B} $$ *Explanation:* Each memory block requires its own directory entry. If $M = 16\,\text{GB}$ and $B = 64\,\text{B}$, then $N_{blocks} = 2^{28}$ blocks. **Step 2 — Size of Each Directory Entry:** Each entry contains: - $P$ bits for the presence vector - $\lceil \log_2 P \rceil$ bits for the owner pointer (to identify the unique Modified owner) - $k$ bits for the state $$ S_{entry} = P + \lceil \log_2 P \rceil + k \quad \text{(bits)} $$ *Explanation:* The presence vector tells us *who* has a copy; the owner pointer tells us *who* has the only valid (Modified) copy; the state tells us *what kind* of copy they have. **Step 3 — Total Directory Storage:** Multiply the number of blocks by the size of each entry: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{total} &= N_{blocks} \times S_{entry} \\ &= \dfrac{M}{B} \times \left( P + \lceil \log_2 P \rceil + k \right) \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* This is the aggregate directory memory needed across all home nodes. **Step 4 — Convert to Practical Units:** Divide by 8 to get bytes: $$ D_{total} = \dfrac{M}{8B} \times \left( P + \lceil \log_2 P \rceil + k \right) \text{ bytes} $$ **Worked Example:** Let $M = 16\,\text{GB} = 2^{34}\,\text{B}$, $B = 64\,\text{B}$, $P = 16$, $k = 2$. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{blocks} &= \dfrac{2^{34}}{2^6} = 2^{28} \text{ blocks} \\ S_{entry} &= 16 + 4 + 2 = 22 \text{ bits} \\ D_{total} &= 2^{28} \times 22 \text{ bits} = 2^{28} \times 22 / 8 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 67{,}108{,}864 \times 2.75 \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 184.5\,\text{MB} \end{aligned} $$ *Interpretation:* Even for a 16-node system, the directory itself consumes **~184 MB** — a significant but acceptable overhead, since modern servers have terabytes of memory. ## 3.2 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time in NUMA/DSM **Given:** - $h$ = fraction of accesses satisfied by local memory - $1-h$ = fraction satisfied by remote memory - $T_{local}$ = local access latency - $T_{remote}$ = remote access latency (includes network traversal and remote directory lookup) **Derivation:** Average access time is a weighted mean of local and remote latencies: $$ T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{local} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{remote} $$ *Explanation:* A proportion $h$ of accesses are fast (local); the remaining $1-h$ are slower (remote). The expected time is the weighted sum. **Worked Numerical Example:** Let $h = 0.7$, $T_{local} = 80\,\text{ns}$, $T_{remote} = 400\,\text{ns}$. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avg} &= 0.7 \times 80 + (1 - 0.7) \times 400 \\ &= 56 + 0.3 \times 400 \\ &= 56 + 120 \\ &= 176\,\text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ *Interpretation:* Even with 70% local hits, the average latency is **176 ns** — dominated by the much larger remote penalty. This is why **data placement and first-touch scheduling** are critical in NUMA programming. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Snooping vs. Directory | Property | Snooping Protocol | Directory Protocol | |---|---|---| | **Scalability** | Poor (≤ 16 nodes) | Excellent (hundreds of nodes) | | **Network Traffic** | O(N) broadcast per action | O(P) targeted per action | | **Directory Storage** | None | $O(M/B \times P)$ bits | | **Latency for Miss** | Low (3 hops) | Higher (5–6 hops, dir lookup) | | **Implementation Complexity** | Simple (state machines) | Complex (directory management) | | **Power Consumption** | High (broadcast) | Lower (targeted) | | **Used In** | Small SMPs, single-socket | Modern multi-socket, HPC, data centers | --- # SECTION 3 END # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DSM System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture of a Distributed Shared Memory system with directory-based coherence. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["NODE A
CPU Core + L1 L2 Cache"] nodeB["NODE B
CPU Core + L1 L2 Cache"] nodeC["NODE C
CPU Core + L1 L2 Cache"] nodeD["NODE D
CPU Core + L1 L2 Cache"] homeMem["HOME NODE MEMORY
Local DRAM + DIRECTORY"] net["INTERCONNECT NETWORK
High Speed Coherent Link"] bus["SHARED ADDRESS SPACE
Globally Visible Logical Memory"] nodeA -->|Rd Wr Msg| net nodeB -->|Rd Wr Msg| net nodeC -->|Rd Wr Msg| net nodeD -->|Rd Wr Msg| net net --> homeMem homeMem -->|Data Response| net net -->|Forwarded Data| nodeA net -->|Forwarded Data| nodeB homeMem -.->|Directory State| bus net -.->|Coherence Traffic| bus ``` *Explanation:* Each node has private caches and sends read/write requests through the interconnect to the home node. The home node consults its directory and either supplies the data directly (if shared) or forwards the request to the current owner (if modified). ## 4.2 Directory Entry State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Uncached Uncached --> Shared : Read Miss by Node A
Add A to presence vector Uncached --> Modified : Write Miss by Node A
Set A as owner Shared --> Shared : Read Miss by Node B
Add B to presence vector Shared --> Modified : Write Miss by Node A
Send Invalidate to all sharers
Wait for Acks Shared --> Uncached : All sharers evict
Presence vector empty Modified --> Shared : Read Miss by Node B
Owner supplies data
State becomes Shared Modified --> Modified : Write Hit by owner
Silent transition Modified --> Uncached : Owner writes back
Data returned to home ``` *Explanation:* This state machine captures the three primary states (Uncached, Shared, Modified) and all legal transitions driven by coherence messages. ## 4.3 Directory-Based Coherence Read Miss Flow (Detailed) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Req as Requesting Node R participant Home as Home Node Directory participant Owner as Current Owner Node O Req->>Home: 1. Read Miss Message Home->>Home: 2. Check Directory State alt State is Modified Home->>Owner: 3. Forwarded Read Request Owner->>Req: 4. Data Forwarded Owner->>Home: 5. Ack with Shared state Home->>Home: 6. Update Presence Vector else State is Shared Home->>Req: 3. Data from Memory Home->>Home: 4. Add R to Presence Vector else State is Uncached Home->>Req: 3. Data from Memory Home->>Home: 4. Set State Shared end ``` *Explanation:* This sequence diagram shows the three branches of a Read Miss depending on the directory state, with all message exchanges clearly labeled. --- # SECTION 4 END # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Distributed Shared Memory. Mention any two advantages. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) is a parallel architecture in which physically distributed memory modules across multiple nodes are presented to the programmer as a single unified, globally shared logical address space. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Programming Simplicity:** Programmers can use the familiar shared-memory programming model (e.g., threads, OpenMP) without explicit message passing. 2. **Scalability:** Combines the scalability of distributed memory systems with the ease of shared memory, supporting hundreds of nodes through directory-based coherence. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between snooping and directory-based coherence protocols. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Snooping Protocol | Directory Protocol | |---|---|---| | Coherence Messages | Broadcast to all nodes | Targeted to specific sharers | | Scalability | Limited (≤ 16 nodes) | High (hundreds of nodes) | | Directory Storage | Not required | Required (presence vector per block) | | Network Traffic | High (O(N) per transaction) | Lower (O(P) worst case) | | Implementation | Simpler | More complex | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Distributed Shared Memory system. Discuss how coherence is maintained using a directory-based protocol. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Architecture:** A DSM system consists of $P$ compute nodes interconnected by a high-speed network. Each node contains one or more processor cores, private L1/L2 caches, and a portion of the global physical memory. The home node of a memory block maintains a **directory entry** for that block. **Diagram (textual representation):** ``` +----------+ +----------+ +----------+ | Node 0 | | Node 1 | | Node 2 | | CPU+L1+L2| | CPU+L1+L2| | CPU+L1+L2| +----------+ +----------+ +----------+ \ | / \ | / \ +-------------------+ / \ | Interconnect Net | / \ +-------------------+ / \ | / \ | / +-------------------+ | HOME NODE | | Memory + Dir | +-------------------+ ``` **Coherence Maintenance via Directory:** 1. **Read Miss:** Requesting node sends a Read message to the home node. The home directory is consulted. If state = Uncached or Shared, the data is returned and the requester is added to the presence vector. If state = Modified, the request is forwarded to the current owner, who supplies the data. 2. **Write Miss:** Requesting node sends a Write message to the home. If state = Shared, the home sends **Invalidate** messages to all nodes in the presence vector. After all acknowledgments, the directory state transitions to Modified and the requester becomes the new owner. 3. **State Transitions:** The directory entry maintains a state (Uncached, Shared, or Modified) and a presence vector that is updated on every coherence action. **Conclusion:** Directory-based coherence avoids broadcast and scales efficiently to large systems. --- **(b)** A 16-node DSM system has 8 GB of distributed memory with a 64-byte block size. Calculate the total directory storage required (in MB) if each directory entry uses a 16-bit presence vector, a 4-bit owner pointer, and a 2-bit state field. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $M = 8\,\text{GB} = 2^{33}\,\text{bytes}$ - $B = 64\,\text{bytes} = 2^6\,\text{bytes}$ - $P = 16$, owner pointer = 4 bits, state = 2 bits, presence vector = 16 bits **Step 1: Number of memory blocks** $$ N_{blocks} = \dfrac{M}{B} = \dfrac{2^{33}}{2^6} = 2^{27} \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 2: Bits per directory entry** $$ S_{entry} = 16 + 4 + 2 = 22 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3: Total directory storage in bits** $$ D_{total} = 2^{27} \times 22 = 22 \times 2^{27} \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4: Convert to MB** $$ D_{total} = \dfrac{22 \times 2^{27}}{8 \times 2^{20}}\,\text{MB} = \dfrac{22 \times 2^7}{1}\,\text{MB} = 22 \times 128\,\text{MB} = 2816\,\text{MB} $$ **Answer:** The total directory storage required is **2816 MB ≈ 2.75 GB**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the three states (Uncached, Shared, Modified) used in a directory-based coherence protocol. Describe the actions taken on a Read miss and a Write miss for each state. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Three Directory States:** 1. **Uncached (U):** No node currently holds a valid copy of the block in its cache. The presence vector is empty. 2. **Shared (S):** One or more nodes hold the block in their caches in a clean (read-only) state. The presence vector lists all sharers. The home node also holds a clean copy in memory. 3. **Modified (M):** Exactly one node (the owner) holds the block in Modified (dirty) state. The owner has the only valid copy; the home memory copy is stale. **Actions on Read Miss:** | Initial State | Action | |---|---| | **Uncached** | Home supplies data from memory. Directory state → Shared. Add requester to presence vector. | | **Shared** | Home (or any sharer) supplies data. Add requester to presence vector. State remains Shared. | | **Modified** | Home forwards request to owner. Owner supplies data to requester. State → Shared. Add requester to presence vector. Owner changes its cache state to Shared. | **Actions on Write Miss:** | Initial State | Action | |---|---| | **Uncached** | Home grants Exclusive access. Requester becomes owner. State → Modified. Presence vector = {requester}. | | **Shared** | Home sends Invalidate to all sharers. After all Acks, state → Modified. Requester becomes owner. Presence vector = {requester}. | | **Modified** (requester ≠ owner) | Home forwards request to owner. Owner writes back data to home. State → Modified. Requester becomes new owner. | | **Modified** (requester = owner) | Silent transition; no coherence action. | --- **(b)** Compare DSM with traditional message-passing systems. Discuss the role of directory-based coherence in enabling efficient scaling of shared-memory multiprocessors. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparison: DSM vs. Message Passing** | Aspect | DSM | Message Passing | |---|---|---| | Address Space | Single global | Multiple private spaces | | Communication | Implicit (memory reads/writes) | Explicit (send/receive) | | Programming Model | Threads, OpenMP, easy | MPI, more complex | | Data Sharing | Automatic via coherence | Manual data movement | | Performance Sensitivity | To data placement, locality | To network topology | | Hardware Support | Directory coherence required | Simple network interface | **Role of Directory-Based Coherence in Scaling:** 1. **Eliminates Broadcast Bottleneck:** Snooping requires O(N) broadcast per transaction. Directory restricts messages to O(P) targeted nodes, enabling scaling to hundreds of cores. 2. **Reduces Network Traffic:** Invalidation messages are sent only to actual sharers, not to all nodes. This dramatically reduces coherence bandwidth. 3. **Supports Distributed Directory Partitioning:** Each home node maintains the directory for its own memory, enabling natural NUMA partitioning and locality. 4. **Foundation for Modern Interconnects:** Industry standards like Intel UPI, AMD Infinity Fabric, and CCIX all rely on directory-based coherence to scale multi-socket and heterogeneous systems. 5. **Trade-off Management:** While directory storage adds overhead, this cost is amortized across the much larger system capacity it enables. **Conclusion:** Directory-based coherence is the **key enabler** of modern scalable shared-memory multiprocessors, allowing the industry to move from a few cores to hundreds without abandoning the productive shared-memory programming model. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing DSM with NUMA hardware-only:** DSM is a *programming abstraction*; NUMA is the *hardware topology* that supports it. State both clearly. > > 2. **Forgetting the state field in directory size calculation:** Always include the $k$ bits for state in the entry size formula. Many students compute only the presence vector. > > 3. **Not specifying which node is the "home":** In any directory protocol explanation, always clarify that the home node is the one that physically owns the memory block. The directory resides at the home. > > 4. **Mixing up "presence vector" and "owner pointer":** Presence vector = all sharers (multiple); Owner pointer = single Modified holder. KTU examiners specifically check this distinction. > > 5. **Skipping invalidation acknowledgment in Write Miss from Shared:** Examiners expect to see *Invalidate → Wait for Acks → State Transition* as a three-step process, not a single step. > > 6. **Unit conversion errors in numerical problems:** Always convert GB → bytes using $2^{30}$ (not $10^9$) and MB → bytes using $2^{20}$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **DSM** presents a unified logical address space over physically distributed memory. - **Directory-based coherence** is mandatory for scalable coherence beyond ~16 nodes. - **Three core states:** Uncached (U), Shared (S), Modified (M). - **Presence vector** = list of all sharers; **Owner pointer** = unique Modified holder. - **Read miss** adds requester to presence vector; **Write miss from Shared** requires **P invalidations** + **P acknowledgments**. - **Directory storage overhead** $= (M/B) \times (P + \log_2 P + k)$ bits. - **Snooping vs. Directory:** Snooping = broadcast (simple, unscalable); Directory = targeted (scalable, complex). - **NUMA average access time:** $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{local} + (1-h) \cdot T_{remote}$ — data placement matters critically. - Modern multi-socket systems (Intel, AMD, ARM) all use directory-based coherence. - **Block size is typically 64 bytes**; remember $B = 2^6$ for binary unit calculations. - Always state **which node is the home** when describing a coherence action. --- # SECTION 5 END

topics.title: Directory-Based Cache Coherence Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET522 - MODERN PROCESSOR ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Directory-Based Cache Coherence Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Directory-Based Cache Coherence Protocol** is a hardware-supported consistency mechanism used in **distributed shared-memory (DSM) multiprocessor architectures** where a logically centralized **directory structure** is maintained to keep track of the sharing status (presence and state) of every memory block across all private processor caches. The directory acts as the **single point of authority** that arbitrates reads, writes, invalidations, and write-backs among processing nodes connected via an **interconnection network** (e.g., bus, mesh, ring, or NoC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Directory protocols are a **snooping-free** alternative to broadcast-based protocols (MESI, MOESI), specifically designed to **scale** to large processor counts where broadcast traffic becomes a bottleneck.

The directory stores, for each memory block, the following critical metadata:

| Metadata Field | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Tag / Block Address** | Uniquely identifies the memory block being tracked. |
| **Dirty / Modified Bit** | Indicates whether any cache holds a *modified (exclusive-dirty)* copy. |
| **Presence Vector (Sharers List)** | A bit vector or pointer list identifying the caches that currently hold a valid copy. |
| **State Field** | One of: *Uncached, Shared (Clean), or Modified (Exclusive-Dirty)*. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Library Borrowing System:** Imagine a central library with one librarian (the **Directory Controller**) and several reading rooms (the **private L1/L2 caches**). When a student (a **processor core**) wants a book (a **memory block**), the librarian checks a register (the **directory entry**). If only one student has the book, that student is the "exclusive owner." If many students have it, the book is in a "shared" state. If a student wants to edit the book (a **write**), the librarian sends notes to all other students saying "return your copies, the new version is coming" (an **invalidate message**). This way, no two students edit the book simultaneously, and the librarian's register always reflects reality — this is the essence of directory-based coherence.

### 1.3 Physical & Architectural Metrics

Standard metrics associated with this protocol include:
- **Interconnection Latency:** typically **30 – 200 cycles** for directory lookups in modern multicore chips.
- **Directory Storage Overhead:** bounded by the equation $\text{Directory Size} = \text{Num\_Blocks} \times \text{Presence\_Vector\_Bits}$, where the vector length scales with the **number of processor cores $P$**.
- **Network Traffic Scaling:** **$O(P)$** per coherence transaction, in contrast to $O(P^2)$ for unordered broadcast snooping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Directory Protocol

The protocol operates through **three primary cache states** and **seven message types**, governed by a finite-state machine:

#### 2.1.1 Directory States (D-State Machine)

- **D-Uncached (UC):** No cache currently holds a valid copy of the block. Directory is the *only* owner.
- **D-Shared (S):** One or more caches hold the block in a *clean* (read-only) state. The main memory copy is up-to-date.
- **D-Modified (M):** Exactly one cache holds the block in *dirty* (modified) state. Main memory is *stale*; the owning cache is the source of truth.

#### 2.1.2 Message Types Between Cache ↔ Directory

1. **Read Miss (`RdMsg`):** Sent by cache to directory requesting a clean copy.
2. **Read-with-Intent-to-Modify / Write Miss (`WrMsg`):** Sent by cache requesting exclusive ownership.
3. **Invalidate (`InvMsg`):** Sent by directory to all other sharers to drop their copies.
4. **Invalidate Acknowledge (`InvAck`):** Sent by sharers back to the directory confirming invalidation.
5. **Data Write-Back (`WB`):** Sent by the dirty owner to main memory on replacement.
6. **Data Value Reply (`Data`):** Sent by directory (or owner) back to the requesting cache.
7. **Shared Signal (`Shared`):** Indicates the presence of other sharers (used for cache-state transitions).

#### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic for Common Transactions

- **Read Miss (Step-by-Step):**
   1. Why: The requesting cache (R) does not have the block or has it in *Invalid* state.
   2. How: R sends a `Read Miss` to the home directory.
   3. Directory checks state: If **D-Modified**, it forwards the request to the current owner (O), which supplies the data.
   4. Directory transitions to **D-Shared** and adds R to the sharers list.
   5. Data is delivered to R; R transitions to **S (Shared)** state.

- **Write Miss / Invalidate Transaction (Step-by-Step):**
   1. Why: R needs to perform a store to the block and currently has it in *S* or *I* state.
   2. How: R sends a `Write Miss` to the home directory.
   3. Directory consults the presence vector and sends `Invalidate` to all other sharers.
   4. Sharers reply with `InvAck` and drop their copies.
   5. If the block was **D-Modified**, the previous owner performs a *write-back* before the directory grants ownership.
   6. Directory transitions to **D-Modified** with R as the sole owner; R transitions to **M (Modified)**.

- **Replacement / Write-Back (Step-by-Step):**
   1. Why: A cache must evict a block to make room for a new line.
   2. How: If the block is *Modified*, the cache sends a `Write-Back` message to the directory along with the data.
   3. Directory updates its tag and state, then writes data to main memory.
   4. If no other cache holds the block, the directory transitions to **D-Uncached**.

### 2.2 Engineering Utility

Directory-based protocols form the **backbone of modern industrial-grade coherence systems**:
- They power **Intel's QPI / UPI-based Xeon** and **AMD's HyperTransport / Infinity Fabric** coherence domains.
- They enable **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)** architectures used in data-centre servers.
- They support **cc-NUMA (cache-coherent NUMA)** which is the de-facto standard in HPC clusters.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Directory Storage Overhead | $D = N_b \times P$ bits | $N_b$ = number of memory blocks, $P$ = number of processors |
| Network Traffic per Miss | $T \propto 2 + k$ | $k$ = current number of sharers |
| Scalability Limit (Full Map) | $O(P)$ storage, $O(1)$ lookup | Ideal but expensive |
| Coarse Vector Scalability | $O(P / S_d)$ storage, $O(S_d)$ lookup | $S_d$ = directory width |
| Bandwidth Saving vs. Snooping | $\text{Saved BW} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{P}$ | Approaches 1 for large $P$ |
| Coherence Latency (3-hop) | $L_{total} = L_{req} + L_{dir} + L_{reply}$ | Sum of request, directory, and reply latencies |
| Mean Memory Access Time | $t_{MMAT} = t_{hit} + (1 - h_{cache}) \times t_{miss}$ | $h_{cache}$ is local cache hit rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Break Rule:** In all LaTeX expressions, the absolute-value / divisibility bar is written as `\vert` (e.g., $N \vert k$), never as a raw pipe `|`, to preserve the markdown table integrity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Directory Storage for an $P$-Core Processor with a $B$-Byte Block Size

We derive the memory overhead required to store a **full-map presence vector** directory.

**Given:**
- Total main memory size: $M$ bytes
- Cache line / block size: $B$ bytes
- Number of processors (caches): $P$
- Each directory entry must record: 1 *dirty* bit, 1 *state* bit (binary), and $P$ *presence* bits.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Number of memory blocks in the system} \quad N_b &= \frac{M}{B} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Bits per directory entry} \quad E_b &= P + 2 \quad (\text{presence} + \text{dirty} + \text{state}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Total directory storage in bits} \quad D_{bits} &= N_b \times E_b = \left(\frac{M}{B}\right)(P + 2) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Convert to bytes} \quad D_{bytes} &= \frac{D_{bits}}{8} = \frac{M(P + 2)}{8B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For a 64-core system ($P=64$) with $M = 16$ GB memory and $B = 64$ bytes per block, the storage overhead is computed as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_b &= \frac{16 \times 2^{30}}{64} = 2^{28} \text{ blocks} \\[4pt]
E_b &= 64 + 2 = 66 \text{ bits per entry} \\[4pt]
D_{bits} &= 2^{28} \times 66 = 17{,}694{,}720 \text{ Kbits} \approx 16.5 \text{ Mbits} \approx 2.07 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the practical infeasibility of *full-map* directories for very large $P$, motivating **limited, sparse, or chained** directory variants.

### 3.2 Derivation: Coherence Transaction Latency for a 3-Hop Request-Reply

**Given:**
- Request propagation latency through the network: $L_{req}$
- Directory lookup + decision latency: $L_{dir}$
- Reply / data propagation latency: $L_{reply}$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{req} + L_{dir} + L_{reply} \\[4pt]
\text{With } L_{req} = L_{reply} = L_{net}, \quad L_{total} &= 2L_{net} + L_{dir}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an invalidate-based write miss with $k$ sharers, an additional $k$ invalidate-acknowledge round-trips occur:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{write} &= 2L_{net} + L_{dir} + k \times (2L_{net}) = (2k + 2)L_{net} + L_{dir}
\end{aligned}
$$

This linear dependence on $k$ is the chief motivation for **silent stores**, **invalidate coalescing**, and **region-based coherence** in real silicon.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Directory vs. Snooping)

| Parameter | Snooping Protocol | Directory Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Broadcast Requirement** | Yes (every transaction) | No (point-to-point) |
| **Scalability** | $O(P^2)$ bandwidth | $O(P)$ bandwidth |
| **Storage Overhead** | Per-cache state only | Per-block directory entry |
| **Latency (Small $P$)** | Lower (single-hop) | Higher (3-hop) |
| **Latency (Large $P$)** | Higher (contention) | Lower (no contention) |
| **Network Topology** | Bus / Ring | Any (mesh, torus, NoC) |
| **Implementation in** | Small multicore (≤ 8 cores) | Large server chips (≥ 16 cores) |
| **Consistency Point** | Shared bus arbiter | Central / distributed directory |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Directory Controller

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a directory controller and its interactions with the requesting cache, the owning cache, and main memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HomeNode["HOME NODE (Directory Controller)"]
        dirEntryA["Directory Entry A\nstate: Modified\nowner: P2"]
        dirCtrl["Directory Lookup\nand Decision FSM"]
        memCopy["Main Memory Copy\nof Block A"]
    end

    subgraph Requestor["REQUESTING PROCESSOR (P1)"]
        L1_P1["L1 Cache P1\nstate: Invalid"]
    end

    subgraph Owner["OWNING PROCESSOR (P2)"]
        L1_P2["L1 Cache P2\nstate: Modified"]
    end

    subgraph Network["INTERCONNECTION NETWORK (NoC)"]
        reqPath["Request Channel"]
        replyPath["Reply Channel"]
    end

    L1_P1 -- "Write Miss" --> reqPath
    reqPath --> dirCtrl
    dirCtrl -- "check entry" --> dirEntryA
    dirCtrl -- "Invalidate\nFetch Data" --> reqPath
    reqPath --> L1_P2
    L1_P2 -- "Data + InvAck" --> replyPath
    replyPath --> dirCtrl
    dirCtrl -- "Data + Grant" --> replyPath
    replyPath --> L1_P1
    dirCtrl -- "Write to Memory" --> memCopy
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram for a Three-State Directory Entry

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> D_Uncached
    D_Uncached --> D_Shared : Read Miss from P1
    D_Uncached --> D_Modified : Write Miss from P1
    D_Shared --> D_Modified : Write Miss + InvAcks received
    D_Shared --> D_Shared : Read Miss from P2
    D_Modified --> D_Shared : Write Back from owner\nno other sharers
    D_Modified --> D_Uncached : Write Back from owner\nno other sharers
    D_Modified --> D_Shared : Read Miss forwarded to owner
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Directory Organisation (Limited / Sparse Variants)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FullMap["Full Map Directory (Ideal, Costly)"]
        f1["One P-bit vector\nper memory block"]
    end

    subgraph Limited["Limited Directory (Tang)"]
        l1["Fixed K pointers\nper memory block"]
        l2["Overflow handled\nby broadcast"]
    end

    subgraph Sparse["Sparse Directory (Hill)"]
        s1["Entries allocated\non demand only"]
    end

    subgraph Chained["Chained Directory (Linked List)"]
        c1["Sharers linked\nvia pointer chain"]
    end

    FullMap --> Limited
    FullMap --> Sparse
    FullMap --> Chained
```

No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. All node labels are quoted strings containing raw uppercase alphanumeric text with `\n` line breaks — no markdown bold/italics or HTML is embedded.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q1.** Define *directory-based cache coherence protocol*. Why is it preferred over snooping protocols in large-scale multiprocessors?

**Model Answer:**
A directory-based cache coherence protocol is a hardware consistency mechanism in which a centralized or distributed **directory** maintains sharing information (presence vector and state) for every memory block across all processor caches. When a cache issues a read or write request, the directory controller decides the appropriate action (supply data, invalidate, or grant ownership) based on the recorded state.
It is preferred over snooping in large-scale systems because snooping requires broadcasting every coherence transaction on a shared bus, leading to $O(P^2)$ bandwidth consumption and bus saturation. Directory protocols use **point-to-point messages** with $O(P)$ bandwidth, enabling scalability to 64, 128, or more cores.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q2.** List any **three directory states** and **two message types** used in a typical directory-based coherence protocol.

**Model Answer:**
- **Directory States:** (i) D-Uncached, (ii) D-Shared (clean), (iii) D-Modified (dirty/exclusive).
- **Message Types:** (i) Read Miss, (ii) Invalidate.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Explain the three directory states (*Uncached, Shared, Modified*) with neat state transition diagrams. Discuss the actions taken by the directory on a **Read Miss** and a **Write Miss** originating from processor $P_1$, assuming the block is initially in the *Shared* state with sharers $\{P_2, P_3\}$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **directory storage overhead** for a system with $M = 8$ GB main memory, $B = 32$ bytes block size, and $P = 32$ processors. Comment on the scalability of full-map directories. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Directory States and Transitions (7 Marks)**

The three canonical directory states are:

- **D-Uncached (UC):** The block is present only in main memory; no private cache holds a valid copy.
- **D-Shared (S):** One or more caches hold the block in *clean* (read-only) state. Main memory copy is up-to-date.
- **D-Modified (M):** Exactly one cache holds the block in a *dirty* state (modified). This cache is the sole authority; main memory is stale.

**State transitions (textual diagram):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{UC} \xrightarrow{\text{Read Miss from } P_1} \text{S} \quad (\text{sharers} = \{P_1\}) \\
&\text{UC} \xrightarrow{\text{Write Miss from } P_1} \text{M} \quad (\text{owner} = P_1) \\
&\text{S} \xrightarrow{\text{Read Miss from } P_2} \text{S} \quad (\text{sharers} = \{P_1, P_2\}) \\
&\text{S} \xrightarrow{\text{Write Miss from } P_1 + \text{InvAcks}} \text{M} \quad (\text{owner} = P_1) \\
&\text{M} \xrightarrow{\text{Write Back, no sharers}} \text{UC} \\
&\text{M} \xrightarrow{\text{Read Miss from } P_2, \text{forwarded to owner}} \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Action on Read Miss from $P_1$ (block in S, sharers $\{P_2, P_3\}$):**
- Directory finds the block in **Shared** state and sees that $P_2$ and $P_3$ already hold it.
- Directory adds $P_1$ to the presence vector → sharers become $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$.
- Directory supplies the data (either from its own memory copy or from any sharer) to $P_1$.
- $P_1$ transitions its cache line to the **Shared** state.
- Directory remains in **D-Shared**.

**Action on Write Miss from $P_1$ (block in S, sharers $\{P_2, P_3\}$):**
- Directory sees the write miss and consults the presence vector $\{P_2, P_3\}$.
- Directory sends **Invalidate** messages to $P_2$ and $P_3$.
- $P_2$ and $P_3$ invalidate their copies and reply with **Invalidate Acknowledge**.
- Once all acks are collected, the directory transitions to **D-Modified** with $P_1$ as the sole owner.
- $P_1$ transitions its cache line to the **Modified** state.
- Directory entry: *sharers = $\{P_1\}$, state = M*.

**(b) Directory Storage Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $M = 8$ GB $= 8 \times 2^{30}$ bytes, $B = 32$ bytes, $P = 32$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_b &= \frac{M}{B} = \frac{8 \times 2^{30}}{32} = 2^{28} \text{ blocks} \\[4pt]
E_b &= P + 2 = 32 + 2 = 34 \text{ bits per entry} \\[4pt]
D_{bits} &= N_b \times E_b = 2^{28} \times 34 = 9{,}126{,}533{,}120 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
D_{bytes} &= \frac{D_{bits}}{8} \approx 1.06 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Scalability:** A full-map directory for an 8 GB memory system already requires over **1 GB** of dedicated storage — nearly 13% of the entire memory. For larger $P$ (e.g., 128 cores) the overhead grows linearly with $P$, making full-map directories impractical. This motivates **limited, sparse, and chained directory** variants, which trade off some precision (occasionally requiring broadcast) for vastly reduced storage.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
>
> **(a)** Compare **directory-based** and **snooping-based** coherence protocols across at least five parameters (bandwidth, scalability, latency, complexity, network requirement). Mention two real-world systems that use each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **full-map directory** organisation. Explain how a **limited directory** differs and why it is preferred in modern systems. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Snooping Protocol | Directory Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | High ($O(P^2)$) | Low ($O(P)$) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (≤ 8–16 cores) | Excellent (≥ 64 cores) |
| **Latency (small $P$)** | Lower (single bus hop) | Higher (3-hop) |
| **Latency (large $P$)** | Higher (bus contention) | Lower (no contention) |
| **Network Requirement** | Shared broadcast medium | Point-to-point / NoC |
| **Storage Cost** | Per-cache state only | Per-block directory entry |
| **Example Real Systems** | Intel Core i7 (older bus), AMD Opteron (early) | Intel Xeon (QPI/UPI), AMD EPYC (Infinity Fabric) |

**(b) Full-Map vs. Limited Directory (7 Marks)**

A **full-map directory** maintains a $P$-bit presence vector for every memory block, indicating exactly which caches hold a valid copy. The organisation includes:
- A **tag array** indexing each memory block.
- A **state field** (2 bits for UC/S/M).
- A **dirty bit** (1 bit).
- A **presence vector** of $P$ bits.

Its advantage is **O(1) lookup time** and precise invalidation targeting. Its disadvantage is the **$O(P)$ per-block storage**, which becomes prohibitive for large $P$.

A **limited directory** restricts the number of pointers per block to a constant $K \ll P$. When the number of sharers exceeds $K$, one of two strategies is used:
- **No-broadcast (overflow eviction):** One existing sharer is evicted (invalidated) to make room.
- **Broadcast fallback:** The directory falls back to broadcasting an invalidation, similar to snooping.

Limited directories are preferred in modern systems because they offer a **configurable storage-precision trade-off**, dramatically reducing directory memory overhead (often by 4×–8×) while only marginally affecting performance, since most blocks are shared by only a small number of cores (typically 1–4 in real workloads).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *directory states* with *cache states*.** The directory has its own FSM (UC / S / M) that is *separate* from the per-cache M/E/S/I states of MESI. Examiners deduct marks if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting to count the dirty bit and state field** when computing directory storage. The correct bits per entry is $P + 2$, not $P$.
> 3. **Omitting the InvAck round-trip** when describing a write miss. A student who only mentions sending invalidates — without the *acknowledgment collection* step — loses at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Using `$\vert$` pipe inside markdown tables.** This breaks table rendering. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX.
> 5. **Stating "directory protocols have no broadcast"** is *incorrect* for limited/sparse variants — they *may* fall back to broadcast on overflow.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core idea:** A directory is a *bookkeeping structure* for cache coherence, eliminating the need for global broadcast.
- **Three directory states:** *Uncached*, *Shared (clean)*, *Modified (dirty)*.
- **Seven message types:** Read Miss, Write Miss, Invalidate, Invalidate Ack, Data, Shared, Write-Back.
- **Scalability advantage:** Bandwidth grows as $O(P)$, not $O(P^2)$.
- **Storage cost:** Full-map needs $\dfrac{M(P+2)}{8B}$ bytes — often 5–15% of main memory.
- **Variants:** Full-Map (ideal), Limited (Tang), Sparse (Hill), Chained (Linked List) — each trading precision for storage.
- **Write miss flow:** Request → Invalidate all sharers → Collect InvAcks → Grant ownership.
- **Replacement:** Modified blocks must *write back* to the directory / memory before eviction.
- **Used in:** Intel Xeon (UPI), AMD EPYC (Infinity Fabric), ARM CCI (Cache Coherent Interconnect), IBM Power, NVIDIA NVLink-coherent GPUs.
- **Key formula to memorize:** $D_{bytes} = \dfrac{M(P+2)}{8B}$ and $L_{write} = (2k+2)L_{net} + L_{dir}$.
- **Examiner tip:** Always state *both* the directory-side action and the cache-side action for any transaction.
